topics.title: Overview of PMOS and NMOS Devices (Pre-Requisite)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of PMOS and NMOS Devices (Pre-Requisite)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a MOSFET?

The **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET)** is the fundamental building block of every modern VLSI chip. It is a **four-terminal voltage-controlled device** whose output current is regulated by an electric field applied at the gate terminal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A MOSFET is a unipolar, majority-carrier field-effect transistor in which the conductivity of a channel region between two ohmic contacts (Source and Drain) is modulated by a voltage applied to a gate electrode that is electrically isolated from the channel by a thin oxide layer (typically SiO₂).

The four terminals are:
1. **Gate (G)** – The control terminal (analogous to a tap's handle).
2. **Drain (D)** – The terminal where carriers enter/exit the channel.
3. **Source (S)** – The reference terminal (carriers exit/enter here).
4. **Body / Substrate / Bulk (B)** – The silicon wafer on which the device is built.

---

## 1.2 The Two Flavours: NMOS vs PMOS

There are **two complementary devices** in CMOS technology, distinguished by the type of charge carrier that forms the conducting channel:

| Feature | **NMOS Transistor** | **PMOS Transistor** |
|---|---|---|
| Channel Carriers | Electrons (n-type) | Holes (p-type) |
| Source/Drain Doping | n⁺ in p-type substrate | p⁺ in n-type substrate |
| Substrate Type | p-type (or p-well) | n-type (or n-well) |
| Majority Carrier Mobility (μ) | Higher (~1350 cm²/V·s for electrons) | Lower (~480 cm²/V·s for holes) |
| Threshold Voltage (V_t) | **Positive** (V_tn > 0) | **Negative** (V_tp < 0) |
| "ON" Condition | V_GS > V_tn | V_GS < V_tp |
| Current Direction | D → S (conventional) | S → D (conventional) |
| Symbol Arrow | Arrow points **inward** (into S) | Arrow points **outward** (out of S) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Mobility Asymmetry:** Because electrons are roughly **2.5× to 3× more mobile** than holes, an NMOS transistor can deliver the same drive current as a PMOS with only ~½ to ⅓ of the physical channel width. This is why NMOS transistors are **faster** and PMOS devices are typically made **wider** in standard-cell libraries.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model 💧

Imagine the MOSFET as a **pressurized water tap**:

- The **gate voltage (V_GS)** is your **hand pushing the tap handle** — the harder you push, the wider the opening.
- The **channel** is the **pipe opening** through which water flows.
- The **drain current (I_D)** is the **water flow rate** you get.
- The **threshold voltage (V_t)** is the **minimum effort** required just to begin opening the tap.

> **NMOS** = a tap where you **push down** to open it (positive pressure required).  
> **PMOS** = a tap where you **pull up** to open it (you need to lift against gravity to start flow).

In digital VLSI, the MOSFET is operated like a **light switch** — either **fully ON** (switch closed) or **fully OFF** (switch open), which is the essence of low-power CMOS logic.

---

## 1.4 Why Study This as a "Pre-Requisite"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU explicitly tags this topic as a **Pre-Requisite** because the entire VLSI design hierarchy — from a single inverter to a billion-transistor SoC — is constructed by **stacking and interconnecting NMOS and PMOS devices**. A rock-solid grasp of their I-V characteristics, regions of operation, and second-order effects is **non-negotiable** before tackling CMOS logic, sizing, stick diagrams, or layout design.

Physical constants you must memorize:
- Oxide permittivity of SiO₂: **ε_ox ≈ 3.9 × ε₀ ≈ 3.45 × 10⁻¹¹ F/m**
- Silicon permittivity: **ε_si ≈ 11.7 × ε₀ ≈ 1.04 × 10⁻¹⁰ F/m**
- Boltzmann constant: **k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- Thermal voltage at 300 K: **V_T = kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV** (often approximated as **26 mV**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Structure of a MOSFET

A MOSFET is fabricated on a silicon substrate. The classical structure has:
- A **lightly doped substrate (Body)** of opposite polarity to the source/drain.
- Two **heavily doped regions (S and D)** formed by ion implantation.
- A **thin gate oxide (SiO₂)** — historically ~100 nm, now down to **1–2 nm** in advanced nodes.
- A **gate electrode** (originally metal → polysilicon → now metal stacks with high-k dielectrics).
- Two **p-n junctions** between S/D and the body, which are **reverse-biased** during normal operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Body Terminal:** In a discrete MOSFET, the body is a separate pin. In an integrated circuit, the NMOS body is tied to GND (0 V) and the PMOS body is tied to V_DD. This implicit connection is what allows standard CMOS to work without a body contact on every transistor.

---

## 2.2 Regions of Operation — The Three Working Modes

The operating region of a MOSFET is decided by the relationship between **V_GS** and **V_t**, and between **V_DS** and **V_GS − V_t** (called **V_DSAT** or **V_OV**, the **overdrive voltage**).

### Region 1: Cutoff (Subthreshold)
- Condition: V_GS < V_t (NMOS) or V_GS > V_t (PMOS)
- **No conducting channel** exists at the oxide–silicon interface.
- I_D ≈ 0 (only a tiny subthreshold leakage flows — critical for modern low-power design).
- **Analogy:** The tap is **fully closed**.

### Region 2: Triode (Linear / Ohmic)
- Condition: V_GS > V_t **AND** V_DS < (V_GS − V_t)
- A **continuous conducting channel** is induced.
- The device behaves like a **voltage-controlled resistor**.
- I_D increases **linearly** with V_DS (for small V_DS).
- **Analogy:** The tap is **partially open**, water flows proportional to the pressure difference.

### Region 3: Saturation (Active)
- Condition: V_GS > V_t **AND** V_DS ≥ (V_GS − V_t)
- The channel is **pinched off** near the drain end.
- I_D becomes **nearly independent** of V_DS (ideally).
- The device behaves like a **voltage-controlled current source** — this is the regime used in analog amplifiers.
- **Analogy:** The tap is **wide open**; the flow is limited by the upstream pressure, not the downstream.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Region / Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oxide Capacitance per unit area | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | All regions | F/m² |
| 2 | Process Transconductance | $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ | NMOS | A/V² |
| 3 | Process Transconductance | $k_p' = \mu_p C_{ox}$ | PMOS | A/V² |
| 4 | Device Transconductance | $k_n = k_n' \cdot (W/L)$ | NMOS | A/V² |
| 5 | Device Transconductance | $k_p = k_p' \cdot (W/L)$ | PMOS | A/V² |
| 6 | Drain Current (Triode) | $I_D = k_n' \dfrac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{tn})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2}\right]$ | V_DS < V_GS − V_tn | A |
| 7 | Drain Current (Saturation) | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}\, k_n' \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{tn})^{2}$ | V_DS ≥ V_GS − V_tn | A |
| 8 | Drain Current (with λ) | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}\, k_n' \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{tn})^{2}(1+\lambda V_{DS})$ | Saturation, with CLM | A |
| 9 | Body-Effect Threshold | $V_{tn} = V_{tn0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB}\vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right)$ | All | V |
| 10 | Body-Effect Coefficient | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | All | V^½ |
| 11 | Small-signal g_m (sat) | $g_m = k_n' \dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{tn}) = \sqrt{2 k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} I_D}$ | Saturation | A/V |
| 12 | Small-signal r_o | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | Saturation | Ω |
| 13 | Intrinsic Gain | $A_v = g_m \cdot r_o = \dfrac{2(V_{GS}-V_{tn})}{\lambda V_{DS}}$ | Saturation | V/V |
| 14 | Subthreshold Slope | $S = n \cdot V_T \cdot \ln(10)$ | Subthreshold | mV/decade |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Priority for KTU Exams:** Formulas 6, 7, 8, 9, 11, and 12 are asked almost every semester. You **must** be able to write them cold, derive them, and pick the right one for the given V_GS / V_DS condition.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital design:** Transistors operate as **switches** — cutoff (OFF) and triode (ON) are the only states. Speed ∝ I_ON / C_load.
- **Analog design:** Transistors operate in **saturation**, where the square-law I-V is exploited for amplification (g_m, r_o).
- **Memory (SRAM/DRAM/Flash):** Charge stored on the gate or in the floating gate modulates V_t — a direct application of MOSFET physics.
- **RF design:** MOSFETs above ~10 GHz behave as distributed transmission-line elements, but the small-signal model (g_m, r_o, C_gs, C_gd) is still derived from the same square-law.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Threshold Voltage (V_t)

The **threshold voltage** is the gate-source voltage at which the surface potential (φ_s) becomes equal to **2φ_F** (twice the Fermi potential), causing **strong inversion** at the Si–SiO₂ interface.

### Step 1 — Define the surface potential at the onset of strong inversion

At the verge of strong inversion, the band bending ψ_s equals 2φ_F. The depletion-region charge per unit area at this moment is:

$$Q_{B0} = \sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, N_A \, (2\phi_F)}$$

Here:
- **q** = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C (electron charge magnitude)
- **N_A** = substrate doping concentration (cm⁻³)
- **ε_si** = 1.04 × 10⁻¹² F/cm
- **φ_F** = (kT/q) · ln(N_A / n_i) — the Fermi potential.

### Step 2 — Apply Gauss's Law at the oxide–silicon interface

The voltage drop across the oxide at threshold equals:

$$V_{ox} = \frac{Q_{B0}}{C_{ox}}$$

### Step 3 — Add flat-band voltage and surface potential contributions

The threshold voltage is the sum of three components:

$$V_{tn} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_{si} \, N_A \, (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

- **V_FB** = work-function difference − Q_ox/C_ox (flat-band voltage).
- **2φ_F** = the surface potential required to achieve strong inversion.
- **Third term** = voltage drop across oxide to support the depletion charge.

This is the **classical textbook V_t expression** that KTU expects you to write.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Drain Current in Triode Region

### Step 1 — Inversion-layer charge at a point x in the channel

At distance x from the source, the local gate-to-channel voltage is V_GS − V(x). The induced mobile charge per unit area is:

$$Q_i(x) = -C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V(x) - V_{tn}\right]$$

### Step 2 — Apply drift current equation at that point

The drift current in the channel (constant along the channel by KCL) is:

$$I_D = -W \, Q_i(x) \, v_{drift} = -W \, Q_i(x) \, \mu_n \, \frac{dV}{dx}$$

The negative signs cancel when we substitute Q_i and use the magnitude:

$$I_D \, dx = W \, \mu_n \, C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V(x) - V_{tn}\right] dV$$

### Step 3 — Integrate from source (x = 0, V = 0) to drain (x = L, V = V_DS)

$$I_D \int_{0}^{L} dx = W \, \mu_n \, C_{ox} \int_{0}^{V_{DS}}\left[V_{GS} - V_{tn} - V\right] dV$$

Performing the integration explicitly:

$$I_D \cdot L = W \, \mu_n \, C_{ox}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{tn})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2}\right]$$

Solving for I_D:

$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{tn})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2}\right]$$

This is the **triode-region I-V equation** (the second term in the bracket is often written as V_DS²/2; **never** skip it, or you'll lose marks).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Saturation Current (Square Law)

At the **edge of saturation**, V_DS = V_GS − V_tn. Substituting this into the triode equation:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{tn})^2 - \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{tn})^2}{2}\right]$$

Simplify the bracket:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\cdot\frac{(V_{GS}-V_{tn})^{2}}{2}$$

Equivalently:

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2}\,k_n'\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{tn})^{2}$$

This is the famous **square-law I-V** that is the basis of nearly every CMOS analog design calculation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For PMOS, simply replace μ_n → μ_p, V_tn → V_tp (negative value), and the I_D flows from Source to Drain.

---

## 3.4 Channel-Length Modulation (CLM) — The "λ Effect"

In reality, when V_DS > V_DSAT, the pinch-off point moves slightly toward the source, **shortening the effective channel length** from L to L − ΔL. The current now increases linearly with V_DS:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\,k_n'\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{tn})^{2}(1+\lambda V_{DS})$$

**Empirical observation:** λ ∝ 1/L — short-channel devices suffer more from CLM. This is why analog designers use **long-channel** (large L) transistors for current sources.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Small-Signal Transconductance g_m

In saturation, taking the partial derivative of I_D with respect to V_GS:

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = k_n'\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{tn})$$

Using the saturation I_D expression to eliminate (V_GS − V_tn):

$$g_m = \sqrt{2\,k_n'\frac{W}{L}\cdot I_D}$$

> These two forms are both **favorite KTU question patterns** — you must know when to use which. The first is best for design calculations; the second is best when only I_D is known.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Dependence on W | Dependence on L | Dependence on V_GS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Saturation I_D | I_D,sat | Linear (∝ W/L) | Linear (∝ 1/L) | Quadratic |
| Triode I_D | I_D,triode | Linear (∝ W/L) | Linear (∝ 1/L) | Linear (in V_DS) |
| Transconductance | g_m | ∝ √(W/L) | ∝ 1/√L | Linear |
| Output Resistance | r_o | Independent | ∝ L | Independent |
| Intrinsic Gain | g_m·r_o | ∝ √(W·L) | ∝ √L | Linear |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NMOS Physical Cross-Section (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph bulk["P-TYPE SUBSTRATE / BODY"]
        A1["SOURCE n+ REGION"]
        A2["DRAIN n+ REGION"]
        A3["P-TYPE BODY TIED TO GND"]
    end
    subgraph ox["GATE OXIDE LAYER SIO2"]
        B1["THIN DIELECTRIC t_ox"]
    end
    subgraph gate["GATE ELECTRODE"]
        C1["POLYSILICON OR METAL STACK"]
    end
    A1 ---|induced channel| A2
    B1 ---|controls inversion| A1
    B1 ---|controls inversion| A2
    C1 ---|V_GS applied here| B1
    A3 ---|reverse biased p-n junctions| A1
    A3 ---|reverse biased p-n junctions| A2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Apply a positive V_GS at the gate. The vertical electric field through the oxide attracts minority carriers (electrons) from the p-substrate to the surface, creating an **n-channel** that bridges the n⁺ source and n⁺ drain.

## 4.2 PMOS Physical Cross-Section (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph bulk2["N-TYPE SUBSTRATE / N-WELL"]
        D1["SOURCE p+ REGION"]
        D2["DRAIN p+ REGION"]
        D3["N-TYPE BODY TIED TO V_DD"]
    end
    subgraph ox2["GATE OXIDE LAYER SIO2"]
        E1["THIN DIELECTRIC t_ox"]
    end
    subgraph gate2["GATE ELECTRODE"]
        F1["POLYSILICON OR METAL STACK"]
    end
    D1 ---|induced channel| D2
    E1 ---|controls inversion| D1
    E1 ---|controls inversion| D2
    F1 ---|V_GS NEGATIVE applied here| E1
    D3 ---|reverse biased p-n junctions| D1
    D3 ---|reverse biased p-n junctions| D2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Apply a **negative** V_GS at the gate (V_GS < V_tp, where V_tp is negative). The field repels electrons and attracts holes to the surface, forming a **p-channel** between p⁺ source and p⁺ drain.

## 4.3 I-V Characteristic Family (Regions of Operation Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph rg["MOSFET OPERATING REGIONS"]
        H1["CUTOFF - V_GS less than V_t - I_D equals 0 - Switch open"]
        H2["TRIODE - V_DS less than V_GS minus V_t - I_D rises linearly with V_DS - Voltage controlled resistor"]
        H3["SATURATION - V_DS greater than or equal V_GS minus V_t - I_D nearly constant - Voltage controlled current source"]
    end
    H1 -->|increase V_GS| H2
    H2 -->|increase V_DS| H3
```

## 4.4 CMOS Inverter — The Marriage of NMOS and PMOS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VDD["V_DD SUPPLY"]:::pmos
    P1["PMOS PULL-UP NETWORK - W_p large - pulls output to V_DD when ON"]:::pmos
    OUT["OUTPUT NODE - F - drives load capacitance"]:::signal
    N1["NMOS PULL-DOWN NETWORK - W_n smaller - pulls output to GND when ON"]:::nmos
    GND["GROUND 0 V"]:::nmos
    IN["INPUT VOLTAGE V_IN - controls both gates"]:::signal
    VDD --> P1 --> OUT
    OUT --> N1 --> GND
    IN -.controls gate.-> P1
    IN -.controls gate.-> N1
    classDef pmos fill:#ffd9d9,stroke:#b30000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nmos fill:#d9e6ff,stroke:#0033b3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef signal fill:#fff7d9,stroke:#b38b00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A CMOS inverter is the canonical example of a complementary pair. When V_IN = V_DD, the PMOS is **OFF** and the NMOS is **ON** → output = 0. When V_IN = 0, the PMOS is **ON** and the NMOS is **OFF** → output = V_DD. **In steady state, no DC current flows** from supply to ground — this is the origin of CMOS's legendary low static power dissipation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define threshold voltage (V_t) of a MOSFET. List the three regions of operation of an NMOS transistor and state the V_GS / V_DS conditions for each.**

**Model Answer:**

The **threshold voltage (V_t)** of a MOSFET is the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to create a conducting channel between the source and drain by **strong inversion** at the Si–SiO₂ interface.

It is given by:

$$V_{tn} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2\,q\,\varepsilon_{si}\,N_A\,(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

The three regions of operation of an NMOS transistor are:

| Region | V_GS Condition | V_DS Condition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutoff** | V_GS < V_tn | Any | No channel; I_D ≈ 0 |
| **Triode (Linear)** | V_GS > V_tn | V_DS < (V_GS − V_tn) | Continuous channel; behaves as resistor |
| **Saturation** | V_GS > V_tn | V_DS ≥ (V_GS − V_tn) | Pinch-off; behaves as current source |

**(3 marks: 1 for V_t definition + equation, 1 for table of regions, 1 for conditions.)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Compare NMOS and PMOS transistors in terms of (i) charge carriers, (ii) mobility, (iii) threshold voltage polarity, and (iv) typical W/L ratio chosen in CMOS design.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **NMOS** | **PMOS** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Charge Carriers | Electrons | Holes |
| (ii) Mobility μ | ~1350 cm²/V·s (higher) | ~480 cm²/V·s (lower) |
| (iii) Threshold V_t | Positive (V_tn > 0) | Negative (V_tp < 0) |
| (iv) Typical W/L in CMOS | Smaller (e.g., W_n = 1 µm, L = 0.5 µm) | Larger (W_p = 2 to 3 × W_n) |

> The lower mobility of holes in PMOS forces designers to use wider PMOS devices to match the drive strength of NMOS. In a symmetric inverter, the typical ratio chosen is **W_p / W_n ≈ 2 to 3**.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for each correct comparison row × 3, plus 0.5 for the W_p/W_n ratio.)**

---

## 📌 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Choose **ONE** of the following

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the drain current of an NMOS transistor in the **triode region** of operation, starting from the basic charge-sheet and drift-current assumptions. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For an NMOS transistor with **μ_n C_ox = 50 µA/V², W/L = 10, V_tn = 0.7 V, λ = 0**, calculate:
1. The overdrive voltage V_OV when I_D = 1 mA.
2. The drain current I_D when V_GS = 2 V and V_DS = 0.5 V.
3. The small-signal transconductance g_m at the bias point of (2).

---

#### **Model Solution — (a) Triode I_D Derivation**

**Assumptions:**
1. The transistor is in **strong inversion** (V_GS > V_tn).
2. **V_DS < V_GS − V_tn** (channel is not pinched off).
3. The gradual-channel approximation (GCA) holds: lateral field ≪ vertical field.
4. No channel-length modulation (λ = 0).
5. Mobility μ_n is constant along the channel.

**Derivation:**

At a point x along the channel (with V(0) = 0 at source, V(L) = V_DS at drain), the local inversion-layer charge per unit area is:

$$Q_i(x) = -C_{ox}\left[V_{GS} - V(x) - V_{tn}\right]$$

The drift current at that point (constant along the channel) is:

$$I_D = W \cdot \mu_n \cdot \left\vert Q_i(x)\right\vert \cdot \frac{dV}{dx}$$

Substitute Q_i(x):

$$I_D = W\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\left[V_{GS}-V_{tn}-V(x)\right]\frac{dV}{dx}$$

Rearrange to separate variables and integrate from source to drain:

$$I_D\,dx = W\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\left[V_{GS}-V_{tn}-V(x)\right]dV$$

$$I_D\int_{0}^{L}dx = W\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\int_{0}^{V_{DS}}\left[V_{GS}-V_{tn}-V(x)\right]dV$$

$$I_D\,L = W\,\mu_n\,C_{ox}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{tn})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2}\right]$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\,\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{tn})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2}\right]}$$

**(7 marks: 1 for assumptions, 2 for setup, 3 for integration, 1 for final boxed answer.)**

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) Numerical**

**Given:** k_n' = μ_n C_ox = 50 µA/V², W/L = 10, V_tn = 0.7 V, λ = 0.

**Step 1: Overdrive voltage V_OV when I_D = 1 mA.**

In saturation, I_D = ½ · k_n' · (W/L) · V_OV².

Rearranging:

$$V_{OV}^{2} = \frac{2 I_D}{k_n' \cdot (W/L)} = \frac{2 \times 1\times 10^{-3}}{50\times 10^{-6}\times 10} = \frac{2\times 10^{-3}}{5\times 10^{-4}} = 4\,\text{V}^{2}$$

$$V_{OV} = \sqrt{4} = 2\,\text{V}$$

Therefore: **V_GS = V_tn + V_OV = 0.7 + 2 = 2.7 V**

---

**Step 2: Drain current I_D when V_GS = 2 V, V_DS = 0.5 V.**

Check the region:
- V_GS − V_tn = 2 − 0.7 = **1.3 V**
- V_DS = 0.5 V
- Since V_DS (0.5 V) < V_GS − V_tn (1.3 V), the transistor is in **TRIODE**.

Apply the triode equation:

$$I_D = k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{tn})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2}\right]$$

$$I_D = 50\times 10^{-6}\times 10\left[(1.3)(0.5) - \frac{(0.5)^{2}}{2}\right]$$

$$I_D = 5\times 10^{-4}\left[0.65 - 0.125\right] = 5\times 10^{-4}\times 0.525$$

$$I_D = 2.625\times 10^{-4}\,\text{A} = \mathbf{262.5\,\mu A}$$

---

**Step 3: Small-signal g_m at this bias point.**

In saturation, g_m = k_n' · (W/L) · V_OV. But here we are in triode, so we use the more general form:

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{V_{DS}} = k_n'\,\frac{W}{L}\cdot V_{DS} = 50\times 10^{-6}\times 10 \times 0.5$$

$$g_m = 2.5\times 10^{-4}\,\text{A/V} = \mathbf{0.25\,mA/V}$$

> Note: Some textbooks use the chord approximation g_m ≈ I_D / (V_GS − V_tn) which gives 262.5 µA / 1.3 V ≈ 202 µA/V — **this is approximate and loses a mark** unless you state it clearly.

**(7 marks: 2 for Step 1, 2 for Step 2, 2 for Step 3, 1 for final numerical verification of units.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat **I_D vs V_DS characteristic curve**, explain the three regions of operation of an NMOS transistor. Mark the **boundary between triode and saturation (V_DSAT)** and the **knee of the curve** clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a **CMOS inverter** with the following parameters:
- NMOS: μ_n C_ox = 60 µA/V², W_n/L_n = 5, V_tn = 0.6 V
- PMOS: μ_p C_ox = 25 µA/V², W_p/L_p = 10, V_tp = −0.7 V
- V_DD = 3.3 V

Calculate:
1. The **DC noise margin** (assuming equal sizing balances rise/fall times, NM_H ≈ NM_L ≈ V_DD/4, compute the exact V_OH − V_IH).
2. The **static power dissipation** in either logic state.
3. The **peak short-circuit current** during switching (approximate, ignoring self-loading).

---

#### **Model Solution — (a) I_D vs V_DS Curve Explanation**

```
   I_D  |
        |          /  /  SATURATION
        |        /  /     (nearly flat)
        |      /  /
   I_DSAT-----*-->------*---  (boundary V_DSAT = V_GS - V_t)
        |   /  /
        | /  /  TRIODE (linear region)
        |/ /       I_D rises linearly with V_DS
        +------------------ V_DS
        0      V_DSAT
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Cutoff (V_GS < V_tn):** I_D = 0. The curve lies flat on the V_DS axis.
2. **Triode (V_GS > V_tn, V_DS < V_GS − V_tn):** I_D rises from 0 in a **parabolic** fashion with V_DS. For small V_DS, the I_D-vs-V_DS curve is approximately linear, so the device behaves as a voltage-controlled resistor with R_on = 1 / [k_n' (W/L) (V_GS − V_tn)].
3. **Saturation (V_GS > V_tn, V_DS ≥ V_GS − V_tn):** The channel **pinches off** at the drain end. I_D becomes nearly constant (ideally flat; with λ ≠ 0, slopes gently upward).

**Boundary at V_DS = V_GS − V_tn:** This is called the **"knee" of the curve**, marking the transition from triode to saturation. At this exact point, I_D = I_DSAT = ½ · k_n' (W/L) (V_GS − V_tn)².

**(7 marks: 2 for sketch, 2 for explaining each region, 2 for the knee/boundary identification, 1 for the V_DSAT expression.)**

---

#### **Model Solution — (b) CMOS Inverter Calculations**

**Given values:**
- k_n' = 60 µA/V², (W/L)_n = 5, V_tn = 0.6 V
- k_p' = 25 µA/V², (W/L)_p = 10, V_tp = −0.7 V
- V_DD = 3.3 V

**Step 1: DC Noise Margin Approximation**

For a symmetric inverter (W_p / W_n = μ_n / μ_p), the switching threshold V_M ≈ V_DD/2. Here, the effective strengths are:
- k_n = 60 × 5 = 300 µA/V²
- k_p = 25 × 10 = 250 µA/V²

Since the NMOS is slightly stronger, V_M shifts slightly below V_DD/2. For an approximate KTU-level answer:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{tn} + \sqrt{\frac{k_n}{k_p}}\,(V_{DD}+V_{tp})}{1+\sqrt{\frac{k_n}{k_p}}}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.6 + \sqrt{300/250}\,(3.3-0.7)}{1+\sqrt{300/250}} = \frac{0.6 + 1.095 \times 2.6}{1 + 1.095} = \frac{0.6+2.847}{2.095} = 1.645\,\text{V}$$

The **DC noise margin** (high) is:

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} \approx V_{DD} - V_M = 3.3 - 1.645 = 1.655\,\text{V}$$

The **DC noise margin** (low):

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} \approx V_M - 0 = 1.645\,\text{V}$$

**Total noise margin** = NM_H + NM_L = 3.3 V (≈ V_DD), confirming balanced operation.

---

**Step 2: Static Power Dissipation**

In any **steady state** (input at GND or V_DD), one transistor is OFF and the other is ON. There is **no DC path from V_DD to GND**.

$$\boxed{P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD} \approx 0\,\text{W}}$$

(Only the nano-ampere leakage contributes in modern CMOS — this is the reason CMOS dominates mobile and battery-powered ICs.)

---

**Step 3: Peak Short-Circuit Current**

During switching, when 0 < V_IN < V_DD, both NMOS and PMOS are momentarily ON. The short-circuit current peaks roughly when V_IN ≈ V_M, and its approximate peak value is:

$$I_{peak} \approx \frac{1}{2}\cdot k_n' \cdot \frac{W_n}{L_n}\cdot (V_M - V_{tn})^{2}$$

$$I_{peak} \approx \frac{1}{2} \times 60\times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times (1.645 - 0.6)^{2}$$

$$I_{peak} \approx 150\times 10^{-6} \times (1.045)^{2} = 150\times 10^{-6} \times 1.092$$

$$I_{peak} \approx 1.638\times 10^{-4}\,\text{A} \approx \mathbf{164\,\mu A}$$

**(7 marks: 3 for V_M + noise margin, 2 for static power reasoning, 2 for short-circuit current calculation.)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks on this topic in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the V_DS²/2 term** in the triode equation. This is worth ~1 mark and the most common slip.
> 2. **Mixing up V_t signs for PMOS.** Always write V_tp as a **negative number** explicitly in the equation — don't just substitute a magnitude.
> 3. **Wrong region identification.** If V_DS > V_GS − V_t and you still use the triode equation, you lose 2 marks instantly. Always state the region check first.
> 4. **Ignoring units in the answer.** Numerical answers **must** carry units (µA, mA, mA/V, etc.) — KTU examiners penalize bare numbers.
> 5. **Confusing g_m formula in triode vs saturation.** In triode, g_m = k' (W/L) V_DS; in saturation, g_m = k' (W/L) (V_GS − V_t). Using the wrong one = 0 marks for that part.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A MOSFET has **4 terminals**: Gate, Drain, Source, Body.
- **NMOS = n-channel on p-substrate; PMOS = p-channel on n-substrate.**
- **Threshold V_tn > 0** for NMOS; **V_tp < 0** for PMOS.
- Three operating regions: **Cutoff (OFF), Triode (resistor), Saturation (current source).**
- **Saturation I_D equation** (most important):

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\,k'\,\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_t)^{2}(1+\lambda V_{DS})$$

- **Transconductance g_m** in saturation:

$$g_m = k'\,\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_t) = \sqrt{2\,k'\,\frac{W}{L}\,I_D}$$

- **Output resistance r_o = 1/(λ I_D)** — valid only in saturation.
- **Intrinsic gain A_v = g_m · r_o = 2 V_OV / (λ V_DS)** — a key analog figure of merit.
- **Body effect** shifts V_t when V_SB ≠ 0:

$$V_t = V_{t0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB}\vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right)$$

- **Electron mobility > hole mobility** → PMOS must be ~2–3× wider than NMOS for matched drive strength.
- **CMOS inverter static power ≈ 0** (only subthreshold leakage) — the reason CMOS dominates low-power VLSI.
- **Channel-length modulation (λ) ∝ 1/L** — short-channel transistors have poorer output resistance.
- **Always state the region** (cutoff/triode/saturation) before plugging into any current equation.
- **Carry units explicitly** in every numerical answer for KTU.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMOS Inverter - Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CMOS Inverter - Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CMOS Inverter (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Inverter):** It is the fundamental building block of any CMOS digital VLSI circuit, constructed by cascading a **PMOS pull-up transistor** (load) and an **NMOS pull-down transistor** (driver) in series between the supply rails **V_DD** and **GND**, sharing a common gate input (V_in) and a common drain output (V_out).

The static CMOS inverter is a **ratio-less** logic gate — unlike the older NMOS-only resistive-load or saturated-load inverter — meaning its output voltage levels in steady state are *independent* of the transistor sizes. This property is the single most important reason CMOS has dominated low-power digital design.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Two-Handed Water Tap:**
> Imagine a vertical pipe carrying water. You have **two hands on a single lever**:
> - Your **left hand (PMOS)** opens the **top valve** connected to a full tank (V_DD).
> - Your **right hand (NMOS)** opens the **bottom valve** connected to a drain (GND).
> 
> When the lever is pushed **left**, the top valve opens (PMOS ON) and the bottom valve closes (NMOS OFF) → pipe fills with water (V_out = V_DD, logic **1**).
> When the lever is pushed **right**, the top valve closes (PMOS OFF) and the bottom valve opens (NMOS ON) → pipe empties (V_out = 0 V, logic **0**).
> 
> **Crucially, the hands never open both valves at the same time (in steady state) → no water flows continuously → no wasted energy.** This is why CMOS is so power-efficient.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded Physical Constants)

- **Supply Voltage:** $V_{DD}$ (typically **5 V**, **3.3 V**, **1.8 V**, or **1.2 V** in modern nodes).
- **NMOS Threshold Voltage:** $V_{Tn} > 0$ (typically **0.4 V to 0.7 V**).
- **PMOS Threshold Voltage:** $V_{Tp} < 0$ (typically **−0.4 V to −0.7 V**).
- **Oxide Capacitance per unit area:** $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ (units: **F/m²**).
- **Process Transconductance:** $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox}$, $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox}$ (units: **A/V²**).
- **Electron vs Hole mobility ratio:** $\mu_n \approx 2.5 \cdot \mu_p$ → to get **matched drive strength**, PMOS width is made ≈ **2.5×** the NMOS width.
- **Load Capacitance:** $C_L$ (sum of drain diffusion, gate, and wire capacitances) — typically in **fF to pF** range.

### 1.4 Circuit Schematic (Textual)

> **Schematic Layout:**
> - **Source of PMOS (M_p)** → connected to **V_DD**
> - **Drain of PMOS (M_p)** → connected to **Drain of NMOS (M_n)** = **Output Node (V_out)**
> - **Source of NMOS (M_n)** → connected to **GND**
> - **Gate of M_p** = **Gate of M_n** = **Input Node (V_in)**
> - **Substrate of M_p** → **V_DD**, **Substrate of M_n** → **GND** (to avoid body-effect on these transistors in the basic inverter analysis)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Steady-State Logic Operation (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Case 1: V_in = 0 V (Logic 0)**
- $V_{GS_n} = 0 < V_{Tn}$ → **NMOS is OFF** ($I_{Dn} = 0$).
- $V_{GS_p} = -V_{DD}$, and $V_{SG_p} = V_{DD} > \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ → **PMOS is ON** (operating in linear/triode region since $V_{SD_p} = V_{DD} - V_{out}$ is small as $V_{out}$ rises toward $V_{DD}$).
- The output node is **charged to V_DD** through the ON PMOS.
- **Result:** $V_{out} = V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ → Logic **1**.

**Case 2: V_in = V_DD (Logic 1)**
- $V_{GS_n} = V_{DD} > V_{Tn}$ → **NMOS is ON** (linear region since $V_{DS_n} = V_{out}$ is small).
- $V_{GS_p} = 0 > V_{Tp}$ → **PMOS is OFF**.
- The output node is **discharged to GND** through the ON NMOS.
- **Result:** $V_{out} = V_{OL} = 0$ → Logic **0**.

**Case 3: Transition Region (0 < V_in < V_DD)**
- Both transistors may conduct **simultaneously** for a brief period.
- The behavior is captured by the **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** — a plot of $V_{out}$ vs. $V_{in}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight (Why CMOS is great):** In *both* steady states, one transistor is OFF. Hence **no DC (static) current flows from V_DD to GND**, and the **static power dissipation is (ideally) zero**. Power is consumed only during *switching* (dynamic power).

### 2.2 The Five Regions of VTC Operation

As $V_{in}$ ramps from **0 → V_DD**, the inverter passes through five distinct operating regions:

| # | Region | V_in Range | NMOS State | PMOS State | V_out Behavior |
|---|--------|-----------|------------|------------|----------------|
| **A** | Initial cutoff | $V_{in} < V_{Tn}$ | **Cutoff (OFF)** | Triode (Linear) | $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ (high) |
| **B** | NMOS saturation | $V_{Tn} < V_{in} < V_{M}$ | **Saturation** | Triode (Linear) | $V_{out}$ falls sharply |
| **C** | Both saturation (switching) | $V_{in} \approx V_{M}$ | **Saturation** | **Saturation** | $V_{out} = V_{M}$ (steepest slope, max gain) |
| **D** | PMOS saturation | $V_{M} < V_{in} < V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ | Triode (Linear) | **Saturation** | $V_{out}$ continues to fall |
| **E** | Final cutoff | $V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ | Triode (Linear) | **Cutoff (OFF)** | $V_{out} = 0$ (low) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** — they appear almost every year in KTU Part B derivations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Region / Condition | Units |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | NMOS drain current (Linear) | $I_{Dn} = k_n \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{Tn}) V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$ | $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{Tn}$ | A |
| 2 | NMOS drain current (Saturation) | $I_{Dn} = \dfrac{k_n}{2} (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^2$ | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{Tn}$ | A |
| 3 | PMOS drain current (Saturation) | $I_{Dp} = \dfrac{k_p}{2} (V_{SG} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2$ | $V_{SD} \geq V_{SG} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ | A |
| 4 | Switching threshold | $V_{M} = \dfrac{r \, (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert) + V_{Tn}}{1 + r}$ | Both in saturation, $r = \sqrt{k_p / k_n}$ | V |
| 5 | Symmetric inverter case | $V_{M} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ | If $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ and $k_n = k_p$ | V |
| 6 | High noise margin | $N_{MH} = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Where slope $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ | V |
| 7 | Low noise margin | $N_{ML} = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Where slope $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ | V |
| 8 | Dynamic switching power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f_{clk}$ | $\alpha$ = activity factor | W |
| 9 | Static power (ideal CMOS) | $P_{static} = 0$ | No DC path in steady state | W |
| 10 | Short-circuit power | $P_{SC} \approx \dfrac{\beta}{12} (V_{DD} - 2V_T)^3 \, \tau \, f$ | During brief transition | W |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microprocessor Cores (Intel i7, AMD Ryzen, ARM Cortex):** Billions of CMOS inverters + NAND/NOR variants form flip-flops, ALUs, cache, and control logic.
- **DRAM & SRAM Memory Cells:** 6T SRAM bit-cell uses a CMOS inverter pair in a cross-coupled latch configuration.
- **I/O Pads & Level Shifters:** Inverters convert between voltage domains (e.g., **3.3 V ↔ 1.8 V**).
- **Ring Oscillators:** Odd number of CMOS inverters in a loop generate clock signals for on-chip timing.
- **Power Gating:** Inverter-based retention flip-flops keep state during sleep mode.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Switching Threshold Voltage $V_M$ (Region C)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The switching threshold $V_M$ (also called $V_{inv}$) is the input voltage at which $V_{in} = V_{out}$. At this point, **both** transistors are in **saturation**, and the current through the PMOS must exactly equal the current through the NMOS (KCL at the output node).

**Step 1 — Define the current expressions (Saturation Region):**

$$
I_{Dn} = \frac{k_n}{2} \left( V_{GS,n} - V_{Tn} \right)^2 = \frac{k_n}{2} \left( V_M - V_{Tn} \right)^2
$$

$$
I_{Dp} = \frac{k_p}{2} \left( V_{SG,p} - \left\vert V_{Tp} \right\vert \right)^2 = \frac{k_p}{2} \left( V_{DD} - V_M - \left\vert V_{Tp} \right\vert \right)^2
$$

**Textual Logic:** Since the PMOS is being driven by $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_M$, and the NMOS by $V_{GS,n} = V_M$, the two expressions are in terms of $V_M$ only.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL (no current into gate, so PMOS current = NMOS current):**

$$
\frac{k_n}{2} \left( V_M - V_{Tn} \right)^2 = \frac{k_p}{2} \left( V_{DD} - V_M - \left\vert V_{Tp} \right\vert \right)^2
$$

**Textual Logic:** At the output node, the current charging the capacitor from the PMOS must equal the current discharging it through the NMOS — they are equal in magnitude (steady-state at $V_M$).

**Step 3 — Cancel the factor ½ on both sides:**

$$
k_n \left( V_M - V_{Tn} \right)^2 = k_p \left( V_{DD} - V_M - \left\vert V_{Tp} \right\vert \right)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root of both sides (both sides are positive in saturation):**

$$
\sqrt{k_n} \left( V_M - V_{Tn} \right) = \sqrt{k_p} \left( V_{DD} - V_M - \left\vert V_{Tp} \right\vert \right)
$$

**Textual Logic:** The square root is taken because both $(V_M - V_{Tn})$ and $(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)$ are positive in saturation. We drop the ± ambiguity since we are in the *positive* overdrive regime.

**Step 5 — Define the ratio parameter $r$ for compactness:**

$$
r = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}
$$

**Step 6 — Divide both sides by $\sqrt{k_n}$ and rearrange:**

$$
V_M - V_{Tn} = r \left( V_{DD} - V_M - \left\vert V_{Tp} \right\vert \right)
$$

**Step 7 — Expand the right-hand side:**

$$
V_M - V_{Tn} = r \cdot V_{DD} - r \cdot V_M - r \cdot \left\vert V_{Tp} \right\vert
$$

**Step 8 — Collect all $V_M$ terms on the left side:**

$$
V_M + r \cdot V_M = r \cdot V_{DD} - r \cdot \left\vert V_{Tp} \right\vert + V_{Tn}
$$

$$
V_M \left( 1 + r \right) = r \left( V_{DD} - \left\vert V_{Tp} \right\vert \right) + V_{Tn}
$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $V_M$ (the final, KTU-board-ready form):**

$$
V_M = \frac{r \left( V_{DD} - \left\vert V_{Tp} \right\vert \right) + V_{Tn}}{1 + r}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case — Symmetric Inverter:** If $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ and $k_n = k_p$ (so $r = 1$), then $V_M = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$. This is the *ideal* case where the VTC is symmetric about $V_{DD}/2$, giving **maximum, equal noise margins** on both sides.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A CMOS inverter in **0.18 µm technology** is operated at $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V. Given $V_{Tn} = 0.4$ V, $V_{Tp} = -0.5$ V, $(W/L)_n = 2$, $(W/L)_p = 5$, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100$ µA/V², $\mu_p C_{ox} = 40$ µA/V². Find $V_M$.

**Step 1 — Compute $k_n$ and $k_p$:**

$$
k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n = 100 \times 2 = 200 \; \mu A / V^2
$$

$$
k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = 40 \times 5 = 200 \; \mu A / V^2
$$

**Textual Logic:** The PMOS is sized 2.5× wider than the NMOS *only when* $\mu_p = \mu_n/2.5$. Here, with the W/L ratios given, we get **equal $k_n$ and $k_p$** — the designer's intent for a symmetric inverter.

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio $r$:**

$$
r = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{200}} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the $V_M$ formula:**

$$
V_M = \frac{1 \times \left( 1.8 - 0.5 \right) + 0.4}{1 + 1} = \frac{1.3 + 0.4}{2} = \frac{1.7}{2} = 0.85 \; V
$$

**Step 4 — Verify:** $V_M = 0.85$ V, which is $1.8/2 = 0.9$ V minus a small offset of 0.05 V due to the asymmetric thresholds ($V_{Tn} \neq \vert V_{Tp} \vert$). **Result is correct.** ✔

### 3.3 Derivation of Noise Margin Boundaries (Approximate Form)

**Step 1 — Recall that $V_{IL}$ is defined where $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ in Region B (NMOS sat, PMOS linear).**

**Step 2 — Equate the currents in Region B:**

$$
k_n \left[ \left( V_{in} - V_{Tn} \right) V_{out} - \frac{V_{out}^2}{2} \right] = \frac{k_p}{2} \left( V_{DD} - V_{in} - \left\vert V_{Tp} \right\vert \right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate both sides w.r.t. $V_{in}$ and set the derivative to −1.**

*(Full derivation is lengthy; for KTU, the standard board-form result is:)*

$$
V_{IL} \approx \frac{1}{8} \left( 3 V_{DD} + 2 V_T \right) \quad \text{(for symmetric case, approximate)}
$$

**Step 4 — Similarly for $V_{IH}$ (in Region D):**

$$
V_{IH} \approx \frac{1}{8} \left( 5 V_{DD} - 2 V_T \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Final noise margins (symmetric case):**

$$
N_{ML} = V_{IL} - V_{OL} \approx \frac{1}{8} \left( 3 V_{DD} + 2 V_T \right)
$$

$$
N_{MH} = V_{OH} - V_{IH} \approx \frac{1}{8} \left( 3 V_{DD} - 2 V_T \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For $V_{DD} = 5$ V, $V_T = 1$ V:** $N_{ML} \approx 2.125$ V, $N_{MH} \approx 1.625$ V. Designers ensure $N_{ML}, N_{MH} > 0.4 \cdot V_{DD}$ for robust noise immunity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CMOS Inverter — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the **functional interaction** between the input signal, the two complementary transistors, the power rails, and the five VTC operating regions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputSig["Vin Input Signal 0 to VDD"]
    vddRail["VDD Power Supply Rail"]
    gndRail["GND Ground Rail"]
    pmosBlock["PMOS Pull Up M_p Source at VDD Gate at Vin"]
    nmosBlock["NMOS Pull Down M_n Source at GND Gate at Vin"]
    outNode["Vout Output Node Drain to Drain Connection"]
    capLoad["C_load Output Capacitance"]
    regA["Region A Vin less than V_Tn NMOS OFF PMOS TRIODE Vout equals VDD Logic 1"]
    regB["Region B Vin in V_Tn to V_M NMOS SAT PMOS TRIODE Vout Falls"]
    regC["Region C Vin equals V_M Both SATURATION Maximum Gain Point"]
    regD["Region D Vin in V_M to VDD minus V_Tp NMOS TRIODE PMOS SAT"]
    regE["Region E Vin greater than VDD minus V_Tp PMOS OFF NMOS TRIODE Vout equals 0"]
    vtcPlot["VTC Plot Vout versus Vin S Shaped Curve"]
    noiseMarg["Noise Margins NML and NMH"]
    powerCalc["Power Dissipation P_dyn and P_static"]

    inputSig --> pmosBlock
    inputSig --> nmosBlock
    vddRail --> pmosBlock
    nmosBlock --> gndRail
    pmosBlock --> outNode
    nmosBlock --> outNode
    outNode --> capLoad
    regA --> vtcPlot
    regB --> vtcPlot
    regC --> vtcPlot
    regD --> vtcPlot
    regE --> vtcPlot
    vtcPlot --> noiseMarg
    vtcPlot --> powerCalc
```

### 4.2 Truth Table & Region-Switching Timeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    state0["State Vin equals 0V NMOS OFF PMOS ON Vout equals VDD Region A"]
    state1["Transition Vin rises 0 to VDD Regions B and C traversed"]
    state2["State Vin equals VDD NMOS ON PMOS OFF Vout equals 0 Region E"]
    state3["Transition Vin falls VDD to 0 Regions D and C traversed back"]
    state4["Back to State Vin equals 0V Region A"]

    state0 --> state1
    state1 --> state2
    state2 --> state3
    state3 --> state4
    state4 --> state0
```

### 4.3 Power & Energy Dissipation Sub-System

```mermaid
graph TD
    pwrInput["DC Supply VDD"]
    pwrStatic["P_static equals 0 ideal in steady state"]
    pwrDynamic["P_dynamic equals alpha C VDD squared f"]
    pwrShort["P_short_circuit during transition"]
    pwrLeakage["P_leakage I_leak times VDD subthreshold"]
    capCharge["Energy Stored 0.5 C VDD squared per cycle"]
    capDischarge["Energy Dissipated as Heat 0.5 C VDD squared per cycle"]
    totalPower["P_total equals P_static plus P_dynamic plus P_short plus P_leakage"]

    pwrInput --> pwrStatic
    pwrInput --> pwrDynamic
    pwrInput --> pwrShort
    pwrInput --> pwrLeakage
    pwrDynamic --> capCharge
    capCharge --> capDischarge
    pwrStatic --> totalPower
    pwrDynamic --> totalPower
    pwrShort --> totalPower
    pwrLeakage --> totalPower
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** Each box in the diagrams above represents a *functional sub-block* of the CMOS inverter system. Tracing the arrows top-to-bottom shows the **flow of signals and energy** from the power supply, through the transistor network, to the output node and the resulting power dissipation budget.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a CMOS inverter. With a neat circuit diagram, explain its basic working principle. Mention the state of both transistors in each input condition.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A CMOS inverter is a digital logic gate that produces the **logical complement** of its input. It is built using a **PMOS transistor (pull-up)** connected between $V_{DD}$ and the output node, and an **NMOS transistor (pull-down)** connected between the output node and **GND**, with their gates tied together as the input.

- When $V_{in} = 0$ V: **NMOS is OFF** (since $V_{GS} < V_{Tn}$), **PMOS is ON** (since $V_{SG} = V_{DD} > \vert V_{Tp} \vert$). Output is pulled to $V_{DD}$ → Logic **1**.
- When $V_{in} = V_{DD}$: **NMOS is ON**, **PMOS is OFF**. Output is pulled to **GND** → Logic **0**.

Since one device is always OFF in steady state, **no static current flows** from $V_{DD}$ to **GND**, making CMOS the lowest-power logic family. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List the five regions of operation of a CMOS inverter in its Voltage Transfer Characteristic. State the condition on $V_{in}$ and the operating region (saturation/triode/cutoff) of each transistor in every region.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The five regions of VTC are:

| # | Condition on V_in | NMOS Region | PMOS Region |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **A** | $V_{in} < V_{Tn}$ | Cutoff | Triode (Linear) |
| **B** | $V_{Tn} < V_{in} < V_M$ | Saturation | Triode (Linear) |
| **C** | $V_{in} = V_M$ | Saturation | Saturation |
| **D** | $V_M < V_{in} < V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ | Triode (Linear) | Saturation |
| **E** | $V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ | Triode (Linear) | Cutoff |

Region **C** corresponds to the **switching threshold** $V_M$ and exhibits the **maximum small-signal gain** of the inverter. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the switching threshold voltage $V_M$ of a CMOS inverter. State the assumption clearly.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption:** At $V_{in} = V_M$, both transistors are operating in the **saturation region**, and the channel-length modulation effect ($\lambda$) is neglected.

**Step 1 — Express the drain currents at $V_{in} = V_M$:**

For the NMOS (saturation):

$$
I_{Dn} = \frac{k_n}{2} (V_M - V_{Tn})^2
$$

For the PMOS (saturation):

$$
I_{Dp} = \frac{k_p}{2} (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the output node (steady state, $I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$):**

$$
\frac{k_n}{2} (V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2} (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $\tfrac{1}{2}$ and take square roots:**

$$
\sqrt{k_n} (V_M - V_{Tn}) = \sqrt{k_p} (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $r = \sqrt{k_p/k_n}$ and rearrange:**

$$
V_M = \frac{r (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert) + V_{Tn}}{1 + r}
$$

**Step 5 — For the symmetric case** ($k_n = k_p$, $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert$): $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.

**(7 Marks) — Final boxed answer as above.**

---

**(b) For a CMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $V_{Tn} = 0.7$ V, $V_{Tp} = -0.8$ V, $(W/L)_n = 3$, $(W/L)_p = 7.5$, $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100$ µA/V², $\mu_p C_{ox} = 40$ µA/V², compute $V_M$ and comment on the inverter's noise immunity.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the device transconductance parameters:**

$$
k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n = 100 \times 3 = 300 \; \mu A/V^2
$$

$$
k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = 40 \times 7.5 = 300 \; \mu A/V^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio $r$:**

$$
r = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = \sqrt{\frac{300}{300}} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the $V_M$ formula:**

$$
V_M = \frac{1 \times (3.3 - 0.8) + 0.7}{1 + 1} = \frac{2.5 + 0.7}{2} = \frac{3.2}{2} = 1.6 \; V
$$

**Step 4 — Comment on noise immunity:** $V_M = 1.6$ V is *less than* the ideal $V_{DD}/2 = 1.65$ V by 0.05 V (due to the slight threshold asymmetry). Since the switching point is near the **mid-rail**, both $N_{ML}$ and $N_{MH}$ are approximately equal and close to their maximum value $\approx 0.4 \, V_{DD} \approx 1.32$ V. The inverter has **excellent symmetric noise immunity**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a) With a neat sketch of the Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC), label the five operating regions of a CMOS inverter and identify the switching threshold $V_M$, $V_{OH}$, $V_{OL}$, $V_{IL}$, and $V_{IH}$ on it.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch Description (VTC curve is an inverted-S):**
- **Y-axis:** $V_{out}$ ranging from **0 to $V_{DD}$**.
- **X-axis:** $V_{in}$ ranging from **0 to $V_{DD}$**.
- **Region A** (left flat top): $V_{out} = V_{OH} = V_{DD}$; spans $0 \leq V_{in} \leq V_{Tn}$.
- **Region B** (steep falling edge, NMOS sat, PMOS linear): $V_{Tn} \leq V_{in} \leq V_M$.
- **Region C** (midpoint — the steepest segment): $V_{in} = V_M$, where $V_{out} = V_{in}$ and gain $\vert dV_{out}/dV_{in} \vert$ is maximum.
- **Region D** (steep falling edge continues, NMOS linear, PMOS sat): $V_M \leq V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert$.
- **Region E** (right flat bottom): $V_{out} = V_{OL} \approx 0$; spans $V_{in} \geq V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert$.

**Identified points:**
- $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ (top-right plateau of the inverse-S curve).
- $V_{OL} = 0$ V (bottom-left plateau of the curve).
- $V_M$ is the point where the curve crosses the **$V_{out} = V_{in}$** line.
- $V_{IL}$ is on the curve where the **slope = −1** in Region B.
- $V_{IH}$ is on the curve where the **slope = −1** in Region D.

**Noise margins are then read off as:**
- $N_{ML} = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ (left horizontal distance).
- $N_{MH} = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ (right horizontal distance).

**(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) Explain the three components of power dissipation in a CMOS inverter and write the expression for the dominant dynamic power component.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The three components of power dissipation are:

**1. Static Power ($P_{static}$):**
In an *ideal* CMOS inverter, one of the two transistors is always OFF in steady state. Hence, no direct DC path exists from $V_{DD}$ to GND, and the static current is **zero**. In *real* deep-submicron devices, subthreshold leakage current $I_{leak}$ causes a small static power:

$$
P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}
$$

**2. Dynamic Switching Power ($P_{dyn}$):**
This is the **dominant** component. Every time the output node capacitance $C_L$ is charged from $0$ to $V_{DD}$, energy $\tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is delivered by the supply, and **all of it** is dissipated as heat in the PMOS when the capacitor is later discharged through the NMOS. With switching activity factor $\alpha$ and clock frequency $f_{clk}$:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}
$$

**3. Short-Circuit Power ($P_{SC}$):**
During the brief transition interval, both transistors conduct simultaneously, creating a direct current path from $V_{DD}$ to GND. This short-circuit current is typically small (≈ 10% of total power) and depends on the input rise/fall time $\tau$:

$$
P_{SC} \approx \frac{\beta}{12} (V_{DD} - 2V_T)^3 \cdot \tau \cdot f_{clk}
$$

**Total Power:** $P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dyn} + P_{SC}$.

**(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the square root** in the $V_M$ derivation → leaves a quadratic in $V_M$ which the examiner marks as *incomplete* (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Sign error on PMOS threshold:** $V_{Tp}$ is *negative*; always write the saturation equation using $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_{in}$ and $\vert V_{Tp} \vert$, not $V_{Tp}$ directly.
> 3. **Confusing $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$:** $V_{IL}$ is on the *low* input side (Region B), $V_{IH}$ is on the *high* input side (Region D). Mixing these flips the noise margin signs.
> 4. **Stating $P_{static} = 0$ without qualification:** Examiner expects the qualifier "*in an ideal CMOS inverter*". In real submicron devices, you must mention **leakage** to score full marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the $\alpha$ (activity factor)** in $P_{dyn}$ — writing $P_{dyn} = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **CMOS Inverter = 1 PMOS (pull-up) + 1 NMOS (pull-down)** with gates tied together.
- ✅ **Output levels:** $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} = 0$ V — *both* are rail-to-rail, *independent* of device ratios (ratio-less property).
- ✅ **Five VTC regions:** A (NMOS off, PMOS lin) → B (NMOS sat, PMOS lin) → C (both sat, the gain peak) → D (NMOS lin, PMOS sat) → E (NMOS lin, PMOS off).
- ✅ **Switching threshold formula:** $V_{M} = \dfrac{r (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert) + V_{Tn}}{1 + r}$, with $r = \sqrt{k_p/k_n}$.
- ✅ **Symmetric design target:** $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ → achieved by making $k_n = k_p$ (this typically requires PMOS width ≈ **2.5×** NMOS width due to $\mu_n \approx 2.5 \mu_p$).
- ✅ **Noise margins:** $N_{MH} = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$, $N_{ML} = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$. For 5 V symmetric inverter, both ≈ **1.5–2 V**.
- ✅ **Static power ≈ 0** in ideal CMOS; real devices have subthreshold leakage.
- ✅ **Dynamic power** is the dominant term: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — this is why designers aggressively **scale $V_{DD}$**.
- ✅ **Key advantage:** CMOS is the only logic family with *zero* static current in steady state — the reason for the mobile/IoT revolution.
- ✅ **Body effect:** In advanced designs, the source of the NMOS is *not* at ground; the body effect raises $V_{Tn}$ and shifts $V_M$.
- ✅ **Rule of thumb for board exams:** Always mention the *assumption* that $\lambda = 0$ (no channel-length modulation) when deriving $V_M$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Transfer Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Voltage Transfer Characteristics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Voltage Transfer Characteristics (VTC)** of a logic gate is the graphical and mathematical relationship between the **output voltage ($V_{out}$)** and the **input voltage ($V_{in}$)** of the gate, plotted as $V_{out}$ versus $V_{in}$ for the entire range $0 \le V_{in} \le V_{DD}$. For the CMOS inverter, the VTC describes how a continuously varying analog input is converted into a sharply defined binary logic output. The CMOS inverter is the fundamental building block of all digital VLSI systems, and its **Voltage Transfer Curve (VTC)** — often called the **DC Transfer Characteristic** — is the single most important analytical tool used to evaluate switching performance, noise immunity, and signal integrity at the transistor level. The four cardinal voltages that define the VTC are: | Symbol | Meaning | Ideal Value | |--------|---------|-------------| | $V_{OH}$ | Output High voltage | **$V_{DD}$** | | $V_{OL}$ | Output Low voltage | **$0 \text{ V}$** | | $V_{IH}$ | Minimum input recognised as logic HIGH | $\approx 0.5 V_{DD}$ | | $V_{IL}$ | Maximum input recognised as logic LOW | $\approx 0.5 V_{DD}$ | | $V_M$ | Switching threshold (trip point) | $\approx 0.5 V_{DD}$ | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — The Automatic Elevator Door Sensor:** > Imagine an elevator door that opens only when you stand close enough. The sensor's input is your distance, and the output is the door state (open/closed). As you slowly approach, nothing happens until a critical distance is reached — then the door opens almost *instantaneously*. The CMOS inverter behaves identically: a gradual analog input produces an abrupt digital transition at the trip point $V_M$. The **steepness** of this transition determines how immune the gate is to electrical noise, just as a sharper sensor response makes the elevator door more reliable. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Thermal Voltage** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85 \text{ mV}}$ at room temperature ($T = 300 \text{ K}$). - **Process transconductance parameters**: $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ (NMOS), $k_p' = \mu_p C_{ox}$ (PMOS). - **Transistor conduction factor ratio**: $k_r = \dfrac{k_p}{k_n} = \dfrac{k_p'(W/L)_p}{k_n'(W/L)_n}$. - **Typical threshold voltages**: $V_{tn} \approx 0.4\text{–}0.7 \text{ V}$, $V_{tp} \approx -0.4\text{–}-0.7 \text{ V}$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The VTC of the CMOS inverter, the calculation of $V_M$, $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, and the derivation of noise margins $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ are examinable as **full 14-mark problems** under Module 1. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The CMOS Inverter — Recap The CMOS inverter consists of a **PMOS pull-up network** and an **NMOS pull-down network** connected in series between $V_{DD}$ and **GND**. The gates are tied together as the input, and the common drain terminal forms the output. ### 2.2 Five Operating Regions of the VTC As $V_{in}$ sweeps from **0 V to $V_{DD}$**, the inverter traverses five distinct regions: - **Region A — NMOS Cutoff ($0 \le V_{in} \le V_{tn}$):** - The 'Why': $V_{GS,n} = V_{in} < V_{tn}$, so the NMOS is OFF ($I_{Dn} = 0$). - The 'How': The PMOS is in triode (linear) with $V_{DS,p} \approx 0$, so $I_{Dp} = 0$ as well — but it pulls the output up to $V_{DD}$. - **Result:** $V_{out} = V_{DD} = V_{OH}$. - **Region B — NMOS Saturation, PMOS Triode ($V_{tn} \le V_{in} \le V_{out} + V_{tn}$):** - The 'Why': $V_{DS,n} = V_{out}$ is large enough that the NMOS pinches off. - The 'How': The PMOS still conducts strongly in the linear regime, but is being throttled as $V_{SD,p} = V_{DD} - V_{out}$ increases. - **Result:** $V_{out}$ begins to fall rapidly. - **Region C — Both in Saturation (around $V_{in} \approx V_M$):** - The 'Why': Both $V_{DS} > V_{GS} - V_t$ for both devices simultaneously. - The 'How': A very high-gain region — a tiny change in $V_{in}$ produces a large change in $V_{out}$. - **Result:** This is the **switching transition**; $V_{out} \approx V_{in}$ at the trip point $V_M$. - **Region D — NMOS Triode, PMOS Saturation ($V_{in} + V_{tp} \le V_{DD} \le V_{in} + V_{tp} + \dots$):** - The 'Why': The PMOS pinch-off region; NMOS is now fully on. - **Result:** $V_{out}$ settles near 0 V. - **Region E — PMOS Cutoff ($V_{in} \ge V_{DD} + V_{tp}$, i.e., $V_{DD} - |V_{tp}| \le V_{in} \le V_{DD}$):** - The 'Why': $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_{in} < |V_{tp}|$, so the PMOS is OFF. - **Result:** $V_{out} = 0 = V_{OL}$. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **canonical reference** for solving any KTU Module 1 VTC problem. Memorise it. | # | Quantity | Formula | Region / Condition | |---|----------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | $V_{OH}$ | $V_{DD}$ | Region A | | 2 | $V_{OL}$ | $0$ | Region E | | 3 | NMOS saturation current | $I_{Dn} = \dfrac{k_n}{2}(V_{in} - V_{tn})^2$ | $V_{DS,n} \ge V_{GS,n} - V_{tn}$ | | 4 | PMOS saturation current | $I_{Dp} = \dfrac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^2$ | $V_{SD,p} \ge V_{SG,p} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$ | | 5 | Trip point $V_M$ | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert + V_{tn}\sqrt{k_r}}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$ | Both in saturation | | 6 | $V_M$ (symmetric) | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ | $k_r = 1$, $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp} \vert$ | | 7 | $V_{IL}$ (symmetric) | $V_{IL} = \dfrac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8}$ | Slope $= -1$ on Region B | | 8 | $V_{IH}$ (symmetric) | $V_{IH} = \dfrac{5 V_{DD} - 2 V_t}{8}$ | Slope $= -1$ on Region D | | 9 | Noise Margin High | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Always positive | | 10 | Noise Margin Low | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Always positive | | 11 | $NM_H = NM_L$ (symmetric) | $\dfrac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8}$ | $k_r = 1$ | > [!NOTE] > In the symmetric case ($k_r = 1$, equal threshold voltages $V_t$), the noise margins are **identically equal** — this is the celebrated design goal of CMOS inverter scaling. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The VTC is the *single document* an IC designer references when: - Choosing **transistor aspect ratios** $(W/L)$ to balance rise/fall times. - Computing **static noise margins** for SRAM cell stability. - Validating **cascaded gate behaviour** — confirming that a chain of inverters does not lose logic levels. - Designing **mixed-signal interfaces** where analog inputs drive digital logic blocks. - Determining **Power-Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)** sensitivity at the gate output. In practice, no modern VLSI chip — from smartphone SoCs to aerospace microcontrollers — is fabricated without an exhaustive VTC simulation of every standard cell at every Process-Voltage-Temperature (PVT) corner. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Switching Threshold $V_M$ **Statement:** Determine the input voltage $V_M$ at which $V_{out} = V_{in}$ for the CMOS inverter. **Step 1 — Identify the operating region at $V_M$.** At $V_M$, both transistors operate in **saturation**. The reason is that as the output swings through the midpoint, both $V_{DS,n}$ and $V_{SD,p}$ exceed their respective $V_{DS,sat}$ values. **Step 2 — Write the saturation current equations.** $$I_{Dn} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS,n} - V_{tn})^2 = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{tn})^2$$ $$I_{Dp} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{SG,p} - |V_{tp}|)^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - |V_{tp}|)^2$$ **Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the output node.** Since no DC current flows into the gate of the next stage, $I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$. $$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - |V_{tp}|)^2$$ **Step 4 — Cancel the factor of $\dfrac{1}{2}$ and take the positive square root of both sides.** We select the positive root because both bracketed terms are positive in the transition region. $$\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_{tn}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - |V_{tp}|)$$ **Step 5 — Define the ratio $k_r = \dfrac{k_p}{k_n}$ and isolate $V_M$.** Divide both sides by $\sqrt{k_n}$: $$(V_M - V_{tn}) = \sqrt{k_r}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - |V_{tp}|)$$ Expand the right-hand side: $$V_M - V_{tn} = V_{DD}\sqrt{k_r} - V_M\sqrt{k_r} - |V_{tp}|\sqrt{k_r}$$ Collect $V_M$ terms on the left-hand side: $$V_M + V_M\sqrt{k_r} = V_{DD}\sqrt{k_r} + V_{tn} - |V_{tp}|\sqrt{k_r}$$ Factor $V_M$: $$V_M(1 + \sqrt{k_r}) = V_{DD}\sqrt{k_r} + V_{tn} - |V_{tp}|\sqrt{k_r}$$ Solve for $V_M$: $$\boxed{V_M = \frac{V_{DD}\sqrt{k_r} + V_{tn} - |V_{tp}|\sqrt{k_r}}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}} = \frac{V_{DD} - |V_{tp}| + V_{tn}\sqrt{k_r}}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}}$$ **Step 6 — Special case (Symmetric Inverter, $k_r = 1$, $V_{tn} = |V_{tp}|$).** $$V_M = \frac{V_{DD} - V_t + V_t \cdot 1}{1 + 1} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$ Hence, a perfectly symmetric CMOS inverter has its trip point exactly at half the supply rail — a critical property exploited in noise-immune design. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of $V_{IL}$ (Symmetric Case) **Step 1 — Operating regime.** In Region B, the NMOS is in saturation and the PMOS is in the triode (linear) region. **Step 2 — Equate drain currents using KCL at the output node.** $$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_{in} - V_t)^2 = k_p\left[(V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_t)(V_{DD} - V_{out}) - \frac{(V_{DD} - V_{out})^2}{2}\right]$$ For a symmetric inverter ($k_n = k_p = k$), substitute $k_n = k_p$: $$\frac{1}{2}(V_{in} - V_t)^2 = (V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_t)(V_{DD} - V_{out}) - \frac{(V_{DD} - V_{out})^2}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the slope condition $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ at $V_{IL}$.** Implicit differentiation of the KCL equation with respect to $V_{in}$, then setting the derivative equal to $-1$: $$(V_{in} - V_t) = -(V_{DD} - V_{out}) + (V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_t) + (V_{DD} - V_{out})$$ The right-hand side simplifies to $2 V_{DD} - 2 V_{out} - V_{in} - V_t$. Therefore: $$V_{in} - V_t = 2 V_{DD} - 2 V_{out} - V_{in} - V_t$$ $$2 V_{in} = 2 V_{DD} - 2 V_{out} \implies V_{out} = V_{DD} - V_{in}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $V_{out} = V_{DD} - V_{in}$ back into the original KCL equation.** $$\frac{1}{2}(V_{in} - V_t)^2 = (V_{DD} - V_{in} - V_t)(V_{in}) - \frac{(V_{in})^2}{2}$$ Expand the right-hand side: $$\frac{1}{2}(V_{in} - V_t)^2 = V_{in} V_{DD} - V_{in}^2 - V_t V_{in} - \frac{V_{in}^2}{2}$$ Multiply throughout by 2: $$(V_{in} - V_t)^2 = 2 V_{in} V_{DD} - 2 V_{in}^2 - 2 V_t V_{in} - V_{in}^2$$ $$V_{in}^2 - 2 V_t V_{in} + V_t^2 = 2 V_{in} V_{DD} - 3 V_{in}^2 - 2 V_t V_{in}$$ Move everything to one side: $$4 V_{in}^2 - 2 V_{in} V_{DD} + V_t^2 = 0$$ **Step 5 — Solve the quadratic in $V_{in}$ (select the smaller root corresponding to Region B).** $$V_{in} = \frac{2 V_{DD} \pm \sqrt{4 V_{DD}^2 - 16 V_t^2}}{8} = \frac{V_{DD} \pm \sqrt{V_{DD}^2 - 4 V_t^2}}{4}$$ For $V_t \ll V_{DD}$, the binomial expansion $\sqrt{V_{DD}^2 - 4 V_t^2} \approx V_{DD} - \dfrac{2 V_t^2}{V_{DD}}$ gives, after selecting the lower root: $$\boxed{V_{IL} = \frac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation of $V_{IH}$ (Symmetric Case) By symmetry, swapping the roles of NMOS and PMOS and replacing $V_{in} \leftrightarrow V_{DD} - V_{in}$ yields: $$\boxed{V_{IH} = \frac{5 V_{DD} - 2 V_t}{8}}$$ A formal derivation mirrors Section 3.2 exactly, with the NMOS in triode and the PMOS in saturation. --- ### 3.4 Derivation of Noise Margins Using the four cardinal voltages: $$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = V_{DD} - \frac{5 V_{DD} - 2 V_t}{8} = \frac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8}$$ $$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = \frac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8} - 0 = \frac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8}$$ The **equality $NM_H = NM_L$** is a remarkable property: the symmetric CMOS inverter exhibits *perfectly balanced* noise immunity against high-going and low-going disturbances. ### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example **Problem:** A CMOS inverter is fabricated with $V_{DD} = 3.3 \text{ V}$, $V_{tn} = |V_{tp}| = 0.5 \text{ V}$, and $k_r = 1$. Compute $V_{OH}$, $V_{OL}$, $V_M$, $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, $NM_H$, and $NM_L$. **Solution:** $$V_{OH} = V_{DD} = 3.3 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{OL} = 0 \text{ V}$$ $$V_M = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{3.3}{2} = 1.65 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{IL} = \frac{3(3.3) + 2(0.5)}{8} = \frac{9.9 + 1.0}{8} = \frac{10.9}{8} = 1.3625 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{IH} = \frac{5(3.3) - 2(0.5)}{8} = \frac{16.5 - 1.0}{8} = \frac{15.5}{8} = 1.9375 \text{ V}$$ $$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 3.3 - 1.9375 = 1.3625 \text{ V}$$ $$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 1.3625 - 0 = 1.3625 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{NM_H = NM_L = 1.3625 \text{ V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Noise immunity} \approx 41.3\% \text{ of } V_{DD}}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 CMOS Inverter — Schematic and Operating Regions Map ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Vin Sweeps 0 to VDD"]:::startNode B["Region A: 0 le Vin le Vtn
NMOS OFF, PMOS Triode
Vout equals VDD"] C["Region B: Vtn le Vin le Vout plus Vtn
NMOS Saturation, PMOS Triode
Vout begins to fall"] D["Region C: Both Saturation
Vout equals Vin equals VM
High Gain Transition"] E["Region D: Vout plus Vtp le Vin le VDD minus Vtp
NMOS Triode, PMOS Saturation
Vout settles low"] F["Region E: Vin greater than VDD minus Vtp
NMOS Triode, PMOS OFF
Vout equals 0"] G["VTC Complete Curve Plotted"]:::endNode A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G classDef startNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef endNode fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 VTC Parameter Mapping — Conceptual Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR XAxis["Vin Axis: 0 to VDD"]:::axisNode YAxis["Vout Axis: 0 to VDD"]:::axisNode P1["Point VOH at Vin = 0
Vout = VDD"]:::cornerNode P2["Point VIL on Curve
Slope = minus 1 Entry Point"]:::midNode P3["Point VM at Trip
Vin = Vout = VDD over 2"]:::tripNode P4["Point VIH on Curve
Slope = minus 1 Exit Point"]:::midNode P5["Point VOL at Vin = VDD
Vout = 0"]:::cornerNode NMH["Region NMH
VOH minus VIH
HIGH Noise Margin"]:::marginHNode NML["Region NML
VIL minus VOL
LOW Noise Margin"]:::marginLNode Forbidden["Forbidden Zone
Slope steepest here
Undefined Logic Region"]:::forbidNode XAxis --- P1 XAxis --- P5 YAxis --- P1 YAxis --- P5 P1 --- P2 P2 --- P3 P3 --- P4 P4 --- P5 P2 -.- NMH P4 -.- NML P3 -.- Forbidden classDef axisNode fill:#eceff1,stroke:#37474f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cornerNode fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef midNode fill:#ff8f00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tripNode fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef marginHNode fill:#388e3c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef marginLNode fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef forbidNode fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 ``` ### 4.3 Effect of Asymmetric Sizing on $V_M$ — Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Choose Transistor Sizes W by L"]:::startNode Q1{"kr equals 1 AND
Vtn equals absolute Vtp"}:::decisionNode Q2{"kr greater than 1
PMOS Stronger"}:::decisionNode Q3{"kr less than 1
NMOS Stronger"}:::decisionNode A1["VM equals VDD over 2
Trip point centered
Noise margins balanced"]:::resultNode A2["VM SHIFTS UP
Trip point greater than VDD over 2
Faster LOW to HIGH transition"]:::resultNode A3["VM SHIFTS DOWN
Trip point less than VDD over 2
Faster HIGH to LOW transition"]:::resultNode Start --> Q1 Q1 -- YES --> A1 Q1 -- NO --> Q2 Q2 -- YES --> A2 Q2 -- NO --> Q3 Q3 -- YES --> A3 classDef startNode fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef decisionNode fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resultNode fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define Voltage Transfer Characteristics (VTC) of a CMOS inverter. List the four critical voltages that define the VTC.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Voltage Transfer Characteristics (VTC) is the plot of the output voltage $V_{out}$ versus the input voltage $V_{in}$ of a CMOS inverter as $V_{in}$ is swept from 0 to $V_{DD}$, describing the DC input-output relationship of the gate. The four critical voltages are: 1. $V_{OH}$ — Output HIGH voltage (ideal: $V_{DD}$). 2. $V_{OL}$ — Output LOW voltage (ideal: 0 V). 3. $V_{IH}$ — Minimum input voltage recognised as logic HIGH. 4. $V_{IL}$ — Maximum input voltage recognised as logic LOW. The switching threshold $V_M$ (where $V_{in} = V_{out}$) is also marked on the curve. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What are noise margins? Derive the expressions for $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ in terms of VTC critical voltages.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Noise margins quantify the maximum disturbance voltage a logic gate can tolerate without flipping its output state. - **$NM_H$ (High Noise Margin):** $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ - **$NM_L$ (Low Noise Margin):** $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ For a symmetric CMOS inverter with $V_{tn} = |V_{tp}| = V_t$ and $k_r = 1$: $NM_H = NM_L = \dfrac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8}$ For robust design, larger noise margins imply greater immunity to coupling noise, supply bounce, and ground bounce. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Choice A — Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, draw and explain the **Voltage Transfer Characteristics (VTC)** of a CMOS inverter, clearly indicating all five regions of operation and the critical voltages. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Derive the expression for the **switching threshold voltage $V_M$** of a CMOS inverter in terms of $V_{DD}$, $V_{tn}$, $|V_{tp}|$, and the ratio $k_r$. What is the special value of $V_M$ for a symmetric inverter? *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a)** *VTC Plot and Regions:* The VTC curve is an S-shaped plot of $V_{out}$ (y-axis) versus $V_{in}$ (x-axis), as the input sweeps from 0 V to $V_{DD}$. | Region | Condition | NMOS | PMOS | Output | |--------|-----------|------|------|--------| | A | $0 \le V_{in} \le V_{tn}$ | Cutoff | Triode | $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ | | B | $V_{tn} \le V_{in} \le V_{out} + V_{tn}$ | Saturation | Triode | $V_{out}$ falls steeply | | C | $V_{in} \approx V_M$ | Saturation | Saturation | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | | D | $V_{in} \ge V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$ (with NMOS in triode) | Triode | Saturation | $V_{out}$ approaches 0 | | E | $V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert \le V_{in} \le V_{DD}$ | Triode | Cutoff | $V_{out} = 0$ | The four cardinal points $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, and $V_{OL} = 0$ are marked; the trip point $V_M$ is at the steepest portion of the curve. *(7 marks — full marks awarded for clear sketch with all five regions labelled and identification of transistor states in each.)* --- **(b)** *Derivation of $V_M$:* (Refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation.) Step 1 — At $V_M$, both transistors are in saturation. Step 2 — Equate $I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$: $$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - |V_{tp}|)^2$$ Step 3 — Take the positive square root and substitute $k_r = k_p/k_n$: $$\sqrt{k_n}(V_M - V_{tn}) = \sqrt{k_p}(V_{DD} - V_M - |V_{tp}|)$$ Step 4 — Solve for $V_M$: $$\boxed{V_M = \frac{V_{DD} - |V_{tp}| + V_{tn}\sqrt{k_r}}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}}$$ **Special Case:** For $k_r = 1$ and $V_{tn} = |V_{tp}| = V_t$: $$V_M = \frac{V_{DD} - V_t + V_t}{2} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$ The trip point is exactly half the supply voltage, giving a perfectly centred switching threshold. *(7 marks — full marks for correct derivation with all intermediate steps and final boxed answer.)* --- ### **Choice B — Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Define **Noise Margin**. Derive the expressions for $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ of a symmetric CMOS inverter, assuming $k_n = k_p = k$ and $V_{tn} = |V_{tp}| = V_t$. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A CMOS inverter is designed with $V_{DD} = 2.5 \text{ V}$, $V_{tn} = |V_{tp}| = 0.4 \text{ V}$, and $k_n = k_p = 100 \, \mu\text{A/V}^2$. Calculate $V_M$, $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, and both noise margins. Comment on the noise immunity. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **(a)** *Noise Margin Definition:* Noise margin is the maximum voltage disturbance that can be superimposed on a logic signal at a gate input without causing the gate to erroneously change its output state. **Derivation of $V_{IL}$:** (Refer to Section 3.2 for complete derivation.) In Region B (NMOS saturation, PMOS triode), equating currents and applying the slope condition $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ leads to: $$\boxed{V_{IL} = \frac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8}}$$ **Derivation of $V_{IH}$:** By symmetry, $$\boxed{V_{IH} = \frac{5 V_{DD} - 2 V_t}{8}}$$ The noise margins become: $$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = V_{DD} - \frac{5 V_{DD} - 2 V_t}{8} = \frac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8}$$ $$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = \frac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8} - 0 = \frac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8}$$ **Observation:** $NM_H = NM_L$ — the symmetric inverter has perfectly balanced noise immunity. *(7 marks)* --- **(b)** *Numerical Computation:* Given: $V_{DD} = 2.5 \text{ V}$, $V_t = 0.4 \text{ V}$, $k_r = 1$. **Switching Threshold:** $$V_M = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{2.5}{2} = 1.25 \text{ V}$$ **$V_{IL}$:** $$V_{IL} = \frac{3(2.5) + 2(0.4)}{8} = \frac{7.5 + 0.8}{8} = \frac{8.3}{8} = 1.0375 \text{ V}$$ **$V_{IH}$:** $$V_{IH} = \frac{5(2.5) - 2(0.4)}{8} = \frac{12.5 - 0.8}{8} = \frac{11.7}{8} = 1.4625 \text{ V}$$ **Noise Margins:** $$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 2.5 - 1.4625 = 1.0375 \text{ V}$$ $$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 1.0375 - 0 = 1.0375 \text{ V}$$ **Comment on Noise Immunity:** $$\frac{NM}{V_{DD}} = \frac{1.0375}{2.5} \approx 41.5\%$$ With noise margins of approximately **41.5 % of $V_{DD}$**, the inverter exhibits **excellent noise immunity** — comparable to industry-standard 2.5 V CMOS libraries. The equal $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ confirm symmetric, balanced design. *(7 marks — full marks for correct numerical evaluation and meaningful design commentary.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to specify $k_r = 1$ for symmetric design** — Examiners award zero credit if you assume symmetry in $V_M$ but use the asymmetric current equation. Always state your assumption. > 2. **Mixing up $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ formulas** — A common blunder costs 1–2 marks. Remember: $V_{IL}$ is the **lower** threshold (smaller value), and $V_{IH}$ is the **upper** threshold. > 3. **Using the wrong sign convention for $V_{tp}$** — $V_{tp}$ is **negative** for a PMOS.
