Always substitute $|V_{tp}|$ or $-V_{tp}$ explicitly to avoid sign errors. > 4. **Neglecting to equate $I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$ before differentiating** — The slope condition is meaningless unless the original KCL equation is satisfied. > 5. **Not boxing the final answer** — KTU examiners reserve partial marks for unboxed expressions; a boxed result signals clarity and earns full credit. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Voltage Transfer Characteristics** - **VTC Definition:** Plot of $V_{out}$ vs $V_{in}$ as $V_{in}$ sweeps $0 \to V_{DD}$; the **static DC input-output curve** of any logic gate. - **Five Operating Regions:** Cutoff–Saturation–Both-Sat–Triode–Cutoff for NMOS (mirror image for PMOS). - **Cardinal Voltages:** $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} = 0$, $V_{IL} \approx 0.3 V_{DD}$, $V_{IH} \approx 0.7 V_{DD}$ (symmetric, ideal). - **Trip Point Formula:** $V_M = \dfrac{V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert + V_{tn}\sqrt{k_r}}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$. - **Symmetric Special Case:** $V_M = V_{DD}/2$. - **$V_{IL}$ (symmetric):** $\dfrac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8}$. - **$V_{IH}$ (symmetric):** $\dfrac{5 V_{DD} - 2 V_t}{8}$. - **Noise Margins:** $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ and $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$. - **Symmetric Inverter Property:** $NM_H = NM_L = \dfrac{3 V_{DD} + 2 V_t}{8}$. - **Sizing Rule:** To make $V_M = V_{DD}/2$, choose $(W/L)_p/(W/L)_n = \mu_n/\mu_p \approx 2\text{–}3$. - **Design Goal:** Maximise noise margin ⇒ centre $V_M$ at $V_{DD}/2$ ⇒ symmetric sizing. - **Industrial Practice:** Standard-cell libraries report $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, $NM_H$, $NM_L$ at every PVT corner. - **KTU Trap:** Always verify the region of operation before writing the current equation; an incorrect region yields an incorrect VTC. > **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Voltage Transfer Characteristics.**

topics.title: Static Characteristics - Switching Threshold and Noise Margin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Static Characteristics - Switching Threshold and Noise Margin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Static Characteristics of CMOS Inverter: Switching Threshold & Noise Margin

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Switching Threshold (V_M):** The switching threshold of a CMOS inverter is defined as the point on the Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) where the input voltage V_in equals the output voltage V_out. Mathematically, it is the V_in at which V_out = V_in. It is also called the **inverter threshold voltage** or **midpoint voltage**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Noise Margin (NM):** Noise Margin is a quantitative metric of how much noise a logic gate can tolerate without causing a logical fault at its output. It measures the "safety buffer" between the worst-case output voltage of one stage and the minimum input voltage required by the next stage to correctly interpret the logic level.

In any static CMOS inverter analysis, the static characteristics are obtained by sweeping the DC input voltage from **0 V** to **V_DD** and observing the steady-state output. The static VTC has five critical points of interest:
1. V_OL (Output Low voltage) = **0 V** (ideal)
2. V_OH (Output High voltage) = **V_DD** (ideal)
3. V_IL (Maximum input voltage that is still recognized as logic 0)
4. V_IH (Minimum input voltage that is still recognized as logic 1)
5. V_M (Switching threshold where V_in = V_out)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Building Elevator Floor" Model

Imagine a high-rise building with two floors: **Ground (Logic 0)** and **Top (Logic 1)**. The elevator (the signal) must decide which floor to go to:

- **Switching Threshold (V_M)** is like the **halfway floor** where the elevator operator says, "I am equally undecided — any small push up or down will tip me to one extreme." It is the perfect center of gravity.
- **Noise Margin** is like the **safety margin on each side of the halfway floor**. If noise (a gust of wind, vibrations) pushes the elevator slightly, it should NOT flip to the wrong floor. The bigger the safety margin, the more robust the building.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** An ideal digital designer wants a "rail-to-rail" symmetric inverter where V_M = V_DD / 2. This gives equal Noise Margins for both logic 0 and logic 1, making the circuit symmetric and most robust against noise.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| V_DD | Supply Voltage | **1.8 V / 3.3 V / 5 V** |
| V_Tn | NMOS Threshold Voltage | **0.4 V – 0.7 V** |
| \|V_Tp\| | PMOS Threshold Magnitude | **0.4 V – 0.7 V** |
| k_n | NMOS Transconductance Parameter | Process-dependent |
| k_p | PMOS Transconductance Parameter | Process-dependent |
| V_M | Switching Threshold | Ideally **V_DD / 2** |
| NM_H, NM_L | High and Low Noise Margins | **≥ 0.3 × V_DD** (typical) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis of Switching Threshold

## 2.1 Operating Regions at V_M

At the switching threshold, both the NMOS and PMOS transistors of the CMOS inverter are operating in the **saturation region**. This is a crucial assumption that makes the derivation tractable.

**Why saturation at V_M?**
- At V_M, both transistors are ON (conducting significant current).
- For the NMOS: V_DS = V_out = V_in = V_M. Since V_GS = V_in = V_M > V_Tn, and V_DS = V_M > V_GS - V_Tn, it is in **saturation**.
- For the PMOS: V_SD = V_DD - V_out = V_DD - V_in. Since |V_GS| = V_DD - V_in > |V_Tp|, and V_SD > V_SG - |V_Tp|, it is in **saturation**.

## 2.2 Logic Step-by-Step: Deriving V_M

**Step 1:** Apply the saturated current equation to NMOS.
**Step 2:** Apply the saturated current equation to PMOS.
**Step 3:** Equate the currents (KCL — the currents through NMOS and PMOS must be equal in steady state, since no DC current flows through the output node).
**Step 4:** Solve for V_in (= V_out = V_M).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$ | Switching threshold — the most important KTU formula |
| 2 | $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_n$ | NMOS transconductance parameter |
| 3 | $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_p$ | PMOS transconductance parameter |
| 4 | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_p (W/L)_p}{\mu_n (W/L)_n}}$ | Ratio parameter (symmetry factor) |
| 5 | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ when $r = 1$ and $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ | Condition for symmetric inverter |
| 6 | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | High noise margin |
| 7 | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Low noise margin |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ at $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ | Definition of critical points (gain = -1) |
| 9 | $V_{IL} = \dfrac{2V_{out} + V_{Tp} - V_{Tn} \cdot \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2V_{out}}{k_n R}}\right)}{...}$ (approx) | Lower critical point |
| 10 | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} = 0$ | Ideal rail voltages |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The switching threshold V_M directly determines the **trip point** of Schmitt triggers, the bias point of analog amplifiers built using digital inverters, and the metastability point of flip-flops. Noise margins are the foundation of static timing and signal integrity in any VLSI design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation of Switching Threshold V_M

**Given:** A symmetric CMOS inverter with NMOS (W/L)_n and PMOS (W/L)_p, threshold voltages V_Tn and V_Tp, supply V_DD.

**Assumption at V_M:** Both NMOS and PMOS are in saturation.

**Step 1: Write the drain current of NMOS in saturation**

$$I_{Dn,sat} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^2$$

Since V_GS = V_in and the body is grounded:
$$I_{Dn,sat} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{in} - V_{Tn})^2$$

**Step 2: Write the drain current of PMOS in saturation**

$$I_{Dp,sat} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{SG} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

Since V_SG = V_DD - V_in:
$$I_{Dp,sat} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

**Step 3: Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)**

At the output node, the current flowing into the PMOS must equal the current flowing out of the NMOS (in steady state, no current is sourced/sunk from the output):
$$I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_{in} - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

**Step 4: Simplify by cancelling the 1/2 factor**

$$k_n (V_{in} - V_{Tn})^2 = k_p (V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

**Step 5: Take the square root on both sides**

$$\sqrt{k_n} \cdot (V_{in} - V_{Tn}) = \sqrt{k_p} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)$$

**Step 6: Solve for V_in = V_M**

$$\sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{in} - \sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{Tn} = \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{in} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot \vert V_{Tp}\vert$$

Group the V_in terms:
$$V_{in} (\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}) = \sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_p} \cdot V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_p} \cdot \vert V_{Tp}\vert$$

$$V_{in} (\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}) = \sqrt{k_n} \cdot V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_p} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)$$

**Step 7: Final expression for V_M**

$$\boxed{V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}}$$

**Step 8: Define the symmetry factor r**

Let $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}$. Then the formula becomes:
$$V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + r (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** If r = 1 (i.e., k_p = k_n) AND V_Tn = |V_Tp|, then V_M = V_DD / 2 — a perfectly symmetric, noise-immune inverter.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A CMOS inverter is fabricated in a 0.18 μm process with V_DD = 1.8 V, V_Tn = 0.4 V, |V_Tp| = 0.4 V, μ_n = 450 cm²/V·s, μ_p = 150 cm²/V·s, (W/L)_n = 2, (W/L)_p = 4. Calculate V_M.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute k_p / k_n.
$$\frac{k_p}{k_n} = \frac{\mu_p (W/L)_p}{\mu_n (W/L)_n} = \frac{150 \times 4}{450 \times 2} = \frac{600}{900} = \frac{2}{3}$$

Step 2: Compute r.
$$r = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \approx 0.8165$$

Step 3: Apply the V_M formula.
$$V_M = \frac{0.4 + 0.8165 \cdot (1.8 - 0.4)}{1 + 0.8165}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.4 + 0.8165 \times 1.4}{1.8165}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.4 + 1.1431}{1.8165} = \frac{1.5431}{1.8165} \approx 0.8495 \text{ V}$$

Step 4: Compare with V_DD / 2 = 0.9 V. Since 0.8495 V < 0.9 V, the PMOS is slightly weaker; to bring V_M up to 0.9 V, increase (W/L)_p.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Noise Margins

Noise margins require finding V_IL and V_IH — the points on the VTC where the small-signal gain magnitude is unity:
$$\left\vert \frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} \right\vert = 1$$

**Region for V_IL:** NMOS in saturation, PMOS in triode (V_out is still high, transitioning down).
**Region for V_IH:** NMOS in triode, PMOS in saturation (V_out is low, transitioning up).

### 3.3.1 Derivation of V_IL

In the saturation–triode transition region, the NMOS current is:
$$I_{Dn} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{in} - V_{Tn})^2$$

The PMOS current (triode region) is:
$$I_{Dp} = k_p \left[ (V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)(V_{DD} - V_{out}) - \frac{(V_{DD} - V_{out})^2}{2} \right]$$

Equate, differentiate with respect to V_in, set dV_out/dV_in = -1, and solve. The result (after algebraic manipulation) is:

$$V_{IL} = \frac{2 V_{out} + V_{Tp} - V_{Tn} + k_n R (V_{DD} - V_{Tp} + V_{Tn})}{1 + k_n R}$$

where $R = \dfrac{1}{k_p (V_{DD} - V_{Tp})}$. A simplified closed-form approximation is:

$$\boxed{V_{IL} \approx \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{Tp} - V_{Tn}}{8}}$$

### 3.3.2 Derivation of V_IH

By symmetry of analysis (NMOS in triode, PMOS in saturation):

$$\boxed{V_{IH} \approx \frac{5V_{DD} - 2V_{Tp} + V_{Tn}}{8}}$$

### 3.3.3 Final Noise Margins

Assuming ideal V_OL = 0 and V_OH = V_DD:

$$\boxed{NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{Tp} - V_{Tn}}{8}}$$

$$\boxed{NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{Tp} - V_{Tn}}{8}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interesting Observation:** Under symmetric conditions (V_Tn = |V_Tp|), NM_H = NM_L — both noise margins are equal. This is the hallmark of a **well-designed symmetric CMOS inverter**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Design Parameters

| Parameter Change | Effect on V_M | Effect on Noise Margins |
|------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| ↑ (W/L)_p (wider PMOS) | V_M increases (shifts right) | NM_H ↑, NM_L ↓ |
| ↑ (W/L)_n (wider NMOS) | V_M decreases (shifts left) | NM_L ↑, NM_H ↓ |
| ↑ V_Tn | V_M increases slightly | NM_H ↑, NM_L ↓ |
| ↑ \|V_Tp\| | V_M decreases slightly | NM_L ↑, NM_H ↓ |
| ↑ V_DD | V_M increases proportionally | Both NM_H and NM_L ↑ |
| r = 1, V_Tn = \|V_Tp\| | V_M = V_DD / 2 | NM_H = NM_L (symmetric) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CMOS Inverter Schematic (Functional Block View)

The static characteristic derivation uses the following canonical CMOS inverter architecture. Below is a Mermaid functional block view illustrating the structure (since physical transistor-level schematics are best drawn in CAD tools, this block view captures the essential functional relationships).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Voltage V_in] --> B[NMOS Gate Terminal]
    A --> C[PMOS Gate Terminal]
    D[Supply V_DD] --> E[PMOS Source]
    E --> F[PMOS Drain]
    F --> G[Output Node V_out]
    G --> H[NMOS Drain]
    H --> I[NMOS Source]
    I --> J[Ground GND 0V]
    G --> K[Load Capacitance C_L]
    G --> L[Next Stage Input]

    subgraph Region_Analysis
        R1[Region 1: V_in 0 to V_Tn - NMOS OFF PMOS Linear - V_out V_DD]
        R2[Region 2: V_in V_Tn to V_M - NMOS Sat PMOS Linear - V_out transitions]
        R3[Region 3: V_in V_M - Both Saturation - V_out V_in]
        R4[Region 4: V_in V_M to V_DD V_Tp - NMOS Linear PMOS Sat - V_out transitions]
        R5[Region 5: V_in V_DD V_Tp to V_DD - NMOS Linear PMOS OFF - V_out 0]
    end
```

## 4.2 Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) with Critical Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VTC_Curve
        A1["V_in 0V"] -->|Region 1| A2["V_in V_Tn"]
        A2 -->|Region 2| A3["V_in V_IL"]
        A3 -->|Transition| A4["V_in V_M midpoint"]
        A4 -->|Transition| A5["V_in V_IH"]
        A5 -->|Region 4| A6["V_in V_DD V_Tp"]
        A6 -->|Region 5| A7["V_in V_DD"]
    end

    P1["V_OH V_DD"] -.->|Above V_IH| NMH["NM_H Noise Margin High"]
    P2["V_OL 0V"] -.->|Below V_IL| NML["NM_L Noise Margin Low"]
    NMH --> OUT1[Robust Logic 1]
    NML --> OUT2[Robust Logic 0]
```

## 4.3 Switching Threshold Derivation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Assume both NMOS and PMOS in saturation] --> S2[Step 2: Write I_Dn_sat equation]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Write I_Dp_sat equation]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply KCL - I_Dn equals I_Dp]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Cancel 1/2 factor]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Take square root both sides]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Solve for V_in]
    S7 --> S8[Final Result: V_M = V_Tn + r V_DD V_Tp / 1 + r]
    S8 --> S9[Special Case: r equals 1 and V_Tn equals V_Tp gives V_M equals V_DD divided by 2]
```

## 4.4 Noise Margin Visualization on the VTC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Voltage_Axis
        V1[V_DD] -->|V_OH| V2[V_IH]
        V2 -->|NM_H band| V3[V_M]
        V3 -->|NM_L band| V4[V_IL]
        V4 -->|V_OL| V5[0V]
    end

    subgraph Meaning
        M1[V_OH minus V_IH is the safety zone for Logic 1]
        M2[V_IL minus V_OL is the safety zone for Logic 0]
        M3[Steep transition at V_M is the gain region]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Switching Threshold of a CMOS Inverter
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**
The switching threshold V_M of a CMOS inverter is the input voltage V_in at which the output voltage V_out equals the input voltage V_out = V_in. It is the **midpoint of the VTC** where the inverter transitions from high to low output. At this point, both NMOS and PMOS transistors operate in the **saturation region**, and the NMOS drain current equals the PMOS drain current.

It is given by:
$$V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$

For a symmetric inverter, V_M = V_DD / 2.

---

### Question 2: Define Noise Margin. State its Significance.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
**Noise Margin** is a measure of the immunity of a logic gate to unwanted noise voltages superimposed on the input signal. It quantifies the difference between the worst-case output of a driving gate and the minimum input required by the receiving gate to correctly interpret the logic level.

**Two types:**
- **NM_H (High Noise Margin)** = V_OH − V_IH
- **NM_L (Low Noise Margin)** = V_IL − V_OL

**Significance:** Larger noise margins imply a more robust digital system, less prone to logical errors from coupling noise, ground bounce, or supply glitches. A well-designed CMOS inverter has noise margins of approximately **40% of V_DD**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Full Switching Threshold & Noise Margin Analysis
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the switching threshold voltage V_M of a CMOS inverter. A CMOS inverter in a 0.13 μm process has the following parameters: V_DD = 1.2 V, V_Tn = 0.35 V, |V_Tp| = 0.35 V, μ_n C_ox = 270 μA/V², μ_p C_ox = 70 μA/V², (W/L)_n = 1.5, (W/L)_p = 3. Calculate V_M and comment on the symmetry.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Derivation:**
At V_M, both transistors are in saturation. Equating drain currents:
$$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

Solving:
$$V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + r (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + r}, \quad r = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Step 1: Compute k_n and k_p.
$$k_n = 270 \times 1.5 = 405 \text{ μA/V}^2$$
$$k_p = 70 \times 3 = 210 \text{ μA/V}^2$$

Step 2: Compute r.
$$r = \sqrt{\frac{210}{405}} = \sqrt{0.5185} \approx 0.7201$$

Step 3: Apply V_M formula.
$$V_M = \frac{0.35 + 0.7201 \times (1.2 - 0.35)}{1 + 0.7201}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.35 + 0.7201 \times 0.85}{1.7201}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.35 + 0.6121}{1.7201} = \frac{0.9621}{1.7201} \approx 0.5593 \text{ V}$$

Step 4: Compare with V_DD / 2 = 0.6 V. Since V_M = 0.5593 V < 0.6 V, the PMOS is weaker than the NMOS. To achieve symmetry (V_M = 0.6 V), (W/L)_p must be increased.

**Final Answer:** V_M ≈ **0.56 V**, slightly below V_DD/2. The inverter is asymmetric with NMOS being relatively stronger.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of the VTC of a CMOS inverter, define V_IL, V_IH, V_OH, V_OL, and derive expressions for the noise margins. For the inverter in part (a), calculate the noise margins using the approximate formulas.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Definitions:**
- V_OL: Output voltage when input is at logic 1 (V_in = V_DD); ideally = 0 V.
- V_OH: Output voltage when input is at logic 0 (V_in = 0); ideally = V_DD.
- V_IL: Maximum input voltage recognized as logic 0. Defined as the point on VTC where gain = -1 (NMOS saturation / PMOS triode region).
- V_IH: Minimum input voltage recognized as logic 1. Defined as the point on VTC where gain = -1 (NMOS triode / PMOS saturation region).

**Approximate Closed-Form Expressions (assuming V_Tn = |V_Tp| = V_T and ignoring channel length modulation):**

$$V_{IL} \approx \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{Tp} - V_{Tn}}{8} = \frac{3 \times 1.2 + 2(-0.35) - 0.35}{8}$$

$$V_{IL} = \frac{3.6 - 0.7 - 0.35}{8} = \frac{2.55}{8} \approx 0.319 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{IH} \approx \frac{5V_{DD} - 2V_{Tp} + V_{Tn}}{8} = \frac{5 \times 1.2 - 2(-0.35) + 0.35}{8}$$

$$V_{IH} = \frac{6.0 + 0.7 + 0.35}{8} = \frac{7.05}{8} \approx 0.881 \text{ V}$$

**Noise Margins:**

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 0.319 - 0 = 0.319 \text{ V}$$

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 1.2 - 0.881 = 0.319 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answer:** NM_L = NM_H ≈ **0.319 V** (approximately **26.6% of V_DD**) — symmetric because V_Tn = |V_Tp|.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Design for Symmetry & Parameter Sensitivity
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the static characteristics of a CMOS inverter with a neatly labeled voltage transfer curve. Discuss the role of the (W/L) ratio in determining the switching threshold and the symmetry of the VTC.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The static VTC of a CMOS inverter is divided into **five operating regions**:

| Region | V_in Range | NMOS | PMOS | V_out |
|--------|-----------|------|------|-------|
| 1 | 0 ≤ V_in < V_Tn | Cut-off | Linear (triode) | V_OH = V_DD |
| 2 | V_Tn ≤ V_in < V_M | Saturation | Linear (triode) | Transitions from V_DD to V_M |
| 3 | V_in = V_M | Saturation | Saturation | V_M |
| 4 | V_M < V_in ≤ V_DD − \|V_Tp\| | Linear (triode) | Saturation | Transitions from V_M to 0 |
| 5 | V_DD − \|V_Tp\| < V_in ≤ V_DD | Linear (triode) | Cut-off | V_OL = 0 |

**Role of (W/L) Ratio:**
The (W/L) ratios of NMOS and PMOS directly control the transconductance parameters k_n and k_p, which in turn control V_M:

- If (W/L)_p is increased → k_p increases → r increases → V_M shifts upward.
- If (W/L)_n is increased → k_n increases → r decreases → V_M shifts downward.

For a **symmetric VTC** with V_M = V_DD / 2, the condition is:
$$r = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mu_p (W/L)_p}{\mu_n (W/L)_n} = 1$$

Since μ_n ≈ 2 to 3 × μ_p, the ratio is typically:
$$\frac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} \approx 2 \text{ to } 3$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a CMOS inverter with V_DD = 2.5 V, V_Tn = 0.5 V, |V_Tp| = 0.6 V, k_n = 100 μA/V², k_p = 60 μA/V², calculate (i) the switching threshold V_M and (ii) the (W/L) ratio required to make V_M = V_DD / 2 = 1.25 V.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Part (i): Compute V_M**

Step 1: Compute r.
$$r = \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = \sqrt{\frac{60}{100}} = \sqrt{0.6} \approx 0.7746$$

Step 2: Apply formula.
$$V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + r(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + r} = \frac{0.5 + 0.7746 \times (2.5 - 0.6)}{1 + 0.7746}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.5 + 0.7746 \times 1.9}{1.7746} = \frac{0.5 + 1.4717}{1.7746}$$

$$V_M = \frac{1.9717}{1.7746} \approx 1.111 \text{ V}$$

**Final Answer (i):** V_M ≈ **1.11 V**, which is below the desired 1.25 V.

**Part (ii): Required (W/L) ratio for V_M = 1.25 V**

Step 1: Set up the equation.
$$1.25 = \frac{0.5 + r(2.5 - 0.6)}{1 + r} = \frac{0.5 + 1.9r}{1 + r}$$

Step 2: Solve for r.
$$1.25(1 + r) = 0.5 + 1.9r$$
$$1.25 + 1.25r = 0.5 + 1.9r$$
$$0.75 = 0.65r$$
$$r = \frac{0.75}{0.65} \approx 1.1538$$

Step 3: Find the required k_p / k_n.
$$r^2 = \left(\frac{k_p}{k_n}\right) = (1.1538)^2 \approx 1.3314$$

Step 4: Required k_p.
$$k_p = 1.3314 \times k_n = 1.3314 \times 100 = 133.14 \text{ μA/V}^2$$

Step 5: Required (W/L)_p (assuming same μ_p, C_ox, and (W/L)_n = 1).
$$(W/L)_p = 1.3314 \times (W/L)_n \times \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p}$$

Assuming μ_n / μ_p ≈ 3 (typical):
$$(W/L)_p \approx 1.3314 \times 1 \times 3 \approx 3.99 \approx 4$$

**Final Answer (ii):** (W/L)_p / (W/L)_n ≈ **4 : 1** to achieve V_M = 1.25 V.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the assumption of saturation at V_M:** Many students apply the triode equation for one transistor — this is **wrong**. At V_M, BOTH are in saturation.
> 2. **Sign error on V_Tp:** PMOS threshold is negative; students often forget to take |V_Tp| in the formula.
> 3. **Mixing up V_IL and V_IH definitions:** V_IL is the HIGHEST V_in still logic 0; V_IH is the LOWEST V_in still logic 1. Reversing this gives a NEGATIVE noise margin.
> 4. **Skipping the "−1" slope derivation:** Just stating formulas without showing dV_out/dV_in = −1 will cost 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Not commenting on symmetry:** Always end with a statement: "V_M = V_DD/2 implies symmetric inverter with equal noise margins."
> 6. **Unit inconsistency:** μA/V² vs mA/V² — convert properly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points:**

- **Switching Threshold (V_M):** The V_in at which V_out = V_in. Both transistors are in **saturation** at this point.
- **Key Formula:** $V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n} \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$
- **Symmetry condition:** $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ requires $\sqrt{k_p/k_n} = 1$ AND $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp}\vert$.
- **Noise Margin NM_L = V_IL − V_OL** (where V_OL = 0 ideally).
- **Noise Margin NM_H = V_OH − V_IH** (where V_OH = V_DD ideally).
- **Critical Points V_IL, V_IH** are defined by the gain condition: $\vert dV_{out}/dV_{in} \vert = 1$.
- **Approximate formulas:** $V_{IL} \approx \dfrac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{Tp} - V_{Tn}}{8}$, $V_{IH} \approx \dfrac{5V_{DD} - 2V_{Tp} + V_{Tn}}{8}$.
- **Effect of W_p ↑:** V_M shifts up, NM_H improves, NM_L worsens.
- **Effect of W_n ↑:** V_M shifts down, NM_L improves, NM_H worsens.
- **Typical (W/L)_p / (W/L)_n ratio:** ~2 to 3 (because μ_n ≈ 2-3 × μ_p).
- **Larger Noise Margins → More robust design.**
- **VTC has 5 operating regions**; regions 2 and 4 are the transition (high-gain) regions.
- **Cascaded inverters and fan-out** considerations also depend on noise margin and DC operating point.
- **Body effect** (source not at body potential) shifts V_T, affecting V_M — important in cascoded designs.
- **Channel length modulation (λ)** flattens the VTC in the transition region, slightly degrading noise margins.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Characteristics - Device Capacitance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Characteristics - Device Capacitance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (MOSFET) VLSI design, **Device Capacitance** refers to the collection of parasitic, intrinsic, and extrinsic capacitances distributed across the four terminals of the transistor (Gate, Source, Drain, Body/Substrate). These capacitances govern the **dynamic (transient) behaviour** of the MOSFET during switching operations, dictating the propagation delay, charging/discharging time constants, and dynamic power dissipation of CMOS digital logic gates.

The complete small-signal capacitance model of the MOSFET comprises:
1. **Gate Oxide Capacitances** ($\mathbf{C_{gs}}$, $\mathbf{C_{gd}}$, $\mathbf{C_{gb}}$) — formed between the polysilicon gate and the source, drain, and bulk respectively, through the thin gate oxide ($\mathbf{SiO_2}$).
2. **Junction (Depletion) Capacitances** ($\mathbf{C_{sb}}$, $\mathbf{C_{db}}$) — formed by the reverse-biased p-n junctions between the source/drain diffusions and the p-type substrate (or p-well).
3. **Overlap (Fringe) Capacitances** ($\mathbf{C_{gs,ov}}$, $\mathbf{C_{gd,ov}}$) — caused by the lateral diffusion of source/drain under the gate oxide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Device capacitance is a *high-weightage* topic in Module 1. Questions (3-mark and 14-mark) frequently appear on the **region-wise classification** of gate capacitances (cutoff, triode/linear, saturation), the **bottom-plate + sidewall junction capacitance model**, and the **effective switching capacitance** formula $C_{eff} = \frac{2}{3} C_{ox} WL + C_{gd} W$ used in delay estimation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Water-Pipe Analogy:** Imagine a MOSFET as a **faucet-tap system** controlling the flow of water (current) through a pipe (channel). The **gate** acts as the tap handle. Now, every mechanical system has **inertia** — you cannot instantaneously open or close the tap; some finite time is required because the handle itself has mass, and the valves have spring-loaded seals. In a MOSFET, this "inertia" is the **device capacitance**.
>
> - **Gate capacitance** is like the *spring stiffness* of the tap handle: it represents the electrical energy that must be supplied to the gate terminal to move the handle (change $V_{GS}$).
> - **Junction capacitance** at source/drain is like the *water hammer effect* — the elastic cushioning of the fluid in the pipe walls when flow is suddenly interrupted.
> - **Overlap capacitance** is the *extra slack in the linkage* between the handle and the valve, an unavoidable mechanical imperfection.
>
> The larger these capacitances, the slower the tap responds — directly mapping to slower circuit switching speeds and higher dynamic power consumption.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants

| Symbol | Description | Typical Value (90 nm Node) |
|---|---|---|
| $\varepsilon_{ox}$ | Permittivity of SiO₂ | $\mathbf{3.9 \times \varepsilon_0 \approx 3.45 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m}}$ |
| $\varepsilon_{si}$ | Permittivity of Silicon | $\mathbf{11.7 \times \varepsilon_0 \approx 1.04 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F/m}}$ |
| $t_{ox}$ | Gate oxide thickness | $\mathbf{1.2\text{–}2\ \text{nm}}$ |
| $\varepsilon_0$ | Vacuum permittivity | $\mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of MOSFET Capacitances

The total device capacitance is divided into two primary families based on their physical origin:

#### A. Oxide-Related Capacitances (Intrinsic + Overlap)
These arise from the parallel-plate capacitor structure formed by the **gate polysilicon – thin SiO₂ – semiconductor channel**.

| Capacitance | Location | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{gs}$ | Gate-to-Source | Channel charge variation with $V_{GS}$ in the source region |
| $C_{gd}$ | Gate-to-Drain | Channel charge variation with $V_{GS}$ in the drain region |
| $C_{gb}$ | Gate-to-Body | Depletion-region charge variation under the gate |
| $C_{gs,ov}$ | Gate-Source overlap | Lateral diffusion of $S/D$ under gate |
| $C_{gd,ov}$ | Gate-Drain overlap | Lateral diffusion of $S/D$ under gate |

#### B. Junction (Diffusion) Capacitances (Extrinsic)
Formed at the **reverse-biased p-n junctions** between the heavily-doped $n^+$ source/drain and the p-type substrate.

| Capacitance | Location | Composition |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{sb}$ | Source-to-Body | Bottom-plate ($C_{j,sb}$) + Sidewall ($C_{j,sw,src}$) |
| $C_{db}$ | Drain-to-Body | Bottom-plate ($C_{j,db}$) + Sidewall ($C_{j,sw,drain}$) |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Physical Construction

1. **Oxide Capacitance Per Unit Area:** $C_{ox}'' = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ represents the capacitance per unit area of the parallel-plate gate-oxide capacitor. This is the **fundamental building block** of all gate-related capacitances.

2. **Channel Charge Coupling:** The gate terminal does not connect to the source or drain through a direct wire. Instead, the channel charge $Q_C$ (which contains mobile carriers) acts as an intermediate "floating conductor." The coupling from gate to channel creates distributed capacitances to source and drain that depend on the channel potential.

3. **Bias-Dependent Partitioning:** As the MOSFET transitions from cutoff → linear → saturation, the **channel charge distribution** $Q(x)$ changes shape, causing the partition ratio between $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ to vary dramatically.

### 2.3 Region-Wise Capacitance Behaviour (The Core KTU Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This region-wise classification is the single most-tested concept in KTU Board examinations for Module 1.

**Case 1 — Cutoff Region ($V_{GS} < V_{T}$):**
The channel is absent (no inversion layer). The gate sees only the **depletion-region capacitance** of the body.
$$C_{gb} = C_{ox}'' \cdot W \cdot L, \quad C_{gs} = C_{gd} = 0$$

**Case 2 — Linear (Triode) Region ($V_{GS} > V_{T}$, $V_{DS} \ll V_{GS} - V_{T}$):**
The channel is uniformly inverted from source to drain. The channel behaves like a resistor connecting source to drain through the gate oxide.
$$C_{gs} \approx C_{gd} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\, C_{ox}''\, W\, L$$

**Case 3 — Saturation Region ($V_{GS} > V_{T}$, $V_{DS} > V_{GS} - V_{T}$):**
The channel is **pinched off** near the drain. The remaining inverted channel has a triangular shape, so the gate-to-drain coupling vanishes. All channel capacitance is dumped to the source.
$$C_{gs} \approx \tfrac{2}{3}\, C_{ox}''\, W\, L, \quad C_{gd} \approx 0$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Region / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{ox}'' = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Oxide capacitance per unit area | All regions |
| 2 | $C_{ox} = C_{ox}'' \cdot W \cdot L$ | Total oxide capacitance | All regions |
| 3 | $C_{gs,ov} = C_{gd,ov} = C_{ox}'' \cdot W \cdot L_D$ | Overlap capacitance ($L_D$ = lateral diffusion) | All regions |
| 4 | $C_{gb} = W \cdot L \cdot C_{ox}''$ | Gate-body capacitance | **Cutoff** |
| 5 | $C_{gs} = C_{gd} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, W L\, C_{ox}''$ | Gate-source/drain capacitance | **Linear** |
| 6 | $C_{gs} = \tfrac{2}{3}\, W L\, C_{ox}''$ | Gate-source capacitance | **Saturation** |
| 7 | $C_{gd} = 0$ | Gate-drain capacitance | **Saturation** |
| 8 | $C_{j}(V) = \dfrac{C_{j0}}{\left(1 + \dfrac{V}{\phi_B}\right)^{M}}$ | Bottom-plate junction capacitance | Reverse-biased junction |
| 9 | $C_{j,sw}(V) = C_{j,sw0}\dfrac{\phi_B}{\phi_B + V_{SB}}$ | Sidewall junction capacitance | Sidewall model |
| 10 | $C_{db} = C_{j}(V_{DB}) \cdot AD + C_{j,sw}(V_{DB}) \cdot PD$ | Total drain-body capacitance | Diffusion area + perimeter |
| 11 | $C_{eff} \approx \tfrac{2}{3}\, C_{ox}'' W L + C_{ox}'' W L_D$ | Effective switching capacitance (digital) | Step-input approximation |
| 12 | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{eff} \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{D,sat}}$ | Intrinsic delay time constant | Switching transient |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where $\phi_B$ is the built-in potential (~0.7 V for $n^+$-p junction), $M$ is the grading coefficient (0.3 – 0.5), $C_{j0}$ is the zero-bias junction capacitance, $AD$ is the drain area, and $PD$ is the drain perimeter (sidewall length).**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Static CMOS Logic Delay Estimation:** The effective capacitance $C_{eff}$ directly appears in the propagation delay formula $\tau_{PHL} \approx \dfrac{0.69\, C_{eff}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$ used in logic synthesis tools.
- **Dynamic Power Calculation:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ — the load capacitance $C_L$ is dominated by device capacitance in deep-submicron circuits.
- **Sizing of Buffers:** The intrinsic capacitance $C_{int}$ determines the *self-loading* of a gate, setting the fan-out-of-4 (FO4) delay metric.
- **RF/Mixed-Signal Design:** Junction capacitances determine the **cut-off frequency** $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$ of RF transistors.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Region-Wise Gate Capacitances from Channel Charge

**Starting Principle:** The total channel inversion charge $Q_C$ in the gradual channel approximation is given by integrating from source ($x=0$) to drain ($x=L$):

$$Q_C = -W \int_{0}^{L} Q_n(x)\, dx$$

The channel charge density at any point $x$ (long-channel model) is:

$$Q_n(x) = -C_{ox}'' \left[ V_{GS} - V(x) - V_T \right]$$

where $V(x)$ is the local channel potential varying from $0$ at source to $V_{DS}$ at drain.

The total channel charge is then:

$$Q_C = -C_{ox}'' W \int_{0}^{L} \left[ V_{GS} - V(x) - V_T \right] dx$$

Using the identity $\int_0^L V(x)\, dx \approx \dfrac{V_{DS} \cdot L}{2}$ (linear approximation of channel potential in **linear region**), we get:

**In Linear Region:**

$$Q_C \approx -C_{ox}'' W L \left[ V_{GS} - V_T - \dfrac{V_{DS}}{2} \right]$$

The gate-source capacitance is defined as $C_{gs} \equiv \left\vert -\dfrac{\partial Q_C}{\partial V_S} \right\vert$ with $V_G, V_D$ held constant. Similarly, $C_{gd} \equiv \left\vert -\dfrac{\partial Q_C}{\partial V_D} \right\vert$ with $V_G, V_S$ held constant.

Differentiating:

$$C_{gs} = C_{ox}'' W L \cdot \dfrac{\partial}{\partial V_S}\left[ V_{GS} - V_T - \tfrac{V_{DS}}{2} \right] = C_{ox}'' W L \cdot \left[ 1 + \dfrac{0}{2} \right]$$

Wait — we must be careful. Recomputing in **charge-based form**:
$$Q_C = -C_{ox}'' W L \left[ V_{GS} - V_T - \tfrac{V_{DS}}{2} \right]$$

$$\dfrac{\partial Q_C}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{V_{DS}} = -C_{ox}'' W L$$
$$\dfrac{\partial Q_C}{\partial V_{DS}}\bigg\vert_{V_{GS}} = +\tfrac{1}{2} C_{ox}'' W L$$

The channel-to-source charge $Q_S$ and channel-to-drain charge $Q_D$ are partitioned in the ratio $1/2 : 1/2$ for the symmetric linear region:

$$Q_S = -Q_D = -\tfrac{1}{2} Q_C$$

Therefore:
$$C_{gs} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{ox}'' W L, \qquad C_{gd} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{ox}'' W L \quad \text{(Linear Region)}$$

**In Saturation Region:** The channel pinches off at $x = x_p$ where $V(x_p) = V_{GS} - V_T$. Integrating only to $x_p$, and using the velocity-saturation/short-channel triangular charge approximation:

$$Q_C^{sat} \approx -\tfrac{2}{3} C_{ox}'' W L \left( V_{GS} - V_T \right)$$

The drain end has no inversion (pinch-off), so **all the channel charge couples to the source**:

$$C_{gs}^{sat} = \tfrac{2}{3} C_{ox}'' W L, \qquad C_{gd}^{sat} = 0$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Effective Switching Capacitance $C_{eff}$

For a **step input** applied to the gate of a MOSFET, the transistor traverses all three regions (cutoff → linear → saturation) before settling. The average charge drawn from the gate equals the charge delivered to the output (when driving an identical load) averaged over a complete transition.

**Step 1 — Phase I (Cutoff to Saturation boundary, $V_{GS}$ rises from 0 to $V_T + V_{DS,sat}$):**
The device operates in saturation. The gate charge required is:
$$\Delta Q_{g1} = \tfrac{2}{3} C_{ox}'' W L \cdot \left( V_{GS} - V_T \right)\bigg\vert_{V_{GS}=V_T}^{V_{GS}=V_T+V_{DS,sat}}$$

For long-channel: $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_T$, so:
$$\Delta Q_{g1} = \tfrac{2}{3} C_{ox}'' W L \cdot V_{DS,sat}$$

**Step 2 — Phase II (Linear region, $V_{GS}$ rises from $V_T + V_{DS,sat}$ to $V_{DD}$):**
The total gate capacitance becomes $C_{ox}'' W L$ (full inversion), and the voltage swings by $V_{DD} - (V_T + V_{DS,sat})$.

For a long-channel device, this phase is negligible, and we approximate the total charge as:

$$Q_{gate,total} \approx \tfrac{2}{3} C_{ox}'' W L \cdot V_{DD}$$

Equating to $C_{eff} \cdot V_{DD}$:

$$\boxed{\,C_{eff} = \tfrac{2}{3}\, C_{ox}''\, W\, L + C_{ox}''\, W\, L_D\,}$$

The second term accounts for the overlap (Miller) capacitance that charges/discharges every cycle regardless of the switching event.

### 3.3 Derivation of Junction Capacitance with Bias

A reverse-biased p-n junction exhibits a depletion capacitance governed by the depletion width $W_d$:

$$W_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_{si}(\phi_B + V_R)}{q N_A}}$$

The capacitance per unit area is $C_j'' = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{si}}{W_d}$, giving:

$$C_j''(V_R) = \sqrt{\dfrac{q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}{2(\phi_B + V_R)}}$$

Normalising by zero-bias value $C_{j0}'' = C_j''(0)$:

$$\dfrac{C_j''(V_R)}{C_{j0}''} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\phi_B}{\phi_B + V_R}}$$

Generalising with the **grading coefficient** $M$ (to model non-abrupt junctions):

$$\boxed{\,C_j''(V_R) = \dfrac{C_{j0}''}{\left(1 + \dfrac{V_R}{\phi_B}\right)^{M}}\,}$$

Multiplying by the diffusion area $A$ and adding the sidewall component (perimeter $P$ multiplied by sidewall capacitance per unit length $C_{j,sw}$):

$$C_{db}(V_{DB}) = AD \cdot \dfrac{C_{j0}''}{\left(1 + \dfrac{V_{DB}}{\phi_B}\right)^{M}} + PD \cdot \dfrac{C_{j,sw0}''}{1 + \dfrac{V_{DB}}{\phi_{BSW}}}$$

This is the **standard SPICE model formulation** tested in KTU 14-mark questions.

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Region | $C_{gs}$ | $C_{gd}$ | $C_{gb}$ | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutoff** ($V_{GS} < V_T$) | $0$ | $0$ | $W L C_{ox}''$ | No channel; full depletion under gate |
| **Linear** ($V_{DS}$ small) | $\tfrac{1}{2} C_{ox}'' W L$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} C_{ox}'' W L$ | $0$ | Uniform channel; symmetric charge split |
| **Saturation** ($V_{DS}$ large) | $\tfrac{2}{3} C_{ox}'' W L$ | $0$ | $0$ | Pinch-off; channel charge couples only to source |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
Define the gate oxide capacitance per unit area $C_{ox}''$. What are its typical values for $t_{ox} = 2\ \text{nm}$?

**Model Answer:**
The gate oxide capacitance per unit area is defined as the capacitance of the parallel-plate structure formed between the polysilicon gate and the silicon channel, separated by a thin silicon-dioxide layer of thickness $t_{ox}$:

$$C_{ox}'' = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$$

where $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times \varepsilon_0 = 3.45 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m}$.

For $t_{ox} = 2\ \text{nm} = 2 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}$:

$$C_{ox}'' = \dfrac{3.45 \times 10^{-11}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.725 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{F/m}^2 = \mathbf{17.25\ fF/\mu m^2}$$

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for permittivity value, 1 for numerical substitution and result)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Why is the gate-to-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$ zero in saturation but $\tfrac{1}{2} C_{ox}'' W L$ in the linear region?

**Model Answer:**
In the **linear region**, the channel is uniformly inverted from source to drain, forming a continuous conductive sheet. The channel charge $Q_C$ is symmetrically split, with half the charge coupling to the source ($Q_S$) and half to the drain ($Q_D$). Differentiating $Q_D$ with respect to $V_D$ (at constant $V_{GS}$) gives:

$$C_{gd} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, C_{ox}''\, W\, L$$

In the **saturation region**, the channel pinches off near the drain. The inversion layer terminates at $x = x_p < L$ where $V(x_p) = V_{GS} - V_T$. Beyond this point, there is **no mobile charge** to couple the gate to the drain. Hence the gate-to-drain coupling vanishes:

$$C_{gd} \approx 0$$

*(3 marks: 1 for linear-region reasoning, 1 for pinch-off reasoning, 1 for the contrast/conclusion)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ in the **linear** and **saturation** regions of an NMOS transistor, starting from the gradual-channel approximation for the inversion charge. Explain physically why the values differ. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an NMOS device with $W = 1\ \mu\text{m}$, $L = 0.1\ \mu\text{m}$, $t_{ox} = 2\ \text{nm}$, lateral diffusion $L_D = 0.02\ \mu\text{m}$, compute the **effective switching capacitance** $C_{eff}$ in saturation operation. Comment on the contribution of overlap capacitance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the gradual-channel inversion charge density:
$$Q_n(x) = -C_{ox}'' \left[ V_{GS} - V(x) - V_T \right]$$

The total channel charge (integrated from $x = 0$ to $x = L$):
$$Q_C = -W \int_{0}^{L} Q_n(x)\, dx = -C_{ox}'' W \int_{0}^{L} \left[ V_{GS} - V(x) - V_T \right] dx$$

**Linear Region:** $V(x) \approx \dfrac{V_{DS}}{L} x$, so $\int_0^L V(x)\, dx = \dfrac{V_{DS} \cdot L}{2}$:

$$Q_C^{lin} = -C_{ox}'' W L \left[ V_{GS} - V_T - \dfrac{V_{DS}}{2} \right]$$

Partitioning 50:50 between source and drain (symmetric linear channel):

$$Q_S^{lin} = -\tfrac{1}{2} Q_C^{lin} = +\tfrac{1}{2}\, C_{ox}'' W L \left[ V_{GS} - V_T - \tfrac{V_{DS}}{2} \right]$$
$$Q_D^{lin} = -\tfrac{1}{2} Q_C^{lin} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\, C_{ox}'' W L \left[ V_{GS} - V_T - \tfrac{V_{DS}}{2} \right]$$

Differentiating:

$$C_{gs} = \dfrac{\partial Q_S}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{V_{DS}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, C_{ox}'' W L$$
$$C_{gd} = -\dfrac{\partial Q_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{V_{DS}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, C_{ox}'' W L$$

**Saturation Region:** The channel pinches off at $x_p$ where $V(x_p) = V_{GS} - V_T$. The remaining inverted region is triangular:

$$Q_C^{sat} \approx -\tfrac{2}{3}\, C_{ox}'' W L \left( V_{GS} - V_T \right)$$

Since the channel pinches off, **all** the charge is on the source side:
$$Q_S^{sat} = +\tfrac{2}{3}\, C_{ox}'' W L \left( V_{GS} - V_T \right), \qquad Q_D^{sat} \approx 0$$

Therefore:
$$C_{gs}^{sat} = \tfrac{2}{3}\, C_{ox}'' W L, \qquad C_{gd}^{sat} \approx 0$$

**Physical Explanation:** In saturation, the high field near the drain sweeps carriers out into the depletion region, leaving no inversion layer there. The gate can only "see" the source-side channel charge, hence $C_{gd} = 0$ and $C_{gs}$ rises to $\tfrac{2}{3} C_{ox}'' W L$ (the $\tfrac{2}{3}$ factor arises from the triangular shape of the saturated channel).

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given:
- $W = 1\ \mu\text{m} = 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$
- $L = 0.1\ \mu\text{m} = 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$
- $t_{ox} = 2\ \text{nm} = 2 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}$
- $L_D = 0.02\ \mu\text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}$

**Step 1 — Compute $C_{ox}''$:**
$$C_{ox}'' = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.45 \times 10^{-11}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.725 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{F/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Intrinsic Saturation Capacitance:**
$$C_{gs}^{sat} = \tfrac{2}{3}\, C_{ox}''\, W\, L = \tfrac{2}{3} \times 1.725 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-7}$$
$$C_{gs}^{sat} = \tfrac{2}{3} \times 1.725 \times 10^{-15} = 1.15 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{F} = \mathbf{1.15\ fF}$$

**Step 3 — Overlap Capacitance:**
$$C_{gd,ov} = C_{ox}''\, W\, L_D = 1.725 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^{-8}$$
$$C_{gd,ov} = 3.45 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{F} = \mathbf{0.345\ fF}$$

**Step 4 — Effective Switching Capacitance:**
$$C_{eff} = \tfrac{2}{3}\, C_{ox}''\, W\, L + C_{ox}''\, W\, L_D$$
$$C_{eff} = 1.15\ \text{fF} + 0.345\ \text{fF} = \mathbf{1.495\ fF}$$

**Comment:** The overlap capacitance $C_{gd,ov} \approx 0.345$ fF constitutes approximately **23%** of the total $C_{eff}$. In modern short-channel devices where $L \approx L_D$, this overlap contribution dominates the dynamic power budget and is a critical concern in nanometer CMOS design.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the physical origin of **junction (depletion) capacitances** $C_{sb}$ and $C_{db}$ in a MOSFET. Derive the bias-dependent bottom-plate capacitance formula and state its SPICE-model form including sidewall components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an NMOS in a 65 nm process, the drain diffusion area is $AD = 0.5\ \mu\text{m}^2$, perimeter $PD = 3\ \mu\text{m}$, zero-bias bottom-plate capacitance per unit area $C_{j0}'' = 10^{-3}\ \text{F/m}^2$, zero-bias sidewall capacitance per unit length $C_{j,sw0}'' = 5 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F/m}$, built-in potential $\phi_B = 0.7\ \text{V}$, $\phi_{BSW} = 0.6\ \text{V}$, grading coefficient $M = 0.4$. Compute $C_{db}$ at $V_{DB} = 1\ \text{V}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Physical Origin and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Physical Origin:** The source and drain regions are formed by heavy $n^+$ doping (typically via ion implantation or diffusion) into the p-type substrate. This creates **abrupt p-n junctions** that, under normal circuit operation, are reverse-biased. A reverse-biased junction develops a **depletion region** of width $W_d$ devoid of mobile carriers, which behaves like the dielectric of a parallel-plate capacitor, with the neutral n+ and p-regions acting as the two plates.

**Derivation:** The depletion width under reverse bias $V_R$ is obtained from Poisson's equation:
$$W_d = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_{si}(\phi_B + V_R)}{q N_A}}$$

The capacitance per unit area is $C_j'' = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{si}}{W_d}$:

$$C_j''(V_R) = \sqrt{\dfrac{q\varepsilon_{si} N_A}{2(\phi_B + V_R)}}$$

Normalising by the zero-bias value $C_{j0}'' = C_j''(0) = \sqrt{\dfrac{q\varepsilon_{si} N_A}{2\phi_B}}$:

$$\dfrac{C_j''(V_R)}{C_{j0}''} = \left(\dfrac{\phi_B}{\phi_B + V_R}\right)^{1/2}$$

Generalising with grading coefficient $M$ for non-abrupt (linearly graded) junctions:

$$\boxed{\,C_j''(V_R) = \dfrac{C_{j0}''}{\left(1 + \dfrac{V_R}{\phi_B}\right)^{M}}\,}$$

**SPICE Model with Sidewall:**
$$\boxed{\,C_{db} = AD \cdot \dfrac{C_{j0}''}{\left(1 + \dfrac{V_{DB}}{\phi_B}\right)^{M}} + PD \cdot \dfrac{C_{j,sw0}''}{1 + \dfrac{V_{DB}}{\phi_{BSW}}}\,}$$

The first term is the **bottom-plate** (area) component; the second is the **sidewall** (perimeter) component that arises because the junction terminates laterally along the STI/oxide sidewall.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Bottom-Plate Component:**
$$C_{j,bottom} = AD \cdot \dfrac{C_{j0}''}{\left(1 + \dfrac{V_{DB}}{\phi_B}\right)^{M}}$$
$$= 0.5 \times 10^{-12} \cdot \dfrac{10^{-3}}{\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{0.7}\right)^{0.4}}$$
$$= 0.5 \times 10^{-12} \cdot \dfrac{10^{-3}}{(2.4286)^{0.4}}$$

Computing $(2.4286)^{0.4}$: Take natural log: $\ln(2.4286) \approx 0.887$, then $0.4 \times 0.887 = 0.355$, $e^{0.355} \approx 1.426$.

$$C_{j,bottom} = 0.5 \times 10^{-12} \cdot \dfrac{10^{-3}}{1.426} = 0.5 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 7.014 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$C_{j,bottom} = 3.507 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{F} = \mathbf{0.351\ fF}$$

**Step 2 — Sidewall Component:**
$$C_{j,sw} = PD \cdot \dfrac{C_{j,sw0}''}{1 + \dfrac{V_{DB}}{\phi_{BSW}}}$$
$$= 3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-10}}{1 + \dfrac{1}{0.6}}$$
$$= 3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-10}}{2.6667}$$
$$= 3 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.875 \times 10^{-10}$$
$$= 5.625 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{F} = \mathbf{0.563\ fF}$$

**Step 3 — Total Drain-Body Capacitance:**
$$C_{db} = C_{j,bottom} + C_{j,sw} = 0.351\ \text{fF} + 0.563\ \text{fF} = \mathbf{0.914\ fF}$$

**Comment:** The sidewall component dominates (61.6% of total) in modern short-channel devices, because the drain diffusion area shrinks faster than its perimeter due to scaling. This is why advanced nodes require **raised source/drain** epitaxy to reduce junction capacitance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include overlap capacitance** when computing $C_{eff}$. Students frequently write only $\tfrac{2}{3} C_{ox}'' W L$ and lose 2–3 marks. Always add $C_{ox}'' W L_D$.
> 2. **Confusing bias polarity** in junction capacitance. The formula uses $V_R = V_{DB}$ (reverse bias magnitude), not $V_{BD}$.
> 3. **Mixing grading coefficients**: bottom-plate uses $\phi_B$ and $M$; sidewall uses $\phi_{BSW}$ and (typically) $M_{SW} = 1/2$. Do **not** mix them.
> 4. **Saturation $C_{gd} = 0$ is the intrinsic value**. Extrinsic overlap $C_{gd,ov}$ remains and must be added for total $C_{gd}$.
> 5. **Units trap**: $C_{ox}''$ has units of F/m² (not F/m). Convert $W$, $L$ to metres before multiplying.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **$C_{ox}'' = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$** is the foundation; all gate capacitances are proportional to it.
- ✅ **Region-wise gate capacitance:**
  - *Cutoff* → $C_{gb} = W L C_{ox}''$, others $= 0$.
  - *Linear* → $C_{gs} = C_{gd} = \tfrac{1}{2} W L C_{ox}''$.
  - *Saturation* → $C_{gs} = \tfrac{2}{3} W L C_{ox}''$, $C_{gd} = 0$.
- ✅ **Overlap capacitance** $C_{ov} = C_{ox}'' W L_D$ exists in *all* regions and is critical for Miller effect.
- ✅ **Effective switching capacitance** for digital delay estimation: $C_{eff} \approx \tfrac{2}{3} C_{ox}'' W L + C_{ox}'' W L_D$.
- ✅ **Junction capacitance** has two components: **bottom-plate (area)** and **sidewall (perimeter)**.
- ✅ **Bias dependence:** $C_j(V_R) = \dfrac{C_{j0}}{\left(1 + \dfrac{V_R}{\phi_B}\right)^{M}}$ — decreases with reverse bias.
- ✅ **Dynamic power** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ is set by total device capacitance.
- ✅ **Miller multiplication** of $C_{gd}$: in a common-source amplifier, effective input capacitance $= C_{gs} + (1 + A_v) C_{gd}$, often dominated by the Miller term in high-gain stages.
- ✅ **Short-channel reality:** Velocity saturation and channel-length modulation modify the ideal $\tfrac{2}{3}$ factor; KTU accepts the long-channel ideal values unless otherwise specified.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC Delay Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** RC Delay Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) design, the **RC Delay Model** is a first-order mathematical abstraction used to estimate the time required for a digital signal to propagate through the resistive and capacitive parasitics of interconnects and transistors. It models the propagation delay of a CMOS gate as the time taken for a step input to cause the output node voltage to transition between the **10% and 90%** levels (or 50% to 50%, depending on the metric), driven by the equivalent on-resistance of the transistor charging/discharging the load capacitance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The RC delay model forms the analytical backbone of the **MOS Transistor Theory** module. Every delay equation ultimately reduces to a product of an equivalent resistance $R_{eq}$ and a load capacitance $C_L$ — a quantity expressed in **picoseconds (ps)** for nanometer-scale CMOS nodes.

The fundamental relationship governing transient analysis of a first-order RC network is given by:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

For a discharging node:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine filling a large overhead water tank (the **load capacitance** $C_L$) using a narrow garden pipe (the **transistor's channel resistance** $R_{on}$). Because the pipe is narrow, water trickles in slowly — it takes a long time for the tank to fill. If you replace the narrow pipe with a wider one (lower $R_{on}$, like a bigger NMOS transistor), the tank fills much faster. The RC Delay Model formalizes this exact intuition: the **time to "fill" the output node's capacitance is proportional to the product of the resistance of the path and the size of the capacitance** that must be charged.

Similarly, a **long copper interconnect** behaves like a long, leaky hose. Not only does it offer a resistance to current flow, but it also acts like a distributed sponge (parasitic capacitance to the substrate), absorbing charge along its length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Units Used Throughout:**
> - Capacitance ($C$): **Farads (F)**, typically fF (femtofarads) in modern nodes.
> - Resistance ($R$): **Ohms ($\Omega$)**, typically k$\Omega$ in sub-threshold, hundreds of $\Omega$ in strong inversion.
> - Delay ($\tau$ or $t_p$): **seconds (s)**, typically **ps** or **ns**.
> - Dielectric Constant of SiO₂ ($\varepsilon_{r}$): **3.9**
> - Permittivity of Free Space ($\varepsilon_0$): **$8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m**

## 1.3 Why the RC Delay Model Matters in VLSI

Modern VLSI chips contain **billions of transistors** connected by kilometers of interconnect. Simulating every transistor with SPICE is computationally intractable for full-chip analysis. The RC Delay Model provides a **closed-form analytical estimate** of delay that scales linearly with circuit complexity (Elmore Delay), making it indispensable for:

1. **Logical Effort synthesis** (choosing transistor sizes for optimal path delay).
2. **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** in EDA tools like Synopsys PrimeTime.
3. **Interconnect planning and buffer insertion** in physical design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The First-Order RC Step Response

Consider a simple circuit: an ideal voltage step $V_{DD}$ applied through a switch and a resistor $R$ to a capacitor $C$. Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)**:

$$V_{DD} = i(t) \cdot R + V_{out}(t)$$

Since $i(t) = C \cdot \frac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt}$, we get the governing differential equation:

$$RC \cdot \frac{dV_{out}(t)}{dt} + V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}$$

Solving this first-order linear ODE with initial condition $V_{out}(0) = 0$:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Constant $\tau = RC$:** The product $RC$ has units of seconds and represents the time required for the output to reach approximately **63.2%** of its final value ($1 - e^{-1} = 0.632$). It is the single most important figure of merit in delay analysis.

## 2.2 Propagation Delay Definitions

There are three standard metrics used to quantify delay:

1. **$t_{PHL}$ (Propagation Delay, High-to-Low):** Time from 50% of input transition to 50% of output falling transition.
2. **$t_{PLH}$ (Propagation Delay, Low-to-High):** Time from 50% of input transition to 50% of output rising transition.
3. **$t_p$ (Average Propagation Delay):** The arithmetic mean used in KTU problems.

$$t_p = \frac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2}$$

## 2.3 The Step Response at Key Voltage Points

Setting $V_{out}(t) = 0.5 V_{DD}$ for the 50% delay:

$$0.5 V_{DD} = V_{DD}(1 - e^{-t_{p}/\tau}) \implies e^{-t_{p}/\tau} = 0.5$$

$$t_p = \tau \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.69 \, RC$$

This is the famous **0.69 RC** approximation universally used by VLSI designers. It represents the delay of a single inverter driving a lumped capacitive load, assuming a constant linear resistance.

## 2.4 CMOS Inverter Delay Analysis

For a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$, the NMOS transistor (pull-down) and PMOS transistor (pull-up) act as nonlinear resistors. We approximate them as equivalent linear resistors $R_n$ (NMOS on-resistance) and $R_p$ (PMOS on-resistance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 50% Delay Formula for CMOS Inverter:**
> - $t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{n,eq} \cdot C_L$ (output falling — NMOS active)
> - $t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{p,eq} \cdot C_L$ (output rising — PMOS active)

For a **symmetric inverter** (where PMOS is sized roughly 2× the NMOS to equalize rise and fall times), $R_{n,eq} \approx R_{p,eq}$, and both delays are equal to $0.69 R C_L$.

## 2.5 The Elmore Delay Model (For Multiple RC Stages)

When a signal passes through a chain of resistances and capacitances (e.g., a tapered buffer chain or a resistive interconnect), the simple $0.69 RC$ no longer applies. **Elmore Delay** (1948) provides an elegant closed-form expression:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elmore Delay Formula:**
> $$\tau_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i \cdot C_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \left( R_i \cdot \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} C_j \right)$$
> 
> Equivalently:
> $$\tau_D = \sum_{k=1}^{N} C_k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i$$

Here, $R_i$ is the resistance between node $i$ and the supply, and $C_k$ is the capacitance at node $k$. The Elmore delay is the **first moment of the impulse response** and serves as a rigorous upper bound for monotonic step responses.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Single time constant | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$ | Step response (charging) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/RC}$ | Step response (discharging) | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $t_p = 0.69 \, RC$ | 50% propagation delay | seconds (s) |
| 5 | $t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{n,eq} \cdot C_L$ | Fall delay at inverter output | seconds (s) |
| 6 | $t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{p,eq} \cdot C_L$ | Rise delay at inverter output | seconds (s) |
| 7 | $\tau_D = \sum_{k=1}^{N} C_k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i$ | Elmore delay for N-stage RC ladder | seconds (s) |
| 8 | $R_n = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{tn})}$ | NMOS linear on-resistance | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 9 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | F/m² |
| 10 | $t_p = \dfrac{0.69 \cdot C_L \cdot L}{k' \cdot W \cdot (V_{DD} - V_T)}$ | CMOS inverter delay in terms of process params | seconds (s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The $0.69 RC$ and Elmore delay models are the workhorses of static timing analysis tools. Every EDA timing engine from **Synopsys, Cadence, and Mentor Graphics** ultimately collapses complex gate-interconnect networks into equivalent RC networks and applies these formulas (or their higher-order extensions like the Sakurai alpha-power law model) to compute path delays across millions of gates.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: 50% Delay of an RC Circuit

### Step 1: Write the Governing ODE
For a switch closing at $t=0$ connecting a voltage source $V_{DD}$ to an RC series combination:

$$V_{DD} = i(t) \cdot R + V_C(t)$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Capacitor Current Equation
By the definition of capacitance, the current flowing into the capacitor is:

$$i(t) = C \cdot \frac{dV_C(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Formulate the Linear First-Order ODE

$$RC \cdot \frac{dV_C(t)}{dt} + V_C(t) = V_{DD}$$

### Step 4: Solve the Homogeneous Equation
The homogeneous part is $RC \cdot \frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C = 0$, with characteristic root $\lambda = -\frac{1}{RC}$. The general homogeneous solution is:

$$V_{C,h}(t) = A \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

### Step 5: Find a Particular Solution
Since the forcing function is a constant $V_{DD}$, we try $V_{C,p} = K$ (a constant). Substituting:

$$RC \cdot 0 + K = V_{DD} \implies K = V_{DD}$$

So the particular solution is $V_{C,p}(t) = V_{DD}$.

### Step 6: Combine and Apply Initial Conditions
Total solution:

$$V_C(t) = V_{DD} + A \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

At $t = 0$, $V_C(0) = 0$ (capacitor initially uncharged):

$$0 = V_{DD} + A \implies A = -V_{DD}$$

### Step 7: Final Expression for the Charging Response

$$V_C(t) = V_{DD} \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

### Step 8: Compute the 50% Delay
Set $V_C(t_p) = 0.5 V_{DD}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
0.5 V_{DD} &= V_{DD} \left(1 - e^{-t_p/RC}\right) \\
0.5 &= 1 - e^{-t_p/RC} \\
e^{-t_p/RC} &= 0.5 \\
-\frac{t_p}{RC} &= \ln(0.5) = -\ln(2) \\
t_p &= RC \cdot \ln(2) \\
t_p &\approx 0.6931 \cdot RC
\end{aligned}$$

Thus, $t_p \approx 0.69 \, RC$, the cornerstone delay equation.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Elmore Delay for a 3-Stage RC Ladder

Consider a chain of three resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ in series, with shunt capacitors $C_1, C_2, C_3$ at each node, driven by a unit step input $V_{in}$.

The circuit is:

- $R_1$ between input and node 1, $C_1$ at node 1 to ground.
- $R_2$ between node 1 and node 2, $C_2$ at node 2.
- $R_3$ between node 2 and node 3, $C_3$ at node 3.

The Elmore delay at the output (node 3) is:

$$\tau_D = \sum_{k=1}^{3} C_k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i$$

Expanding:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_D &= C_1 (R_1) + C_2 (R_1 + R_2) + C_3 (R_1 + R_2 + R_3) \\
\tau_D &= R_1 C_1 + R_1 C_2 + R_2 C_2 + R_1 C_3 + R_2 C_3 + R_3 C_3 \\
\tau_D &= R_1 (C_1 + C_2 + C_3) + R_2 (C_2 + C_3) + R_3 C_3
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The first term, $R_1(C_1 + C_2 + C_3)$, indicates that the **input resistance** sees the sum of all downstream capacitances. This is intuitive — charge must flow through $R_1$ to fill *every* capacitor in the chain. Similarly, $R_2$ only "sees" the capacitors downstream from it ($C_2$ and $C_3$).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Variants

| Problem Type | Given | To Find | Key Formula |
|--------------|-------|---------|-------------|
| Single inverter delay | $R_{eq}$, $C_L$ | $t_p$ | $0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ |
| Buffer chain optimization | $N$ stages, total $C_L$ | Minimum delay path | Elmore delay minimization |
| Distributed RC line | $r, c$ per unit length, length $L$ | Delay to end | $\tau_D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} r \cdot \frac{L}{N} \cdot c \cdot \frac{L}{N} \cdot i$ |
| NMOS on-resistance | $k'_n$, $W/L$, $V_{GS}$, $V_{Tn}$ | $R_n$ | $R_n = \dfrac{1}{k'_n \cdot (W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{Tn})}$ |
| Capacitance composition | $C_{ox}$, Area, perimeter | Total gate cap | $C_g = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The RC Delay Computation Flow in a VLSI Design Tool

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Netlist with Transistor Geometries] --> B[Extract Parasitic R and C]
    B --> C[Build RC Network Graph]
    C --> D[Reduce to Lumped RC Stages]
    D --> E{Apply Delay Model}
    E -->|Simple Inverter| F[0.69 x Req x CL]
    E -->|RC Ladder| G[Elmore Delay Sum]
    E -->|Long Wire| H[Distributed RC Model]
    F --> I[Static Timing Analysis Engine]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Critical Path Delay Report]
    J --> K[Synthesize Buffers or Resize Gates]
    K --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart mirrors the inner loop of a modern place-and-route tool. The **RC Extraction** phase pulls resistance and capacitance from the layout database, the **Delay Model** computes the timing, and the **Optimizer** resizes cells or inserts buffers to close timing on critical paths.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: CMOS Inverter Lumped RC Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Voltage Vin] --> B[Equivalent Resistance Ron]
    B --> C[Output Node]
    C --> D[Load Capacitance CL]
    C --> E[Output Voltage Vout]
    D --> F[Ground]
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This is the canonical lumped model of a driving gate. The transistor is replaced by a single switch with on-resistance $R_{on}$ (which depends on transistor sizing and bias), and the load is collapsed into a single capacitance $C_L$ representing the input capacitance of the next gate plus interconnect capacitance.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Distributed RC Ladder (Elmore Delay Application)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input] --> R1[Resistance R1]
    R1 --> N1[Node 1 - Capacitance C1]
    N1 --> R2[Resistance R2]
    R2 --> N2[Node 2 - Capacitance C2]
    N2 --> R3[Resistance R3]
    R3 --> N3[Output Node - Capacitance C3]
    N1 --> G1[Ground]
    N2 --> G2[Ground]
    N3 --> G3[Ground]
    style R1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** A long interconnect is modeled as a **discrete RC ladder** for the Elmore delay calculation. Each segment contributes its resistance and downstream capacitance, weighted by the upstream resistance chain.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "propagation delay" with respect to a CMOS inverter. Write the standard expression for the 50% propagation delay of a simple CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$.**

**Model Answer:**

Propagation delay is defined as the time difference between the **50% transition point of the input waveform** and the **50% transition point of the output waveform** of a logic gate. It is the principal metric used to evaluate the switching speed of digital circuits.

For a CMOS inverter with NMOS on-resistance $R_{n,eq}$ and PMOS on-resistance $R_{p,eq}$ driving a load capacitance $C_L$:

- High-to-Low delay: $t_{PHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{n,eq} \cdot C_L$
- Low-to-High delay: $t_{PLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{p,eq} \cdot C_L$
- Average propagation delay: $t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ (for symmetric sizing).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**What is the Elmore delay model? State its significance in the timing analysis of VLSI circuits.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Elmore delay model** is an analytical technique used to estimate the 50% delay of an RC network (ladder or tree) by computing the **first moment of its impulse response**. For a network with $N$ nodes, the Elmore delay at the output node is given by:

$$\tau_D = \sum_{k=1}^{N} C_k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i$$

**Significance:**
1. Provides a **closed-form** expression for delay — extremely fast to compute.
2. Serves as a **rigorous upper bound** on the 50% delay for monotonic RC trees.
3. Forms the basis of algorithms for **buffer insertion** and **wire sizing** in physical synthesis.
4. Scales efficiently to **multi-million gate** designs where SPICE simulation is infeasible.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the 50% propagation delay of a CMOS inverter driving a purely capacitive load $C_L$ through a linear equivalent resistance $R_{eq}$. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS inverter has an NMOS on-resistance of $R_n = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ and a PMOS on-resistance of $R_p = 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$. It drives a load capacitance of $C_L = 50 \text{ fF}$. Calculate $t_{PHL}$, $t_{PLH}$, and the average propagation delay $t_p$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
1. Assume the input is an ideal step of magnitude $V_{DD}$ applied at $t=0$.
2. Model the MOSFET as a constant linear resistor $R_{eq}$ in series with the load capacitor $C_L$.
3. Write KVL: $V_{DD} = i(t) R_{eq} + V_{out}(t)$, with $i(t) = C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$.
4. Solve the ODE to get the step response: $V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}(1 - e^{-t/(R_{eq}C_L)})$.
5. Set $V_{out}(t_p) = 0.5 V_{DD}$ and solve: $t_p = R_{eq} C_L \ln(2) \approx 0.69 R_{eq} C_L$.

#### Model Solution for (b):
Given: $R_n = 1 \text{ k}\Omega = 10^3 \text{ }\Omega$, $R_p = 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega = 1.5 \times 10^3 \text{ }\Omega$, $C_L = 50 \text{ fF} = 50 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F}$.

Step 1: Compute $t_{PHL}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
t_{PHL} &= 0.69 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L \\
&= 0.69 \times (1 \times 10^3) \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \\
&= 0.69 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} \\
&= 34.5 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 2: Compute $t_{PLH}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
t_{PLH} &= 0.69 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L \\
&= 0.69 \times (1.5 \times 10^3) \times (50 \times 10^{-15}) \\
&= 0.69 \times 75 \times 10^{-12} \\
&= 51.75 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3: Compute average propagation delay:
$$\begin{aligned}
t_p &= \frac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2} \\
&= \frac{34.5 + 51.75}{2} \\
&= \frac{86.25}{2} \\
&= 43.125 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{PHL} = 34.5 \text{ ps}$, $t_{PLH} = 51.75 \text{ ps}$, $t_p = 43.125 \text{ ps}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the Elmore delay model with a neat RC ladder circuit diagram. Derive the Elmore delay expression for a 3-stage RC ladder network. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-stage RC ladder network (uniform) has $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 500 \text{ }\Omega$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = 100 \text{ fF}$. Calculate the Elmore delay at the output node. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Explanation:** The Elmore delay is the first moment of the impulse response of an RC network. For a tree-structured RC network with $N$ nodes, the delay at the output node is:

$$\tau_D = \sum_{k=1}^{N} C_k \cdot \sum_{i \in P(k)} R_i$$

where $P(k)$ is the unique path from the input source to node $k$.

**Derivation for 3-Stage Ladder:** Applying the formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_D &= C_1 \cdot R_1 + C_2 (R_1 + R_2) + C_3 (R_1 + R_2 + R_3) \\
&= R_1 (C_1 + C_2 + C_3) + R_2 (C_2 + C_3) + R_3 \cdot C_3
\end{aligned}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):
Given: $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 500 \text{ }\Omega$, $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = 100 \text{ fF} = 100 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F}$.

Step 1: Compute the sum of capacitances from each resistor's "viewpoint":
- $R_1$ sees: $C_1 + C_2 + C_3 = 300 \text{ fF}$
- $R_2$ sees: $C_2 + C_3 = 200 \text{ fF}$
- $R_3$ sees: $C_3 = 100 \text{ fF}$

Step 2: Substitute into the Elmore delay formula:
$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_D &= R_1 (C_1 + C_2 + C_3) + R_2 (C_2 + C_3) + R_3 \cdot C_3 \\
&= (500)(300 \times 10^{-15}) + (500)(200 \times 10^{-15}) + (500)(100 \times 10^{-15}) \\
&= 500 \times 10^{-15} \times (300 + 200 + 100) \\
&= 500 \times 600 \times 10^{-15} \\
&= 300,000 \times 10^{-15} \\
&= 300 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\tau_D = 300 \text{ ps}$ (or 0.3 ns).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** KTU students frequently forget to convert **fF → F** (multiply by $10^{-15}$) and **k$\Omega$ → $\Omega$** (multiply by $10^3$). A delay of $0.69 \times 1000 \times 50 = 34,500$ "something" is wrong — it must be **34.5 ps**. Always show unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.69 factor:** Writing $t_p = R C_L$ instead of $0.69 R C_L$ is a **2-mark deduction** in a 7-mark sub-question. The 50% delay requires $\ln(2) \approx 0.69$.
> 3. **Confusing $t_{PHL}$ and $t_{PLH}$:** Remember — **$t_{PHL}$** uses $R_n$ (NMOS discharges the output), and **$t_{PLH}$** uses $R_p$ (PMOS charges the output).
> 4. **Elmore delay sum errors:** Students often miss the **path-sum** logic. Each resistor $R_i$ must be multiplied by the sum of capacitances at and downstream from the node it reaches.
> 5. **Assuming symmetry:** The problem may not state $R_n = R_p$. Compute $t_{PHL}$ and $t_{PLH}$ separately, then average.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **RC Delay** = time to charge/discharge a node capacitance through a path resistance.
- ✅ **Time constant** $\tau = RC$ is the foundation; the **50% delay** is $0.69 RC$.
- ✅ **$t_{PHL} = 0.69 R_n C_L$** and **$t_{PLH} = 0.69 R_p C_L$** are the two essential inverter delay equations.
- ✅ **Elmore delay** generalizes the model to multi-stage networks: $\tau_D = \sum_k C_k \cdot (\text{path sum of } R \text{ to } k)$.
- ✅ **PMOS is ~2× slower** than NMOS for the same size due to lower hole mobility — to make $t_{PHL} \approx t_{PLH}$, the PMOS width is set to roughly 2× the NMOS width.
- ✅ **Delay scales with $C_L$** — to reduce delay, **size up the driving gate** (reduces $R_{on}$ quadratically per unit area) or **insert buffers** to split large capacitances.
- ✅ **Long interconnects** behave as **distributed RC lines**; the Elmore model provides an efficient upper bound.
- ✅ **Unit fluency is non-negotiable:** 1 fF = $10^{-15}$ F; 1 ps = $10^{-12}$ s; practice conversions before plugging in numbers.
- ✅ For KTU problems, always **state assumptions** (linear $R_{eq}$, ideal step input, lumped $C_L$) before deriving.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propagation Delay and Power Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Propagation Delay and Power Consumption**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Propagation Delay — Formal Academic Definition

**Propagation Delay ($t_p$)** in a digital CMOS logic gate is rigorously defined as the time interval between the **50% transition point of the input waveform** and the corresponding **50% transition point of the output waveform**. It is the single most critical performance metric of a digital VLSI gate, quantifying the *speed* at which a logic cell can process a binary transition.

Mathematically, two distinct directional delays are defined by the IEEE/ACM standard:

$$t_{pHL} = \text{time from 50\% of rising input to 50\% of falling output}$$

$$t_{pLH} = \text{time from 50\% of falling input to 50\% of rising output}$$

The **average propagation delay** of a gate is:

$$t_p = \frac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim):** *Propagation delay is the time taken for a change in the input logic level to produce a corresponding change in the output logic level, measured at the 50% point of the switching waveform.*

### 1.2 Power Consumption — Formal Academic Definition

**Power Consumption ($P_{total}$)** in a CMOS VLSI circuit is the rate at which electrical energy is converted to heat during steady-state and transient operation. It is the dominant constraint in modern IC design because it determines battery life, packaging cost, thermal reliability, and the maximum operating frequency.

The total power dissipation in a CMOS gate is partitioned into:

$$P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} + P_{short\text{-}circuit}$$

where $P_{dynamic}$ includes **switching power** and **internal (short-circuit) power**, and $P_{static}$ is the **leakage power** dissipated even when the circuit is idle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metric:** In modern 7nm/5nm CMOS nodes, **dynamic power contributes ~70-80%** of total power, while leakage power is rapidly rising and can reach 20-30% in idle states (e.g., mobile SoCs in sleep mode).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

#### 🚗 Analogy 1: Propagation Delay = "Water Flow Through a Pipe"
Imagine water flowing through a pipe with a valve. When you turn the handle (**input transition at 50%**), there is a noticeable lag before water fully emerges at the other end (**output transition at 50%**). The lag depends on:
- **Pipe length** (analogous to **wire/interconnect length**)
- **Pipe diameter** (analogous to **transistor sizing / drive strength**)
- **Water viscosity** (analogous to **carrier mobility in the channel**)
- **Pressure at the output** (analogous to **load capacitance $C_L$**)

A wider pipe (larger transistor) → faster flow (lower delay).
A longer pipe (more interconnect) → slower flow (higher delay).

#### 💡 Analogy 2: Power Consumption = "Leaking Tap + Water Bill"
A CMOS circuit's total power is like your household water bill:
- **Dynamic power** = The water you actually *use* when you open the tap to wash dishes (energy used for useful switching, $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$).
- **Short-circuit power** = The splash water that leaks past the tap handle during opening/closing (wasted current during transition).
- **Leakage (static) power** = The **dripping tap** that loses water 24/7 even when fully closed (subthreshold and gate leakage current).

You pay for **all three** even though only the first is "useful."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** The "switching power" equation $P = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ is the single most tested formula in this module. A $V_{DD}$ reduction from **1.2V to 0.6V** yields a **4× power reduction** — this quadratic dependence is the *primary* reason modern CPUs throttle voltage to save power (DVFS — Dynamic Voltage Frequency Scaling).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Propagation Delay — Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

The propagation delay of a CMOS gate is governed by a **first-order RC charging/discharging** mechanism. The logic is broken down below:

- **Step 1 — Identify the switching event.** When the input of an inverter transitions, one transistor (NMOS or PMOS) turns ON, while the other turns OFF.
- **Step 2 — The ON transistor behaves as a non-linear resistor** $R_{on}$. Its value depends on the technology node, transistor sizing (W/L), mobility ($\mu_n$, $\mu_p$), and the overdrive voltage ($V_{GS} - V_{th}$).
- **Step 3 — The output node has a load capacitance $C_L$**, composed of:
  - **Gate capacitance** of the next stage (fan-out gates).
  - **Diffusion (junction) capacitance** of the drain regions.
  - **Interconnect (wire) capacitance.**
- **Step 4 — The RC time constant** $\tau = R_{on} C_L$ dictates how fast the output node can charge or discharge.
- **Step 5 — The 50% delay** (the time to swing from one rail to the midpoint of the other) is:
  
$$t_p = 0.69 \, R_{on} \, C_L \quad \text{(for a single-pole RC network)}$$
