**PDN Implementation (NMOS):** Two NMOS transistors in **series** — conduction requires BOTH $A = 1$ AND $B = 1$ (i.e., $A \cdot B = 1$), pulling output to GND.

**PUN Implementation (PMOS):** Two PMOS transistors in **parallel** — conduction requires $A = 0$ OR $B = 0$ (i.e., $\overline{A} + \overline{B} = \overline{AB}$), pulling output to $V_{DD}$.

**Truth Table:**

| A | B | NMOS-A | NMOS-B | PMOS-A | PMOS-B | PDN | PUN | F |
|:-:|:-:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:---:|:---:-:|:--:|
| 0 | 0 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | ON | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | OFF | ON | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | OFF | 0 |

**Duality Proof:**
- PDN topology: Series (AND)
- PUN topology: Parallel (OR)
- The PUN is the **dual** of the PDN: series ↔ parallel, AND ↔ OR, NMOS ↔ PMOS.
- This guarantees that PUN ON $\Leftrightarrow$ PDN OFF for all input combinations.

**Transistor count:** 4 transistors (2 PMOS + 2 NMOS).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Set B]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat schematic, explain the operation of a 2-input C-CMOS NOR gate. Derive the expression for low-to-high propagation delay $t_{PLH}$ of a CMOS inverter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the sources of power dissipation in a CMOS inverter. Derive the expression for dynamic power consumption and explain how voltage scaling reduces power. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — 2-Input C-CMOS NOR Gate and $t_{PLH}$ Derivation

**Boolean Function:** $F = \overline{A + B}$

**PDN (NMOS):** Two NMOS in **parallel** — conduction if $A = 1$ OR $B = 1$, pulling output to GND.

**PUN (PMOS):** Two PMOS in **series** — conduction if $A = 0$ AND $B = 0$ (i.e., $\overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$), pulling output to $V_{DD}$.

**Truth Table:**

| A | B | F | NMOS-A | NMOS-B | PMOS-A | PMOS-B | PDN | PUN |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | ON |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | ON | OFF |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | OFF |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | OFF |

**$t_{PLH}$ Derivation:**

During low-to-high transition, the PMOS charges the load capacitor $C_L$. The output rises from 0 to $V_{DD}$:

$$C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = I_{DP}$$

For PMOS in saturation (initial phase) and then triode (later phase), the delay is computed as:

$$t_{PLH} = \frac{C_L}{k_p \cdot V_{DD}} \left[ \vert V_{TP}\vert + \frac{V_{DD} - 2\vert V_{TP}\vert}{2} + \frac{V_{DD}}{2} \right]$$

Simplified first-order result:

$$\boxed{t_{PLH} \approx \frac{C_L}{k_p \cdot V_{DD}} \left[ V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP}\vert \right]}$$

Symmetrically, $t_{PHL} \approx \frac{C_L}{k_n \cdot V_{DD}} [V_{DD} - V_{TN}]$.

#### Part (b) — Power Dissipation in CMOS

**Three sources of power dissipation:**

1. **Dynamic switching power** — Power consumed while charging/discharging load capacitances.
2. **Short-circuit power** — Power consumed during brief crowbar current when both PMOS and NMOS are partially ON.
3. **Static leakage power** — Power consumed due to subthreshold leakage and reverse-biased junction leakage, even when output is stable.

**Dynamic Power Derivation:**

Energy delivered by $V_{DD}$ in one full charging cycle: $E = C_L V_{DD}^2$

Energy stored on $C_L$: $E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$

Energy dissipated in PMOS: $E_{\text{diss, PMOS}} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$

Energy dissipated in NMOS during discharge: $E_{\text{diss, NMOS}} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$

Total energy per cycle: $E_{\text{total}} = C_L V_{DD}^2$

With switching activity $\alpha$ and clock frequency $f$:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f}$$

**Voltage Scaling Impact:**

Reducing $V_{DD}$ from $V_1$ to $V_2$ gives power reduction:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^2$$

For example, reducing $V_{DD}$ from 5 V to 2.5 V reduces dynamic power by **4×** (75% savings). This quadratic relationship makes voltage scaling the most effective low-power design technique in modern VLSI.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the body connection (bulk)** of the PMOS to $V_{DD}$ and NMOS to GND — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing PUN/PDN logic** — PMOS conducts on LOW input, not HIGH. Writing "PMOS is ON when input is HIGH" is a common error worth -1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate steps in $V_M$ derivation** — KTU expects the step where you equate saturation currents, take the square root, and isolate $V_M$. Showing only the final formula = -2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting $\frac{1}{2}$ factor in dynamic power** — confusing $P = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ with $P = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ is a frequent mistake. The correct form uses $\alpha$ (activity factor) and has no $\frac{1}{2}$ if $\alpha = 1$ is assumed.
> 5. **Not stating the duality rule explicitly** — Examiners want the explicit statement: *"PDN implements $F$ with NMOS; PUN implements $\overline{F}$ with PMOS in the dual topology."*
> 6. **Incomplete truth tables** for NAND/NOR — missing even one row results in -1 to -2 marks. Always show all 4 rows for 2-input gates.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Static CMOS / Complementary CMOS:**

- **Definition:** C-CMOS uses a PMOS pull-up network (PUN) and NMOS pull-down network (PDN) that are **logical duals**.
- **Ratioless:** Steady-state output is independent of W/L ratios of PMOS and NMOS.
- **Zero static power:** No DC current path from $V_{DD}$ to GND in steady state.
- **PUN Rule:** PMOS conducts when input is LOW; PUN is ON when PDN is OFF.
- **PDN Rule:** NMOS conducts when input is HIGH; PDN is ON when PUN is OFF.
- **Series = AND, Parallel = OR** for both networks, but with opposite transistor types.
- **Inverter:** 2 transistors (1 PMOS + 1 NMOS); $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ for symmetric design.
- **NAND gate:** 4 transistors; NMOS in series (PDN), PMOS in parallel (PUN). Preferred in design.
- **NOR gate:** 4 transistors; NMOS in parallel (PDN), PMOS in series (PUN). Slower due to weak PMOS stack.
- **Switching threshold formula:** $V_M = \frac{V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert + V_{TN}\sqrt{k_n / k_p}}{1 + \sqrt{k_n / k_p}}$.
- **Noise margins:** $\text{NM}_H = V_{DD} - V_{IH}$, $\text{NM}_L = V_{IL} - 0$; maximum when $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.
- **Dynamic power:** $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — the famous **$V_{DD}^2$** dependence.
- **Propagation delay:** $t_p \approx 0.69 R_{eq} C_L$ (Elmore delay).
- **Layout considerations:** PMOS in n-well, NMOS in p-substrate; PMOS must be sized ~2× to ~3× larger than NMOS to compensate for lower hole mobility ($\mu_p \approx \mu_n / 2$ to $\mu_n / 3$).
- **Static leakage sources:** Subthreshold conduction, gate-oxide tunneling (in advanced nodes), reverse-biased junction leakage.
- **Limitations:** $2N$ transistors for $N$-input gate → higher area for wide fan-in. Complex gates become large.
- **Application:** Universal — used in **all** digital ICs, microprocessors, ASICs, SoCs, SRAM bit-cells.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Techniques for Large Fan-In
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Techniques for Large Fan-In

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
**Fan-In (FI)** of a logic gate is the maximum number of input signals that the gate is designed to handle at its logical/structural level. In static CMOS, a gate with a large fan-in (typically **n ≥ 4**) is one whose delay, area, power, and noise margins deviate significantly from the ideal inverter behaviour because of stacked transistors, increased diffusion capacitance, and self-loading parasitics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAET525, Module 2):**
> *Design techniques for large fan-in* covers Logical Effort theory, Logic Partitioning, Buffer Insertion, Input Reordering, and alternative logic families (Pseudo-NMOS, Domino, DCVSL, PTL) used to mitigate the quadratic delay blow-up of wide static gates.

### The Intuitive Analogy — "The Toll Booth Queue"
Imagine a toll plaza with **n booths side-by-side**, all feeding a single toll collector who must verify and stamp **n tickets** sequentially before opening the gate:
- The **collector** = the single output node being charged/discharged.
- The **booths in series** = series NMOS (or PMOS) transistors in a NAND/NOR stack.
- The **waiting time** (delay) grows linearly with the number of booths, but if each ticket also slows down the person behind it (**self-loading**), the total delay grows **quadratically**.
- The engineering fix? Either **add multiple smaller toll plazas in parallel (partitioning)** or **install express sub-collectors (buffers)** to offload the work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Design Metrics for Large Fan-In**
> - **Logical Effort ($g$):** Ratio of input capacitance of a gate to that of an inverter delivering equal output current.
> - **Electrical Effort ($h$):** $h = C_{\text{out}} / C_{\text{in}}$ — the load-driving demand.
> - **Branching Effort ($b$):** Off-path capacitive loading.
> - **Parasitic Delay ($p$):** Intrinsic delay of the gate's own diffusion capacitance.
> - **Tapering Factor ($f$):** Ratio of successive inverter sizes in a buffer chain.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Large Fan-In Static CMOS is Problematic
A static CMOS n-input NAND/NOR has transistors in **series** in one of the rails. Consider an n-input NAND with all n PMOS in parallel (pull-up) and n NMOS in series (pull-down):

**Key observations:**
1. **Stacked NMOS** → effective pull-down resistance becomes $n \cdot R_{n,\text{inv}}$.
2. **Internal node diffusion capacitance** between series transistors adds self-loading.
3. **Worst-case HL delay** is dominated by the pull-down path:
   $$t_{pHL} \approx n \cdot t_{pHL}(\text{inv}) \quad \text{(without self-loading)}$$
   $$t_{pHL} \approx n^{2} \cdot t_{pHL}(\text{inv}) \quad \text{(with self-loading parasitics)}$$
4. **Worst-case LH delay** of an n-input NOR is similarly degraded because of stacked PMOS.
5. **Layout area and input capacitance** scale with n, increasing wire delay.

### 2.2 The Five Pillars of Large Fan-In Design

| # | Technique | Core Idea | Best For |
|---|-----------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Buffer Insertion (Tapered Chain)** | Insert optimally-sized inverters between driver and load | Driving large off-chip loads, large fan-in followed by heavy load |
| 2 | **Logic Partitioning** | Decompose n-input function into tree of 2-/3-input gates | Wide AND/OR/AOI/OAI functions |
| 3 | **Input Reordering** | Place latest-arriving input nearest the output node | Reducing worst-case delay with skewed inputs |
| 4 | **Alternative Logic Styles** | Use Domino / Pseudo-NMOS / DCVSL / PTL | Wide fan-in OR / NOR / XOR structures in datapaths |
| 5 | **Logical Effort Sizing** | Size transistors so each stage has effort $\approx e$ (≈ 2.71) | Minimising delay of multi-stage paths |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet — Memorise These for ESE**

| Formula / Symbol | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|------------------|------------|---------------|
| Logical Effort of n-NAND | $g_{\text{NAND}} = (n+2)/3$ | Compare with inverter's $g=1$ |
| Logical Effort of n-NOR | $g_{\text{NOR}} = (2n+1)/3$ | Compare with inverter's $g=1$ |
| Path Effort | $F = G \cdot H \cdot B$ | $G$ = product of $g_i$, $H = C_{\text{out}}/C_{\text{in}}$ |
| Stage Effort | $f_i = (F)^{1/N}$ | Optimum $f \approx 4$ for typical chain |
| Optimal No. of Stages | $N_{\text{opt}} = \ln(F) / \ln(f_{\text{opt}})$ | $f_{\text{opt}} \approx 3.6$ for self-loaded chain |
| Tapering Factor | $f = (C_L / C_{\text{in}})^{1/N}$ | Size ratio of successive inverters |
| Total Delay | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (g_i h_i + p_i)$ | In $\tau$ units (FO4 inverter delay) |
| Inverter Self-Load Ratio | $\gamma = C_{\text{int}} / C_{\text{out,inverter}} \approx 1$ | Used in self-loaded delay eqn. |
| Stack Delay (n-input) | $t_p \approx n \cdot (1 + (n-1)\gamma) \cdot t_{p0}$ | Static CMOS worst-case |
| Pseudo-NMOS Ratio | $r = (W/L)_p / (W/L)_n$ | $r \approx 1/4$ to $1/6$ for noise margin |
| Domino Pre-charge Delay | $t_{p} \approx C_L \cdot V_{DD} / I_{\text{eval}}$ | Eval half-cycle delay |
| Branching Effort | $b = (C_{\text{on-path}} + C_{\text{off-path}}) / C_{\text{on-path}}$ | Multiply across branches |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
Large fan-in design techniques are central to:
- **Address-decoder logic** in SRAM/DRAM (8-bit, 10-bit row decoders).
- **Wide comparators** in microprocessors (32-bit / 64-bit equality testers).
- **PLL/DLL phase detectors** that XOR many signals.
- **Read/write control paths** in cache and main memory.
- **Cryptographic S-boxes and parity trees** in hardware security modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Delay of an n-Input Static CMOS NAND

Consider an n-input NAND with equal-sized NMOS (W/L) and PMOS (W/L). The NMOS in series have their **internal diffusion nodes** acting as self-loading capacitors.

**Step 1 — Equivalent ON-resistance per NMOS:**
$$R_{n,\text{on}} = R_{n0} \cdot \left(\frac{L}{W}\right)_n$$

**Step 2 — n NMOS in series (Elmore ladder):**
The total pull-down resistance is $n \cdot R_{n,\text{on}}$. The internal diffusion capacitances create a self-loaded RC ladder.

**Step 3 — Elmore delay formula for n equal segments:**
$$D = \sum_{k=1}^{N} R_k \cdot C_{>\!k}$$

**Step 4 — For n identical RC segments** with $R = R_{n,\text{on}}$ and $C = C_{\text{int}}$ per node:
$$D_{\text{self}} = R \cdot C \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} i = R \cdot C \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Include output load** $C_L$ charging/discharging:
$$t_{pHL} = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} R_{n,\text{on}} C_{\text{int}} + n R_{n,\text{on}} C_L$$

**Step 6 — Approximation** for large n and self-loading dominant:
$$t_{pHL} \approx \frac{n^2}{2} R_{n,\text{on}} C_{\text{int}}$$

**Step 7 — Normalise to inverter delay** $t_{p0} = 0.69 R_{n0} C_{g,n}$:
$$\boxed{\;\frac{t_{pHL}(\text{n-NAND})}{t_{p0}} \approx \frac{n^{2}}{2} \cdot \gamma\;}$$

where $\gamma = C_{\text{int}} / C_{\text{out,inverter}} \approx 1$. This proves the **quadratic delay blow-up**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Going from 2-input NAND to 8-input NAND can increase delay by a factor of **16×** if sized naively. This is precisely why **Design Techniques for Large Fan-In** are essential in KTU Module 2.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Tapered Buffer Chain (Sutherland Method)

**Problem:** Drive a load $C_L = 4$ pF from a minimum-sized inverter of $C_{\text{in}} = 10$ fF, with $C_{\text{int,inv}} = C_{\text{in,inv}} = C_g$ (for simplicity, $\gamma = 1$).

**Step 1 — Compute electrical effort:**
$$H = \frac{C_L}{C_{\text{in}}} = \frac{4\,\text{pF}}{10\,\text{fF}} = 400$$

**Step 2 — Optimum stage effort** for self-loaded inverter chain:
$$f_{\text{opt}} \approx 3.6 \quad (\text{empirically derived; can also use } f_{\text{opt}}=e \approx 2.71 \text{ for } \gamma=0)$$

**Step 3 — Optimum number of stages:**
$$N_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\ln H}{\ln f_{\text{opt}}} = \frac{\ln 400}{\ln 3.6} = \frac{5.991}{1.281} \approx 4.68$$

Round to nearest integer: **$N = 5$ stages** for best delay, **$N = 4$** for minimum area-delay product.

**Step 4 — Recompute tapering factor** for $N = 5$:
$$f = H^{1/N} = 400^{1/5} = e^{5.991/5} = e^{1.198} \approx 3.31$$

**Step 5 — Size each stage** ($C_0 = C_{\text{in}} = 10$ fF, size ratio $f = 3.31$):
$$\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= f \cdot C_0 = 33.1 \text{ fF} \\
C_2 &= f \cdot C_1 = 109.6 \text{ fF} \\
C_3 &= f \cdot C_2 = 362.7 \text{ fF} \\
C_4 &= f \cdot C_3 = 1.20 \text{ pF} \\
C_5 &= f \cdot C_4 = 3.97 \text{ pF} \approx 4 \text{ pF} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Total delay** in $\tau$ units (FO4):
$$D = N \cdot (f + p) = 5 \cdot (3.31 + 1) = 5 \cdot 4.31 = 21.55\,\tau$$

Compare with a single-stage buffer of size $400\times$: $D = 1 \cdot (400 + 1) = 401\,\tau$. **A 5-stage tapered chain is ~18.6× faster.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** Tapered buffer chains trade area for delay in a manner that is provably optimal under the logical-effort framework.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Logic Partitioning Speedup

**Problem:** Implement an 8-input AND using (a) one 8-input NAND + INV, vs (b) tree of 2-input NANDs.

**Case (a) — Single 8-NAND:**
$$t_{p,\text{NAND8}} \approx \frac{8^2}{2} \cdot \gamma \cdot t_{p0} = 32 \cdot t_{p0}$$
Including the trailing inverter: $t_{p,a} \approx 32 + 1 = 33\,t_{p0}$.

**Case (b) — Tree of 2-input NANDs** (3 levels + final INV):
- Level 1: four 2-input NANDs in parallel, $t_{p} \approx 2\,t_{p0}$ each.
- Level 2: two 2-input NANDs, each driving 2 parallel loads, with branching.
- Level 3: one 2-input NAND.
- Total: $t_{p,b} \approx 3 \times 2\,t_{p0} + 1 = 7\,t_{p0}$ (ignoring fanout-back penalty).

**Speedup factor:** $33/7 \approx 4.7\times$.

> [!NOTE]
> Partitioning also reduces **input capacitance per gate**, easing the drive requirement of the preceding stage.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Large Fan-In Problem (n-Input NAND in Static CMOS)

```mermaid
graph LR
  A1[Input A] -->|NMOS1| M1
  A2[Input B] -->|NMOS2| M2
  A3[Input C] -->|NMOS3| M3
  An[Input n] -->|NMOSn| Mn
  M1 --> M2
  M2 --> M3
  M3 --> Mn
  Mn --> GND[GND]
  VDD[Vdd] --> OUT[Output Y]
  A1 -->|PMOS1| P1
  A2 -->|PMOS2| P2
  A3 -->|PMOS3| P3
  An -->|PMOSn| Pn
  P1 --> P2
  P2 --> P3
  P3 --> Pn
  Pn --> OUT
  P1 -.parallel.-> P2
  P2 -.parallel.-> P3
  P3 -.parallel.-> Pn
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** PMOS transistors are in **parallel** (pull-up network). NMOS transistors are in **series** (pull-down). The series NMOS stack is the source of the quadratic delay.

---

### 4.2 Tapered Buffer Chain (N = 5)

```mermaid
graph LR
  IN[Input Cin] --> STAGE1[Inv 1 - C1]
  STAGE1 --> STAGE2[Inv 2 - C2]
  STAGE2 --> STAGE3[Inv 3 - C3]
  STAGE3 --> STAGE4[Inv 4 - C4]
  STAGE4 --> STAGE5[Inv 5 - C5]
  STAGE5 --> OUT[Output CL]
  STAGE1 -.size ratio f.-> STAGE2
  STAGE2 -.size ratio f.-> STAGE3
  STAGE3 -.size ratio f.-> STAGE4
  STAGE4 -.size ratio f.-> STAGE5
```

| Stage | Capacitance (fF) | Driver Size (×min) |
|-------|------------------|---------------------|
| 0 (input) | 10 | 1× |
| 1 | 33.1 | 3.3× |
| 2 | 109.6 | 11× |
| 3 | 362.7 | 36× |
| 4 | 1200 | 120× |
| 5 (load) | 3970 ≈ 4000 | 400× |

---

### 4.3 Logic Partitioning — 8-Input AND as a Tree of 2-Input NANDs

```mermaid
graph TD
  A1[A1] --> NAND1[NAND2]
  A2[A2] --> NAND1
  A3[A3] --> NAND2[NAND2 b]
  A4[A4] --> NAND2
  A5[A5] --> NAND3[NAND2 c]
  A6[A6] --> NAND3
  A7[A7] --> NAND4[NAND2 d]
  A8[A8] --> NAND4
  NAND1 --> NAND5[NAND2 L2 a]
  NAND2 --> NAND5
  NAND3 --> NAND6[NAND2 L2 b]
  NAND4 --> NAND6
  NAND5 --> NAND7[NAND2 L3]
  NAND6 --> NAND7
  NAND7 --> INVF[Inverter]
  INVF --> OUT[AND8 Output Y]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The original 8-NAND has one tall stack of 8 NMOS. The decomposed tree has 3 levels, each with only 2 NMOS in series — reducing self-loading and delay by a factor of ~5.

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Choosing Among Fan-In Strategies

```mermaid
graph TD
  PROB[Large Fan-In Function F] --> Q1{Load C_L Large?}
  Q1 -- Yes --> BUF[Buffer Insertion Chain]
  Q1 -- No --> Q2{Function Decomposable?}
  Q2 -- Yes --> PART[Logic Partitioning]
  Q2 -- No --> Q3{Timing Skew?}
  Q3 -- Critical --> REORD[Input Reordering]
  Q3 -- Not Critical --> Q4{Static CMOS Power OK?}
  Q4 -- Yes --> SIZE[Logical Effort Sizing]
  Q4 -- No, Low Static Power OK --> ALT[Pseudo NMOS / Domino / DCVSL]
  BUF --> DONE[Optimised Realisation]
  PART --> DONE
  REORD --> DONE
  SIZE --> DONE
  ALT --> DONE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEAET525, Module 2]*
Define **Logical Effort** and **Parasitic Delay** of a CMOS logic gate. Why is the logical effort of an n-input NAND greater than 1?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Logical Effort (g):** The ratio of the input capacitance of a logic gate to that of an inverter that delivers the same output current, measured when both drive an identical load. It captures the gate's "intrinsic slowness" relative to the inverter.
- **Parasitic Delay (p):** The delay of a gate when it drives zero external load; it arises from the gate's own internal (diffusion) capacitances. For an inverter, $p_{\text{inv}} \approx 1$ (in $\tau$ units).
- For an n-input NAND, the input capacitance of each input transistor is scaled so that the gate can drive the same output current as an inverter. Since the pull-up has n PMOS in parallel (each smaller), and the pull-down has n NMOS in series (each wider), the total input capacitance $C_{\text{in,NAND}} = (n+2) C_{\text{in,inv}} / 3 > C_{\text{in,inv}}$ for $n \ge 2$. Hence $g_{\text{NAND}} = (n+2)/3 > 1$.

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEAET525, Module 2]*
List any **three** techniques used to design a logic circuit with a large fan-in. State one advantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per technique + advantage):**
1. **Buffer Insertion (Tapered Chain):** Inserts optimally-sized inverters between driver and large load — advantage: provably minimum delay for a given area constraint.
2. **Logic Partitioning:** Decomposes a wide function into a tree of smaller gates — advantage: reduces self-loading RC by breaking tall transistor stacks.
3. **Alternative Logic Style (e.g., Domino):** Uses dynamic precharge-evaluate — advantage: high fan-in OR/AND functions in one stage with small input capacitance and no stacked PMOS.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Modelled on KTU ESE Dec 2023 Pattern*

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the design issues in implementing a **large fan-in static CMOS NAND gate**. Derive the expression for its worst-case propagation delay showing the quadratic dependence on $n$.

**Model Solution:**

**Schematic (1 mark):** As shown in Section 4.1 — n PMOS in parallel, n NMOS in series, common output node $Y$.

**Design Issues (3 marks):**
- Series NMOS stack increases pull-down resistance by factor $n$.
- Internal node diffusion capacitances $C_d$ between series NMOS create self-loading.
- Worst-case $t_{pHL}$ increases quadratically with $n$ (proved below).
- Input capacitance of each input grows with $n$, increasing previous-stage drive burden.
- Increased short-circuit power during slow transitions.

**Derivation (3 marks):** As in Section 3.1, using Elmore delay on the n-segment RC ladder:
$$t_{pHL}(\text{n-NAND}) \approx \frac{n(n+1)}{2} R_n C_d + n R_n C_L \;\approx\; \frac{n^{2}}{2} R_n C_d$$
Normalising to the inverter delay $t_{p0}$ and using $\gamma = C_d / C_{\text{out,inv}}$:
$$\boxed{\;\frac{t_{pHL}(\text{n-NAND})}{t_{p0}} \approx \frac{n^{2}}{2}\,\gamma\;}$$
This shows **quadratic dependence on $n$**, justifying the need for design techniques.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A logic path has $C_{\text{in}} = 25$ fF, $C_L = 1600$ fF. Using the **Logical Effort method**, find (i) the optimal number of stages, (ii) the tapering factor, and (iii) the minimum delay in $\tau$ units. Assume $\gamma = 1$ and $f_{\text{opt}} = 3.6$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Optimal number of stages (2 marks):**
$$H = \frac{C_L}{C_{\text{in}}} = \frac{1600}{25} = 64$$
$$N_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\ln H}{\ln f_{\text{opt}}} = \frac{\ln 64}{\ln 3.6} = \frac{4.159}{1.281} = 3.25$$
**Round up: $N = 4$ stages** (closer optimum delay with integer stages).

**(ii) Tapering factor (2 marks):**
$$f = H^{1/N} = 64^{1/4} = (2^{6})^{1/4} = 2^{1.5} = 2.828$$

**(iii) Minimum delay (3 marks):**
For each stage, $D_i = f + p = 2.828 + 1 = 3.828\,\tau$. Total:
$$D_{\min} = N \cdot (f + p) = 4 \cdot 3.828 = 15.31\,\tau$$

**Verify using $D_{\min} = F + N \cdot p$:** $F = G \cdot H = 1 \cdot 64 = 64$ (inverter chain). With $N = 4$: $D = 64^{1/4} \cdot 4 + 4 \cdot 1 = 4 \cdot 2.828 + 4 = 15.31\,\tau$ ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Logic Partitioning technique** for large fan-in design. Using a tree of 2-input NAND gates, implement an **8-input AND** function and compute the speedup factor over a single 8-input NAND + INV realisation.

**Model Solution:**

**Explanation (2 marks):** Logic partitioning decomposes a wide logic function into a multi-level tree of smaller gates (typically 2- or 3-input). Each level has only a few series transistors, eliminating tall stacks. Although more levels are introduced, the **per-stage delay** is much smaller because self-loading scales as $\mathcal{O}(n^{2})$ in a single tall stack but only as $\mathcal{O}(1)$ in a balanced tree.

**8-Input AND Implementation (3 marks):** As shown in Section 4.3:
- Level 1: Four 2-input NANDs (8 inputs consumed).
- Level 2: Two 2-input NANDs.
- Level 3: One 2-input NAND.
- Level 4: One Inverter (DeMorgan: NAND-NAND-INV ≡ AND).

**Delay Computation (2 marks):**
- Single 8-NAND + INV: $t_a = 32\,t_{p0} + 1\,t_{p0} = 33\,t_{p0}$.
- Tree: 3 levels × $2\,t_{p0}$ + 1 INVERTER = $6 + 1 = 7\,t_{p0}$.
- **Speedup factor = $33 / 7 \approx 4.71\times$.**

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare **static CMOS**, **pseudo-NMOS**, and **Domino logic** for implementing a **wide OR/NOR** function. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each for large fan-in.

**Model Solution (Tabular form for clarity — 2 marks per style, 1 mark for comparison summary):**

| Style | Circuit Idea | Advantage for Large Fan-In | Disadvantage |
|-------|--------------|----------------------------|--------------|
| **Static CMOS** | n PMOS series (NOR) or parallel (OR after NAND) | Rail-to-rail output, no static power | Stacked PMOS/NMOS → quadratic delay for $n \geq 4$ |
| **Pseudo-NMOS** | Pull-up network = single grounded-gate PMOS load; pull-down = n NMOS in parallel | Single small load device, no PMOS stack → fast, small area | **Static short-circuit current** when output is LOW; needs careful W/L ratio ($r \approx 1/4$ to $1/6$) |
| **Domino** | Dynamic precharge + evaluate with single NMOS stack; inverting only | No PMOS stack at all, very fast for wide OR/NOR/AND | Requires clock, has charge-sharing and leakage issues, inverting only (cascaded) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For wide OR/NOR functions in high-speed datapaths (e.g., priority encoders, PLAs), **Domino logic** is preferred. For low-static-power designs, **static CMOS with partitioning** wins. **Pseudo-NMOS** is used only in low-duty-cycle, area-critical paths.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 ESE (Module 2):**
> 1. **Forgetting the parasitic delay** ($p = 1$ for inverter) in total delay formulas — Examiner deducts 1 mark if you write $D = N \cdot f$ instead of $D = N \cdot (f + p)$.
> 2. **Confusing static CMOS NOR delay with NAND delay** — In an n-input NOR, the PMOS are in series, so $t_{pLH}$ (rising) is degraded, not $t_{pHL}$. Many students blindly quote the NAND formula.
> 3. **Ignoring self-loading factor $\gamma$** — For deep submicron, $\gamma < 1$, but for long-channel analysis in KTU problems, take $\gamma = 1$ unless stated otherwise. Writing $t_p = n^2 t_{p0}/2$ *without* $\gamma$ loses a mark.
> 4. **Not drawing the schematic of a pseudo-NMOS gate** in questions asking for "implementation" — Examiner expects the grounded-gate PMOS load and parallel NMOS pulldown network.
> 5. **Mixing up Tapering Factor $f$ and Stage Effort $f_i$** — They are numerically the same for an inverter chain, but conceptually distinct: $f$ sizes the chain, $f_i$ is the per-stage logical + electrical effort.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fan-in problems** in static CMOS arise from **stacked transistors** and **self-loading**; delay scales as $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ for an n-input NAND/NOR.
- **Logical Effort** $g$ of n-NAND = $(n+2)/3$; of n-NOR = $(2n+1)/3$. Memorise these for instant comparison.
- **Path Effort** $F = G \cdot B \cdot H$. For minimum delay, each stage effort $f_i = F^{1/N}$.
- **Optimal number of stages** $N_{\text{opt}} = \ln(F) / \ln(f_{\text{opt}})$ with $f_{\text{opt}} \approx 3.6$ for self-loaded chain.
- **Buffer insertion (tapered chain)** is the most-used technique; $f = (C_L / C_{\text{in}})^{1/N}$ gives the size ratio.
- **Logic partitioning** trades levels for shorter stacks; an 8-input AND via 2-NAND tree gives $\approx 4.7\times$ speedup.
- **Input reordering** can give 10–30% delay improvement without any area penalty; place the **latest arriving** input nearest the output node.
- **Pseudo-NMOS** uses a single grounded-gate PMOS load, ratio $r = (W/L)_p / (W/L)_n \approx 1/4$ to $1/6$.
- **Domino logic** offers single-stage, high-speed wide fan-in but needs clock, inverting-only, and suffers from charge sharing.
- **DCVSL** (Differential Cascode Voltage Switch Logic) provides differential outputs with no static power; used in high-performance datapaths.
- **Pass Transistor Logic (PTL)** is compact but has a threshold-voltage drop problem; use **transmission gates** for full-swing operation.
- Always **draw the schematic**, **state the design issue**, and **derive the formula** in KTU 14-mark answers — the valuation scheme rewards methodology, not just final numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ratioed Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ratioed Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ratioed Logic** is a class of CMOS logic families in which the steady-state output voltage (specifically the low logic level $V_{OL}$ and high logic level $V_{OH}$) is determined by the *geometric ratio* of the transconductances — and hence the $(W/L)$ aspect ratios — of the **pull-up (load) network** and the **pull-down (driver) network**.

Unlike *ratio-less* static CMOS logic (where the output is always pulled to a clean rail through a fully complementary network), ratioed logic uses a permanently-ON load device whose strength must be "defeated" by the driver transistors. Therefore, the *ratio* $\beta_{driver} / \beta_{load}$ becomes a critical design parameter that directly sets the DC transfer characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the PEAET525 VLSI System Design course, Ratioed Logic is a sub-topic of *Static CMOS Logic Styles*. The two principal ratioed families examined are:
> 1. **Pseudo-nMOS Logic**
> 2. **Differential Cascode Voltage Switch (DCVS) Logic**

The two most important ratioed families are:

| Family | Load Device | Driver Network | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pseudo-nMOS | Grounded-gate pMOS (always ON) | nMOS pull-down tree | High-speed, low-area NOR-type arrays |
| DCVS | Cross-coupled pMOS latch | nMOS pull-down trees (true & complementary) | High-performance differential logic |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **tug-of-war** between two teams. One team (the *driver*) is dynamically formed based on the input — sometimes it has many strong players, sometimes none. The other team (the *load*) is a *permanent* team that is always on the rope, always pulling toward $V_{DD}$.

- If the driver team has **no players** (input = 0 in an inverter), the load wins → the output is pulled to **HIGH** ($V_{OH} = V_{DD}$).
- If the driver team has **any player at all** (input = 1 in an inverter), the load must "fight" the driver. The output is pulled down, but **only as far as the strength ratio allows**. If the load is much stronger, the rope doesn't move much → we get a bad $V_{OL}$ (not a clean zero).

Therefore, to get a **clean LOW**, the driver must be made **physically larger** than the load — hence the name *ratioed* logic: design correctness depends on the *ratio of the team sizes*.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of it this way: in **static CMOS** you need *two complete complementary networks* ($2N$ transistors for an $N$-input gate). In **ratioed logic** you replace the entire pMOS network with a *single* permanently-on load transistor, saving area and reducing input capacitance — at the cost of *static power dissipation* and *careful ratio sizing*.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard design metrics** govern every ratioed-logic analysis:

- **Process transconductance parameter** for nMOS: $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox}$
- **Process transconductance parameter** for pMOS: $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox}$
- **Typical mobility ratio**: $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2$ to **3** in a standard 0.18 µm to 0.25 µm process
- **Threshold voltage (typical)**: $V_{Tn} \approx 0.4$ to **0.7 V**, $V_{Tp} \approx -0.4$ to $-0.7$ V
- **Supply voltage**: $V_{DD}$ (e.g., **1.8 V**, **3.3 V**, or **5 V** depending on technology node)
- **Design ratio** for pseudo-nMOS: $r = (W/L)_{driver} / (W/L)_{load} \approx 4:1$ to **10:1** for a usable $V_{OL} \approx 0.1 V_{DD}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Principle — Why Ratioed Logic Exists

Static CMOS is the gold standard for **noise immunity** and **zero static power**, but suffers from two drawbacks:

1. **Area**: An $N$-input NAND/NOR gate needs $2N$ transistors.
2. **Input capacitance on high-fan-in gates**: The pMOS network contributes significant gate capacitance, slowing the gate.

**Ratioed logic sacrifices the zero-static-power property to recover the area and speed advantages.** It does this by replacing the entire pMOS pull-up network with **one (or a few) permanently-ON pMOS load transistor(s)**. The gate input(s) drive only the smaller nMOS network, reducing load capacitance and hence improving switching speed.

## 2.2 Pseudo-nMOS Logic — Step-by-Step Analysis

### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology

A pseudo-nMOS inverter (the building block) consists of:
- One **nMOS driver** (input = IN) connected between OUT and GND.
- One **pMOS load** with its **gate tied to GND** (i.e., $V_{GS} = -V_{DD}$, always ON in saturation or linear region) connected between $V_{DD}$ and OUT.

For an $N$-input pseudo-nMOS NOR gate, the single grounded-gate pMOS is retained, and the single nMOS is replaced by an $N$-input nMOS pull-down tree.

### 2.2.2 DC Operation — Two Cases

**Case 1: Input LOW (IN = 0)**
- nMOS driver: OFF ($V_{GS} < V_{Tn}$).
- pMOS load: ON, pulling OUT toward $V_{DD}$.
- Steady-state: $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ (clean high).
- **Static current**: $\approx 0$.

**Case 2: Input HIGH (IN = $V_{DD}$)**
- nMOS driver: ON, attempting to pull OUT toward GND.
- pMOS load: ON, attempting to pull OUT toward $V_{DD}$.
- **Both transistors conduct simultaneously** → a steady DC current flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND.
- The output settles at $V_{OL}$, where the nMOS pull-down current exactly equals the pMOS pull-up current:
$$I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$$

This is the **defining trait** of ratioed logic: when the output is LOW, there is a continuous short-circuit-like current → **static power dissipation**.

### 2.2.3 Analytical Operating Regions at $V_{OL}$

For small $V_{OL}$ (typically $< 0.2 V_{DD}$):
- **nMOS driver** is in the **linear (triode) region** because $V_{DS} = V_{OL}$ is small.
- **pMOS load** is in the **saturation region** because $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - V_{OL} \approx V_{DD}$ is large, and $V_{GS} = -V_{DD}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table** — it covers ~70% of numerical problems asked on Ratioed Logic in KTU examinations.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Driver transconductance ratio | $\beta_n = k_n \cdot (W/L)_n$ | Units: A/V² |
| 2 | Load transconductance ratio | $\beta_p = k_p \cdot (W/L)_p$ | Units: A/V² |
| 3 | Process mobility ratio | $r_{kp} = k_p / k_n$ | Typically **0.3 to 0.5** |
| 4 | Sizing ratio | $r = \beta_n / \beta_p$ | Critical design parameter |
| 5 | Pseudo-nMOS $V_{OL}$ (general) | Solve $I_{Dn,lin} = I_{Dp,sat}$ | Implicit — iterate or use closed form |
| 6 | Pseudo-nMOS $V_{OL}$ (closed form, simplified) | $V_{OL} \approx \dfrac{V_{Tn} \cdot r}{r+1} \cdot \left[\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{r \cdot V_{Tn}} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{r}\right)} - 1\right]$ | Linear-region nMOS, sat pMOS |
| 7 | Worst-case $V_{OL}$ upper bound (linear-region approx.) | $V_{OL} \le \dfrac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2}{2 r \cdot V_{Tn} \cdot (1 + \lambda_{...})}$ | Used in back-of-envelope design |
| 8 | DCVS regenerative gain | $g_m \cdot r_o$ | Sets static noise margin |
| 9 | Static power dissipation (input HIGH) | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{stat}$ | $I_{stat} = I_{Dp} \mid_{V_{out}=V_{OL}}$ |
| 10 | Switching energy (per transition) | $E_{sw} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Independent of ratioed style |
| 11 | Low-to-high propagation delay | $t_{pLH} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{p,eq} \cdot C_L$ | Limited by single weak pMOS |
| 12 | High-to-low propagation delay | $t_{pHL} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{n,eq} \cdot C_L$ | Fast — strong nMOS pulls down |
| 13 | Noise margin low (NM_L) | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | $V_{OL}$ is degraded in ratioed logic |
| 14 | Ratio requirement for $V_{OL} \le 0.1 V_{DD}$ | $r \ge 4$ to **5** (rule of thumb) | Empirically validated |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe-symbol safety:** In all derivations below, the absolute-value operator is rendered as `\vert` to keep markdown tables intact. For example, the magnitude of $V_{Tp}$ is written as $\vert V_{Tp}\vert$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Read-Only Memories (ROMs) and Programmable Logic Arrays (PLAs):** Pseudo-nMOS is widely used in the AND-plane and OR-plane because the input capacitance per row is dramatically lower, allowing higher density and faster access.
- **Domino logic pre-charge stages:** A pMOS pre-charge transistor (always on during pre-charge) is essentially a pseudo-nMOS-like element.
- **Content-Addressable Memories (CAMs):** Use DCVS-style match-line sensing for fast comparison.
- **High-performance arithmetic units (older designs):** Use DCVS for full-adders and XORs because of the cross-coupled regenerative feedback.

The principal **engineering cost** of ratioed logic is *static power dissipation* — a major reason modern low-power designs prefer static CMOS even though it costs more area.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Low Voltage $V_{OL}$ of Pseudo-nMOS Inverter

**Given:**
- $V_{DD}$ supply
- nMOS driver: $(W/L)_n$, threshold $V_{Tn}$
- pMOS load: $(W/L)_p$, threshold $V_{Tp} < 0$ (i.e., $\vert V_{Tp}\vert$ is positive)
- Input IN = $V_{DD}$ (driver ON, both transistors conducting)

**Goal:** Find $V_{OL}$ at the output node.

### Step 1: Identify the operating regions of the two transistors at $V_{out} = V_{OL}$

Since $V_{OL}$ is small and positive (typically $< 0.2 V_{DD}$), the nMOS driver has:
- $V_{GS,n} = V_{DD} - 0 = V_{DD}$ (large, well above $V_{Tn}$)
- $V_{DS,n} = V_{OL}$ (small, less than $V_{GS,n} - V_{Tn}$)

Therefore, the nMOS is in the **linear (triode) region**.

The pMOS load has:
- $V_{GS,p} = 0 - V_{DD} = -V_{DD}$ (large in magnitude)
- $V_{DS,p} = V_{DD} - V_{OL} \approx V_{DD}$ (large, close to $V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$)

Therefore, the pMOS is in the **saturation region**.

### Step 2: Write the two drain-current equations

For the nMOS in linear region:
$$I_{Dn} = k_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \left[ (V_{GS,n} - V_{Tn}) \cdot V_{DS,n} - \frac{V_{DS,n}^2}{2} \right]$$

Substituting the values:
$$I_{Dn} = k_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \left[ (V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^2}{2} \right]$$

For the pMOS in saturation:
$$I_{Dp} = \frac{k_p}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p \left(V_{GS,p} - V_{Tp}\right)^2 = \frac{k_p}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

### Step 3: Equate the two currents (steady-state condition)

At $V_{out} = V_{OL}$, the current supplied by the pMOS equals the current sunk by the nMOS:

$$k_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \left[ (V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^2}{2} \right] = \frac{k_p}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

### Step 4: Rearrange to introduce the sizing ratio

Divide both sides by $k_n (W/L)_n$ and let $r = \dfrac{k_n (W/L)_n}{k_p (W/L)_p}$:

$$(V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^2}{2} = \frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2}{2 r}$$

### Step 5: Solve the quadratic for $V_{OL}$

Rearranging into standard quadratic form $a V_{OL}^2 + b V_{OL} + c = 0$:

$$\frac{1}{2} V_{OL}^2 - (V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) V_{OL} + \frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2}{2 r} = 0$$

Multiplying through by 2:

$$V_{OL}^2 - 2(V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) V_{OL} + \frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2}{r} = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula $V_{OL} = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:

$$V_{OL} = (V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) \pm \sqrt{(V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^2 - \frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2}{r}}$$

### Step 6: Select the physically meaningful root

We require $0 < V_{OL} < V_{DD}$. The minus sign gives the smaller, physical root:

$$\boxed{\,V_{OL} = (V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) - \sqrt{(V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^2 - \frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2}{r}}\,}$$

### Step 7: Limiting-case sanity check

If $r \to \infty$ (driver infinitely stronger than load): the square-root term tends to $(V_{DD} - V_{Tn})$, so $V_{OL} \to 0$ ✓.

If $r \to 1$ (driver and load matched): $V_{OL} \approx V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$ — the gate fails to pull down — confirms the **necessity of proper sizing**.

### Step 8: Approximate short-form (frequently used in KTU exams)

Assuming $V_{Tn} \approx \vert V_{Tp}\vert = V_T$ (symmetric thresholds), the equation simplifies to:

$$V_{OL} \approx V_T \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + r}} \right] \cdot 2 \quad \text{(see expanded working below)}$$

Starting from $V_{OL} = (V_{DD} - V_T) - \sqrt{(V_{DD} - V_T)^2 - \dfrac{(V_{DD} - V_T)^2}{r}}$ and factoring:

$$V_{OL} = (V_{DD} - V_T)\left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{r}} \right]$$

Using the binomial identity $\sqrt{1 - x} \approx 1 - x/2$ for small $x$ (valid when $r \gg 1$):

$$V_{OL} \approx (V_{DD} - V_T) \cdot \frac{1}{2r} = \frac{V_{DD} - V_T}{2r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-cited approximation** for $V_{OL}$ in KTU numerical problems and the most common derivation asked in Part B.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Sizing Ratio for a Target $V_{OL, max}$

**Given:** $V_{OL} \le 0.1 V_{DD}$ (typical design constraint), and $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp}\vert = 0.2 V_{DD}$.

**Goal:** Find the minimum $r$.

From the exact formula:

$$0.1 V_{DD} = (V_{DD} - 0.2 V_{DD}) - \sqrt{(0.8 V_{DD})^2 - \frac{(0.8 V_{DD})^2}{r}}$$

$$0.1 V_{DD} = 0.8 V_{DD} - 0.8 V_{DD}\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{r}}$$

$$\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{r}} = \frac{0.8 - 0.1}{0.8} = 0.875$$

$$1 - \frac{1}{r} = 0.765625$$

$$\frac{1}{r} = 0.234375$$

$$r \approx 4.27$$

**Conclusion:** A sizing ratio of **at least 4.3 : 1** is required. Designers typically use $r = 4$ to $10$ for robust noise margin.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Static Power Dissipation in Pseudo-nMOS

With the input held HIGH, a DC current flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND. The static current is the pMOS saturation current evaluated at $V_{out} = V_{OL}$:

$$I_{stat} = \frac{k_p}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

The static power dissipation is:

$$\boxed{\,P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{stat} = \frac{k_p}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p V_{DD} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2\,}$$

**Numerical example:** For $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $\vert V_{Tp}\vert = 0.7$ V, $k_p = 40$ µA/V², $(W/L)_p = 1$:

$$I_{stat} = \frac{40\,\mu A/V^2}{2} \cdot 1 \cdot (2.6)^2 = 20 \cdot 6.76 = 135.2\ \mu A$$

$$P_{stat} = 3.3 \cdot 135.2\ \mu A \approx 446\ \mu W \text{ per gate}$$

With millions of such gates on a die, this static power becomes prohibitive — the **fundamental reason pseudo-nMOS is rarely used in low-power CMOS today**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Noise Margin Analysis (DCVS)

In **Differential Cascode Voltage Switch (DCVS)** logic, two complementary nMOS pull-down networks drive a pair of outputs ($\overline{Q}$ and $Q$) which are cross-coupled by a pMOS latch. The latch provides **positive feedback**: whichever output is being pulled down turns ON the cross-coupled pMOS that reinforces the other output being pulled high.

The **regenerative gain** that makes the latch bistable is:

$$A_{regen} = g_{m,p} \cdot r_{o,eq}$$

where $r_{o,eq}$ is the parallel combination of the pMOS and nMOS output resistances. For stable bistability, $A_{regen} > 1$ is required; in practice, $A_{regen} \ge 2$ to $3$ for sharp transitions and good noise margin.

The noise margin in DCVS is **substantially better** than pseudo-nMOS because:
1. The pull-down is a full rail-to-rail $V_{OL} \approx 0$ (clean LOW).
2. The cross-coupled latch actively reinforces the HIGH output to $V_{DD}$ (clean HIGH).
3. There is **no static current** in either stable state (latch isolates the supply from the pull-down networks).

This is why DCVS is sometimes called *"ratioed logic without the static-power penalty."*

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison of Logic Families

| Parameter | Static CMOS | Pseudo-nMOS | DCVS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transistors per $N$-input gate | $2N$ | $N + 1$ | $2N + 4$ (approx.) |
| $V_{OL}$ | $\approx 0$ | Degraded (ratio-dependent) | $\approx 0$ |
| $V_{OH}$ | $V_{DD}$ | $V_{DD}$ (when driver OFF) | $V_{DD}$ |
| Static power | **Zero** | **Non-zero** (DC short) | **Zero** |
| Noise margin (Low) | Excellent | **Poor** | Excellent |
| Input capacitance | Higher (2N gates) | Lower (only N nMOS gates) | Moderate |
| Speed (high fan-in) | Slow | **Fast** | Fast |
| Sizing sensitivity | None | **High** (must satisfy $r$) | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic 1 — Pseudo-nMOS Inverter Circuit Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD["V_DD Supply Rail"]:::supply
    Pgate["PMOS Gate = GND always ON"]:::load
    Psrc["PMOS Source"]:::load
    Pdrn["PMOS Drain"]:::load
    OutNode["Output Node V_OUT"]:::output
    Ndrn["NMOS Drain"]:::driver
    Ngate["NMOS Gate = V_IN"]:::driver
    Nsrc["NMOS Source"]:::driver
    GND["GND Rail"]:::supply

    VDD --- Psrc
    Pgate -.always ON.-> Psrc
    Pdrn --- OutNode
    OutNode --- Ndrn
    Ngate -.driven by input.-> Ndrn
    Nsrc --- GND

    classDef supply fill:#222,color:#fff,stroke:#888,stroke-width:1px
    classDef load fill:#e8c1c1,color:#000,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef driver fill:#c1d8e8,color:#000,stroke:#06a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef output fill:#fff2a8,color:#000,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block above renders the *functional connectivity* of a pseudo-nMOS inverter. The PMOS load's gate is hard-wired to GND (permanently ON), while the NMOS driver's gate is the only signal input.

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic 2 — DCVS Gate Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    InA["Input A"]:::in
    InB["Input B"]:::in
    PDN_T["nMOS Pull-Down Tree True Function F(A,B)"]:::tree
    PDN_F["nMOS Pull-Down Tree Complement Function Fbar(A,B)"]:::tree
    Qout["Output Q"]:::out
    Qbarout["Output Qbar"]:::out
    Latch["Cross-Coupled PMOS Latch Positive Feedback"]:::latch
    VDDrail["V_DD"]:::supply
    GNDrail["GND"]:::supply

    InA --> PDN_T
    InB --> PDN_T
    InA --> PDN_F
    InB --> PDN_F
    PDN_T --- Qbarout
    PDN_F --- Qout
    Qout <--> Latch
    Qbarout <--> Latch
    Latch --- VDDrail
    PDN_T --- GNDrail
    PDN_F --- GNDrail

    classDef in fill:#d5e8d4,color:#000,stroke:#383,stroke-width:1px
    classDef tree fill:#c1d8e8,color:#000,stroke:#06a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef out fill:#fff2a8,color:#000,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:2px
    classDef latch fill:#e8c1c1,color:#000,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef supply fill:#222,color:#fff,stroke:#888,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart 3 — Design Decision Flow for Choosing a Ratioed Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Need a logic gate"]:::start
    B{"Is static power acceptable?"}:::decide
    C["Use Static CMOS full complementary"]:::cmos
    D{"Need single-ended output AND high density?"}:::decide
    E["Use Pseudo-nMOS Size driver to be 4x to 10x stronger than load"]:::pseudo
    F{"Need differential output AND zero static power?"}:::decide
    G["Use DCVS Implement complementary pull-down trees"]:::dcvs
    H["End: Topology finalized Verify sizing ratio r"]:::end

    A --> B
    B -- Yes --> C
    B -- No --> D
    D -- Yes --> E
    D -- No --> F
    F -- Yes --> G
    F -- No --> C
    C --> H
    E --> H
    G --> H

    classDef start fill:#222,color:#fff,stroke:#888,stroke-width:1px
    classDef decide fill:#fff2a8,color:#000,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:1px
    classDef cmos fill:#c1d8e8,color:#000,stroke:#06a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef pseudo fill:#e8c1c1,color:#000,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef dcvs fill:#d5e8d4,color:#000,stroke:#383,stroke-width:1px
    classDef end fill:#222,color:#fff,stroke:#888,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Conceptual Diagram 4 — Current-Balance at $V_{OL}$

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["PMOS Load in Saturation I_DP = 0.5 kp W/Lp V_DD minus V_Tp squared"]:::load
    B["Equals at V_OUT = V_OL"]:::center
    C["NMOS Driver in Triode I_DN = kn W/Ln VGS minus VTn times V_DS minus V_DS squared over 2"]:::driver
    D["Solve quadratic for V_OL Choose smaller physical root"]:::solve

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D

    classDef load fill:#e8c1c1,color:#000,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef center fill:#fff2a8,color:#000,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:1px
```
