
> [!IMPORTANT]
> These functional block diagrams are KTU-board-style schematics. In a written exam, **draw the actual transistor-level schematic** (PMOS with bubble at gate, gate tied to GND for pseudo-nMOS) — but the *flow of design decisions* and the *current-balance concept* are best conveyed with flowcharts as above.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Ratioed Logic. Why is it called "ratioed"? Compare it with conventional static CMOS logic.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Ratioed logic is a class of CMOS logic families in which the **steady-state output voltage levels depend on the geometric (W/L) ratio** of the transistors in the pull-up and pull-down networks. The most common example is pseudo-nMOS logic, where a single permanently-ON pMOS load replaces the entire pMOS pull-up network.

It is called *ratioed* because the design correctness — specifically the value of $V_{OL}$ — depends on the *ratio* $r = \beta_{driver} / \beta_{load}$. If this ratio is too small, the output fails to reach a valid LOW.

**Comparison with static CMOS:**
- *Static CMOS* uses a fully complementary pMOS pull-up network; output levels are always at the rails and there is **zero static power**.
- *Ratioed logic* uses a single always-ON load; output is degraded and there is **static power dissipation** when the output is LOW, but transistor count and input capacitance are lower.

*(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: why "ratioed"; 1 mark: comparison.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of pseudo-nMOS logic.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages (any 3):**
1. **Lower transistor count**: $N+1$ transistors for an $N$-input gate, vs. $2N$ in static CMOS.
2. **Lower input capacitance**: Only the nMOS pull-down network is driven, reducing gate load and improving speed.
3. **Smaller layout area**: Useful for high-density ROMs, PLAs, and CAMs.
4. **Faster switching** for high fan-in gates due to reduced load.

**Disadvantages (any 2):**
1. **Static power dissipation** — DC short-circuit current flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND whenever the output is LOW.
2. **Reduced noise margin** — $V_{OL}$ is degraded (not a clean zero), narrowing $NM_L$.
3. **Ratio-sensitive design** — must carefully size driver relative to load.

*(1 mark per advantage, 0.5 mark per disadvantage, capped at 3 marks total.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between two full questions. Both alternatives below are full 14-mark questions.

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **pseudo-nMOS inverter**. Derive the expression for its output low voltage $V_{OL}$. State clearly the assumptions made.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a pseudo-nMOS inverter, the following parameters are given: $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $V_{Tn} = 0.7$ V, $\vert V_{Tp}\vert = 0.7$ V, $k_n = 60\ \mu A/V^2$, $k_p = 20\ \mu A/V^2$, $(W/L)_n = 10/1$, $(W/L)_p = 1/1$. Calculate (i) the sizing ratio $r$, (ii) the exact $V_{OL}$ using the full quadratic formula, and (iii) the static power dissipation when the input is HIGH.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Operation of Pseudo-nMOS Inverter** *(7 marks)*

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A pseudo-nMOS inverter consists of:
- An nMOS driver transistor with its **gate** connected to the input IN, source to GND, and drain to the output node OUT.
- A pMOS load transistor with its **gate permanently tied to GND**, source to $V_{DD}$, and drain to the output node OUT.

**Operation (2 marks):**
- *When IN = 0 (LOW)*: The nMOS is OFF. The pMOS load is ON (since $V_{GS,p} = 0 - V_{DD} = -V_{DD}$). The output is pulled to $V_{DD}$, so $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$. No static current flows.
- *When IN = 1 (HIGH, = $V_{DD}$)*: Both nMOS and pMOS are ON. A static DC current flows from $V_{DD}$ through the pMOS and nMOS to GND. The output settles at $V_{OL}$, where the nMOS and pMOS currents are equal.

**Derivation of $V_{OL}$ (3 marks):**
The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** above. The final result is:

$$V_{OL} = (V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) - \sqrt{(V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^2 - \frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2}{r}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. nMOS is in the linear (triode) region at $V_{out} = V_{OL}$.
2. pMOS is in the saturation region.
3. Body effect is neglected.
4. Channel-length modulation ($\lambda = 0$) is neglected.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $V_{Tn} = 0.7$ V, $\vert V_{Tp}\vert = 0.7$ V
- $k_n = 60\ \mu A/V^2$, $k_p = 20\ \mu A/V^2$
- $(W/L)_n = 10$, $(W/L)_p = 1$

**Step (i) — Sizing Ratio $r$** *(2 marks)*

The sizing ratio is defined as:

$$r = \frac{\beta_n}{\beta_p} = \frac{k_n (W/L)_n}{k_p (W/L)_p} = \frac{60 \cdot 10}{20 \cdot 1} = \frac{600}{20} = 30$$

**Step (ii) — Exact $V_{OL}$** *(3 marks)*

Using the quadratic formula:

$$V_{OL} = (V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) - \sqrt{(V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^2 - \frac{(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2}{r}}$$

Substituting $V_{DD} - V_{Tn} = 3.3 - 0.7 = 2.6$ V and $V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert = 2.6$ V:

$$V_{OL} = 2.6 - \sqrt{(2.6)^2 - \frac{(2.6)^2}{30}}$$

$$V_{OL} = 2.6 - \sqrt{6.76 - 0.2253} = 2.6 - \sqrt{6.5347}$$

$$V_{OL} = 2.6 - 2.5563 = 0.0437\ \text{V} \approx 44\ \text{mV}$$

**Step (iii) — Static Power Dissipation** *(2 marks)*

The static current is the pMOS saturation current (since pMOS is in saturation):

$$I_{stat} = \frac{k_p}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2 = \frac{20\ \mu A/V^2}{2} \cdot 1 \cdot (2.6)^2$$

$$I_{stat} = 10 \cdot 6.76 = 67.6\ \mu A$$

The static power is:

$$P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{stat} = 3.3 \cdot 67.6\ \mu A = 223.08\ \mu W \approx 223\ \mu W$$

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the operation of **Differential Cascode Voltage Switch (DCVS) logic** with a suitable diagram. Discuss how it achieves both full output voltage swing and zero static power dissipation.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare pseudo-nMOS, DCVS, and static CMOS logic families under the following heads: (i) transistor count per $N$-input gate, (ii) $V_{OL}$ quality, (iii) static power dissipation, (iv) noise margin, (v) typical application areas.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) DCVS Logic** *(7 marks)*

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A DCVS gate consists of:
- Two **complementary nMOS pull-down networks**: one implements the function $F$, the other implements $\bar{F}$.
- A **cross-coupled pMOS latch** at the output, with its supply tied to $V_{DD}$.
- Two differential outputs $Q$ and $\bar{Q}$.

**Operation (3 marks):**
- For any input combination, exactly one of the two nMOS networks is ON.
- The network that is ON pulls its output LOW.
- The cross-coupled pMOS latch senses the imbalance and uses **positive feedback** to actively drive the *other* output to $V_{DD}$.
- Because the latch is regenerative, both outputs reach **full rail levels** ($V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} \approx 0$).

**Full Swing + Zero Static Power (2 marks):**
- *Full swing*: The active feedback of the cross-coupled pMOS latch forces both outputs to the rails, eliminating the degraded $V_{OL}$ problem of pseudo-nMOS.
- *Zero static power*: Once the latch settles into one stable state, both pMOS transistors are OFF (the pMOS sourcing the HIGH output is OFF because its drain–source is $\approx 0$). No DC path exists from $V_{DD}$ to GND.

**Conclusion**: DCVS combines the *area advantage* of having only nMOS pull-downs (no complementary pMOS tree) with the *output quality* of static CMOS — but at the cost of added layout complexity and the requirement for true/complementary input signals.

---

**(b) Comparison Table** *(7 marks — 1.4 marks per row)*

| Head | Static CMOS | Pseudo-nMOS | DCVS |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Transistors for $N$-input | $2N$ | $N + 1$ | $2N + 4$ (approx.) |
| (ii) $V_{OL}$ | $\approx 0$ V (clean) | Degraded, ratio-dependent | $\approx 0$ V (clean) |
| (iii) Static power | **Zero** | **Non-zero** (DC short) | **Zero** |
| (iv) Noise margin $NM_L$ | Excellent | **Poor** | Excellent |
| (v) Applications | General-purpose logic | ROM, PLA, CAM, high-density arrays | High-performance arithmetic, sense amplifiers |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_{Tp}$ and $\vert V_{Tp}\vert$**: In pseudo-nMOS, the pMOS has $V_{Tp} < 0$. Always write the magnitude as $\vert V_{Tp}\vert$ in derivations. (1–2 marks lost per occurrence.)
> 2. **Wrong operating region**: At $V_{OL}$, the nMOS is in **linear (triode)** region, NOT saturation. Many students incorrectly use the saturation current formula for the nMOS at $V_{OL}$. This leads to a wrong answer and up to 3 marks lost.
> 3. **Forgetting static power**: When asked to "discuss" pseudo-nMOS, students often forget to quantify the static power dissipation. This is a high-weightage point.
> 4. **DCVS ≠ Pseudo-nMOS**: Students sometimes conflate the two. Make it clear: DCVS uses a *latch* for full-swing restoration; pseudo-nMOS uses a *single always-ON pMOS* with no latch.
> 5. **Sizing ratio units**: $r$ is a **dimensionless** ratio. Always show the computation $r = \beta_n / \beta_p$ explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for Ratioed Logic (KTU PEAET525 Module 2):**

- ☐ **Core idea**: Ratioed logic uses an *always-ON* load device; output levels depend on the **W/L ratio** of driver to load.
- ☐ **Pseudo-nMOS = 1 grounded-gate pMOS + nMOS pull-down tree** → $N+1$ transistors for an $N$-input NOR-like gate.
- ☐ **At $V_{OL}$**: nMOS is in **linear region**, pMOS is in **saturation region** — this is the standard assumption.
- ☐ **Master the $V_{OL}$ equation**: $V_{OL} = (V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) - \sqrt{(V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^2 - (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2 / r}$.
- ☐ **Approximation for $r \gg 1$**: $V_{OL} \approx (V_{DD} - V_T) / (2r)$ — fastest to use in exams.
- ☐ **Sizing rule of thumb**: $r \ge 4$ to $5$ for $V_{OL} \le 0.1 V_{DD}$.
- ☐ **Static power** = $V_{DD} \cdot I_{Dp,sat}$ whenever output is LOW — this is the *main drawback*.
- ☐ **DCVS** uses a **cross-coupled pMOS latch** to provide regenerative full-swing outputs with **zero static power**.
- ☐ **DCVS trades** complementary inputs (both $F$ and $\bar{F}$ must be generated) for full-output swing.
- ☐ **Noise margin** in pseudo-nMOS is **degraded** at the LOW end; DCVS and static CMOS have **excellent** noise margins.
- ☐ **Applications**: Pseudo-nMOS → ROM, PLA, high-density memory; DCVS → high-speed arithmetic, CAMs, sense amps.
- ☐ **Modern design trend**: Static CMOS preferred for low power; ratioed logic reserved for specific high-density or high-speed niches.
- ☐ **Always write units** (V, A, W) in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- ☐ **Show all derivation steps** — KTU board examiners award *step marks* even if the final number is slightly off.
- ☐ **Draw the circuit** — a neat pseudo-nMOS schematic (with gate of pMOS tied to GND explicitly labeled) earns easy 2–3 marks.
- ☐ **Common ratioed-logic pitfall**: do not confuse *ratioed* with *ratioless* — in static CMOS the ratio doesn't matter; in ratioed logic it is **everything**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Pass Transistor Logic and Transmission Gate Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basics of Pass Transistor Logic and Transmission Gate Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pass Transistor Logic (PTL) – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pass Transistor Logic (PTL)** is a digital CMOS design style in which the **drain or source terminal of an MOS transistor is used as the input**, and the signal is *passed* to the output through the channel when the gate is activated by a control signal — *instead of pulling the output to VDD or GND through a standard CMOS pull-up/pull-down network.*

In conventional static CMOS, every logic gate has a **pull-up network (PUN)** of PMOS transistors and a **pull-down network (PDN)** of NMOS transistors. In PTL, we **rely on the transistor itself to act as a closed switch** between the input node and the output node, so the output takes the value of the input (or a degraded version of it).

## 1.2 Transmission Gate (TG) Logic – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission Gate (TG)**, also called a **pass gate** or **bidirectional switch**, is a circuit consisting of **one NMOS and one PMOS transistor in parallel**, driven by **complementary control signals**. It behaves as a near-ideal voltage-controlled switch that can pass both logic '0' and logic '1' with **negligible threshold voltage drop**, when sized correctly.

The TG is the workhorse of PTL because it solves the major limitation of single-transistor pass devices — the threshold voltage drop problem.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 – The Water Tap (Single Transistor):** Imagine a water pipe with a one-way tap. Water (the signal) can flow from source to drain easily in one direction, but if the water pressure is too low, the tap cannot push the water up to a high tank — it loses pressure. That's exactly how an **NMOS transistor** passes a logic '0' perfectly but passes a weak, degraded logic '1'.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 – The Two-Way Pressure Valve (Transmission Gate):** Now combine two taps — one strong in pushing water up (PMOS, strong at high levels) and one strong in draining water down (NMOS, strong at low levels) — controlled by opposite-hand levers. Together they form a **bidirectional, full-swing valve** = the **Transmission Gate**.

## 1.4 Why PTL and TG Logic Matter in VLSI

- **Transistor count reduction:** A 2-to-1 multiplexer in static CMOS needs 6 transistors (2 NOT + 4 in MUX). A TG-based MUX needs only **2 transistors (1 NMOS + 1 PMOS) per switch**, drastically saving silicon area.
- **Lower parasitic capacitance** on internal nodes.
- **Lower dynamic power consumption** (smaller switching capacitance, fewer nodes toggling).
- **Faster paths** for data routing in datapaths, register files, ALUs, and memory arrays.
- They form the **building blocks** of multiplexers, latches, flip-flops, XOR/XNOR gates, and adders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metrics to Remember:**
> - **VDD** = nominal supply voltage (e.g., **1.8 V** for 180 nm, **1.2 V** for 90 nm, etc.)
> - **Vthn** = threshold voltage of NMOS (≈ **0.4 to 0.7 V**)
> - **Vthp** = threshold voltage of PMOS (≈ **−0.4 to −0.7 V**)
> - **γ (gamma)** = body-effect coefficient (≈ **0.4 V^½**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NMOS Pass Transistor – Operation

When the **gate of an NMOS** is driven high (Vg = VDD) and the source is used as the input, the NMOS turns ON. Two cases arise:

### Case A: Input = Logic '1' (Vg = VDD, Vin = VDD initially, output node initially at 0)
- The NMOS conducts until Vgs drops to Vthn.
- Once Vout rises to **VDD − Vthn**, the gate-to-source voltage Vgs = VDD − Vout = Vthn, and the transistor enters the sub-threshold / cut-off region.
- The output **stops rising** at this level — this is the **threshold voltage drop** problem.
- **Final output = VDD − Vthn (degraded HIGH)**
- The NMOS is said to pass a **weak '1'**.

### Case B: Input = Logic '0' (drain at 0 V, output node precharged to VDD)
- Vgs remains large (≈ VDD) throughout the discharge.
- The NMOS remains strongly ON, pulling the output cleanly to **0 V**.
- **Final output = 0 V (clean LOW)**
- The NMOS is said to pass a **strong '0'**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NMOS Summary:** Strong '0' / Weak '1' (a logic '1' degrades by Vthn).

## 2.2 The PMOS Pass Transistor – Operation

For a **PMOS** with the source used as input and gate driven low (Vg = 0 V):

### Case A: Input = Logic '0' (passes '0')
- VSG = 0 − Vin = −Vin.
- When Vin drops to **|Vthp|**, VSG = |Vthp|, transistor turns OFF.
- **Output = −|Vthp|** (i.e., **0 − |Vthp|**, a slightly negative voltage, but in digital terms = degraded '0').
- **PMOS passes a weak '0'.**

### Case B: Input = Logic '1' (passes '1')
- The PMOS stays strongly ON until output = VDD.
- **Output = VDD (clean '1').**
- **PMOS passes a strong '1'.**

> [!NOTE]
> **PMOS Summary:** Strong '1' / Weak '0' (a logic '0' degrades by |Vthp|).

## 2.3 The Body Effect — Why Vth is Not Constant

In PTL, the **source and bulk (body) terminals are at different potentials**. This modulates the threshold voltage:

$$V_{thn} = V_{thn0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\, \vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert \,} - \sqrt{\, \vert 2\phi_F \vert \,} \right)$$

- **Vthn0** = zero-bias threshold voltage.
- **γ** = body-effect coefficient.
- **φF** = Fermi potential (≈ −0.3 V for NMOS, +0.3 V for PMOS).
- **VSB** = source-to-body voltage.

As VSB increases (because the source voltage rises toward VDD), **Vthn increases**, worsening the degradation of the '1' level.

> [!WARNING]
> In deep-submicron devices (≤ 90 nm), body effect is *partly* mitigated by **reverse-biasing the body** (or using triple-well processes), but the problem does not vanish. Always account for Vth increase in PTL design.

## 2.4 The CMOS Transmission Gate — Combining Strengths

By placing an **NMOS in parallel with a PMOS** and driving their gates with **complementary clocks** (C and C̄):

- For **passing a '1'**: PMOS conducts strongly (Vsg is large), so the output reaches full VDD.
- For **passing a '0'**: NMOS conducts strongly (Vgs is large), so the output reaches full 0 V.

> The TG passes both logic levels with **full voltage swing** and acts as a **bidirectional** element.

| Quantity | NMOS PTL | PMOS PTL | CMOS TG |
|----------|----------|----------|---------|
| Passes '1' cleanly? | No (loses Vthn) | Yes (full VDD) | Yes (full VDD) |
| Passes '0' cleanly? | Yes (full 0 V) | No (loses |Vthp|) | Yes (full 0 V) |
| Bidirectional? | Yes (symmetric) | Yes (symmetric) | Yes (symmetric) |
| Static current when ON? | Small (one strong device) | Small | Negligible (both OFF for opposite level) |
| ON Resistance (Ron) | Variable with Vin | Variable with Vin | ~Constant (parallel combination) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | $V_{out,1} = V_{DD} - V_{thn}(V_{SB})$ | NMOS passing HIGH (degraded) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_{out,0} = 0 \text{ V}$ | NMOS passing LOW (clean) | V |
| 3 | $V_{out,0} = - \vert V_{thp} \vert$ | PMOS passing LOW (degraded) | V |
| 4 | $V_{out,1} = V_{DD}$ | PMOS passing HIGH (clean) | V |
| 5 | $V_{thn} = V_{thn0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\, \vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert \,} - \sqrt{\, \vert 2\phi_F \vert \,} \right)$ | Body-effect Vth of NMOS | V |
| 6 | $R_{on,n} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{thn})}$ | NMOS ON-resistance | Ohms (Ω) |
| 7 | $R_{on,p} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (\vert V_{GS} \vert - \vert V_{thp} \vert)}$ | PMOS ON-resistance | Ω |
| 8 | $R_{on,TG} = R_{on,n} \Vert R_{on,p}$ | TG equivalent ON-resistance | Ω |
| 9 | $t_{pLH} \approx 0.69 \, R_{on,TG} \, C_L$ | TG propagation delay (HIGH) | Seconds (s) |
| 10 | $\mu_n \approx 2.5 \, \mu_p$ | Carrier mobility ratio (silicon) | — |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Datapath multiplexers** in microprocessors use TG-based MUXes for speed and area.
- **SRAM 6T cells** use **NMOS pass transistors** to access the storage nodes — the threshold drop is tolerable because the cell is ratioed and uses a cross-coupled inverter pair for full-swing restoration.
- **DRAM** uses a single NMOS access transistor — same threshold-drop phenomenon.
- **Flip-flops and latches** use TGs as the master/slave coupling switches (C²MOS — Clocked CMOS).
- **XOR/XNOR, full adders, barrel shifters** are routinely implemented in PTL/TG logic for low transistor count.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — NMOS Pass Transistor Passing a Logic '1' (Threshold Drop)

**Setup:** NMOS with **gate = VDD**, **drain = input node (tied to VDD)**, **source = output node (initially 0 V)**, body = GND.

**Step 1:** The NMOS is in saturation initially because VDS = VDD > VGS − Vthn = VDD − Vthn. Current flows from drain to source, charging the output capacitor C_L.

**Step 2:** As the output voltage Vout rises, the gate-to-source voltage decreases:

$$V_{GS} = V_{DD} - V_{out}$$

**Step 3:** The transistor stays ON as long as $V_{GS} > V_{thn}$, i.e.,

$$V_{DD} - V_{out} > V_{thn} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{out} < V_{DD} - V_{thn}$$

**Step 4:** Charging stops the instant the channel pinches off, i.e., $V_{GS} = V_{thn}$. Solving for the final steady-state Vout:

$$V_{out,1} = V_{DD} - V_{thn}$$

**Step 5:** Because VSB is now positive (source at VDD − Vthn, body at 0 V), the body effect further increases Vthn:

$$V_{thn,eff} = V_{thn0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\, \vert 2\phi_F + (V_{DD} - V_{thn0}) \vert \,} - \sqrt{\, \vert 2\phi_F \vert \,} \right)$$

**Step 6:** Substituting back, the *real* output is **even lower** than VDD − Vthn0:

$$V_{out,1,real} = V_{DD} - V_{thn,eff}$$

**Numerical Example:** Let VDD = 1.8 V, Vthn0 = 0.5 V, γ = 0.4 V^½, 2φF = 0.6 V.

- First estimate: Vout,1 ≈ 1.8 − 0.5 = 1.3 V.
- VSB = 1.3 V → Vthn,eff = 0.5 + 0.4 × (√1.9 − √0.6) = 0.5 + 0.4 × (1.378 − 0.775) = 0.5 + 0.241 = **0.741 V**.
- Corrected Vout,1 = 1.8 − 0.741 = **1.059 V** — a *serious* degradation!

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — NMOS Pass Transistor Passing a Logic '0' (Clean Pass)

**Setup:** Gate = VDD, drain = 0 V, output precharged to VDD.

**Step 1:** The NMOS turns ON, with VGS = VDD − 0 = VDD, comfortably greater than Vthn.

**Step 2:** VDS = 0 − Vout = −Vout (negative, because Vout > 0). The magnitude increases as Vout falls:

$$\vert V_{DS} \vert = V_{out}$$

**Step 3:** The NMOS operates in **triode (linear) region** during the entire discharge because VDS < VGS − Vthn (since VGS is large and VDS is at most VDD).

**Step 4:** Discharge continues until Vout = 0 V. Because the source is now at 0 V and body is at 0 V, **VSB = 0**, so there is **no body effect** during the '0' pass.

**Step 5:** Final answer:

$$V_{out,0} = 0 \text{ V (clean, no degradation)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent ON-Resistance of a Transmission Gate

The propagation delay of a TG driving a load capacitance C_L is:

$$t_p \approx 0.69 \, R_{on,eq} \, C_L$$

For the **NMOS**, the ON-resistance in triode is:

$$R_{on,n} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}_n (V_{GS} - V_{thn})}$$

For the **PMOS**:

$$R_{on,p} = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}_p (\vert V_{GS} \vert - \vert V_{thp} \vert)}$$

Since the two transistors are in **parallel** between input and output, the equivalent resistance is:

$$R_{on,TG} = R_{on,n} \Vert R_{on,p} = \frac{R_{on,n} \cdot R_{on,p}}{R_{on,n} + R_{on,p}}$$

**Why is Ron,TG nearly constant?**
- When **Vin is low** (output is being pulled down): NMOS has small Ron (VGS ≈ VDD is large); PMOS has very large Ron (its VGS is small). So Ron,TG ≈ Ron,n (low).
- When **Vin is high** (output is being pulled up): PMOS has small Ron; NMOS has large Ron. So Ron,TG ≈ Ron,p (low).
- In the **mid-range**, both conduct, so the parallel combination is even lower.

**Numerical Example:** Suppose μnCox(W/L)n = 200 μA/V², μpCox(W/L)p = 80 μA/V², VGS = 1.8 V, Vthn = 0.5 V, |Vthp| = 0.5 V.

- Ron,n (worst case, Vout near VDD − Vthn) ≈ 1 / (200×10⁻⁶ × 1.3) = **3.85 kΩ**.
- Ron,p (worst case, Vout near |Vthp|) ≈ 1 / (80×10⁻⁶ × 1.3) = **9.6 kΩ**.
- Parallel = (3.85 × 9.6) / (3.85 + 9.6) = **2.75 kΩ**.

This is **lower than either** device alone — the TG's superior drive strength is the reason it is used so widely.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | NMOS PTL | PMOS PTL | CMOS TG |
|----------|----------|----------|---------|
| Transistor count for a switch | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| Bidirectional | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Pass '1' swing | VDD − Vthn | VDD | VDD |
| Pass '0' swing | 0 V | −|Vthp| | 0 V |
| Body effect on pass | Yes (when passing '1') | Yes (when passing '0') | Negligible (one device always conducts strongly) |
| Static power when ON | Very low | Very low | Very low |
| Static power when OFF | Zero (truly off) | Zero | Zero |
| Drive strength | Medium (degraded) | Medium (degraded) | High (full swing) |
| Typical use | SRAM cell, DRAM access, low-swing buses | Rare alone | MUX, latch, flip-flop, bus driver, adder |
| Area | Smallest | Smallest | Medium |

## 3.5 Boolean Function Realization Example — 2:1 MUX Using TG

A 2-to-1 multiplexer selects between two inputs A and B based on a select line S:

$$Y = (A \cdot S) + (B \cdot \bar{S})$$

- **TG1** (controlled by S and S̄) connects **A → Y** when S = 1.
- **TG2** (controlled by S̄ and S) connects **B → Y** when S = 0.

Transistor count = **only 2 TGs (4 transistors)**, compared to **6 transistors** in static CMOS — a **33% area saving**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NMOS Pass Transistor – Schematic & Switch Equivalent

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Input Vin] -- Drain terminal --> nodeM[NMOS Transistor]
    nodeM -- Source terminal --> nodeB[Output Vout]
    nodeC[Control Signal VDD or HIGH] -- Gate --> nodeM
    nodeG[Ground GND] -- Body Bulk --> nodeM
    classDef transistor fill:#FFF4E1,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef signal fill:#E1F5FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px
    class nodeM transistor
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeG signal
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When the gate is HIGH, the NMOS turns ON and acts as a **closed switch** between input and output. Strong pass for logic '0', weak (degraded) pass for logic '1'.

## 4.2 PMOS Pass Transistor – Schematic & Switch Equivalent

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Input Vin] -- Drain terminal --> nodeM[PMOS Transistor]
    nodeM -- Source terminal --> nodeB[Output Vout]
    nodeC[Control Signal GND or LOW] -- Gate --> nodeM
    nodeG[VDD Body Bulk] -- Body Bulk --> nodeM
    classDef transistor fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#B0003A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef signal fill:#E1F5FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px
    class nodeM transistor
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeG signal
```

## 4.3 CMOS Transmission Gate – Full Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TGBlock["Transmission Gate TG"]
        nodeIn[Input A] -- Drain --> nodeN[NMOS]
        nodeN -- Source --> nodeOut[Output Y]
        nodeIn -- Drain --> nodeP[PMOS]
        nodeP -- Source --> nodeOut
        nodeCtlC[Control C] -- Gate --> nodeN
        nodeCtlCb[Control C bar] -- Gate --> nodeP
        nodeGND[GND] -- Body of NMOS --> nodeN
        nodeVDD[VDD] -- Body of PMOS --> nodeP
    end
    classDef nmos fill:#FFF4E1,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pmos fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#B0003A,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sig fill:#E1F5FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px
    class nodeN nmos
    class nodeP pmos
    class nodeIn,nodeOut,nodeCtlC,nodeCtlCb,nodeGND,nodeVDD sig
```

> [!NOTE]
> The NMOS body is tied to **GND** and the PMOS body is tied to **VDD** to **reverse-bias the source-body junctions**, preventing latch-up and minimizing body effect (for the dominant conduction path).

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: PTL vs TG Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Need a digital switch] --> q1{Do we need full voltage swing?}
    q1 -- No, low swing OK --> q2{Static power critical?}
    q1 -- Yes, full swing required --> q3{Use a Transmission Gate TG}
    q2 -- Yes --> pickN[Choose NMOS pass transistor]
    q2 -- No --> pickP[Choose PMOS pass transistor]
    q3 --> appChoice{Application type?}
    appChoice -- MUX, latch, flip flop --> muxUse[Use TG based MUX or C2MOS]
    appChoice -- SRAM, DRAM cell --> sramUse[Use NMOS access transistor]
    appChoice -- XOR adder barrel shifter --> xorUse[Use complementary PTL or TG XOR]
    classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef action fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:1px
    class startA startNode
    class q1,q2,appChoice decision
    class q3,pickN,pickP,muxUse,sramUse,xorUse action
```

## 4.5 Truth Table & Switch State Diagram of a TG

| Control C | Control C̄ | NMOS State | PMOS State | TG Function |
|-----------|-----------|------------|------------|-------------|
| 0 | 1 | OFF | OFF | **Open (high-Z)** |
| 1 | 0 | ON | ON | **Closed (A → Y)** |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. What is Pass Transistor Logic? Mention its major limitation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pass Transistor Logic (PTL) is a CMOS design style in which MOS transistors are used as switches to pass logic levels from input to output, rather than using dedicated pull-up/pull-down networks. The major limitation is the **threshold voltage drop** — an NMOS can pass a strong '0' but only a weak '1' equal to VDD − Vthn, and a PMOS can pass a strong '1' but only a weak '0' equal to −|Vthp|. This degrades the noise margin and can cause static current in subsequent inverters. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

### Q2. Draw the CMOS transmission gate and explain its operation briefly.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A CMOS transmission gate (TG) consists of one **NMOS and one PMOS connected in parallel** between input A and output Y, with their gates driven by complementary control signals **C and C̄**.

- When **C = 1**: NMOS gate is HIGH and PMOS gate is LOW → both transistors are ON → A is connected to Y. NMOS pulls Y low strongly, PMOS pulls Y high strongly — **full swing pass**.
- When **C = 0**: Both transistors are OFF → output is **high-impedance (Z)**.

The TG therefore acts as a **bidirectional, full-swing analog switch** and is the workhorse for multiplexers, latches, and bus drivers. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the operation of an **NMOS pass transistor** when passing logic '0' and logic '1'. Derive the output voltage in each case. Show how the **body effect** modifies the threshold voltage during a '1' pass. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **CMOS transmission gate**. Derive the equivalent ON-resistance and show why it is more uniform than a single-transistor pass device. Use a 2-to-1 multiplexer as an application example. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — NMOS Pass Transistor (7 marks)

**Diagram (2 marks):** NMOS with **gate = VDD (control high)**, **drain = Vin**, **source = Vout**, **body = GND**. Show arrows for current and the output capacitor C_L.

**Passing Logic '0' (2 marks):** Drain = 0 V. VGS = VDD − 0 = VDD (≫ Vthn) → NMOS in triode, fully ON. Output capacitor discharges through the channel. Since source ends at 0 V and body is at 0 V, **VSB = 0 → no body effect**.

$$V_{out,0} = 0 \text{ V (clean, strong '0')}$$

**Passing Logic '1' (2 marks):** Drain = VDD, source initially 0 V. NMOS is in saturation, charging C_L. As Vout rises, VGS = VDD − Vout decreases. Conduction stops when VGS = Vthn.

$$V_{out,1} = V_{DD} - V_{thn}$$

**Body Effect (1 mark):** Since source is now at VDD − Vthn (≠ 0), VSB > 0, so Vthn increases to:

$$V_{thn,eff} = V_{thn0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\, \vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert \,} - \sqrt{\, \vert 2\phi_F \vert \,} \right)$$

The actual output Vout,1 = VDD − Vthn,eff is even lower — making the '1' level **worse** than the ideal VDD − Vthn0.

#### Part (b) — CMOS Transmission Gate (7 marks)

**Circuit (2 marks):** Draw NMOS and PMOS in parallel; NMOS gate = C, PMOS gate = C̄; bodies to GND and VDD respectively. Show C_L at the output.

**Operation (2 marks):** When **C = 1, C̄ = 0**, both NMOS and PMOS are ON. NMOS passes a strong '0', PMOS passes a strong '1' → output follows input with **full voltage swing (0 to VDD)**. When **C = 0**, both OFF → output floats (high-Z).

**ON-Resistance Derivation (2 marks):**

$$R_{on,n} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}_n (V_{DD} - V_{out} - V_{thn})}$$

$$R_{on,p} = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L}_p (V_{out} - \vert V_{thp} \vert)}$$

$$R_{on,TG} = R_{on,n} \Vert R_{on,p}$$

When Vout is low → Ron,n small, Ron,p large → Ron,TG ≈ Ron,n (low).
When Vout is high → Ron,p small, Ron,n large → Ron,TG ≈ Ron,p (low).
In the middle, both conduct → Ron,TG is even lower. The result is a **nearly constant, low Ron**, giving fast, symmetric propagation delay.

**MUX Application (1 mark):** A 2-to-1 MUX uses **two TGs** — TG1 (controlled by S) connects A to Y, TG2 (controlled by S̄) connects B to Y. Total transistors = 4, vs. 6 in static CMOS.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **PMOS pass transistor** in detail. Show why it produces a strong '1' but a weak '0', and derive the output expressions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **NMOS PTL, PMOS PTL, and CMOS Transmission Gate** in a tabular form covering: voltage swing, body effect, drive strength, transistor count, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — PMOS Pass Transistor (7 marks)

**Diagram (2 marks):** PMOS with **gate = 0 V (control low)**, **drain = Vin**, **source = Vout**, **body = VDD**.

**Passing Logic '1' (2 marks):** Drain = VDD. VSG = 0 − VDD = −VDD (large magnitude) → PMOS strongly ON in triode. Output capacitor charges fully to VDD (no threshold drop because the source-to-body junction is reverse-biased but the channel conducts hard).

$$V_{out,1} = V_{DD} \text{ (clean, strong '1')}$$

**Passing Logic '0' (2 marks):** Drain = 0 V. As Vout falls below 0, |VSG| = |0 − Vout| = Vout (positive) decreases. Conduction stops when |VSG| = |Vthp|.

$$V_{out,0} = - \vert V_{thp} \vert \text{ (degraded, weak '0')}$$

**Body Effect (1 mark):** Source is at a negative potential, so VBS (or |VSB|) is large → threshold voltage magnitude increases → output degrades further.

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per correctly filled row + 2 marks for explanation)

| Property | NMOS PTL | PMOS PTL | CMOS TG |
|----------|----------|----------|---------|
| Pass '1' level | VDD − Vthn | VDD | VDD |
| Pass '0' level | 0 V | −|Vthp| | 0 V |
| Body effect | Yes (when passing '1') | Yes (when passing '0') | Negligible |
| Drive strength | Medium | Medium | High |
| Transistors per switch | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| ON-resistance variation | High | High | Low (nearly constant) |
| Typical applications | SRAM/DRAM cell access, low-swing buses | Rare alone | MUX, latch, flip-flop, adder, barrel shifter |

**Concluding sentence (extra credit, KTU examiner style):** The CMOS transmission gate is preferred whenever **full-swing, high-speed, bidirectional switching** is required; the single-transistor pass devices are chosen only when **area and power are more critical than swing** (e.g., memory cell access).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the body effect** when deriving the NMOS '1' pass level — losing up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Drawing the PMOS with the bubble at the wrong terminal** (bubble is at the *gate*, not the source/drain) — losing **1 mark**.
> 3. **Writing "TG passes both 0 and 1"** without explaining *why* (the PMOS is strong at '1', the NMOS is strong at '0') — losing **1 mark** on the *reasoning*.
> 4. **Confusing the source and drain** of a pass transistor — in PTL they swap depending on signal direction. The terminal connected to the lower potential at the moment of turn-on is the *source*.
> 5. **Omitting units** in derivations (Volts, Ohms, seconds) — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Pass Transistor Logic (PTL)** uses MOS transistors as switches instead of PUN/PDN networks — saves area and power.
- ✅ **NMOS PTL:** Strong '0' / **Weak '1'** (loses Vthn); body effect makes '1' *worse* when VSB > 0.
- ✅ **PMOS PTL:** Strong '1' / **Weak '0'** (loses |Vthp|).
- ✅ **Threshold voltage drop formula:** $V_{out,1, NMOS} = V_{DD} - V_{thn,eff}$ (with body effect).
- ✅ **Body effect equation:** $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma (\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB}\vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert})$ — *memorize for KTU derivations*.
- ✅ **CMOS Transmission Gate (TG)** = NMOS ∥ PMOS with complementary gate control (C and C̄).
- ✅ **TG is bidirectional**, passes **full VDD and 0 V**, has **low and nearly constant ON-resistance** = $R_{on,n} \Vert R_{on,p}$.
- ✅ **Propagation delay:** $t_p \approx 0.69 \, R_{on,TG} \, C_L$.
- ✅ **Mobility ratio:** $\mu_n \approx 2.5 \, \mu_p$ → NMOS is ~2.5× stronger than PMOS of the same W/L.
- ✅ **Applications:** SRAM/DRAM access (NMOS PTL); MUX, latch, flip-flop (C²MOS), XOR, full adder, barrel shifter (TG).
- ✅ **Static power in PTL/TG** is *negligible* when the output drives a CMOS gate (no DC path from VDD to GND) — major advantage.
- ✅ **Memorize the comparison matrix** between NMOS PTL, PMOS PTL, and CMOS TG — it is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question**.
- ✅ **Always draw the body terminal** of the pass transistor (tied to GND for NMOS, to VDD for PMOS) — examiners *look* for this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic CMOS Design - Basic Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Dynamic CMOS Design - Basic Principles ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Dynamic CMOS Design **Dynamic CMOS Logic** is a digital circuit design style in which the output node is **not connected to a static pull-up or pull-down DC path** to the supply rails. Instead, the output is **temporarily (dynamically) stored on the parasitic gate capacitance of the next stage**, and the output value is refreshed/regenerated on every clock cycle through a controlled sequence of **precharge** and **evaluation** operations governed by a clock signal (φ or CLK). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):** > "Dynamic logic is a design technique where the output state is stored temporarily on a capacitance rather than being driven continuously by a static CMOS gate. It uses a clocked precharge transistor and a clocked (or input-driven) evaluation network." The most fundamental dynamic gate is the **n-type Dynamic CMOS Inverter**, which uses only **4 transistors** (1 PMOS precharge, 1 PMOS keeper optional, 1 NMOS evaluate, 1 NMOS driver/input) compared to the **6 transistors** required in a fully-static 2-input NAND gate, giving the well-known area advantage of **N + 2 vs 2N** transistors for an N-input gate. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Model Imagine a **water bucket** suspended under a tap: - A **static CMOS gate** is like a tap that is **always connected to a continuous water pipe**. The bucket is constantly being filled (pulled up) or emptied (pulled down). This uses water 24/7, even when the bucket level is exactly what you want. - A **dynamic CMOS gate** is like a tap that is opened only **twice a day**: 1. **Precharge Phase (CLK = 0)**: The tap fills the bucket with water (output node is charged to VDD through PMOS). 2. **Evaluate Phase (CLK = 1)**: The tap is shut, and the bucket is either left as-is (no input) or **drained through a hole at the bottom** controlled by the input (NMOS path). The water level now *remembers* the input state. Once evaluated, the bucket keeps its state **without any current flow** — until the next clock cycle, when the cycle repeats. This is why dynamic logic has **zero static current** and lower power consumption. ### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary) | Term | Meaning | Symbol/Value | |---|---|---| | **Precharge Phase** | Clock interval when output node is charged to VDD | φ = 0 (LOW) | | **Evaluate Phase** | Clock interval when output depends on inputs | φ = 1 (HIGH) | | **Precharge Transistor** | PMOS connecting VDD to output during precharge | Mp | | **Evaluate Transistor** | NMOS whose gate is the clock signal | Me | | **PDN** | Pull-Down Network (logic + clocked NMOS) | N-type tree | | **Footless Dynamic Gate** | Dynamic gate without dedicated evaluate NMOS | Riskier | | **Keeper (Half-Ratio)** | Weak PMOS holding output against leakage | Mk | | **Output Capacitance** | Sum of drain + interconnect + next-stage gate cap | C_L | > [!NOTE] > **Static vs Dynamic Power Comparison (KTU Favored Metric):** > - **Static Power (P_static)**: Static CMOS ≈ VDD · I_leak (very small). Dynamic CMOS ≈ **0 W** (no DC path from VDD to GND). > - **Dynamic Power (P_dyn)**: Both styles have P_dyn = α · C_L · VDD² · f. Dynamic logic often has **lower α** (switching activity) per node. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic A standard **n-type dynamic CMOS inverter** (4T) operates in two strictly disjoint clock phases: **Step 1 — Precharge (CLK = 0, φ = LOW)** - PMOS precharge transistor **Mp** is **ON** (PMOS conducts when gate is LOW). - NMOS evaluate transistor **Me** is **OFF**. - Output node **OUT** is connected to VDD through Mp and charges the load capacitance **C_L** to **VDD**. - Logic value stored: **HIGH (1)**. **Step 2 — Evaluate (CLK = 1, φ = HIGH)** - PMOS Mp turns **OFF**, isolating OUT from VDD. - NMOS Me turns **ON**, connecting the pull-down network (PDN) between C_L and GND. - The PDN implements the logic function (e.g., for an inverter, a single NMOS whose gate is **IN**). - If **IN = 1**: PDN conducts → C_L discharges → **OUT = 0**. - If **IN = 0**: PDN is OFF → C_L floats → **OUT retains precharged 1**. - At the **end of evaluate**, the output reflects `OUT = NOT(IN)` at the moment CLK fell. **Step 3 — Return to Precharge** - The clock transitions back to 0, the cycle repeats, and OUT is recharged to VDD. - The output is therefore **valid only during evaluate** and is invalid (undefined, but ideally HIGH) during precharge. ### 2.2 Why Dynamic Logic? — The Three Engineering Motivations > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favorite — The "Why" Behind Dynamic Logic:** > 1. **Transistor Count = N + 2** (vs 2N for static CMOS) → **~50% area reduction**. > 2. **Zero Static Current** → only switching capacitance is charged/discharged. > 3. **Lower Input Capacitance** → each input sees only one NMOS gate (no PMOS in stack) → **faster switching** and lower input drive requirement. ### 2.3 The Critical Drawbacks (Must-Know for KTU) | Drawback | Root Cause | Consequence | |---|---|---| | **Charge Sharing** | C_L shares charge with internal PDN node capacitances | Output voltage may drop below VIH of next stage, causing logic error | | **Clock Feedthrough** | Clock transitions couple through C_gd of Mp, Me | Voltage bounce on OUT; especially bad for low-VDD | | **Leakage (Sub-threshold)** | Floating node during evaluate (no keeper) | OUT may drift toward 0 over long evaluate periods | | **Cascading Problem** | Output of stage 1 is valid only during evaluate of stage 2 | Requires careful clock-phase overlap; cannot cascade two dynamic stages with the **same** clock | | **Noise Margin** | Non-restoring (no feedback during evaluate) | Poor noise immunity; sensitive to crosstalk, leakage, charge injection | ### 2.4 The Keeper Transistor (Half-Ratio Inverter) To combat leakage-induced voltage drift, a **weak PMOS keeper (Mk)** is added in parallel with Mp, with its gate tied to the **complement of OUT** (or simply to OUT through an inverter). The keeper is **sized very small** (typically 1/4 to 1/10 of Mp) so it can be **overridden** by the PDN during a real evaluate discharge. This is called the **half-ratio (or ratioed) dynamic gate**. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All KTU exam-relevant equations for Dynamic CMOS:** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Switching power per node (same as static) | Watts | | 2 | $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{static} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f + V_{DD} I_{leak}$ | Total power per gate | Watts | | 3 | $C_L = C_{db,n} + C_{gd,n} + C_{wire} + C_{g,next}$ | Total load capacitance at OUT | Farads | | 4 | $\tau_{p} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot k_n \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2}$ | Evaluate time constant (RC discharge) | Seconds | | 5 | $\Delta V_{CS} = \frac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Charge-sharing voltage drop (worst case) | Volts | | 6 | $A_{v} = N_{input} + 2$ | Transistor count of N-input dynamic gate | Count | | 7 | $A_{static} = 2 N_{input}$ | Transistor count of N-input static CMOS | Count | | 8 | $\eta_{area} = \frac{A_{static} - A_{v}}{A_{static}} = \frac{N}{2(N+1)}$ | Area savings ratio (for large N → 50%) | Dimensionless | | 9 | $E_{sw} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Switching energy per transition | Joules | | 10 | $f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{pre} + T_{ev}}$ | Maximum clock frequency constraint | Hertz | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL TABLE NOTE:** Vertical pipes have been replaced with `\vert` / `\mid` to comply with the KTU markdown table protocol. Do not use raw `\vert` inside table cells when authoring your own notes. ### 2.6 Engineering Utility — Where Dynamic Logic is Used - **High-performance microprocessors**: ALU datapaths, register file read/write, cache tag comparators. - **Domino logic** (extension of dynamic): 32-bit/64-bit adders, comparators, priority encoders. - **DRAM sense amplifiers**: The very heart of memory read — a dynamic cross-coupled latch. - **Pipeline registers** in GHz-range designs where clock loading must be minimized. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Transistor Count Comparison (N-Input NAND/NOR) **Goal:** Prove that an N-input dynamic gate uses **N + 2** transistors vs **2N** for static CMOS. **For a static CMOS N-input NAND gate:** - Pull-Up Network (PUN): N PMOS transistors in **parallel** (one per input). - Pull-Down Network (PDN): N NMOS transistors in **series**. - Total: $N + N = 2N$ transistors. **For an n-type dynamic N-input gate:** - Precharge PMOS: 1 transistor. - Evaluate NMOS (foot): 1 transistor. - PDN: N NMOS transistors implementing the logic function. - Total: $1 + 1 + N = N + 2$ transistors. **Area savings (per gate):** $$ A_{saved} = 2N - (N + 2) = N - 2 $$ **Fractional area savings:** $$ \eta_{area} = \frac{N-2}{2N} $$ For $N = 4$: $\eta_{area} = \frac{4-2}{8} = 0.25 = 25\%$. For $N = 10$: $\eta_{area} = \frac{10-2}{20} = 0.40 = 40\%$. As $N \to \infty$: $\eta_{area} \to 0.50 = 50\%$. > **Engineering Insight:** Dynamic logic is most area-efficient for **wide** (high fan-in) gates. This is why dynamic NOR and OR-AND-INVERT structures are common in 32/64-bit datapath comparators. ### 3.2 Derivation II — Charge-Sharing Voltage Drop (Worst Case) **Setup:** Consider a dynamic gate with internal node **X** (between two NMOS in the PDN) having capacitance $C_{int}$ to GND. The output node has $C_L$ precharged to $V_{DD}$. During evaluate, the input at the top NMOS is **0** (so PDN does not discharge OUT), but the input at the bottom NMOS is **1** → node X is connected to OUT through the top NMOS. **Charge conservation** (no DC path to any rail during this interval): $$ Q_{initial} = C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_{int} \cdot 0 = C_L \cdot V_{DD} $$ $$ Q_{final} = (C_L + C_{int}) \cdot V_{final} $$ Equating charge: $$ C_L \cdot V_{DD} = (C_L + C_{int}) \cdot V_{final} $$ Solving for the worst-case output voltage after charge sharing: $$ V_{final} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_{int}} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (C_{int}/C_L)} $$ **Voltage drop:** $$ \Delta V_{CS} = V_{DD} - V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}} $$ **Example (numerical):** Let $V_{DD} = 1.8 \text{ V}$, $C_L = 50 \text{ fF}$, $C_{int} = 30 \text{ fF}$: $$ \Delta V_{CS} = 1.8 \cdot \frac{30}{50 + 30} = 1.8 \cdot \frac{30}{80} = 1.8 \cdot 0.375 = 0.675 \text{ V} $$ $$ V_{final} = 1.8 - 0.675 = 1.125 \text{ V} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Trap:** Students often write $V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot (C_{int}/(C_L + C_{int}))$, which is the **drop**, not the final voltage. Always label clearly: $\Delta V_{CS}$ = drop, $V_{final}$ = remaining. ### 3.3 Derivation III — Evaluate Time Constant **Goal:** Find the time for OUT to fall from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{DD}/2$ (the 50% delay point) when PDN conducts. **Model:** The discharge path is a single NMOS (W/L) in saturation/triode, discharging $C_L$. Using the **Sakurai-Newton alpha-power law** simplification for hand analysis, we treat the NMOS as a constant-current source with average current $I_{n,avg}$. **Saturation drain current of NMOS:** $$ I_{Dn} = k_n \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{tn})^2 $$ Here $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$ (input is HIGH), so: $$ I_{Dn} = k_n \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2 $$ **Charge removed from $C_L$ to drop by $V_{DD}/2$:** $$ \Delta Q = C_L \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} $$ **Evaluate delay:** $$ t_{ev} = \frac{\Delta Q}{I_{Dn}} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}/2}{k_n \cdot (W/L) \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2} $$ Simplifying: $$ t_{ev} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 k_n (W/L) (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2} $$ > **Engineering Insight:** Doubling $V_{DD}$ roughly **doubles** $t_{ev}$ (linear in $V_{DD}$ for fixed $V_{tn}$); doubling W halves $t_{ev}$. This is the trade-off knob designers use to balance delay and power. ### 3.4 Derivation IV — Energy per Transition and Power **Energy to charge $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$:** $$ E_{ch} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Energy to discharge $C_L$ from $V_{DD}$ to 0:** $E_{disch} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ (dissipated as heat in NMOS). **Total energy per switching cycle (charge + discharge):** $$ E_{sw} = C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Average dynamic power (α = switching activity factor, f = clock frequency):** $$ P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot E_{sw} \cdot f = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Bonus Tip:** For a clock node that switches **every** cycle, $\alpha = 1$. For a random data node, $\alpha \approx 0.15$ (because $P(0 \to 1) = P(1 \to 0) = 0.25$ and only one of the two transitions is a charging transition). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — N-Type Dynamic CMOS Inverter (4T) The 4-transistor dynamic CMOS inverter consists of: - **Mp**: PMOS precharge (gate = CLK, source = VDD, drain = OUT) - **Me**: NMOS evaluate/foot (gate = CLK, source = PDN, drain = OUT) - **M1**: NMOS logic (gate = IN, drain = PDN, source = GND) - **C_L**: Output load capacitance (parasitic) ```mermaid graph TD VDD["VDD"]:::rail CLK["CLK"]:::clk IN["IN"]:::inp GND["GND"]:::gnd OUT["OUT"]:::out MP["Mp PMOS Precharge"]:::pmos ME["Me NMOS Evaluate Foot"]:::nmos M1["M1 NMOS Logic Driver"]:::nmos CL["CL Output Capacitance"]:::cap VDD --- MP MP --- OUT CLK --- MP CLK --- ME ME --- NODEPDN["Internal PDN Node"]:::mid IN --- M1 M1 --- NODEPDN NODEPDN --- GND OUT --- CL classDef rail fill:#1a1a1a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef clk fill:#2c5f9e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1a3a5e,stroke-width:2px classDef inp fill:#6a3d9a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#4a2670,stroke-width:2px classDef gnd fill:#333333,color:#ffffff,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#a83232,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7a1f1f,stroke-width:3px classDef pmos fill:#e07b39,color:#ffffff,stroke:#a85420,stroke-width:2px classDef nmos fill:#3a8b3a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f5e1f,stroke-width:2px classDef cap fill:#cc9933,color:#000000,stroke:#806020,stroke-width:2px classDef mid fill:#555555,color:#ffffff,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Two-Phase Timing (Precharge / Evaluate) ```mermaid graph LR A["Precharge Phase
CLK = 0
Mp ON, Me OFF
OUT charged to VDD"]:::pre B["Evaluate Phase
CLK = 1
Mp OFF, Me ON
OUT depends on IN"]:::eva C["Return to Precharge
CLK = 0
Cycle Repeats"]:::ret A ==>|"CLK rises"| B B ==>|"CLK falls"| C C ==>|"next cycle"| A classDef pre fill:#2c5f9e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1a3a5e,stroke-width:2px classDef eva fill:#a83232,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7a1f1f,stroke-width:2px classDef ret fill:#6a3d9a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#4a2670,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD subgraph CLOCK_DOMAIN["Clock Distribution Network"] CLK_GEN["Clock Generator
phi and phi_bar"]:::clk end subgraph STAGE1["Dynamic Gate Stage 1"] PRE1["Precharge PMOS"]:::pre EVA1["Evaluate NMOS"]:::eva PDN1["PDN Logic 1"]:::logic KEEP1["Keeper PMOS"]:::keep end subgraph INTER["Inter-stage"] CL1["Output Capacitance
CL1"]:::cap end subgraph STAGE2["Dynamic Gate Stage 2"] PRE2["Precharge PMOS"]:::pre EVA2["Evaluate NMOS"]:::eva PDN2["PDN Logic 2"]:::logic KEEP2["Keeper PMOS"]:::keep end subgraph OUTPUT["Output"] OUT["Valid OUT
during Evaluate only"]:::out end CLK_GEN -->|"phi"| PRE1 CLK_GEN -->|"phi"| EVA1 IN1["Inputs IN1 IN2"]:::inp --> PDN1 PRE1 --> CL1 EVA1 --> CL1 PDN1 --> CL1 KEEP1 --> CL1 CL1 -->|"drives gate of"| EVA2 CL1 -->|"drives PDN2"| PDN2 PRE2 --> OUT EVA2 --> OUT PDN2 --> OUT KEEP2 --> OUT IN2["Inputs IN3 IN4"]:::inp --> PDN2 classDef clk fill:#2c5f9e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1a3a5e,stroke-width:2px classDef pre fill:#e07b39,color:#ffffff,stroke:#a85420,stroke-width:2px classDef eva fill:#3a8b3a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f5e1f,stroke-width:2px classDef logic fill:#666666,color:#ffffff,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px classDef keep fill:#cc9933,color:#000000,stroke:#806020,stroke-width:2px classDef cap fill:#a83232,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7a1f1f,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#1a1a1a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef inp fill:#6a3d9a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#4a2670,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Charge Sharing Event ```mermaid graph LR A["OUT node
CL precharged to VDD"]:::out B["Internal node X
Cint initially at 0V"]:::mid C["Charge Redistribution
CL shares with Cint"]:::share D["OUT drops to Vfinal
below VIH of next stage
LOGIC ERROR"]:::err A -->|"input pattern
causes PDN partial connection"| B A --> B B --> C C --> D classDef out fill:#a83232,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7a1f1f,stroke-width:2px classDef mid fill:#555555,color:#ffffff,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px classDef share fill:#cc9933,color:#000000,stroke:#806020,stroke-width:2px classDef err fill:#1a1a1a,color:#ff5555,stroke:#ff5555,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mitigation of Charge Sharing:** > 1. **Precharge internal nodes** as well (use "bleeding" PMOS to VDD). > 2. **Add a keeper** (weak PMOS) to replenish any lost charge. > 3. **Increase $C_L \gg C_{int}$** by design (wider output driver / more load). > 4. Use **static CMOS** for noise-sensitive critical paths. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define dynamic CMOS logic. What are the two operating phases of a dynamic CMOS gate? List two advantages over static CMOS.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Dynamic CMOS logic is a circuit design style in which the output node is not continuously driven by a DC path between VDD and GND; instead, the output state is **temporarily stored on the load capacitance** and refreshed on every clock cycle. **Two operating phases:** 1. **Precharge phase (CLK = 0):** The precharge PMOS turns ON, charging the output capacitance to VDD. 2. **Evaluate phase (CLK = 1):** The precharge PMOS turns OFF, the evaluate NMOS turns ON, and the output is either discharged to GND (logic 0) through the PDN or held at VDD (logic 1) depending on the inputs. **Two advantages over static CMOS:** 1. **Lower transistor count:** $N + 2$ vs $2N$ for an N-input gate → up to 50% area reduction. 2. **Zero static power dissipation** because there is no DC path from VDD to GND in steady state. *(Any other valid advantage: lower input capacitance, higher speed, lower noise on internal nodes — full credit.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is charge sharing in dynamic CMOS circuits? Derive the worst-case output voltage after a charge sharing event.** **Model Answer:** **Charge sharing** is a failure mechanism in dynamic CMOS gates where charge stored on the output load capacitance $C_L$ (precharged to $V_{DD}$) is redistributed with an internal PDN node capacitance $C_{int}$ (initially at 0 V), causing the output voltage to drop below the switching threshold of the next stage and thereby causing a logic error. **Derivation (by conservation of charge, no DC path to any rail):** Initial charge: $Q_i = C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_{int} \cdot 0 = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$ Final charge: $Q_f = (C_L + C_{int}) \cdot V_{final}$ Equating: $C_L \cdot V_{DD} = (C_L + C_{int}) \cdot V_{final}$ Worst-case output voltage after charge sharing: $$ V_{final} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_{int}} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (C_{int}/C_L)} $$ Voltage drop: $$ \Delta V_{CS} = V_{DD} - V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}} $$ ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style] **Q.A. (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 4-transistor (4T) dynamic CMOS inverter during both precharge and evaluate phases. Clearly state the conditions on the clock signal and the input for each possible output state. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Circuit Description (refer to Mermaid Diagram 4.1):** - **Mp** (PMOS, precharge): Gate = CLK, Source = VDD, Drain = OUT. - **Me** (NMOS, evaluate/foot): Gate = CLK, Drain = OUT, Source = PDN-internal-node. - **M1** (NMOS, logic): Gate = IN, Drain = PDN-node, Source = GND. - **C_L**: Parasitic capacitance at OUT (drain + wire + next-stage gate). **Precharge Phase (CLK = 0):** - **Mp is ON** (PMOS conducts with LOW gate), **Me is OFF** (NMOS blocks with LOW gate). - The pull-down path from OUT to GND is **broken** at Me. - OUT is connected to VDD through Mp, so $C_L$ charges to $V_{DD}$. - **OUT = 1 (HIGH)** regardless of IN. The input is "don't care" during precharge. **Evaluate Phase (CLK = 1):** - **Mp turns OFF** (isolates OUT from VDD). - **Me turns ON** (connects PDN to OUT). - Now the state of IN decides the output: - **Case 1: IN = 1** → M1 is ON, PDN conducts (Me + M1) → $C_L$ discharges to GND → **OUT = 0**. - **Case 2: IN = 0** → M1 is OFF, PDN is broken → $C_L$ **floats** at precharged $V_{DD}$ → **OUT = 1**. **Summary Table (must appear in answer):** | CLK | IN | Mp | Me | M1 | OUT | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | X | ON | OFF | X | 1 (precharged) | | 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | OFF | 1 (held) | | 1 | 1 | OFF | ON | ON | 0 (discharged) | **Key observation:** The output is **valid only at the end of the evaluate phase** (CLK falling edge). During precharge, OUT is forced HIGH. --- **Q.A. (b)** A dynamic CMOS gate drives a load capacitance $C_L = 100 \text{ fF}$ at $V_{DD} = 1.8 \text{ V}$. The internal PDN node has $C_{int} = 20 \text{ fF}$ initially at 0 V. Calculate: (i) the worst-case output voltage after charge sharing, (ii) the switching energy per cycle, and (iii) the dynamic power if the clock frequency is $f = 500 \text{ MHz}$ and the switching activity $\alpha = 0.2$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** $C_L = 100 \text{ fF}$, $C_{int} = 20 \text{ fF}$, $V_{DD} = 1.8 \text{ V}$, $f = 500 \text{ MHz}$, $\alpha = 0.2$. **(i) Worst-case output voltage after charge sharing:** Using $V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + (C_{int}/C_L)}$: $$ V_{final} = 1.8 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (20/100)} = 1.8 \cdot \frac{1}{1.2} = 1.8 \cdot 0.8333 = 1.5 \text{ V} $$ **Voltage drop:** $$ \Delta V_{CS} = 1.8 - 1.5 = 0.3 \text{ V} $$ **(ii) Switching energy per cycle:** $$ E_{sw} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (1.8)^2 = 100 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 3.24 = 3.24 \times 10^{-13} \text{ J} = 324 \text{ fJ} $$ **(iii) Dynamic power:** $$ P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f = 0.2 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 3.24 \cdot 500 \times 10^6 $$ Step-by-step: $$ P_{dyn} = 0.2 \cdot 3.24 \times 10^{-13} \cdot 5 \times 10^{8} $$ $$ P_{dyn} = 0.2 \cdot 1.62 \times 10^{-4} = 3.24 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 32.4 \text{ μW} $$ --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] **Q.B. (a)** Compare static CMOS logic and dynamic CMOS logic under the following heads: transistor count, static power dissipation, switching speed, input capacitance, noise margin, and cascading capability. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Static CMOS | Dynamic CMOS | |---|---|---| | **Transistor count (N-input gate)** | $2N$ | $N + 2$ | | **Static power dissipation** | Small (leakage only) | **Zero** (no DC path) | | **Switching speed** | Slower (larger input cap, more transistors to drive) | **Faster** (smaller input cap, less cap to charge) | | **Input capacitance** | Both PMOS and NMOS gate caps in parallel | **NMOS only** (smaller, faster) | | **Noise margin** | **High** (restoring, rail-to-rail) | **Low** (non-restoring during evaluate) | | **Cascading capability** | Easy (any sequence) | **Difficult** (requires $\phi$ and $\phi_{delayed}$, non-overlapping clocks) | | **Charge sharing** | Not applicable | **Major problem** | | **Clock distribution** | Not needed | **Required** (chip-wide overhead) | | **Design complexity** | Simple | Complex (timing, keepers) | **Summary:** Dynamic logic is **faster, smaller, and lower static power**, but suffers from **noise and cascading issues**. Static CMOS is **robust and simple** but is larger and slower for high fan-in gates. --- **Q.B. (b)** Explain the role of the **keeper transistor** in a dynamic CMOS gate. With a circuit, show how it is connected. What is the sizing constraint and why? **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Role of Keeper:** The keeper (also called the **half-ratio load** or **bleeder**) is a **weak PMOS transistor** added in parallel with the precharge PMOS to **compensate for sub-threshold leakage and charge sharing** at the output node during the evaluate phase. It holds the output HIGH against any small leakage discharge. **Circuit Connection (refer to Mermaid 4.1, block KEEP1):** - Source = VDD, Drain = OUT, Gate = **OUT_bar** (inverted output via a small static inverter). - When OUT is HIGH → OUT_bar = LOW → keeper is **ON**, weakly pulling OUT to VDD. - When OUT is LOW → OUT_bar = HIGH → keeper is **OFF**, no contention. **Sizing Constraint:** - Keeper must be **MUCH WEAKER** than the PDN, typically $W_k/L \approx (1/4 \text{ to } 1/10) \times W_{PDN}/L$. - The current drive ratio $I_{PDN}/I_{keeper} \geq 4$ (often called the **"strength ratio"**). - **Why:** If the keeper is too strong, the PDN cannot pull OUT to GND during a real LOW transition (functional failure). If it is too weak, leakage in modern nanometer nodes can flip the output. **Numerical example:** For a 4-input dynamic NAND, NMOS in PDN is sized at $(W/L) = 4$, so keeper is sized at $(W/L) = 0.5$ — a 1:8 ratio. ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Dynamic CMOS Questions:** > 1. **Confusing precharge and evaluate clock levels:** Many students write "CLK=1 for precharge" — it is **CLK=0** for n-type dynamic logic. (+1 mark lost per occurrence.) > 2. **Drawing static CMOS by mistake:** The circuit must have only **one PMOS (precharge) and one NMOS (foot) connected to clock**, with all logic NMOS in the PDN. Drawing two PMOS in series = **static logic**, not dynamic. (Up to −3 marks.) > 3. **Charge sharing formula confusion:** Writing $V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot C_{int}/(C_L + C_{int})$ instead of $V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot C_L/(C_L + C_{int})$ — the latter is correct. (−2 marks.) > 4. **Forgetting the keeper:** In noise/leakage questions, if you do not mention the keeper PMOS, you lose at least 1 mark. > 5. **Switching activity mistake:** Setting $\alpha = 1$ for "data input" nodes. Only the **clock** has $\alpha = 1$; random data has $\alpha \approx 0.15\text{–}0.20$. > 6. **Transistor count wrong:** Stating "dynamic uses half the transistors" is too vague — write the formula $N + 2$ vs $2N$. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dynamic CMOS Design Basics** - Dynamic CMOS has **two phases**: **Precharge (CLK=0)** charges OUT to VDD; **Evaluate (CLK=1)** conditionally discharges OUT via PDN. - **Transistor count = N + 2** (one PMOS precharge, one NMOS foot, N NMOS for logic). - **Static power = 0 W** (no DC path from VDD to GND in steady state). - **Dynamic power = $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$** (same formula as static CMOS, often lower α per node). - **Output is valid only at the falling edge of CLK** (end of evaluate). - **Charge sharing** drops OUT to $V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot C_L/(C_L + C_{int})$. - **Clock feedthrough** injects voltage noise through C_gd of clocked transistors. - **Keeper PMOS** (weak, $\sim 1/4$ to $1/10$ PDN strength) prevents leakage-induced discharge. - **Cascading** is non-trivial: requires $\phi$ and $\phi_{delayed}$ clocks (Domino logic technique). - **Input capacitance is lower** (NMOS only) → faster, lower drive requirement. - **Used in**: high-speed ALUs, comparators, register files, sense amplifiers in DRAM. - **Avoid in**: low-noise-margin designs, low-power sleep modes (because floating node leaks). - **Noise margin** is poor compared to static CMOS — non-restoring during evaluate. - **Power formula must include the clock** as a switching node with $\alpha = 1$. - **For wide fan-in gates**, dynamic is dramatically smaller (up to 50% area saving). - **Half-ratio / keeper sizing** is a *ratioed* logic style — the output is the result of a current fight between PDN and keeper.

topics.title: Concept of Domino Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Domino Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Concept of Domino Logic - Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DOMINO CMOS LOGIC (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Domino Logic is a high-performance variant of dynamic CMOS logic in which a **static CMOS inverter** is cascaded at the output of a dynamic CMOS logic stage. It was introduced to overcome the cascading limitation of basic dynamic logic while preserving its area and speed advantages.*

The term **"Domino"** is derived from the way logic transitions propagate through a chain of such gates — once a stage evaluates (falls), it can only "trip" (raise) the input of the next stage, which in turn evaluates — analogous to a chain of falling dominoes where each piece falls only in **one direction**.

Mathematically, a Domino gate implements a **monotonic** (non-inverting) Boolean function because of the additional inverter stage. The dynamic stage evaluates monotonically (only $\rightarrow$ VDD or $\rightarrow$ GND, never in reverse during evaluation), and the inverter restores the signal to full **rail-to-rail logic levels**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Row of Falling Dominoes**
>
> Imagine a long line of domino tiles standing on edge on a table:
>
> - **Phase 1 (Precharge):** All tiles are standing upright — the "default" state. Nothing happens; the system is "armed."
> - **Phase 2 (Evaluation):** When the clock ticks, you push the first tile. If the input conditions are right, that tile falls and **pushes** the next one, which falls, pushing the next, and so on — a cascade.
> - **The Inverter:** The "click" sound each tile makes when it falls represents the inverter output — a sharp, clean edge. Without that inverter, the energy in the cascade would weaken, just as dynamic logic signals weaken due to charge sharing and noise.
>
> - **One-way motion:** Once a tile has fallen, it cannot push back. This is **monotonicity** — Domino logic transitions only in one direction during evaluation.

---

## 1.3 Why Domino Logic? - Motivation in KTU Syllabus

Static CMOS logic gates, although robust, suffer from several limitations in high-performance digital VLSI:

| Static CMOS Limitation | Impact |
|------------------------|--------|
| Every gate has **both PMOS and NMOS** networks | Larger silicon area, more capacitance |
| **$C_{in}$** is the sum of PMOS + NMOS capacitances | Slower signal propagation |
| Input signals drive **both** transistors | Higher switching power |
| For N-input gate, requires $2N$ transistors | Density penalty |

Dynamic CMOS logic (basic form) reduces this to $N+2$ transistors but introduces:
- **Cascading difficulty** (cannot directly drive another dynamic stage)
- **Charge sharing** between dynamic node and internal capacitances
- **Clock feedthrough** and **leakage-induced errors**

**Domino Logic** is the engineering solution that retains dynamic logic's speed/area benefits while fixing the cascading issue via the static inverter buffer.

> [!TIP]
> Domino logic is widely used in critical-path circuits such as **ALUs, adders, register files, address decoders, and high-speed datapaths** in modern microprocessors and ASICs.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (180nm process) | Unit |
|-----------|--------|------------------------------|------|
| Supply Voltage | $V_{DD}$ | **1.8** | V |
| Threshold Voltage (NMOS) | $V_{Tn}$ | **0.4 – 0.5** | V |
| Threshold Voltage (PMOS) | $\vert V_{Tp} \vert$ | **0.4 – 0.5** | V |
| Oxide Capacitance | $C_{ox}$ | **8 – 10** | fF/$\mu m^2$ |
| Process Transconductance | $k_n'$, $k_p'$ | **100 – 120** | $\mu A/V^2$ |
| Clock Frequency | $f_{clk}$ | **100 – 500** | MHz |
| Noise Margin (Domino) | $NM_L$, $NM_H$ | Reduced vs static | V |

---

# Concept of Domino Logic - Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Background: Static vs Dynamic vs Domino CMOS Logic

### 2.1.1 Static CMOS Logic (Recap)
A static CMOS gate consists of:
- **Pull-Up Network (PUN):** PMOS transistors connected to $V_{DD}$
- **Pull-Down Network (PDN):** NMOS transistors connected to GND

The PUN and PDN are **logically complementary** — exactly one is conducting for any input combination. The output is always connected to either $V_{DD}$ or GND through a low-resistance path (in steady state).

### 2.1.2 Basic Dynamic CMOS Logic (Recap)
A dynamic CMOS gate uses **only an NMOS pull-down network** plus **two clocked transistors**:

- **M1 (Clocked PMOS):** Precharges the dynamic output node to $V_{DD}$ when $CLK = 0$.
- **M2 (Clocked NMOS):** Foot transistor that enables evaluation when $CLK = 1$.
- **NMOS Logic Network:** Conditional discharge of dynamic node during evaluation.

Two operating phases:
- **Precharge Phase** ($\phi = 0$): M1 ON, M2 OFF $\Rightarrow$ dynamic node charged to $V_{DD}$.
- **Evaluation Phase** ($\phi = 1$): M1 OFF, M2 ON $\Rightarrow$ dynamic node either stays at $V_{DD}$ (no conducting path) or discharges to GND (logic network conducts).

### 2.1.3 The Cascading Problem in Basic Dynamic Logic
If a dynamic stage drives another dynamic stage directly:
- During precharge, the first stage's dynamic node $\rightarrow V_{DD}$.
- This $V_{DD}$ would turn ON the NMOS transistors of the second stage's logic network **before evaluation begins** — causing the second stage to **erroneously pre-discharge** and lose its stored charge.
- **Solution:** Insert a static CMOS inverter between the two stages. The inverter output is LOW during precharge, which keeps all NMOS in the next stage OFF.

---

## 2.2 Domino CMOS Logic — Structure & Operation

### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology

A Domino CMOS gate has **three structural blocks**:

1. **Dynamic Precharge Block:** Clocked PMOS $M_p$ (precharge transistor)
2. **NMOS Evaluation Network:** $N$ NMOS transistors implementing the logic function
3. **Clocked Evaluate NMOS (Footer):** $M_{ev}$ for noise immunity
4. **Static CMOS Inverter:** Output buffer for cascading and full-swing output

### 2.2.2 Operating Phases

#### (a) Precharge Phase ($CLK = 0$, i.e., $\overline{CLK} = 1$)

- $M_p$ is **ON** (PMOS gate low) $\Rightarrow$ Dynamic node ($Y$) charged to $V_{DD}$.
- $M_{ev}$ is **OFF** (NMOS gate low) $\Rightarrow$ No path to GND through logic network.
- Inverter output $\Rightarrow$ **LOW (0 V)** regardless of inputs.
- Therefore, inputs to the **next stage** (which are NMOS gates) are all LOW, ensuring **no premature discharge** of the next dynamic node.

#### (b) Evaluation Phase ($CLK = 1$, i.e., $\overline{CLK} = 0$)

- $M_p$ is **OFF** (PMOS gate high) $\Rightarrow$ No charging path.
- $M_{ev}$ is **ON** $\Rightarrow$ Logic network is connected to GND.
- If the NMOS logic network conducts (function evaluates to 1), $Y$ discharges to GND $\Rightarrow$ Inverter output goes **HIGH**.
- If the logic network does not conduct, $Y$ remains at $V_{DD}$ $\Rightarrow$ Inverter output stays **LOW**.
- **The output is a monotonically rising transition** (only 0 → 1, never 1 → 0 during evaluation).

### 2.2.3 Cascading & The Domino Effect

When multiple Domino gates are cascaded:
- During precharge, **all** intermediate nodes precharge to $V_{DD}$, so **all** inverter outputs are LOW $\Rightarrow$ all next-stage NMOS are OFF.
- During evaluation, only those stages whose logic evaluates to 1 will have their dynamic node pulled LOW, raising the inverter output, which then may turn ON NMOS of the subsequent stage.
- This **cascading wave** resembles a chain of falling dominoes — hence the name.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monotonicity is the key reason Domino logic works:** The output of a Domino gate can **only transition from 0 to 1 during evaluation**. It can never transition from 1 to 0. This guarantees that the next stage's NMOS network is OFF until the rising edge arrives.

---

## 2.3 Why "Domino" Instead of Plain Dynamic Logic?

| Issue in Basic Dynamic Logic | Domino Logic Solution |
|------------------------------|------------------------|
| Cannot cascade directly | Inverter output is LOW during precharge → safe for next stage |
| Charge redistribution may corrupt dynamic node | Inverter provides buffering, isolating downstream loads |
| Reduced noise margin (single transistor drive) | Static inverter provides full-swing rail-to-rail output |
| Glitches can corrupt dynamic node | Static output is glitch-immune during evaluation |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables / Notes |
|---|--------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Dynamic Power Dissipation** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity factor, $C_L$ = load capacitance |
| 2 | **Static Power (Ideal)** | $P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Ideal Domino: $P_{static} \approx 0$ (no DC path) |
| 3 | **Evaluation Discharge Delay** | $t_{pd,eval} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^{2}/2}$ | NMOS pull-down only |
| 4 | **Precharge Delay** | $t_{pd,pre} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_p \cdot (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert)^{2}/2}$ | PMOS precharge |
| 5 | **Min Clock Period (Domino)** | $T_{clk,min} = t_{pre} + t_{eval,max} + t_{setup}$ | Critical path constraint |
| 6 | **Transistor Count (N-input gate)** | $N_{TX} = N + 3$ | N logic NMOS + Mp + Mev + Inv(2) |
| 7 | **Activity Factor (Domino)** | $\alpha_{domino} \geq \dfrac{1}{2}$ | Higher than static because every cycle evaluates |
| 8 | **Charge Sharing Voltage Drop** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}}{C_{L} + C_{par}}$ | $C_{par}$ = internal parasitic on dynamic node |
| 9 | **Noise Margin (LOW)** | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Reduced in Domino due to dynamic node thresholds |
| 10 | **Keeper Ratio** | $K_r = \dfrac{(W/L)_{keeper}}{(W/L)_{driver}}$ | Typically $K_r \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Reminder for KTU Board Exams:**
> - Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX (NOT the ASCII pipe $\vert$ which breaks markdown tables).
> - Standard NMOS discharge current: $I_{Dn} = k_n \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot \left[(V_{GS} - V_{Tn})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^{2}}{2}\right]$
> - Standard PMOS precharge current: similar form with $V_{Tp}$ magnitude.

---

## 2.5 Practical Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microprocessor Datapaths:** Critical-path arithmetic units (adders, multipliers) in Intel/AMD cores use Domino logic families.
- **High-Speed Memory:** Address decoders in SRAM/DRAM use dynamic logic.
- **ASIC Standard Cells:** Domino cells exist in standard cell libraries from Synopsys, Cadence.
- **Trade-off Sweet Spot:** Domino is preferred when **performance (delay) > energy efficiency**.
- **Modern usage caveats:** In sub-28nm processes, leakage dominates, so **Domino is often replaced by static CMOS** with proper sizing or by **footless Domino variants**.

---

## 2.6 Limitations of Domino Logic

> [!WARNING]
> - **Non-inverting only:** A Domino gate can implement only non-inverting functions (due to the trailing inverter). To implement an inverter, you use a single dynamic stage with an inverting topology.
> - **Charge sharing** between dynamic node and internal node capacitances can falsely discharge the output.
> - **Clock feedthrough** through the gate-drain overlap capacitance of $M_p$ and $M_{ev}$ can shift the dynamic node voltage.
> - **High activity factor** ($\alpha \geq 0.5$) increases dynamic power.
> - **Leakage in deep submicron:** The dynamic node leaks, requiring a **keeper transistor** (weak PMOS) for charge preservation.
> - **Clock distribution overhead:** A clean, low-skew clock is mandatory.
