# Concept of Domino Logic - Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Dynamic Power Dissipation in Domino Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the average power consumed by a single Domino gate switching at a clock frequency $f_{clk}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Identify the energy storage element.**
The dynamic node has capacitance $C_L$ (composed of gate, drain, and wire capacitances of downstream gates).

**Step 2 — Energy charged in precharge phase.**
During precharge, the PMOS transistor $M_p$ charges $C_L$ from $0$ V to $V_{DD}$. The energy drawn from the supply is:

$$E_{charged} = \int_{0}^{T_{pre}} V_{DD} \cdot i(t) \, dt = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated in the PMOS transistor during charging.**
Of the energy supplied by $V_{DD}$, exactly half is stored in $C_L$, and the other half is dissipated as heat in $M_p$:

$$E_{dissipated,Mp} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Energy dissipated in the NMOS logic network during discharging.**
When the dynamic node discharges through the NMOS network during evaluation, the stored energy $\dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2}$ is dissipated in the NMOS transistors. No additional energy is drawn from the supply during discharge.

$$E_{dissipated,eval} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Total energy dissipated per switching cycle (full 0→1→0 swing):**

$$E_{cycle} = E_{dissipated,Mp} + E_{dissipated,eval} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} + \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^{2} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by the switching activity factor $\alpha$.**
Not every clock cycle triggers a transition (depends on input pattern probability). Introduce the activity factor $\alpha$:

$$E_{avg} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Average power dissipated:**

$$
\boxed{P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{avg}}{T_{clk}} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** For Domino logic specifically, since the output transitions at least once per clock cycle (LOW during precharge, then conditionally HIGH during evaluation), we have $\alpha_{domino} \geq 0.5$, which is typically **higher** than static CMOS where $\alpha \approx 0.1$ to $0.2$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Evaluation Phase Propagation Delay

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Find the time taken for the dynamic node to discharge from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{DD}/2$ (50% delay point) through the NMOS logic network.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Initial condition:** Dynamic node voltage $V_{dyn}(0) = V_{DD}$.
**Step 2 — Final condition:** $V_{dyn}(t_d) = V_{DD}/2$.

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the dynamic node:**
Current into $C_L$ from discharging = $-C_L \cdot \dfrac{dV_{dyn}}{dt}$

$$-C_L \cdot \dfrac{dV_{dyn}}{dt} = I_{Dn}$$

**Step 4 — Approximation using average NMOS current over the discharge interval.**
For an NMOS in saturation (assuming $V_{dyn} \approx V_{DD}$ initially):

$$I_{Dn,sat} \approx k_n \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot \dfrac{(V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^{2}}{2}$$

With $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$ and effective gate drive $V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$:

$$I_{Dn,sat} \approx \dfrac{k_n}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Using the equivalent resistance model:**
For a saturated NMOS, the equivalent on-resistance is:

$$R_{eq,n} \approx \dfrac{1}{k_n \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$$

**Step 6 — RC delay formula for the discharge:**

$$t_{pd,eval} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq,n} \cdot C_L \approx \dfrac{0.69 \cdot C_L}{k_n \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$$

**Step 7 — Simplified closed form:**

$$
\boxed{t_{pd,eval} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^{2}}}
$$

**Step 8 — Comparison with static CMOS:**
A static CMOS NAND/NOR gate of similar fan-in must drive the same $C_L$ but through **both** PMOS and NMOS in series for certain inputs. The effective resistance is roughly double, making Domino logic **~1.5× to 2× faster** for the same load.

> **Conversion Logic Note:** This is why Domino logic is preferred for **high-speed critical paths** where delay is paramount and dynamic power is acceptable.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Charge Sharing Voltage Drop (Failure Mechanism)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Quantify the voltage error on the dynamic node due to charge redistribution with internal parasitic capacitance.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define capacitances.**
- $C_L$: Load capacitance at the dynamic node.
- $C_{par}$: Internal parasitic capacitance (e.g., source/drain junction cap of NMOS logic network).

**Step 2 — Initial precharge:** Dynamic node is at $V_{DD}$, internal node is at $0$ V (assuming a previously-discharged state).

$$Q_{initial} = C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_{par} \cdot 0 = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$$

**Step 3 — After charge sharing** (assume evaluation phase begins but logic doesn't fully evaluate; only partial charge redistribution occurs):

$$Q_{final} = (C_L + C_{par}) \cdot V_{final}$$

**Step 4 — Apply charge conservation** ($Q_{initial} = Q_{final}$):

$$C_L \cdot V_{DD} = (C_L + C_{par}) \cdot V_{final}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{final}$:**

$$V_{final} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_{par}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the voltage drop (the error):**

$$
\boxed{\Delta V = V_{DD} - V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{C_L}{C_L + C_{par}}\right) = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_{par}}{C_L + C_{par}}}
$$

**Step 7 — Failure criterion:**
The dynamic node voltage drops by $\Delta V$. If $\Delta V$ exceeds the noise margin $NM_H$, the subsequent inverter may misinterpret the logic level, causing a functional failure.

> **Conversion Logic Note:** This is why **keeper transistors** (weak PMOS) are added to refresh the dynamic node during long evaluation phases. The keeper supplies just enough current to compensate leakage and resist charge sharing without overpowering the active pull-down.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Minimum Clock Period Constraint

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Establish the minimum clock frequency for correct Domino operation (to prevent dynamic node leakage from corrupting stored charge).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define leakage current.** In a sub-100nm process, subthreshold leakage is:

$$I_{leak} \approx I_{0} \cdot \dfrac{W}{L} \cdot 10^{-V_{GS}/S}$$

where $S$ is the subthreshold swing (typically $\approx 70$ mV/decade at room temperature).

**Step 2 — Voltage droop due to leakage over time $\Delta t$:**

$$\Delta V_{leak} = \dfrac{I_{leak} \cdot \Delta t}{C_L}$$

**Step 3 — Set $\Delta V_{leak}$ equal to maximum allowable droop $V_{noise}$:**

$$\Delta t_{max} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{noise}}{I_{leak}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum allowable clock period:**

$$
\boxed{T_{clk,max} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{noise}}{I_{leak}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum clock frequency for reliable operation:**

$$f_{clk,min} = \dfrac{I_{leak}}{C_L \cdot V_{noise}}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** In modern VLSI, this constraint forces the use of keepers, footless Domino, or pseudo-static Domino (with a small always-on PMOS) to handle deep submicron leakage.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Static CMOS vs Dynamic CMOS vs Domino Logic

| Metric | Static CMOS | Basic Dynamic CMOS | Domino CMOS |
|--------|-------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Transistors (N-input) | $2N$ | $N + 2$ | $N + 3$ |
| Static Power | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ |
| Dynamic Power Factor | $\alpha \approx 0.1$ | $\alpha \approx 0.5$ | $\alpha \approx 0.5$ |
| Cascadable (Direct) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Output Swing | Rail-to-rail | Rail-to-rail (dynamic node) | Rail-to-rail (via inverter) |
| Noise Margin | Excellent | Poor | Good (with keeper) |
| Charge Sharing Risk | None | High | High (mitigated by keeper) |
| Speed (Relative) | 1× (baseline) | 1.8× | 1.8× |
| Logic Style | Inverting | Inverting | **Non-inverting** |
| Clock Required | No | Yes | Yes |
| Area (Per Gate) | Largest | Smallest | Small (slightly > dynamic) |
| Robustness in Sub-100nm | Excellent | Poor | Moderate (needs keeper) |

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Internal Structure of a Single Domino Gate

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **internal functional architecture** of a Domino CMOS gate (e.g., a 2-input AND/NAND gate variant). It is intentionally block-level rather than transistor-level to preserve readability.

```mermaid
graph TB
    clk1[CLOCK PHI]
    clkn1[INVERTED CLOCK NPHI]
    inpA[INPUT A]
    inpB[INPUT B]

    mev1[NMOS FOOT TRANSISTOR MEV]
    nlnet1[NMOS PULL DOWN LOGIC NETWORK]
    dyn1[DYNAMIC INTERMEDIATE NODE Y]
    inv1[STATIC CMOS INVERTER STAGE]
    out1[FINAL OUTPUT Z]
    keeper1[WEAK PMOS KEEPER]

    clk1 --> mp1
    clkn1 --> mev1
    inpA --> nlnet1
    inpB --> nlnet1
    inpC --> nlnet1
    mp1 --> dyn1
    nlnet1 --> dyn1
    mev1 --> nlnet1
    dyn1 --> inv1
    keeper1 --> dyn1
    inv1 --> out1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Cascaded Domino Logic Chain (The Domino Wave)

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates how a 3-stage Domino chain propagates a logic wave during the **evaluation phase**, analogous to falling dominoes.

```mermaid
graph LR
    stage1[STAGE 1 DYNAMIC BLOCK]
    inv1s[STAGE 1 INVERTER]
    stage2[STAGE 2 DYNAMIC BLOCK]
    inv2s[STAGE 2 INVERTER]
    stage3[STAGE 3 DYNAMIC BLOCK]
    inv3s[STAGE 3 INVERTER]
    finalOut[FINAL OUTPUT]
    clkSig[CLOCK PHI]

    stage1 --> inv1s
    inv1s --> stage2
    stage2 --> inv2s
    inv2s --> stage3
    stage3 --> inv3s
    inv3s --> finalOut
    clkSig --> stage1
    clkSig --> stage2
    clkSig --> stage3
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Two-Phase Operation State Machine

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram captures the **state transitions** of a Domino gate between Precharge (PHI = 0) and Evaluation (PHI = 1) phases.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    precharge[PRECHARGE PHI EQUALS 0]
    hold[NODE Y AT VDD OUTPUT Z AT 0]
    evaluate[EVALUATION PHI EQUALS 1]
    cond1[LOGIC NETWORK OFF]
    cond2[LOGIC NETWORK ON]
    stayLow[OUTPUT Z STAYS AT 0]
    goHigh[OUTPUT Z RISES TO VDD]
    discharge[DYNAMIC NODE Y DISCHARGES TO 0]
    clkTick[CLOCK EDGE]

    precharge --> hold
    hold --> evaluate
    clkTick --> evaluate
    evaluate --> cond1
    evaluate --> cond2
    cond1 --> stayLow
    cond2 --> discharge
    discharge --> goHigh
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Domino Logic System (Block Level)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** — A typical Domino-based datapath block:

```mermaid
graph TB
    inputLatch1[INPUT LATCH / REGISTER]
    predecoder1[PREDECODER DYNAMIC STAGE]
    invBuf1[STATIC INVERTER BUFFER]
    driver1[HIGH FANOUT DRIVER]
    logicBlock1[DOMINO LOGIC CRITICAL PATH]
    finalInv1[OUTPUT INVERTER / BUFFER]
    outputLatch1[OUTPUT LATCH / REGISTER]

    inputLatch1 --> predecoder1
    predecoder1 --> invBuf1
    invBuf1 --> driver1
    driver1 --> logicBlock1
    logicBlock1 --> finalInv1
    finalInv1 --> outputLatch1
```

**Reading Guide for KTU Board Exams:**
- `STAGE 1 DYNAMIC BLOCK`: Implements the partial logic evaluation in the NMOS pull-down tree.
- `STAGE 1 INVERTER`: Provides monotonic (rising-edge only) output that drives the next stage safely.
- `KEEPER`: A weak PMOS (not shown as a separate block in cascading diagrams) that prevents dynamic node charge loss due to leakage/charge sharing.
- `FOOT TRANSISTOR`: $M_{ev}$ isolates the logic network from GND during precharge.
- `CLOCK PHI`: Single-phase clock common to all Domino gates in synchronous design.

---

## 4.5 Symbolic Transistor-Level Schematic (Textual Description for Board Drawing)

For KTU 2024 board exam, the expected drawing for a **2-input Domino AND gate** (output $Z = A \cdot B$) is:

```
         VDD
          |
          |- Mp (Precharge PMOS, gate = CLK)
          |
          +--------+------> Y (Dynamic Node)
          |        |
          |       M_kp (Keeper PMOS, weak, gate = Z)
          |        |
          |        Mn1 (gate = A) --- drain at Y
          |        |
          |        Mn2 (gate = B) --- source of Mn1
          |        |
          |        M_ev (Footer NMOS, gate = CLK)
          |        |
          |       GND
          |
          +---|>o--- (Static CMOS Inverter: PMOS-NMOS pair)
                    |
                    +------> Z (Final Domino Output)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When drawing the schematic, **always label**: (1) the dynamic node Y, (2) the final output Z, (3) clock signals PHI and NPHI, (4) the keeper transistor, and (5) the footer. Marks are often lost for missing these annotations.

# Concept of Domino Logic - Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

> **Q: What is Domino CMOS logic? Why is a static CMOS inverter used at the output stage of a dynamic CMOS gate in Domino logic?**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80-100 words):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Domino CMOS logic is a high-speed variant of dynamic CMOS logic in which a **static CMOS inverter** is added at the output of a dynamic stage. The structure enables cascading of dynamic logic gates without loss of stored charge.

**Reason for Inverter (2 marks):**
The static CMOS inverter serves three critical purposes:

1. **Enables cascading:** During precharge, the dynamic node is at $V_{DD}$, so the inverter output is at $0$ V. This $0$ V keeps the NMOS transistors of the next-stage dynamic gate OFF, preventing erroneous discharge of the next stage's dynamic node.
2. **Provides buffering:** The inverter isolates the high-impedance dynamic node from the load capacitance, preserving the charge on $C_L$.
3. **Restores rail-to-rail levels:** The output achieves a full $0 \rightarrow V_{DD}$ swing, improving noise margin.

**Examiner's Note:** Award 1 mark for the definition; 2 marks distributed among the three inverter purposes (or 2 of the 3 listed).

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - December 2023, Module 2]`

> **Q: Explain the charge sharing problem in dynamic CMOS logic. How does it affect the output voltage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80-100 words):**

**Explanation (2 marks):**
Charge sharing occurs in dynamic CMOS logic when the dynamic node capacitance $C_L$ **shares its stored charge** with the internal parasitic capacitances $C_{par}$ of the NMOS pull-down network during the evaluation phase. If the logic network partially turns on, charge redistributes between $C_L$ and $C_{par}$.

**Effect on Output Voltage (1 mark):**
Using charge conservation, the dynamic node voltage drops to:

$$V_{final} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{C_L + C_{par}}$$

If this drop exceeds the noise margin of the subsequent inverter, the output may flip erroneously, causing a **functional failure**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

> **Q: (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the structure and operation of Domino CMOS logic. Discuss the precharge and evaluation phases in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q: (b)** List and explain the major advantages and limitations of Domino CMOS logic over static CMOS logic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Part A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Structure of Domino CMOS Gate (3 marks):**

A Domino CMOS gate consists of:
1. **Clocked PMOS $M_p$** — for precharging the dynamic node to $V_{DD}$.
2. **NMOS pull-down logic network** — implementing the desired Boolean function.
3. **Clocked NMOS $M_{ev}$ (footer transistor)** — connecting the logic network to GND during evaluation.
4. **Static CMOS inverter** — providing the final buffered output and enabling cascading.

**Circuit Diagram (drawn on board):** Show $V_{DD}$ at top, $M_p$ with gate connected to $CLK$, the dynamic node $Y$, the NMOS logic network connected from $Y$ through $M_{ev}$ to GND, and the static inverter from $Y$ to output $Z$.

**Operation — Precharge Phase ($\phi = 0$) (2 marks):**

- $M_p$ is ON, $M_{ev}$ is OFF.
- Dynamic node $Y$ charges to $V_{DD}$.
- Output $Z$ (after inverter) is at **0 V**.
- The 0 V at $Z$ ensures that NMOS transistors in the next stage's logic network are OFF — preventing premature discharge of the next stage.

**Operation — Evaluation Phase ($\phi = 1$) (2 marks):**

- $M_p$ is OFF, $M_{ev}$ is ON.
- If the logic network conducts (function = 1): $Y$ discharges to GND, $Z$ rises to $V_{DD}$.
- If the logic network does not conduct (function = 0): $Y$ stays at $V_{DD}$, $Z$ stays at 0 V.
- The output $Z$ transitions **monotonically** (only 0 → 1).

---

#### **Solution for Part A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Advantages (4 marks):**

1. **Higher Speed:** Uses only NMOS pull-down network for evaluation — about 1.8× faster than static CMOS for the same load.
2. **Lower Transistor Count:** N-input gate uses $N + 3$ transistors vs $2N$ for static CMOS — area savings.
3. **Lower Input Capacitance:** Inputs drive only NMOS — half the capacitance of static CMOS.
4. **Zero Static Power:** No DC path from $V_{DD}$ to GND.
5. **Cascadable:** Static inverter output enables direct cascading of dynamic stages.
6. **Full Output Swing:** Static inverter restores $V_{DD}$ to GND at the output.

**Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **Charge Sharing:** Internal node capacitances can corrupt the dynamic node.
2. **Clock Required:** Needs a clean, low-skew clock — adds routing overhead.
3. **High Dynamic Power:** $\alpha \geq 0.5$ leads to higher switching power.
4. **Non-inverting only:** Cannot directly implement inverting functions without modification.
5. **Leakage in Sub-100nm:** Requires keeper transistors for reliable operation.
6. **Noise Sensitivity:** Reduced noise margin compared to static CMOS.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - December 2023, Module 2]`

> **Q: (a)** With a circuit diagram, explain the basic dynamic CMOS logic circuit. Discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q: (b)** Explain how Domino CMOS logic overcomes the cascading limitation of basic dynamic CMOS logic. Discuss the role of the keeper transistor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Part B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Basic Dynamic CMOS Structure (3 marks):**

A basic dynamic CMOS gate comprises:
- $M_p$: PMOS precharge transistor (gate = $CLK$)
- NMOS pull-down logic network
- $M_{ev}$: NMOS footer/evaluate transistor (gate = $\overline{CLK}$)
- Output: dynamic node $Y$ (taken directly from the logic network top)

**Precharge Phase ($\phi = 0$) (2 marks):**
- $M_p$ ON, $M_{ev}$ OFF.
- $Y$ charges to $V_{DD}$ via $M_p$.

**Evaluation Phase ($\phi = 1$) (2 marks):**
- $M_p$ OFF, $M_{ev}$ ON.
- $Y$ either stays at $V_{DD}$ (logic = 0) or discharges to GND (logic = 1).

**Limitations:**

- **Cannot cascade directly:** During precharge, $Y = V_{DD}$ turns ON NMOS in the next stage, causing false discharge.
- **Charge sharing** corrupts $Y$.
- **Clock feedthrough** shifts $Y$ voltage.
- **High-impedance output** is susceptible to noise and leakage.

---

#### **Solution for Part B(b) — 7 Marks**

**How Domino Logic Overcomes Cascading Issue (4 marks):**

Domino logic inserts a **static CMOS inverter** between the dynamic node $Y$ and the output $Z$. During precharge:
- $Y = V_{DD}$
- $Z = 0$ V (after inverter)

Since $Z$ drives the next stage's NMOS gates, the next stage's NMOS network remains **OFF** during precharge. Therefore, the next stage's dynamic node **retains its precharged $V_{DD}$** safely.

During evaluation, the inverter produces a **monotonically rising** output (only 0 → 1), which can safely trigger the next stage.

**Role of Keeper Transistor (3 marks):**

The keeper is a **weak PMOS transistor** added in parallel with $M_p$. Its gate is tied to the output $Z$ (after the inverter):

- When $Z = 0$ (no evaluation occurred), keeper is ON → continuously refreshes $Y$ back to $V_{DD}$, counteracting **leakage** and **charge sharing**.
- When $Z = 1$ (evaluation occurred), keeper is OFF → does not interfere with the active pull-down.

The keeper is sized to be much weaker than the pull-down network (typical ratio $K_r \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$) so that active evaluation can still dominate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> Students commonly lose marks in Domino logic questions for the following reasons:
>
> 1. **Confusing Domino with plain dynamic logic** — forgetting that Domino ALWAYS includes a static inverter at the output. Always draw and label the inverter.
> 2. **Missing the keeper transistor** — In modern VLSI designs and in KTU 2024 scheme questions on noise/leakage, examiners expect the keeper to be mentioned. Not mentioning it costs 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Writing "Domino is non-inverting" without justification** — The reason is monotonicity, not "just because." Explain WHY the inverter makes it non-inverting.
> 4. **Confusing precharge and evaluation conditions** — Remember: $M_p$ is ON when $CLK = 0$; $M_{ev}$ is ON when $CLK = 1$. Getting these wrong is fatal.
> 5. **Ignoring the clock feedthrough and charge-sharing equations** — Marks are reserved for analytical expressions like $\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot C_{par}/(C_L + C_{par})$.
> 6. **Using $|V_{Tp}|$ notation inconsistently** — Always write $\vert V_{Tp} \vert$ (with vertical bars for magnitude) in board answers.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention monotonicity** — This is the single most important property enabling cascading.
> 8. **Not labeling the dynamic node Y and final output Z distinctly** — They are different signals; conflating them loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams — Domino CMOS Logic**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Domino logic = dynamic CMOS stage + static CMOS inverter
- ☐ Name derived from cascading wave behavior (like falling dominoes)
- ☐ Single-phase clocked operation ($\phi$ and $\overline{\phi}$)
- ☐ Two phases: Precharge ($\phi = 0$) and Evaluation ($\phi = 1$)

### Structural Elements
- ☐ Clocked PMOS $M_p$ for precharge
- ☐ Clocked NMOS $M_{ev}$ (footer) for evaluation
- ☐ NMOS pull-down logic network (no PMOS logic network)
- ☐ Static CMOS inverter at output (always present)
- ☐ Optional: Keeper PMOS for charge preservation

### Operating Rules
- ☐ During **precharge**: dynamic node $Y = V_{DD}$, output $Z = 0$
- ☐ During **evaluation**: $Y$ stays $V_{DD}$ or discharges; $Z$ stays 0 or rises
- ☐ Output **monotonically rises** during evaluation (only 0 → 1)
- ☐ Cannot implement inverting functions directly

### Key Equations (Must Memorize)
- ☐ $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{clk}$
- ☐ $\Delta V_{charge\_share} = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_{par}}{C_L + C_{par}}$
- ☐ $t_{pd,eval} \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^{2}}$
- ☐ Transistor count for N-input gate: $N + 3$
- ☐ Activity factor: $\alpha_{domino} \geq 0.5$

### Advantages vs Limitations
- ☐ **Advantages:** Faster (~1.8×), smaller area, lower $C_{in}$, zero static power, cascadable
- ☐ **Limitations:** Charge sharing, clock feedthrough, non-inverting only, leakage in sub-100nm (needs keeper), high dynamic power

### Comparison Triggers
- ☐ Static CMOS: $2N$ transistors, inverting, no clock, robust
- ☐ Dynamic CMOS: $N+2$ transistors, inverting, clocked, not cascadable
- ☐ Domino CMOS: $N+3$ transistors, non-inverting, clocked, cascadable, monotonic output

### Engineering Applications
- ☐ Critical-path arithmetic units (ALUs, adders)
- ☐ High-speed decoders (SRAM, cache)
- ☐ Register file read/write paths
- ☐ Modern ASIC standard cell libraries

### Common Exam Pitfalls
- ☐ Don't forget the **inverter** — it's the defining feature
- ☐ Don't forget the **keeper** for noise/leakage robustness
- ☐ Don't claim "no clock needed" — Domino is clocked
- ☐ Don't confuse with pass-transistor logic (PTL) — different technique
- ☐ Always specify monotonicity when explaining cascading

### Recommended Board Drawing Order
1. Draw $V_{DD}$ rail, $M_p$ (gate = CLK), dynamic node $Y$
2. Draw NMOS logic network below $Y$
3. Draw $M_{ev}$ (gate = $\overline{CLK}$) at the bottom, connected to GND
4. Draw keeper PMOS in parallel with $M_p$ (gate = $Z$)
5. Draw static CMOS inverter from $Y$ to final output $Z$
6. Label all signals, nodes, and clock phases
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed and Power Dissipation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Speed and Power Dissipation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speed** in a CMOS digital gate refers to how quickly the output node can charge or discharge in response to a change at the input. It is quantitatively measured by **Propagation Delay (t_p)**, defined as the time between the 50% transition point of the input waveform and the corresponding 50% transition point of the output waveform.
>
> **Power Dissipation** is the rate at which a CMOS circuit consumes electrical energy during operation. The **Average Power Dissipation (P_avg)** is the time-averaged energy drawn from the supply per clock cycle.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic is mapped to:
- **CO2:** Design and analyze CMOS combinational and sequential logic circuits.
- **Bloom's Level:** Apply / Analyze (L3-L4)

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water-Tank Analogy:**
> - **Speed** is like how fast you can fill or empty a water tank through a pipe. A wider pipe (lower resistance, R) and a smaller tank (lower capacitance, C) make the process faster. **Delay ∝ R × C**.
> - **Power Dissipation** is like the water bill. Every time you open the tap (switching activity, α) and let water flow into the tank (charging/discharging C) at a higher pressure (supply voltage, V_DD), the more units of water (energy) you consume.
> - The **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** is the total water used per fill-cycle — engineers minimize this to design energy-efficient chips like those in your smartphone.

### 1.3 Core Performance Metrics (Industry Standard)

| Metric | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Propagation Delay (High-to-Low) | $t_{pHL}$ | Time for output to fall from 90% to 10% | seconds (s) |
| Propagation Delay (Low-to-High) | $t_{pLH}$ | Time for output to rise from 10% to 90% | seconds (s) |
| Average Propagation Delay | $t_p$ | Arithmetic mean of $t_{pHL}$ and $t_{pLH}$ | seconds (s) |
| Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn}$ | Power consumed during switching | Watts (W) |
| Static Power | $P_{static}$ | Power consumed even when idle (leakage) | Watts (W) |
| Power-Delay Product | $PDP$ | Energy per switching event | Joules (J) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Speed Analysis — The RC Delay Model

When a CMOS inverter switches, the output node, loaded with capacitance $C_L$, must be charged or discharged through the **ON-resistance ($R_{eq}$)** of the MOSFET. This is the classic **first-order RC network** approximation.

#### Key Analytical Steps:

1. **Identify the load capacitance** at the output node:
   $$C_L = C_{wire} + C_{next\_gate}$$
2. **Find the equivalent ON-resistance** of the PMOS/NMOS transistor:
   $$R_{eq} = \frac{1}{k_n \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$$
   (using the average-resistance approximation over the switching interval).
3. **Apply the RC charging/discharging equation.** The output voltage across a capacitor being charged through a resistor is:
   $$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$
4. **Compute the 50% delay** by setting $V_{out}(t) = 0.5 V_{DD}$:
   $$0.5 = 1 - e^{-t/RC} \implies t = RC \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.69 \, RC$$
5. Therefore, the standard propagation delay is:
   $$t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why "0.69"?** Because $\ln(2) = 0.693$. This is the **Elmore delay** constant for a single-pole RC network. KTU examiners frequently test this derivation — **memorize the 0.69 constant**.

#### High-to-Low and Low-to-High Delay (Asymmetric Inverter):

Because PMOS and NMOS have different mobilities ($\mu_p < \mu_n$), $R_p > R_n$. The high-to-low delay (NMOS discharging) is **faster** than the low-to-high delay (PMOS charging):

$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L$$

$$t_{pLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L$$

$$t_p = \frac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2}$$

### 2.2 Logical Effort — Scaling the Delay

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logical Effort (g)** is a dimensionless number that captures how much worse a gate is at driving current compared to a reference inverter. It allows **technology-independent** delay estimation.

The **stage delay (d)** for a logic gate is given by:

$$d = g \cdot h + p$$

where:
- $g$ = **logical effort** of the gate
- $h = C_{out}/C_{in}$ = **electrical effort (fanout)**
- $p$ = **parasitic delay** (intrinsic to the gate structure)

**Normalized delay form:**

$$d = f + p \quad \text{where} \quad f = g \cdot h = \frac{g \cdot C_{out}}{C_{in}}$$

**Key Inverter Reference Values (Logical Effort Table):**

| Gate Type | Logical Effort (g) | Parasitic Delay (p) |
|---|---|---|
| Inverter | 1 | 1 |
| 2-input NAND | 4/3 | 2 |
| 2-input NOR | 5/3 | 2 |
| XOR | 4 | 4 |
| 4-input NAND | 6/3 = 2 | 3 |

**Minimum Delay Condition** — the optimum number of stages $N$ in a buffer chain to drive a load $C_L$ from input capacitance $C_{in}$:

$$N = \ln\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in}}\right) / \ln(\gamma)$$

where $\gamma$ is the optimum tapering factor (size ratio between successive stages), typically $\gamma \approx 3.6$ for minimum delay (using self-logic-effort-based optimization: $\gamma = e \approx 2.718$ for a chain of inverters with $f = \text{opt}$).

The **minimum delay through a path** is:
$$D = N \cdot F_{inv} + P_{inv}$$
where $F_{inv}$ and $P_{inv}$ are the **optimal effort per stage** and **parasitic delay per stage** of an inverter (≈ 0.6 to 4 depending on technology).

### 2.3 Power Dissipation — Component Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theorem:** The total power dissipated by a CMOS gate has two fundamentally different origins — power consumed **doing work** (dynamic) and power **wasted as leakage** (static).

$$P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static}$$

$$P_{dynamic} = P_{switching} + P_{short-circuit}$$

$$P_{static} = P_{leakage}$$

#### (A) Switching Power (Dynamic Capacitive Power)

This is the dominant component in modern CMOS. Every time a gate output toggles $0 \rightarrow 1$, energy $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$ is drawn from the supply, and half is stored in the capacitor, half is dissipated as heat in the PMOS during charging.

**Average switching power:**

$$P_{switching} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = **switching activity factor** (probability of a $0 \rightarrow 1$ transition per clock cycle). Typical: $\alpha = 0.1$ to $0.3$.
- $C_L$ = load capacitance
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage
- $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency

> [!TIP]
> **Quadratic Voltage Dependence:** Halving $V_{DD}$ reduces dynamic power by **4×**. This is the single most exploited knob in low-power VLSI design (used in **DVFS — Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling** in mobile CPUs).

#### (B) Short-Circuit Power

When both PMOS and NMOS are simultaneously ON during the brief input transition, a direct current path exists from $V_{DD}$ to GND.

$$P_{short-circuit} = I_{mean} \cdot V_{DD}$$

For an inverter with input rise/fall time $t_r$, the short-circuit power is approximately:

$$P_{SC} = \frac{\beta}{12} (V_{DD} - 2V_T)^3 \cdot t_r \cdot f_{clk}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For matched rise/fall times and slow transitions, $P_{SC}$ can be 10–20% of $P_{switching}$. For step inputs ($t_r \to 0$), $P_{SC} \to 0$.

#### (C) Static (Leakage) Power

In modern deep-submicron CMOS (≤ 65 nm), leakage dominates the standby power:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leakage}$$

**Major leakage mechanisms:**

| Leakage Component | Symbol | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| Sub-threshold conduction | $I_{sub}$ | Weak inversion current when $V_{GS} < V_T$ |
| Reverse-biased junction leakage | $I_{rev}$ | Band-to-band tunneling at drain-body junction |
| Gate-oxide tunneling | $I_{gate}$ | Direct tunneling through thin SiO₂ |
| Punch-through | $I_{pt}$ | Drain field reaches source in short-channel devices |

**Sub-threshold leakage** (dominant in modern nodes):
$$I_{sub} = \mu C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_T^2 \exp\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_T}{n V_T}\right)\left[1 - \exp\left(\frac{-V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

> [!WARNING]
> In **nanometer CMOS**, the $V_{DD}$ scaling race has slowed because reducing it further increases delay exponentially (since $t_p \propto V_{DD}/(V_{DD}-V_T)^2$). This **"power wall"** is why multi-core processors replaced clock-frequency scaling after 2005.

### 2.4 Figure of Merits — Power-Delay Product (PDP) & Energy-Delay Product (EDP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PDP (Energy per operation):** A small PDP means the gate uses little energy per switching event.
> **EDP (Energy-quality product):** A small EDP means the gate is both fast *and* energy-efficient.

$$PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

(when $P_{avg} \approx P_{dynamic}$ and at unit switching activity)

$$EDP = PDP \cdot t_p = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot t_p$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ | First-order inverter delay |
| 2 | $d = g \cdot h + p$ | Stage delay (logical effort) |
| 3 | $F = \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ | Fanout / electrical effort |
| 4 | $N = \ln(C_L/C_{in}) / \ln(\gamma)$ | Optimum buffer chain stages |
| 5 | $P_{sw} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Switching power |
| 6 | $P_{sc} \propto (V_{DD} - 2V_T)^3 t_r f$ | Short-circuit power |
| 7 | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Leakage power |
| 8 | $PDP = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Energy per switching event |
| 9 | $EDP = PDP \cdot t_p$ | Quality metric |
| 10 | $E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Energy stored on a capacitor per transition |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The 0.69 RC Propagation Delay (Elmore Model)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU ESE — it appears almost every year in Part B.

**Setup:** Consider a CMOS inverter with ON-resistance $R$ charging a load capacitor $C_L$ from $0$ to $V_{DD}$. The output node voltage obeys the first-order differential equation:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Identify the 50% measurement point. The propagation delay is defined when $V_{out} = 0.5 \, V_{DD}$:

$$0.5 \, V_{DD} = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t_p / RC}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by $V_{DD}$:

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-t_p/RC}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$e^{-t_p/RC} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$

**Step 4:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\frac{t_p}{RC} = \ln(0.5)$$

**Step 5:** Use the identity $\ln(0.5) = -\ln(2)$:

$$-\frac{t_p}{RC} = -\ln(2) \implies \frac{t_p}{RC} = \ln(2)$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $t_p$:

$$t_p = RC \cdot \ln(2)$$

**Step 7:** Numerical evaluation:

$$\ln(2) = 0.693147... \quad \therefore \quad t_p \approx 0.69 \, R \, C$$

$$\boxed{t_p \approx 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, C_L}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Connection to Logical Effort:** When this is generalized using Logical Effort, the empirical factor of 0.69 is absorbed into the technology-dependent constant $\tau$ (intrinsic delay), giving $t_p = \tau(g \cdot h + p)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimum Buffer Chain (Number of Stages)

**Problem:** Design a buffer chain to drive a large load capacitance $C_L$ from a small input capacitance $C_{in}$ in **minimum delay**, where each inverter stage has the same size ratio $\gamma$ (tapering factor).

**Step 1:** Stage sizing. If each stage is $\gamma$ times larger than the previous:

$$C_{g,1} = C_{in}$$
$$C_{g,2} = \gamma \cdot C_{in}$$
$$C_{g,3} = \gamma^2 \cdot C_{in}$$
$$\vdots$$
$$C_{g,N} = \gamma^{N-1} C_{in} = C_L$$

**Step 2:** Express $\gamma$ in terms of $N$:

$$\gamma^{N-1} = \frac{C_L}{C_{in}}$$

**Step 3:** Take natural logarithm:

$$(N-1) \ln(\gamma) = \ln\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in}}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $N$:

$$N = 1 + \frac{\ln(C_L/C_{in})}{\ln(\gamma)}$$

**Step 5:** Per-stage delay (assuming a unit-sized inverter has delay $t_{p0}$):

$$t_{p,\text{stage}} = t_{p0}\left(1 + \frac{\gamma}{\gamma}\right) = \text{const}$$

**Step 6:** Total path delay:

$$t_{p,\text{total}} = N \cdot t_{p0} \cdot (\text{function of } \gamma)$$

**Step 7:** To minimize total delay, take $dt_p/d\gamma = 0$. The optimum taper for equal-stage delay is:

$$\boxed{\gamma_{opt} = e \approx 2.718}$$

**Step 8:** Substituting back into the formula for $N$:

$$\boxed{N_{opt} = \ln\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in}}\right) / \ln(e) = \ln\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in}}\right)}$$

**Step 9:** Minimum total delay for an $N$-stage buffer chain:

$$t_{p,\min} = N_{opt} \cdot t_{p0} \cdot (1 + 1/e) \cdot (\text{stage effort factor})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** If $C_L = 1000 \, C_{in}$, then $N_{opt} = \ln(1000) \approx 6.9$, so use **7 buffer stages** with each stage $\approx 2.72 \times$ larger than the previous.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Dynamic Switching Power (Energy per Cycle)

**Setup:** A CMOS gate output toggles once per clock cycle. Compute the energy drawn from $V_{DD}$.

**Step 1:** Energy stored on $C_L$ when charged to $V_{DD}$:

$$E_{stored} = \int_0^\infty V(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt = \int_0^\infty V \cdot C \frac{dV}{dt} \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $C \, dV = i \, dt$ and integrate from $0$ to $V_{DD}$:

$$E_{stored} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} C \cdot V \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 3:** Energy drawn from the supply during charging:

$$E_{supply} = \int_0^{T} V_{DD} \cdot i(t) \, dt = V_{DD} \int_0^{V_{DD}} C \, dV = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 4:** Energy dissipated in the PMOS during charging (the "lost" half):

$$E_{dissipated} = E_{supply} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 5:** During the next half-cycle, the capacitor discharges through the NMOS, dissipating the stored $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$ as heat. So **total energy per full cycle** (charge + discharge):

$$E_{cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 6:** If the gate switches with probability $\alpha$ per clock cycle at frequency $f$:

$$P_{sw} = \frac{E_{cycle}}{T_{cycle}} = \alpha \cdot f \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 7:** **Final form (the KTU-required equation):**

$$\boxed{P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why "energy recycled is not free":** Although the capacitor stores and releases energy, the *charge* is always supplied at full $V_{DD}$. Hence the **throughput current** from the supply is always $V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2$, half of which is dissipated as heat in the charging transistor.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Speed vs. Power Trade-offs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner **loves** asking "What is the effect on power/delay of halving $V_{DD}$ or doubling the size?" Use this table to revise.

| Action | Effect on $t_p$ | Effect on $P_{sw}$ | Effect on $P_{leak}$ | Effect on Area |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Halve $V_{DD}$** | $\uparrow$ (worsens) | $\downarrow$ **4×** | $\downarrow$ exponentially | None |
| **Double $W/L$** (size up) | $\downarrow$ (improves) | $\uparrow$ 2× (more $C_L$) | $\uparrow$ slightly | $\uparrow$ 2× |
| **Add buffer stage** | $\downarrow$ (optimal) | $\uparrow$ slightly | $\uparrow$ slightly | $\uparrow$ |
| **Reduce $\alpha$** (clock gating) | None | $\downarrow$ linearly | None | None |
| **Use lower $V_T$** | $\downarrow$ slightly | None | $\uparrow$ exponentially | None |
| **Multi-$V_{DD}$ design** | $\downarrow$ on critical | $\downarrow$ on non-critical | None | $\uparrow$ routing |
| **Power gating (sleep)** | None | None | $\downarrow$ 100× | $\uparrow$ (sleep transistors) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CMOS Inverter with Parasitic RC Network (Block Architecture)

This Mermaid diagram illustrates how the resistive transistor and capacitive load form the canonical RC delay model used in speed analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Vin"] --> B["NMOS Driver Stack"]
    B --> C["Drain Node with Parasitic Capacitance Cgd"]
    C --> D["Output Node Vout"]
    D --> E["Load Capacitance CL"]
    D --> F["PMOS Driver Stack"]
    F --> G["Supply VDD"]
    E --> H["Ground GND"]
    B -.discharges CL.-> H
    F -.charges CL.-> G
    C --> I["Effective ON Resistance Req"]
    I --> J["Propagation Delay tp equals 0.69 times Req times CL"]
```

**Interpretation:** The NMOS discharges $C_L$ through $R_n$ (during $0 \to 1$ input), while the PMOS charges $C_L$ through $R_p$ (during $1 \to 0$ input). The two paths have different ON-resistances, leading to asymmetric $t_{pLH}$ and $t_{pHL}$.

---

### 4.2 Total Power Dissipation Decomposition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Power P total"] --> B["Dynamic Power"]
    A --> C["Static Power"]
    B --> D["Switching Power"]
    B --> E["Short Circuit Power"]
    D --> D1["alpha times CL times VDD squared times f"]
    E --> E1["Beta over 12 times VDD minus 2VT cubed times tr times f"]
    C --> C1["Subthreshold Leakage I sub"]
    C --> C2["Gate Oxide Tunneling I gate"]
    C --> C3["Junction Leakage I rev"]
    C --> C4["Punchthrough I pt"]
    C1 --> F["Dominant in nanometer CMOS"]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> F
    D1 --> G["Dominant in older nodes above 180 nm"]
    E1 --> G
```

**Interpretation:** In **older technology nodes (≥ 180 nm)**, dynamic/switching power dominates. In **modern nanometer nodes (≤ 65 nm)**, static/leakage power can equal or exceed dynamic power — the **"power wall"** phenomenon.

---

### 4.3 Optimum Buffer Chain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Driver with input cap C in"] --> B["Stage 1 size gamma times C in"]
    B --> C["Stage 2 size gamma squared times C in"]
    C --> D["Stage 3 size gamma cubed times C in"]
    D --> E["Stage N size gamma to the N minus 1 times C in equals C L"]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333
    B -.each stage tapered.-> C
    C -.each stage tapered.-> D
    D -.each stage tapered.-> E
```

**Interpretation:** Each stage is $\gamma \approx 2.718$ times wider than the previous, producing a geometric progression of transistor sizes that minimizes total propagation delay through the chain. The final stage is large enough to drive $C_L$ with sub-nanosecond delay.

---

### 4.4 Logical Effort Delay Path (Stage-by-Stage Analysis)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Gate 1 Logical Effort g1 Parasitic p1"] --> B["Gate 2 Logical Effort g2 Parasitic p2"]
    B --> C["Gate 3 Logical Effort g3 Parasitic p3"]
    C --> D["Output Load C L"]
    A --> A1["Stage Delay d1 = g1 times h1 + p1"]
    B --> B1["Stage Delay d2 = g2 times h2 + p2"]
    C --> C1["Stage Delay d3 = g3 times h3 + p3"]
    D --> D1["Total Path Delay D = d1 + d2 + d3"]
    A1 --> D1
    B1 --> D1
    C1 --> D1
```

**Interpretation:** Each gate contributes a stage delay $d = gh + p$. The path delay is the sum. To minimize the total, the path effort $F = G \cdot H \cdot B$ is distributed such that each stage has the **same stage effort** $f = F^{1/N}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define propagation delay in a CMOS inverter. Derive the expression for the propagation delay of a CMOS inverter using the RC delay model.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The propagation delay $t_p$ of a CMOS inverter is the time difference between the **50% transition point of the input waveform** and the **50% transition point of the output waveform**.

**Derivation:** When the input of a CMOS inverter transitions, the output node (loaded by capacitance $C_L$) is charged or discharged through the ON-resistance $R_{eq}$ of the conducting MOSFET. The output voltage follows a first-order RC response:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t/(R_{eq} C_L)}\right)$$

Setting $V_{out} = 0.5 V_{DD}$ and solving:

$$0.5 = 1 - e^{-t_p/(R_{eq} C_L)}$$

$$e^{-t_p/(R_{eq} C_L)} = 0.5 \implies t_p = R_{eq} C_L \cdot \ln(2)$$

$$\boxed{t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L}$$

The **0.69 factor** arises from $\ln(2) = 0.693$ and is the canonical 50%-delay of a single-pole RC network. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
List the major sources of power dissipation in a CMOS circuit. Write the expression for dynamic switching power.

**Model Answer:**

The major sources of power dissipation in CMOS are:

1. **Dynamic Switching Power ($P_{sw}$):** Power consumed to charge and discharge load capacitances. (Dominant in older nodes)
2. **Short-Circuit Power ($P_{SC}$):** Direct current from $V_{DD}$ to GND when both PMOS and NMOS are momentarily ON during input transition.
3. **Static Leakage Power ($P_{leak}$):** Power consumed even when the circuit is idle due to:
   - Sub-threshold conduction
   - Gate-oxide tunneling
   - Reverse-biased junction leakage

**Expression for dynamic switching power:**

$$\boxed{P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}}$$

where $\alpha$ is the switching activity factor, $C_L$ is the load capacitance, $V_{DD}$ is the supply voltage, and $f_{clk}$ is the clock frequency. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the different components of power dissipation in CMOS circuits. Compare the dominant power component in 180 nm and 28 nm technology nodes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance of 50 fF. The ON-resistance of the NMOS is 1 kΩ and that of the PMOS is 2 kΩ. If the supply voltage is 1.8 V, compute:
   (i) $t_{pHL}$ and $t_{pLH}$
   (ii) Average propagation delay
   (iii) Energy dissipated per switching cycle
   (iv) Average dynamic power at clock frequency 500 MHz and switching activity $\alpha = 0.2$ **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Power Dissipation Components in CMOS:**

**1. Dynamic Power** — Power consumed during switching activity:
   - **Switching Power:** $P_{sw} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$
   - **Short-Circuit Power:** $P_{SC} = \frac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_T)^3 t_r f$

**2. Static Power** — Power consumed when the circuit is idle:
   - **Sub-threshold Leakage:** $I_{sub} \propto e^{(V_{GS}-V_T)/nV_T}$
   - **Gate-oxide Tunneling:** Direct tunneling through ultra-thin SiO₂
   - **Junction Leakage:** Reverse-biased pn-junction currents

**Technology Comparison:**

| Aspect | 180 nm Node | 28 nm Node |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{DD}$ (typical) | 1.8 V | 0.9 V |
| Dominant Power | **Switching Power** (~80%) | **Leakage Power** (~50%) |
| $V_T$ scaling | Moderate | Near-threshold |
| Leakage current | Negligible | Significant |
| Power optimization focus | Reduce $\alpha$, $C_L$, $V_{DD}$ | Multi-$V_T$, power gating |

At 180 nm, **dynamic switching power dominates** because leakage currents are minimal due to thicker gate oxide and higher $V_T$. At 28 nm, the **gate oxide is so thin (~1 nm)** that tunneling leakage becomes comparable to switching power, and **sub-threshold leakage** also grows because $V_T$ has been scaled close to the sub-threshold slope limit. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $C_L = 50 \, fF = 50 \times 10^{-15} \, F$
- $R_n = 1 \, k\Omega = 10^3 \, \Omega$ (NMOS ON-resistance)
- $R_p = 2 \, k\Omega = 2 \times 10^3 \, \Omega$ (PMOS ON-resistance)
- $V_{DD} = 1.8 \, V$
- $f_{clk} = 500 \, MHz = 5 \times 10^8 \, Hz$
- $\alpha = 0.2$

**(i) Computation of $t_{pHL}$ and $t_{pLH}$:**

For the **high-to-low transition**, the NMOS discharges $C_L$ through $R_n$:

$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L = 0.69 \times 10^3 \times 50 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \times 50 \times 10^{-12} = 34.5 \, ps$$

For the **low-to-high transition**, the PMOS charges $C_L$ through $R_p$:

$$t_{pLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L = 0.69 \times 2 \times 10^3 \times 50 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$t_{pLH} = 0.69 \times 100 \times 10^{-12} = 69 \, ps$$

**(ii) Average propagation delay:**

$$t_p = \frac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2} = \frac{34.5 + 69}{2} = \frac{103.5}{2} = 51.75 \, ps$$

**(iii) Energy dissipated per switching cycle:**

The energy dissipated per full transition (charge + discharge) is:

$$E_{cycle} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.8)^2$$

$$E_{cycle} = 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.24 = 162 \times 10^{-15} \, J = 162 \, fJ$$

(Equivalent to $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2 = 81 \, fJ$ per half-cycle.)

**(iv) Average dynamic power:**

$$P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.8)^2 \times 5 \times 10^8$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.24 \times 5 \times 10^8$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 3.24 \times 5 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.2 \times 810 \times 10^{-7} = 162 \times 10^{-7} = 16.2 \, \mu W$$

**Summary of results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $t_{pHL}$ | 34.5 ps |
| $t_{pLH}$ | 69 ps |
| $t_p$ | 51.75 ps |
| $E_{cycle}$ | 162 fJ |
| $P_{sw}$ | 16.2 μW |

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Logical Effort**. How is it used to determine the minimum delay of a multi-stage logic path? Derive the condition for the optimum number of buffer stages to drive a large load. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stage buffer chain drives a load capacitance of 8 pF. Each inverter has an input capacitance of 10 fF and an intrinsic output capacitance of 10 fF. The effective resistance of a unit inverter is 1 kΩ.
   (i) Calculate the optimum size ratio $\gamma$ if the delay is to be minimized.
   (ii) Compute the total propagation delay of the chain.
   (iii) If a single inverter of equivalent drive is used instead, compare the delays and comment. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Logical Effort — Definition:**

Logical Effort is a **dimensionless** methodology to estimate and optimize the delay of CMOS logic gates in a **technology-independent** manner. The **stage delay** is given by:

$$d = g \cdot h + p$$

where $g$ is the logical effort (relative current-driving capability), $h = C_{out}/C_{in}$ is the electrical effort (fanout), and $p$ is the parasitic delay (intrinsic capacitance normalized to the input capacitance of an inverter).

**Reference:** An **inverter** has $g = 1$ and $p = 1$ (definition). A NAND gate has $g = 4/3$, a NOR has $g = 5/3$.

**Minimum Delay of a Multi-Stage Path:**

The total path delay is:

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(g_i h_i + p_i) = \sum g_i h_i + P_{path}$$

Using the **AM-GM inequality** (or by setting all stage efforts equal: $g_i h_i = f$ for all $i$):

$$D_{min} = N \cdot (G \cdot B \cdot H)^{1/N} + P_{path}$$

where $G = \prod g_i$, $B$ is the branching effort, $H = C_{out}/C_{in}$.

The **optimum stage effort** is $f_{opt} \approx 4$ for a typical 180 nm process (more precisely $f_{opt} = \rho + \sqrt{\rho^2 + 1}$ where $\rho$ is the self-load factor).

**Derivation of Optimum Buffer Stages:**

Consider a chain of $N$ inverters with tapering factor $\gamma$ (each stage $\gamma$× larger). Total load driven:

$$C_L = C_{in} \cdot \gamma^{N-1}$$

$$\gamma^{N-1} = \frac{C_L}{C_{in}} \implies (N-1) \ln \gamma = \ln\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{N_{opt} = \ln\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in}}\right) / \ln(\gamma)}$$

For minimum delay, each stage must have equal effort. With self-logic-effort $\rho = 1$ for an inverter:

$$\gamma_{opt} = e \approx 2.718$$

Therefore:

$$N_{opt} = \ln\left(\frac{C_L}{C_{in}}\right)$$

**Proof of $\gamma_{opt} = e$:** Total path delay $D(\gamma) = N \cdot \tau \cdot (1 + \gamma)$. Substituting $N = 1 + \ln(C_L/C_{in})/\ln \gamma$ and setting $dD/d\gamma = 0$ yields $\gamma = e$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $C_L = 8 \, pF = 8 \times 10^{-12} \, F$
- $C_{in} = 10 \, fF = 10 \times 10^{-15} \, F$ (per inverter input)
- Intrinsic output cap $C_{out,int} = 10 \, fF$
- $R_{eq} = 1 \, k\Omega$ (unit inverter)

**(i) Optimum size ratio $\gamma$:**

For a chain of identical inverters (logical effort $g = 1$, parasitic $p = 1$):

$$\gamma_{opt} = e \approx 2.718$$

(Rounded to a practical value: $\gamma \approx 3$.)

**(ii) Total propagation delay of the 4-stage chain:**

The number of stages is given as $N = 4$. Compute the actual $\gamma$ implied by the load:

$$\gamma^{N-1} = \frac{C_L}{C_{in}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-12}}{10 \times 10^{-15}} = 800$$

$$\gamma = 800^{1/3} = 9.28$$

The per-stage delay (using the standard inverter model) is:

$$t_{p,\text{stage}} = 0.69 \cdot R_i \cdot (C_{int} + C_{next,in})$$

Stage 1: input cap = 10 fF, drives stage 2 input cap = $\gamma \cdot 10 \, fF = 92.8 \, fF$:

$$t_{p1} = 0.69 \times 1 \, k\Omega \times (10 + 92.8) \, fF = 0.69 \times 102.8 \times 10^{-12} = 70.93 \, ps$$

Stage 2: input cap = 92.8 fF, drives 861.2 fF:

$$t_{p2} = 0.69 \times 1 \, k\Omega \times (92.8 + 861.2) \, fF = 0.69 \times 954 \, ps = 658.3 \, ps$$

Stage 3: drives 7988 fF:

$$t_{p3} = 0.69 \times 1 \, k\Omega \times (861.2 + 7988) \, fF = 0.69 \times 8849.2 \, ps = 6106 \, ps$$

Stage 4: drives 8000 fF (the load):

$$t_{p4} = 0.69 \times 1 \, k\Omega \times (7988 + 8000) \, fF = 0.69 \times 15988 \, ps = 11031.7 \, ps$$

**Total:**

$$D_{chain} = 70.93 + 658.3 + 6106 + 11031.7 \approx 17.87 \, ns$$

**(iii) Comparison with a single equivalent inverter:**

A single inverter must have $W/L$ scaled to drive the full load. Its input cap becomes $C_{in,eq} = C_L = 8 \, pF$. The propagation delay is:

$$t_{p,single} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq,small} \cdot C_L \cdot (\text{scaling})$$

If we scale a unit inverter by $\gamma_{eq} = C_L/C_{in} = 800$, then $R_{eq,new} = R_{eq}/800$. Delay:

$$t_{p,single} = 0.69 \times \frac{1000}{800} \times 8 \times 10^{-12} = 0.69 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-12} = 6.9 \, ns$$

**Comment:** The 4-stage buffer chain has a larger delay (~17.87 ns) for this non-optimal $\gamma$ choice. For the chain to outperform the single inverter, $\gamma$ must be closer to $e \approx 2.7$ and the number of stages should be $N_{opt} = \ln(800) \approx 6.7 \to 7$ stages. The principle of **staging** is most effective when $\gamma_{opt} \approx 3$ and $N_{opt}$ is chosen correctly. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.69 factor:** Many students write $t_p = RC$ instead of $0.69 RC$. Always include the $\ln(2) = 0.69$ multiplier — it is a **3-mark penalty** item in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Confusing $t_{pHL}$ and $t_{pLH}$:** Remember — $t_{pHL}$ uses the **NMOS** resistance (NMOS pulls down), and $t_{pLH}$ uses the **PMOS** resistance (PMOS pulls up). Mixing them up loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Using $\alpha$ wrongly in power formula:** $\alpha$ is the **switching activity factor** (probability of a $0 \to 1$ transition per cycle), **not** the clock duty cycle. If not mentioned in the question, assume $\alpha = 1$ for a worst-case estimate.
> 4. **Ignoring units:** $C_L$ in fF and $f$ in MHz must be converted to SI (Farads and Hz) before computing power in Watts. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for unit inconsistency.
> 5. **Mixing $P_{sw}$ with $E_{cycle}$:** $P_{sw}$ is power (W), $E_{cycle}$ is energy (J). Power = Energy × frequency × activity. Do not interchange them.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Speed** is governed by the **RC time constant** of the output node: $t_p = 0.69 R_{eq} C_L$.
- The **0.69** factor is $\ln(2)$ — derived from the 50% measurement point of the RC step response.
- **$t_{pHL}$** uses **NMOS** ON-resistance; **$t_{pLH}$** uses **PMOS** ON-resistance. PMOS is ~2× slower due to lower hole mobility.
- **Logical Effort** normalizes delay: $d = g \cdot h + p$, with inverter as reference ($g = 1, p = 1$).
- **Optimum buffer chain** has $\gamma_{opt} = e \approx 2.718$ and $N_{opt} = \ln(C_L/C_{in})$.
- **Total Power** = $P_{dynamic} + P_{static}$. In old nodes, $P_{sw}$ dominates. In nanometer nodes, $P_{leak}$ dominates.
- **Dynamic Switching Power** = $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — the **single most tested equation** in KTU ESE.
- **Energy per cycle** = $C_L V_{DD}^2$. Half is stored, half is dissipated as heat in the charging transistor.
- **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** = energy per operation, a key figure of merit. $PDP = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2$.
- **EDP (Energy-Delay Product)** = $PDP \times t_p$ — used to compare technologies holistically.
- **Short-circuit power** is non-zero only when the input rise/fall time is finite; for step inputs, $P_{SC} = 0$.
- **Sub-threshold leakage** grows exponentially as $V_T$ is reduced — the fundamental limit to $V_{DD}$ scaling.
- **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage-Frequency Scaling)** exploits the $V_{DD}^2$ dependence to reduce power in mobile/embedded chips.
- **Power gating, clock gating, multi-$V_T$, and multi-$V_{DD}$** are the four industry-standard low-power techniques.
- For a CMOS gate, the **load capacitance** $C_L$ includes the gate capacitance of the next stage, drain diffusion capacitance, and interconnect capacitance.
- In **deep submicron (< 90 nm)** designs, the **power wall** is the primary constraint — multi-core architectures replaced frequency scaling after 2005.
- The **Elmore delay** model extends the single-RC result to RC ladder networks: $t_D = \sum_i R_i C_i$ (sum of partial RC products).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Integrity Issues in Dynamic Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Signal Integrity Issues in Dynamic Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Dynamic CMOS Logic: The Foundational Concept

**Dynamic Logic** is a digital CMOS logic family in which a result is evaluated and stored temporarily on a high-impedance parasitic capacitance at an internal **dynamic (floating) node**, rather than via a static pull-up/pull-down network. Operation is governed by a periodic **clock (CLK/φ)** that alternates between two distinct phases: a **Precharge Phase (φ = 0)** and an **Evaluate Phase (φ = 1)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> In dynamic logic, the output is stored on the parasitic gate capacitance of subsequent stages (or an explicit load capacitor) instead of being driven continuously by a pull-up network. This single-transistor pull-up (PMOS) and NMOS pull-down network makes dynamic logic **2× faster** and uses **2× fewer transistors** than equivalent static CMOS, but it sacrifices **noise immunity, robustness, and rail-to-rail drive strength**.

The intermediate storage node is called the **dynamic node** (or *floating node*, *evaluate node*, or *τ-node*). Its voltage is **not** held by a low-resistance path to either V_DD or GND during most of the clock cycle — making it acutely vulnerable to external disturbances.

## 1.2 What is "Signal Integrity" in This Context?

> [!NOTE]
> **Signal Integrity (SI)** in dynamic logic refers to the ability of the dynamic node voltage to *maintain its intended logic level* despite unwanted electrical disturbances such as leakage currents, capacitive coupling from neighboring wires, charge redistribution with internal parasitic nodes, and clock feedthrough.

When SI is violated, the dynamic node:
- **Falsely discharges** during evaluate → logic error (0 instead of 1)
- **Falsely charges** during precharge → logic error (1 instead of 0)
- Causes **reduced noise margin** in the cascaded static gate
- Leads to a **dynamic ** **logic failure** that is extremely difficult to debug because it is *data-dependent, timing-dependent, and PVT-sensitive*.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bucket of Water" Model

Imagine a **leaky wooden bucket** placed on a wobbly table:
- The **bucket** = the dynamic node capacitance (C_L).
- The **water level** in the bucket = the stored voltage (V_out).
- The **wooden leak** = subthreshold/leakage current discharging C_L.
- The **wobbly table** = the capacitive coupling from adjacent wires.
- The **other buckets sharing the same water pipe** = internal parasitic capacitances (C_PD) that share charge with the main bucket.
- You (the circuit) **fill the bucket only once** (precharge) and **tip it over only when needed** (evaluate). If the table shakes at the wrong moment or the bucket is half-empty, you spill at the wrong time.

> This is exactly what happens in dynamic CMOS: a node that is supposed to "hold" its charge passively is in reality a victim of *charge leakage, charge sharing, and noise coupling* — all of which are the **Signal Integrity Issues** we must engineer around.

## 1.4 The Core Dynamic Logic Circuit (Reference)

The canonical **n-type dynamic (domino) logic** gate is shown structurally as:

| Phase | Clock φ | PMOS (M_p) | NMOS Network (M_n) | Dynamic Node X | Output Y (inverter) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Precharge | 0 | ON (conducting) | OFF (blocked) | Pulled to V_DD | Inverter output = 0 |
| Evaluate | 1 | OFF (isolated) | Active per inputs | Either V_DD (held) or GND (discharged) | Valid logic level |

The **dynamic node X** is the SI-critical node. Every SI issue below attacks this node X (or its output Y through cascading).

## 1.5 High-Yield Classification of SI Issues (KTU Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to enumerate, identify the root cause, and propose mitigation for the following **five canonical SI issues**:
> 1. **Charge Sharing (Charge Redistribution)**
> 2. **Capacitive Coupling / Crosstalk Noise**
> 3. **Leakage-Induced Voltage Degradation** (Subthreshold + Gate Tunneling)
> 4. **Clock Feedthrough (Clock Injection)**
> 5. **Supply Bounce / Ground Bounce (Power Rail Noise)**
> 
> Secondary but commonly examined: **Back-gate (Body) coupling**, **Minority carrier charge injection**, **Hot-carrier effects**, and **Ratioed logic dependency**.

Each issue is dissected in the next sections with formulas, derivations, diagrams, and KTU-style questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Issue #1: Charge Sharing (Charge Redistribution)

### 2.1.1 Mechanism (The "Why")

During the **evaluate phase**, the PMOS pull-up is OFF and the dynamic node X is **floating** at V_DD. If the NMOS pull-down network is also OFF (no input combination turns it ON), node X should remain at V_DD. However, the **internal nodes within the NMOS pull-down network** have **parasitic capacitances (C_PD)** to ground.

When the inputs now switch ON only the *upper* part of the NMOS network, a **new conductive path from X to ground through C_PD** is formed. Charge originally stored on C_L (at X) is **redistributed** with the previously uncharged C_PD. The result: **X drops from V_DD to a smaller value**, potentially below the switching threshold of the following inverter → **functional failure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Two capacitors in parallel share charge. The big one (C_L) loses voltage to the small one (C_PD) until they reach a common voltage. If C_PD is too big, V_X dips dangerously low.

### 2.1.2 Analytical Steps

1. **Precharge phase** ends → V_X = V_DD, all internal C_PD nodes = 0 V (they were discharged in previous cycles or are at known states).
2. **Evaluate phase** begins. Selected NMOS transistors turn ON, creating a conductive bridge between X and ground *via* C_PD.
3. Charge conservation (no path to V_DD since PMOS is OFF, no DC path to ground since final transistor is OFF) gives:

   $$Q_{\text{before}} = Q_{\text{after}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_L V_{DD} = (C_L + C_{PD}) V_{X,\text{final}}$$

4. Solving:

   $$V_{X,\text{final}} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_{PD}}$$

5. **Failure condition** (for a standard inverter with switching threshold V_M ≈ V_DD/2):

   $$V_{X,\text{final}} < V_M \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{logic error}$$

### 2.1.3 Engineering Mitigation Techniques

| Technique | Working Principle | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Precharge internal nodes** | Add weak PMOS to precharge C_PD during φ=0 | Extra area, slight loading |
| **Keeper (bleeder) transistor** | Weak PMOS M_kp holds X near V_DD during evaluate | Contention current, slows evaluation |
| **Increase C_L** | Larger fan-out or explicit cap on X | Slower gate, more area |
| **Use np-CMOS (dual-clock)** | Precharge *all* internal nodes via auxiliary clock | More complex clocking |
| **Reduce C_PD** | Lay out smaller drain diffusions, share nodes | Limited by logic structure |

---

## 2.2 Issue #2: Capacitive Coupling (Crosstalk Noise)

### 2.2.1 Mechanism

Adjacent interconnect wires (or gate-to-drain overlap) have a **mutual (coupling) capacitance C_c**. When a neighboring "aggressor" wire switches with a large dV/dt, it injects a **displacement current** into the dynamic node X:

$$i_c = C_c \cdot \frac{dV_{\text{aggressor}}}{dt}$$

This current flows through the impedance of node X, causing a voltage bounce:

$$\Delta V_X = \frac{C_c}{C_c + C_{\text{node}}} \cdot \Delta V_{\text{aggressor}}$$

Because X is a **high-impedance node** in evaluate phase, the resulting ΔV_X can be catastrophic (full rail-to-rail bounce on X is possible when C_c ≫ C_node).

### 2.2.2 Failure Modes

- **Coupling-up (positive glitch)** on X: Can falsely turn ON the following NMOS pull-down of next stage → cascading error.
- **Coupling-down (negative glitch)** on X: Can drop V_X below V_M → reads as logic 0 erroneously.

### 2.2.3 Mitigation

- **Shield wires** (route V_DD or GND lines between critical dynamic signals).
- **Wider spacing** between aggressor and victim → reduce C_c.
- **Differential / twisted routing** for clocks and dynamic buses.
- **Keeper transistor** to absorb the injected charge.

---

## 2.3 Issue #3: Leakage-Induced Voltage Degradation

### 2.3.1 Subthreshold Leakage

Even when V_GS = 0, a weak current flows:

$$I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th} + \eta V_{DS}}{n V_T}} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}}\right)$$

For V_DS ≫ V_T (deep submicron):

$$I_{sub} \approx I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}}$$

Where **V_T = kT/q ≈ 26 mV at 300 K** (bold thermal voltage), **n ≈ 1.3–1.5** (subthreshold swing factor), **I_0** is the zero-bias current, and **η** is the DIBL coefficient.

For a dynamic node held at V_DD during a long evaluate phase, **all** the OFF NMOS transistors in the pull-down network leak in parallel, slowly discharging C_L:

$$V_X(t) = V_{DD} - \frac{I_{\text{leak,total}} \cdot t}{C_L}$$

The maximum tolerable evaluate time is:

$$t_{\text{max}} = \frac{C_L \cdot (V_{DD} - V_M)}{I_{\text{leak,total}}}$$

### 2.3.2 Gate Oxide Tunneling Leakage

For t_ox < 2 nm (45 nm and below), direct quantum-mechanical tunneling through the gate oxide produces a current density:

$$J_{gate} \propto e^{-\alpha \cdot t_{ox}} \quad \text{where } \alpha \approx 10^{10} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

Gate leakage **discharges the dynamic node** even if all source/drain terminals are grounded. Mitigated by:
- **High-k gate dielectrics** (HfO₂, HfSiON) — increases physical thickness, reduces tunneling.
- **Multi-V_t design** — high-V_t devices in non-critical paths.

### 2.3.3 Reverse-Bias Junction Leakage

Drain-to-body junctions in OFF transistors contribute:

$$I_{jun} = A \cdot J_s \left(e^{V_{diode}/V_T} - 1\right)$$

For V_diode < 0, this is small but non-zero, especially with **halo-doped, retrograde wells** in modern processes.

---

## 2.4 Issue #4: Clock Feedthrough (Clock Injection)

### 2.4.1 Mechanism

The gate-to-drain overlap capacitance of the **precharge PMOS** (C_GD,p) and **evaluate NMOS** (C_GD,n) couples the clock transition directly into node X. When CLK falls (precharge ends), the negative dV/dt on the gate couples through C_GD,p to X, **dropping V_X by**:

$$\Delta V_X = -C_{GD,p} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{C_{GD,p} + C_L}$$

A similar (but positive) feedthrough occurs at the evaluate edge via the evaluate NMOS.

### 2.4.2 Why It Matters in Modern Nodes

With C_L shrinking (sub-fF) and clock edges becoming faster (< 50 ps), the feedthrough fraction (C_GD,p / (C_GD,p + C_L)) can exceed 30%, directly corrupting logic state.

### 2.4.3 Mitigation

- **Minimum-size clock transistors** where possible.
- **Clock buffering / decoupling** to slow the edge rate intentionally.
- **Differential signaling** or **complementary clocks**.
- **Use np-CMOS** (precharge internal nodes with delayed clock) to cancel feedthrough by superposition.

---

## 2.5 Issue #5: Power Supply Noise (Ground/V_DD Bounce)

The dynamic gate draws a **large transient current** at the evaluate edge (especially when many outputs switch simultaneously). With package/wire bond inductance L_pkg, this causes:

$$V_{\text{bounce}} = L_{pkg} \cdot \frac{dI}{dt}$$

The noise on V_DD and GND rails:
- Reduces effective V_GS of transistors → slower switching.
- Modulates V_th via the **body effect**:

$$V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right)$$

- Causes **secondary SI failures** in nearby dynamic nodes by varying their stored voltage.

Mitigation: **Decoupling capacitors**, **multiple V_DD/GND pins**, **on-chip bypass caps**, **clock gating to reduce simultaneous switching**.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for KTU ESE / Internal Assessment — every row is a guaranteed 2-3 mark question on this topic.**

| # | Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Failure Condition | Engineering Constants (Bold) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Charge Sharing | $V_{X,f} = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_L}{C_L + C_{PD}}$ | $V_{X,f} < V_M \approx \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$ | **V_DD** typically 0.7–1.2 V |
| 2 | Capacitive Coupling | $\Delta V_X = \dfrac{C_c}{C_c + C_{\text{node}}} \cdot \Delta V_{agg}$ | $\Delta V_X > V_M - V_{X,\text{nominal}}$ | **C_c** scales with wire length, inverse of spacing |
| 3 | Subthreshold Leakage | $I_{sub} \approx I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-V_{th}}{n V_T}\right)$ | $V_X(t) = V_{DD} - \dfrac{I_{leak} t}{C_L} < V_M$ | **V_T ≈ 26 mV (300 K)**, **n ≈ 1.3–1.5** |
| 4 | Gate Tunneling | $J_{ox} \propto e^{-\alpha t_{ox}}$ | Voltage drops as $J \cdot t / C_{ox}$ | **α ≈ 10¹⁰ m⁻¹** |
| 5 | Clock Feedthrough | $\Delta V_X = -\dfrac{C_{GD}}{C_{GD} + C_L} V_{clk,swing}$ | $\vert \Delta V_X \vert > V_{DD} - V_M$ | C_GD set by overlap & LDD |
| 6 | Supply Bounce | $V_{bounce} = L_{pkg} \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dt}$ | $V_{bounce} > 0.1 V_{DD}$ | **L_pkg ≈ 1–10 nH** |
| 7 | Body Effect (V_th modulation) | $V_{th} = V_{th0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right)$ | Increased V_th slows discharge | **γ ≈ 0.3–0.7 V^½**, **φ_F ≈ -0.3 to -0.4 V** |
| 8 | Maximum Evaluate Time | $t_{max} = \dfrac{C_L (V_{DD} - V_M)}{I_{leak}}$ | Clock period must exceed t_max | Drives minimum clock frequency |
| 9 | Noise Margin | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}, \quad NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Sum should exceed ~0.6 V_DD for robust logic | Static ratio > 1.4 typically |
| 10 | Logical Effort (for sizing) | $g = \dfrac{C_{in,gate}}{3 C_{inv}}$ | Used to size keeper to avoid contention | **C_inv = 3C_g** per fan-out-1 inverter |

> **Note on notation:** All absolute-value bars in formulas use the **\vert** rendering (e.g., $\vert 2\phi_F \vert$) to remain compatible with the markdown table structure above.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Signal integrity issues in dynamic logic are not academic — they dictate:
- **Why modern CPUs use mostly static CMOS** at the register-transfer level and reserve dynamic logic for *performance-critical* paths (e.g., **Domino in ALUs, sense amplifiers in SRAM, dynamic OR-arrays in PLAs, register files, high-speed adders**).
- **Why the 2 GHz clock of your laptop would be impossible** without careful management of charge sharing, leakage, and coupling in dynamic blocks.
- **The trade-off frontier:** dynamic logic trades **robustness for speed**. Every KTU problem in this area asks you to balance these.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1: Charge Sharing Voltage Drop

> **Problem setup (typical KTU Part B):** A 4-input dynamic NAND gate has dynamic node capacitance C_L = 50 fF. The internal parasitic capacitance of the worst-case pull-down path is C_PD = 30 fF. V_DD = 1.0 V. Inverter switching threshold V_M = 0.45 V. Determine if charge sharing causes functional failure.

**Step 1 — Identify the dynamic node voltage before sharing.**

During precharge, the PMOS pull-up drives the dynamic node X fully to V_DD.

$$V_{X}(0^-) = V_{DD} = 1.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the voltage on the parasitic node before sharing.**

The parasitic capacitance C_PD is connected (through conducting NMOS transistors of the upper sub-network) to node X, but its *other* terminal is at ground (through the OFF lower NMOS). Hence:

$$V_{C_{PD}}(0^-) = 0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Apply charge conservation.**

When the upper NMOS transistors turn ON, a conductive path connects X and C_PD. There is **no** path to V_DD (PMOS is OFF) and **no** path to ground through the final stage (lower NMOS is OFF for this input combination). Therefore, the total charge on the isolated X + C_PD island is conserved.

$$Q_{\text{before}} = C_L \cdot V_X(0^-) + C_{PD} \cdot V_{C_{PD}}(0^-)$$

$$Q_{\text{before}} = (50 \text{ fF})(1.0 \text{ V}) + (30 \text{ fF})(0 \text{ V}) = 50 \text{ fC}$$

**Step 4 — Express the final voltage (single common-mode voltage V_f).**

After charge redistribution, both capacitors reach the same voltage V_f because they are now connected in parallel.

$$Q_{\text{after}} = (C_L + C_{PD}) \cdot V_f$$

**Step 5 — Equate and solve.**

$$(C_L + C_{PD}) \cdot V_f = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$$

$$V_f = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_{PD}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$V_f = 1.0 \text{ V} \cdot \frac{50 \text{ fF}}{50 \text{ fF} + 30 \text{ fF}} = 1.0 \cdot \frac{50}{80} = 0.625 \text{ V}$$

**Step 7 — Compare with switching threshold.**

$$V_f = 0.625 \text{ V} \quad \text{vs} \quad V_M = 0.45 \text{ V}$$

Since $V_f > V_M$, the inverter output **does not flip** — the gate is **functionally correct** for this case. (Margin = 0.625 − 0.45 = **0.175 V**.)

**Step 8 — Sensitivity check (for KTU full-marks).**

What if C_PD were 70 fF instead?

$$V_f = 1.0 \cdot \frac{50}{50 + 70} = 0.417 \text{ V} < V_M = 0.45 \text{ V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FAILURE}$$

This shows that **for the same logic, charge-sharing failure is conditional on layout** — a key KTU insight.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2: Maximum Evaluate Time from Leakage

> **Problem:** A dynamic gate with C_L = 20 fF holds V_X = 1.0 V. Total subthreshold leakage from all OFF NMOS devices in evaluate = 100 nA. V_M = 0.5 V. Find t_max.

**Step 1 — Identify the discharge equation.**

With C_L constant (no charge sharing in this case), the dynamic node discharges linearly at first order:

$$\frac{dV_X}{dt} = -\frac{I_{leak}}{C_L}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over time.**

$$V_X(t) = V_{DD} - \frac{I_{leak}}{C_L} \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Set the failure boundary.**

The node fails when V_X(t_max) = V_M:

$$V_M = V_{DD} - \frac{I_{leak} \cdot t_{max}}{C_L}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for t_max.**

$$t_{max} = \frac{C_L (V_{DD} - V_M)}{I_{leak}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute values.**

$$t_{max} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F} \times (1.0 - 0.5) \text{ V}}{100 \times 10^{-9} \text{ A}}$$

$$t_{max} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.5}{100 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-15}}{10^{-7}} = 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 100 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

A clock period of 1 µs (1 MHz) gives 10× margin. A clock period of 1 ns (1 GHz) gives 100× less margin → **leakage-induced failure is severe at low clock frequencies in nanometer CMOS** — a critical KTU insight.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation #3: Capacitive Coupling Glitch Magnitude

> **Problem:** A dynamic node X (C_L = 10 fF, C_GD,p = 1 fF) is adjacent to an aggressor wire (C_c = 5 fF) that switches 1.0 V in 100 ps. Find the worst-case glitch on X.

**Step 1 — Static-coupling (charge sharing) model.**

In the limit of fast clock edges, the coupling is governed by the capacitive divider:

$$\Delta V_X = \frac{C_c}{C_c + C_{\text{node}}} \cdot \Delta V_{agg}$$

**Step 2 — Identify C_node.**

C_node = C_L + (any other capacitance to ground at X). Assume C_node ≈ C_L = 10 fF.

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$\Delta V_X = \frac{5}{5 + 10} \cdot 1.0 \text{ V} = \frac{5}{15} = 0.333 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Failure analysis.**

If V_X was supposed to be 1.0 V during evaluate, a downward 0.333 V bounce brings it to 0.667 V. If V_M = 0.5 V, this **does not flip** the next stage. However, if V_M were 0.7 V (e.g., low-V_t inverter), it **would fail**. Hence **layout + device sizing must be co-designed**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| SI Issue | Primary Cause | Affected Phase | Observable Symptom | Primary Fix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Charge Sharing | Internal C_PD sharing charge with C_L | Evaluate | Voltage dip on X | Keeper / precharge internal nodes |
| Capacitive Coupling | C_c to adjacent switching wire | Evaluate (most) | Positive or negative glitch | Shielding, spacing |
| Subthreshold Leakage | V_GS < V_th but I_DS ≠ 0 | Evaluate (long) | Slow discharge of X | High-V_t, multi-V_t |
| Gate Tunneling | Thin t_ox (< 2 nm) | Both | Direct discharge of X | High-k dielectric |
| Clock Feedthrough | C_GD × dV_CLK/dt | Both (edges) | Offset on V_X | Smaller clock device, edge rate control |
| Supply Bounce | L_pkg × dI/dt | Switching | Body effect, V_th shift | Decoupling caps |
| Body (Back-gate) Coupling | V_SB variation | Evaluate | V_th modulation | Body ties, twin-tub process |
| Minority Carrier Injection | Forward-biased source-body | Switching | Latch-up, charge injection | Guard rings, separate wells |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Heuristic (for KTU 14-markers):** When asked to "improve robustness of a dynamic gate," the **canonical layered defense** is:
> 1. Precharge *all* internal nodes (np-CMOS or charge-sharing precharge PMOS).
> 2. Add a **keeper** sized ¼–⅒ of evaluate strength.
> 3. Use a **secondary footer** transistor at the bottom of the NMOS tree (allows skew-tolerant evaluation and reduces leakage).
> 4. Shield critical nets and increase C_L intentionally.
> 5. Use a **differential domino** structure (X and X_bar) for noise-immune comparison.
