<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between a latch and a flip-flop. Which is preferred for synchronous design and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **latch** is a level-sensitive memory element that is transparent when the enable signal is active; output follows input continuously. (1 mark)
- A **flip-flop** is an edge-triggered memory element that samples input only on the active clock edge (rising/falling). (1 mark)
- **Flip-flops are preferred** for synchronous design because they are **immune to transparent glitches** during the active clock level, ensuring deterministic behavior within a clock period. Latches may introduce hold-time violations and race conditions in pipelined datapaths. (1 mark)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "generate" and "propagate" signals in a Carry Lookahead Adder? Write their Boolean expressions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Generate ($G_i$):** A signal indicating that the $i^{th}$ stage *produces* a carry-out independent of $C_{in}$. $G_i = A_i \cdot B_i$ (1 mark)
- **Propagate ($P_i$):** A signal indicating that the $i^{th}$ stage *transmits* $C_{in}$ to $C_{out}$. $P_i = A_i \oplus B_i$ (1 mark)
- Carry out expression: $C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i \cdot C_i$. The CLA uses these signals to compute all carries in $\log N$ time instead of $N$ stages of an RCA. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design a 4-bit synchronous up-counter using JK flip-flops. Draw the state diagram, excitation table, and logic circuit. Explain the timing advantage over a ripple counter.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the carry equations for a 4-bit Carry Lookahead Adder. Compute the worst-case delay and compare it with a 4-bit Ripple Carry Adder. Given: $t_{FA} = 120$ ps, $t_{AND-OR} = 80$ ps.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: State Diagram** — States cycle through 0000 → 0001 → 0010 → ... → 1111 → 0000. (1 mark)

**Step 2: JK Excitation Table**

| $Q \to Q_{next}$ | $J$ | $K$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 → 0 | 0 | X |
| 0 → 1 | 1 | X |
| 1 → 0 | X | 1 |
| 1 → 1 | X | 0 |

**Step 3: K-map for $J_0, K_0, J_1, K_1, ...$** (2 marks)

- $J_0 = K_0 = 1$ (always toggle LSB)
- $J_1 = K_1 = Q_0$
- $J_2 = K_2 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1$
- $J_3 = K_3 = Q_0 \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2$

**Step 4: Circuit** — 4 JK flip-flops clocked by a common CLK; $J_i K_i$ inputs driven by the AND tree shown above. (1 mark)

**Step 5: Timing Advantage** — All flip-flops share the same clock; output delay = $t_{cq} + t_{AND} = 50 + 80 = 130$ ps, *independent* of N. In a ripple counter, the delay is $4 \times 130 = 520$ ps. (2 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Derive carry equations for a 4-bit CLA** (3 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= G_0 + P_0 C_0 \\
C_2 &= G_1 + P_1 G_0 + P_1 P_0 C_0 \\
C_3 &= G_2 + P_2 G_1 + P_2 P_1 G_0 + P_2 P_1 P_0 C_0 \\
C_4 &= G_3 + P_3 G_2 + P_3 P_2 G_1 + P_3 P_2 P_1 G_0 + P_3 P_2 P_1 P_0 C_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Delay Calculation** (2 marks)

- **RCA (4-bit):** $T_{RCA} = 4 \times t_{FA} = 4 \times 120 = 480$ ps.
- **CLA (4-bit):** $T_{CLA} = t_{AND-OR} + (N-1) t_{AND} = 80 + 3 \times 80 = 320$ ps. (Assumes 2-level logic.)
- **Speedup:** $480/320 = 1.5\times$ faster.

**Step 3: Critical Path in CLA:** longest gate chain is $C_0 \to P_0 P_1 P_2 P_3 \cdot C_0 \to C_4$ which is 3 AND levels + 1 OR = $4 \times 80 = 320$ ps. (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students incorrectly compute the CLA delay as $4 \times t_{AND-OR}$ = 320 ps by summing all 4 carry equations, but the *critical path* is the **longest single chain**, not the sum. Read the question carefully!

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure and operation of a Master-Slave D flip-flop using transmission gates. Draw the circuit and derive its characteristic equation. Why is it edge-triggered?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a 4-bit array multiplier. Show the partial product generation, summation network, and compute the worst-case delay. How does a Wallace tree multiplier improve this delay?

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Master-Slave Structure** (2 marks)
- Two gated D latches cascaded: **Master** (active when CLK=0) and **Slave** (active when CLK=1).
- They are **never transparent simultaneously**, so output Q is isolated from input D except for a brief moment around the clock edge.

**Step 2: Transmission Gate CMOS Circuit** (2 marks)
- Each latch is implemented as a CMOS transmission gate (parallel NMOS + PMOS) with an inverter for feedback.
- Master uses CLK to control its TG; Slave uses $\bar{CLK}$ for its TG, ensuring complementary operation.

**Step 3: Characteristic Equation** (1 mark)

$$
Q_{next} = D \quad \text{on rising edge of CLK}
$$

**Step 4: Edge-Triggered Explanation** (2 marks)
- On CLK rising edge: Master becomes opaque (holds D), Slave becomes transparent (passes Q to output). Within $\Delta t$ of the edge, Q = D sampled just before the edge. This defines "edge-triggered" behavior.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: 4-bit Array Multiplier Partial Products** (2 marks)

For $A = a_3 a_2 a_1 a_0$ and $B = b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$, four partial product rows are generated:

| Row | Partial Product |
|---|---|
| PP0 | $a_0 \cdot b_3 \; a_0 \cdot b_2 \; a_0 \cdot b_1 \; a_0 \cdot b_0$ |
| PP1 | $a_1 \cdot b_3 \; a_1 \cdot b_2 \; a_1 \cdot b_1 \; a_1 \cdot b_0$ (shifted 1) |
| PP2 | $a_2 \cdot b_3 \; a_2 \cdot b_2 \; a_2 \cdot b_1 \; a_2 \cdot b_0$ (shifted 2) |
| PP3 | $a_3 \cdot b_3 \; a_3 \cdot b_2 \; a_3 \cdot b_1 \; a_3 \cdot b_0$ (shifted 3) |

**Step 2: Summation Network** (2 marks)
- 16 AND gates for partial products + an array of 12 full adders arranged in 4 rows + 1 final HA for the highest bit.
- Layout is regular and easy to route.

**Step 3: Worst-Case Delay** (1 mark)

$$
T_{array} = (N-1) \cdot t_{FA} + t_{AND} = 3 \times 120 + 60 = 420 \text{ ps}
$$

**Step 4: Wallace Tree Improvement** (2 marks)
- A Wallace tree uses **3-to-2 (carry-save) compressors** to reduce partial products in a logarithmic tree. The number of adder stages becomes $\log_{1.5}(N) \approx O(\log N)$ instead of $O(N)$.
- For 4-bit: $T_{Wallace} = t_{AND} + 2 t_{CSA} + t_{CPA} = 60 + 2(80) + 120 = 340$ ps.
- Trade-off: **irregular layout**, harder routing, higher area.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Array Multiplier):** A common mistake is to compute the delay as $N^2 \cdot t_{AND}$ — this is **wrong**. The delay is governed by the **ripple path through the FA array**, not the AND gates, since all AND gates operate in parallel.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3**

- ✅ **Latches vs Flip-Flops:** Latch = level-sensitive, FF = edge-triggered. FFs are preferred for synchronous design.
- ✅ **SR Latch Forbidden State:** $S = R = 1$ is illegal; on release, output is non-deterministic.
- ✅ **D Flip-Flop Equation:** $Q_{next} = D$ on the active clock edge.
- ✅ **Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** Data must be stable *before* clock edge. **Hold Time ($t_h$):** Data must remain stable *after* clock edge.
- ✅ **Setup Slack:** $T_{clk} - (t_{cq} + t_{logic} + t_{su}) \ge 0$. Hold Slack: $t_{cq} + t_{logic,min} - t_h \ge 0$.
- ✅ **Hold time** is independent of clock period — it can never be fixed by slowing the clock.
- ✅ **Shift Register Types:** SISO, SIPO, PISO, PIPO. Memorize applications.
- ✅ **Synchronous vs Ripple Counter:** Sync is faster but uses more combinational logic; ripple has linear delay.
- ✅ **Half Adder:** 1 XOR + 1 AND. **Full Adder:** 2 XOR + 2 AND + 1 OR (in classic CMOS).
- ✅ **RCA Delay:** $O(N)$ linear. **CLA Delay:** $O(\log N)$ with parallel-prefix.
- ✅ **CLA Generate:** $G_i = A_i B_i$. **Propagate:** $P_i = A_i \oplus B_i$. **Carry out:** $C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i C_i$.
- ✅ **Wallace Tree:** Uses 3:2 compressors → $\log N$ delay. **Dadda Multiplier:** Minimal compressor stages.
- ✅ **Multiplier Critical Path (Array):** $(N-1) t_{FA} + t_{AND}$, NOT $N^2 t_{AND}$.
- ✅ **Barrel Shifter:** Combinational N-bit rotator/shifter using a matrix of pass transistors.
- ✅ **CMOS Inverter Power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$. Static power ≈ $V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$.
- ✅ **Master-Slave FF:** Two gated latches with opposite clock phases → edge-triggered behavior.
- ✅ **2's Complement Subtraction:** A − B = A + (~B) + 1 → reuse the adder as a subtractor.
- ✅ **Magnitude Comparator:** 4-bit can be built from 4x 1-bit comparators + AND tree of equality bits.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for CLA:** "*G*o *P*ropagate the *C*arry" — Generate makes its own carry, Propagate passes it forward.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequential Logic Circuits - Timing Metrics for Sequential Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sequential Logic Circuits - Timing Metrics for Sequential Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Timing Metrics for Sequential Circuits** are the quantitative electrical parameters that describe how fast, reliably, and deterministically data can propagate through a clocked (synchronous) digital system composed of registers (flip-flops/latches) and combinational logic between them. They form the mathematical basis of the **Sequencing Element Dynamic Discipline**, which is the cornerstone of synchronous VLSI design.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) framework, the timing metrics of a sequential circuit are categorized into:

1. **Register-internal timing metrics:** Propagation delay ($t_{ccq}$), Contamination delay ($t_{pcq}$), Setup time ($t_{su}$), Hold time ($t_h$).
2. **Combinational logic timing metrics:** Propagation delay ($t_{pd}$), Contamination delay ($t_{cd}$).
3. **System-level sequential timing metrics:** Clock period ($T_c$), Maximum allowable clock skew ($t_{skew}$), Time borrowing ($\delta$), and Slack ($S$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A sequential circuit is *functionally correct* if and only if the **Setup Time Constraint** and the **Hold Time Constraint** are simultaneously satisfied at every register in the pipeline. This dual-constraint rule is a guaranteed question in every KTU ESE for Module 3.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bucket-brigade fire-fighting line** at a college sports meet (a classic KTU viva analogy). Each student (representing a *register*) holds a water bucket for exactly one whistle beat (the **clock period**). Between whistle beats, each student must:

- *Receive* the bucket from the previous student (this minimum hand-off time is the **setup time**).
- *Pour* the water forward (this is the **clock-to-Q delay**).
- *Not let go* of the empty bucket before the next student has a firm grip (this is the **hold time**).

If any student pours the water *too late*, the relay fails — this is a **setup time violation**. If a student *lets go too early*, the bucket falls — this is a **hold time violation**. The whistle-blowing frequency is the **maximum clock frequency**.

A second analogy: think of a **roller-coaster station** where a new car cannot enter the platform until the previous one has cleared the brakes (setup), and the brake-arm cannot release until the next car is fully locked onto the rail (hold). Timing metrics are the *kinematic equations* of this digital roller-coaster.

### 1.3 Standard Physical / Engineering Constants and Metrics (Bold)

- **Minimum Logic High Voltage ($V_{IH}$)** and **Maximum Logic Low Voltage ($V_{IL}$)** define the noise margins.
- **Setup time ($t_{su}$):** Minimum time *before* the active clock edge that the data input (D) must be stable. **Typical CMOS range: 30 ps – 500 ps.**
- **Hold time ($t_h$):** Minimum time *after* the active clock edge that D must remain stable. **Typical CMOS range: 20 ps – 100 ps.**
- **Clock-to-Q contamination delay ($t_{ccq}$)** and **propagation delay ($t_{pcq}$):** Typically in tens to hundreds of picoseconds.
- **Combinational contamination delay ($t_{cd}$)** and **propagation delay ($t_{pd}$):** Function of logical effort, fan-out, and parasitic capacitance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention adopted by KTU 2024 (Weste/Harris textbook convention):**
> - $t_{ccq}$ = *contamination* Clock-to-Q delay (earliest possible output change).
> - $t_{pcq}$ = *propagation* Clock-to-Q delay (latest possible output change).
> The "c" stands for contamination and "p" stands for propagation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sequencing Element Dynamic Discipline (Step-by-Step Logic)

For a properly functioning synchronous sequential circuit, **every** register must obey two non-negotiable rules at every active clock edge:

**Rule 1 — Setup Time Constraint (Long-Path / Setup Slack):**

$$
S_{setup} = T_c - (t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}) \;\geq\; 0
$$

This guarantees that the data arriving at the *next* register's D input has become stable **at least $t_{su}$ seconds BEFORE** the next rising clock edge.

**Rule 2 — Hold Time Constraint (Short-Path / Hold Slack):**

$$
S_{hold} = t_{ccq} + t_{cd} - t_h \;\geq\; 0
$$

This guarantees that the new data emerging from a register does *not* propagate through the combinational logic so fast that it corrupts the D input of the *same-stage* register **at least $t_h$ seconds AFTER** the clock edge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why both constraints?**
> - The **Setup Constraint** prevents the *next* cycle's data from arriving too late.
> - The **Hold Constraint** prevents the *current* cycle's data from arriving too early (which would clobber the current state).
> Notice that the Setup Constraint **depends on the clock period $T_c$** but the Hold Constraint **does NOT depend on $T_c$**. This is why hold violations cannot be fixed by slowing down the clock — they require circuit-level changes (inserting buffers, re-routing).

### 2.2 Time-of-Arrival and Time-of-Required (Static Timing Analysis Foundation)

In modern Static Timing Analysis (STA) tools used in VLSI design:

- **Time of Arrival (TOA)** or **Data Arrival Time (A):** The earliest/latest time a signal can reach a node.
- **Time of Required (TOR)** or **Data Required Time (R):** The latest/earliest time a signal must be present to avoid a timing violation.

The **slack** at any node is:

$$
S = R - A
$$

A **negative slack** indicates a timing violation (red flag in Synopsys PrimeTime / Cadence Tempus reports).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric / Equation | Formula | Meaning / Constraint | Typical Unit |
|---|-------------------|---------|----------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Setup Slack | $S_{setup} = T_c - (t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su})$ | Must be $\geq 0$ | s (seconds) |
| 2 | Hold Slack | $S_{hold} = t_{ccq} + t_{cd} - t_h$ | Must be $\geq 0$ | s |
| 3 | Max Clock Frequency | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{c,min}}$ | Upper bound on operation | Hz |
| 4 | Minimum Clock Period | $T_{c,min} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}$ | Critical-path sum | s |
| 5 | Data Arrival Time | $A_{i+1} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd,i}$ | Latest arrival at next flop | s |
| 6 | Data Required Time | $R_{i+1} = T_c - t_{su}$ | Latest allowed arrival | s |
| 7 | Time Borrowing | $\delta_{borrow} = T_c - (t_{pcq,A} + t_{pd,A}) - t_{su,B}$ | Borrowable into next stage | s |
| 8 | Max Allowable Skew (Setup) | $t_{skew} < T_c - (t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su})$ | For setup safety | s |
| 9 | Max Allowable Skew (Hold) | $t_{skew} < t_{ccq} + t_{cd} - t_h$ | For hold safety | s |
| 10 | Sequential Logical Effort (delay) | $D = \sum d_i + d_{seq}$ | Sum of stage + seq delays | FO4 inverter units |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These timing metrics are not academic abstractions — they directly drive:

- **ASIC/FPGA Synthesis:** Tools like *Synopsys Design Compiler* and *Xilinx Vivado* use these exact metrics to perform Static Timing Analysis (STA) and generate Slack reports.
- **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS):** The skew formulas dictate the maximum tolerable mismatch in clock distribution networks.
- **Pipeline Depth Optimization:** Datapath designers use $T_{c,min}$ to decide whether to split a long combinational path across two pipeline stages (reducing $t_{pd}$ per stage but adding registers and thus $t_{pcq} + t_{su}$).
- **High-Performance CPU Design:** Modern out-of-order processors (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen) use time borrowing to balance timing across pipelines.
- **IOT/Edge-AI Chips:** Power-aware designs trade $f_{max}$ for lower $V_{DD}$ using these same equations.

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Metric — Explanatory Logic Chain

1. **Why $t_{pcq}$ (propagation Clock-to-Q) is needed:** Because the internal CMOS cross-coupled inverters in a flip-flop take finite time to flip their state after the clock edge; the output Q is guaranteed valid only *after* $t_{pcq}$.
2. **Why $t_{su}$ exists:** The master latch of a master-slave flop needs the data to charge the input capacitance before the clock-edge-driven transmission gate closes; otherwise the data is missed.
3. **Why $t_h$ exists:** To prevent a race condition where new data arrives at the slave latch before the master has fully transferred the previous value.
4. **Why both $t_{cd}$ and $t_{pd}$ exist:** Because CMOS gates have an inherent rise/fall asymmetry and parasitic capacitances; the contamination delay (fastest possible change) is dominated by the *fastest* transistors in the pull-up/pull-down network, while propagation delay (latest possible change) is dominated by the *slowest* transistors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Setup Time Constraint from First Principles

**Statement to derive:** $T_c \geq t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}$

**Step 1 — Define the system.**
Consider two edge-triggered flip-flops, $FF_1$ (source) and $FF_2$ (destination), separated by a combinational logic block with propagation delay $t_{pd}$.

**Step 2 — Identify the active clock edge.**
Let the active clock edge be the rising edge ($\uparrow$). Assume $FF_1$ captures data on edge at time $t = 0$.

**Step 3 — Trace the earliest possible invalid event and the latest possible valid event.**

| Event | Time | Reason |
|-------|------|--------|
| Clock edge at $FF_1$ (rising) | $0$ | Reference time origin |
| Earliest Q output change at $FF_1$ | $t_{ccq}$ | Contamination Clock-to-Q |
| Latest Q output change at $FF_1$ | $t_{pcq}$ | Propagation Clock-to-Q |
| Latest D arrival at $FF_2$ | $t_{pcq} + t_{pd}$ | Sum of propagation delays |
| Next clock edge at $FF_2$ | $T_c$ | Clock period |

**Step 4 — Apply the setup requirement.**
For the data at the D input of $FF_2$ to be stable *at least* $t_{su}$ seconds **before** the next clock edge:

$$
t_{pcq} + t_{pd} \leq T_c - t_{su}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the Setup Constraint.**

$$
T_c \geq t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Slack form** by subtracting the right-hand side from $T_c$:

$$
S_{setup} = T_c - t_{pcq} - t_{pd} - t_{su} \;\geq\; 0
$$

**This completes the derivation.** $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Hold Time Constraint from First Principles

**Statement to derive:** $t_{ccq} + t_{cd} \geq t_h$

**Step 1 — Reference frame.** The hold time is checked *immediately after* the same active clock edge that captured the data.

**Step 2 — Trace the earliest possible new data arrival at $FF_2$.**

| Event | Time | Reason |
|-------|------|--------|
| Clock edge at $FF_1$ | $0$ | Active edge |
| Earliest Q output change at $FF_1$ | $t_{ccq}$ | Contamination Clock-to-Q |
| Earliest D arrival at $FF_2$ | $t_{ccq} + t_{cd}$ | Through combinational contamination delay |

**Step 3 — Apply the hold requirement.**
The new data must NOT reach the D input of $FF_2$ until *at least* $t_h$ seconds **after** the clock edge (otherwise it corrupts the data still being held by the master stage):

$$
t_{ccq} + t_{cd} \geq t_h
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Slack form.**

$$
S_{hold} = t_{ccq} + t_{cd} - t_h \;\geq\; 0
$$

**Note that $T_c$ does not appear here.** $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Clock Frequency

Starting from the setup constraint $T_c \geq t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}$, the minimum allowable clock period is obtained when the slack equals zero:

$$
T_{c,min} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}
$$

The maximum operating frequency is the reciprocal:

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{c,min}} = \frac{1}{t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Time-Borrowing (Cycle Stealing)

**Scenario:** A combinational path has a delay shorter than the cycle time, and the next stage's combinational path exceeds the cycle time.

**Step 1 — Original slack in Stage A.**

$$
S_A = T_c - (t_{pcq} + t_{pd,A}) - t_{su,A}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the borrowed time $\delta$.**
If Stage A finishes $t_{pcq} + t_{pd,A} + \delta$ time after the clock edge, then the borrowable amount is:

$$
\delta = (T_c - t_{su,A}) - (t_{pcq} + t_{pd,A})
$$

**Step 3 — Ensure Stage B can still meet setup.**
The required arrival time at the next flop is shifted forward by $\delta$:

$$
(t_{pcq} + t_{pd,A} + \delta) + t_{pd,B} \leq T_c - t_{su,B}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum borrowable time $\delta_{max}$.**

$$
\delta_{max} = T_c - t_{pcq} - t_{pd,A} - t_{pd,B} - t_{su,B}
$$

**Step 5 — Validation rule:** $\delta \geq 0$ (Stage A must not borrow *negatively*, i.e., it cannot borrow time from a *previous* stage). $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 ESE Style)

**Problem:** A sequential circuit has the following parameters:
- $t_{pcq} = 100 \text{ ps}$
- $t_{ccq} = 60 \text{ ps}$
- $t_{pd} = 350 \text{ ps}$
- $t_{cd} = 80 \text{ ps}$
- $t_{su} = 50 \text{ ps}$
- $t_h = 40 \text{ ps}$

**Find:** (a) Maximum clock frequency, (b) Verify hold constraint, (c) Determine the slack at the destination register if $T_c = 600 \text{ ps}$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Maximum frequency:**

$$
T_{c,min} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su} = 100 + 350 + 50 = 500 \text{ ps}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^{-12}} = 2 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz} = 2 \text{ GHz}
$$

**(b) Hold check:**

$$
t_{ccq} + t_{cd} = 60 + 80 = 140 \text{ ps}
$$

Since $140 \text{ ps} \geq t_h = 40 \text{ ps}$, the hold constraint is **satisfied**.

**(c) Slack at $T_c = 600 \text{ ps}$:**

$$
S_{setup} = 600 - 100 - 350 - 50 = 100 \text{ ps} \geq 0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

$$
S_{hold} = 140 - 40 = 100 \text{ ps} \geq 0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

The circuit has **100 ps positive setup slack** and **100 ps positive hold slack** at 600 ps clock period.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Setup Constraint | Hold Constraint |
|--------|------------------|-----------------|
| Path type | Long path (max delay) | Short path (min delay) |
| Depends on $T_c$? | Yes | **No** |
| Failure mode | Data arrives too late | Data arrives too early |
| Fix | Increase $T_c$ / pipeline deeper | Insert delay buffers |
| Worst case for | High-performance datapaths | Fast bypass/feed-through paths |
| Verification tool | STA with setup checks | STA with hold checks |
| Exam weightage (KTU) | ~70% of marks | ~30% of marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Synchronous Pipeline

The diagram below illustrates the canonical register-to-register timing model used universally in synchronous VLSI design. It is the single most important figure for the "Timing Metrics" topic in KTU 2024.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ClkSrc[Global Clock Source] -->|clock signal| ClkTree[Clock Tree Network]
    ClkTree -->|CLK| FF1[Register FF1 Source]
    ClkTree -->|CLK| FF2[Register FF2 Destination]
    D1[Data Input D] -->|arrives before clock edge| FF1
    FF1 -->|after t_ccq earliest, t_pcq latest| Q1[Q output of FF1]
    Q1 -->|through combinational logic| CombLogic[Combinational Logic Block]
    CombLogic -->|after t_cd earliest, t_pd latest| D2[D input of FF2]
    FF2 -->|captured on clock edge| Q2[Q output of FF2]
    
    FF1 -. setup check .-> SC[Setup Constraint Checkpoint]
    FF2 -. hold check .-> HC[Hold Constraint Checkpoint]
    
    SC -->|S_setup >= 0| Valid[Valid Timing]
    HC -->|S_hold >= 0| Valid
    
    style ClkSrc fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style ClkTree fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style FF1 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style FF2 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style CombLogic fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style Valid fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:** The clock tree distributes a synchronized clock to both registers. The data path is broken into two segments — the **launch path** (FF1 → combinational) and the **capture path** (arrival at FF2). Both segments must be timed simultaneously against the setup and hold constraints.

---

### 4.2 Timing Waveform Diagram (Edge-Triggered Flip-Flop)

The following flowchart represents the temporal sequence of events on a single data bit being launched from $FF_1$ and captured at $FF_2$. It is rendered as a functional flow because pure ASCII waveforms are not optimal in this format.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clock Edge 1 at t = 0] -->|captures D| B[FF1 internal master latches D]
    B -->|t_ccq earliest| C[Q1 starts to change]
    B -->|t_pcq latest| D[Q1 fully stable]
    D -->|enters| E[Combinational logic block]
    E -->|t_pd worst case| F[D2 signal arrives at FF2]
    F -->|must be stable t_su before next edge| G[Clock Edge 2 at t = Tc]
    G -->|captures valid D2| H[FF2 outputs new Q2]
    
    A -->|parallel| P[Q1 ALSO reaches FF2 via t_cd contamination]
    P -->|must NOT change until t_h after edge 1| Q[Hold time requirement satisfied]
    
    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style D fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style E fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style F fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style G fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style H fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style P fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style Q fill:#e377c2,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart for KTU Exam Problem Solving

A handy visual for solving numerical questions in the KTU 2024 ESE — given circuit parameters, identify the type of timing constraint violated.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given: Timing parameters t_ccq, t_pcq, t_pd, t_cd, t_su, t_h, Tc] --> Calc1[Compute Setup Slack: Tc minus t_pcq minus t_pd minus t_su]
    Calc1 --> Check1{S_setup greater than or equal to 0?}
    Check1 -->|Yes| Calc2[Compute Hold Slack: t_ccq plus t_cd minus t_h]
    Check1 -->|No| ViolationA[Setup Violation: Increase Tc or shorten t_pd]
    Calc2 --> Check2{S_hold greater than or equal to 0?}
    Check2 -->|Yes| Pass[Both Constraints Satisfied]
    Check2 -->|No| ViolationB[Hold Violation: Insert delay buffers or shorten t_cd]
    
    style Start fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style Pass fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style ViolationA fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style ViolationB fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.4 Pipelined Multi-Stage Timing Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP[Input D] -->|latched| R1[Register R1]
    R1 -->|t_pd1| CL1[Combo Stage 1]
    CL1 -->|latched| R2[Register R2]
    R2 -->|t_pd2| CL2[Combo Stage 2]
    CL2 -->|latched| R3[Register R3]
    R3 -->|t_pd3| CL3[Combo Stage 3]
    CL3 -->|latched| R4[Register R4]
    R4 --> OUTP[Output Q]
    
    CLK[Clock] -.-> R1
    CLK -.-> R2
    CLK -.-> R3
    CLK -.-> R4
    
    style R1 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style R2 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style R3 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style R4 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style CL1 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style CL2 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style CL3 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style CLK fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
```

**Key insight for this diagram:** Each pipeline stage independently obeys the Setup and Hold constraints. Time borrowing can occur *between* stages but not *across* clock-domain boundaries without explicit synchronization (FIFO, handshake).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the terms Setup Time and Hold Time of a sequential element. Why are both required?**

**Model Answer:**

**Setup Time ($t_{su}$):** It is the minimum amount of time that the data input (D) of a flip-flop must remain stable **before** the active edge of the clock signal arrives, so that the data is reliably latched.

**Hold Time ($t_h$):** It is the minimum amount of time that the data input (D) must remain stable **after** the active edge of the clock signal, to prevent corruption of the data being captured.

Both are required because the master-slave internal transmission gates of a CMOS flip-flop need finite time to charge the input capacitance (setup) and finite time to safely isolate the master and slave latches (hold). Without both, race conditions and metastability can occur, leading to unpredictable circuit behavior.

**(3 Marks — Definition 1 mark each + Explanation 1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Clock-to-Q contamination delay ($t_{ccq}$) and Clock-to-Q propagation delay ($t_{pcq}$).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | $t_{ccq}$ (Contamination) | $t_{pcq}$ (Propagation) |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Definition | Earliest possible time after the clock edge that the output Q can begin to change | Latest possible time after the clock edge that the output Q becomes stable |
| Used in | Hold time constraint | Setup time constraint |
| Affected by | Fastest transistors in the pull-up/pull-down network | Slowest transistors in the pull-up/pull-down network |
| Typical magnitude | Smaller | Larger |

**(3 Marks — Tabular comparison with both definitions and uses)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

**(a)** Derive the Setup Time Constraint and Hold Time Constraint for a sequential circuit having edge-triggered flip-flops separated by combinational logic. Clearly state all the timing parameters involved and explain their physical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sequential circuit has the following timing parameters:
$t_{pcq} = 80$ ps, $t_{ccq} = 50$ ps, $t_{pd} = 450$ ps, $t_{cd} = 90$ ps, $t_{su} = 60$ ps, $t_h = 40$ ps.
Determine: (i) the minimum clock period, (ii) the maximum operating frequency, (iii) the setup slack when $T_c = 700$ ps, and (iv) whether the hold constraint is satisfied. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Setup Time Constraint Derivation:**

Consider two edge-triggered flip-flops, $FF_1$ and $FF_2$, with combinational logic between them. Let $T_c$ be the clock period, $t_{pcq}$ the propagation clock-to-Q delay of $FF_1$, $t_{pd}$ the propagation delay of the combinational logic, and $t_{su}$ the setup time of $FF_2$.

The data captured by $FF_1$ at clock edge $t=0$ appears at its Q output by time $t_{pcq}$. This propagates through the combinational logic and reaches the D input of $FF_2$ at time $t_{pcq} + t_{pd}$. For this data to be reliably captured by $FF_2$ on the next clock edge at $t=T_c$, it must arrive at least $t_{su}$ before that edge:

$$
t_{pcq} + t_{pd} \leq T_c - t_{su}
$$

$$
\therefore \;\; T_c \geq t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}
$$

**Hold Time Constraint Derivation:**

The hold time constraint is checked against the *earliest* possible data arrival. The earliest Q output of $FF_1$ changes at $t_{ccq}$ after the clock edge, and the earliest arrival at $FF_2$'s D input is $t_{ccq} + t_{cd}$. This new data must NOT disturb the data being captured at the same edge. The hold time $t_h$ requirement is:

$$
t_{ccq} + t_{cd} \geq t_h
$$

**Physical Significance of Parameters:**

- $t_{pcq}$ and $t_{ccq}$: Defined by the internal CMOS latch physical capacitances and transistor strengths.
- $t_{pd}$ and $t_{cd}$: Defined by the combinational gate topology, logical effort, fan-out, and parasitic capacitances.
- $t_{su}$ and $t_h$: Defined by the master-slave transmission gate design and internal clock distribution skew.

**(7 Marks — Setup derivation 2.5, Hold derivation 2.5, Physical significance 2)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

**(i) Minimum Clock Period:**

$$
T_{c,min} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su} = 80 + 450 + 60 = 590 \text{ ps}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Operating Frequency:**

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{c,min}} = \frac{1}{590 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 1.695 \text{ GHz}
$$

**(iii) Setup Slack at $T_c = 700$ ps:**

$$
S_{setup} = T_c - t_{pcq} - t_{pd} - t_{su} = 700 - 80 - 450 - 60 = 110 \text{ ps}
$$

Since $S_{setup} = 110 \text{ ps} \geq 0$, the setup constraint is **satisfied** with 110 ps of margin.

**(iv) Hold Constraint Check:**

$$
t_{ccq} + t_{cd} = 50 + 90 = 140 \text{ ps}
$$

Since $140 \text{ ps} \geq t_h = 40 \text{ ps}$, the hold constraint is **satisfied** with 100 ps of margin.

**(7 Marks — 1.5 marks each for (i)-(iii), 2.5 marks for (iv) with full justification)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a)** Explain the concept of **time borrowing (cycle stealing)** in sequential circuits with a suitable diagram. Derive the expression for the maximum borrowable time between two adjacent pipeline stages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pipelined datapath has three combinational stages with the following propagation delays: $t_{pd,1} = 200$ ps, $t_{pd,2} = 450$ ps, $t_{pd,3} = 250$ ps. The register parameters are $t_{pcq} = 70$ ps, $t_{su} = 50$ ps, $t_h = 30$ ps, and $t_{ccq} = 45$ ps. Calculate: (i) the maximum achievable clock frequency without time borrowing, (ii) the maximum borrowable time from Stage 1 into Stage 2, and (iii) the new effective clock frequency after time borrowing is optimally applied. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Concept of Time Borrowing:**
Time borrowing (also called *cycle stealing*) occurs when the combinational logic between two registers does not fully consume the available clock period. The leftover slack can be *borrowed* by the next stage to allow it to use a longer combinational path, provided the final destination register still meets its setup time.

**Diagrammatic representation:** (Conceptually) two registers $R_A$ and $R_B$ separated by combinational stages. If Stage A finishes early, the time between $R_A$'s clock edge and Stage A's output is *less* than $T_c - t_{su}$. The surplus time $\delta$ can be added to Stage B's allowable path length.

**Derivation:**
Let $\delta$ be the amount borrowed. The original slack in Stage A is:

$$
\delta = T_c - (t_{pcq,A} + t_{pd,A} + t_{su,B})
$$

For the borrow to be valid, Stage B's total path delay must satisfy:

$$
t_{pd,A} + \delta + t_{pd,B} \leq T_c - t_{pcq,B} - t_{su,B}
$$

Solving for the maximum borrowable time:

$$
\delta_{max} = T_c - t_{pcq,A} - t_{pd,A} - t_{pd,B} - t_{su,B}
$$

**Constraint:** $\delta_{max} \geq 0$, else borrowing is impossible (the path is too long to begin with).

**(7 Marks — Concept 2 marks, Diagram 2 marks, Derivation 3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

**(i) Maximum frequency without time borrowing:**

The critical path is the longest combinational stage delay, $t_{pd,2} = 450$ ps.

$$
T_{c,min} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd,critical} + t_{su} = 70 + 450 + 50 = 570 \text{ ps}
$$

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{570 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 1.754 \text{ GHz}
$$

**(ii) Maximum borrowable time from Stage 1 into Stage 2:**

$$
\delta_{max} = T_c - t_{pcq} - t_{pd,1} - t_{pd,2} - t_{su} = 570 - 70 - 200 - 450 - 50 = -200 \text{ ps}
$$

Since $\delta_{max} < 0$, **no time can be borrowed** from Stage 1 — Stage 2 already exceeds the budget. (This shows that time borrowing only helps when the leading stage has slack; it cannot rescue a path that is fundamentally too long.)

**(iii) New effective clock frequency after optimization:**

Since time borrowing is not possible, the optimal solution is to **pipeline Stage 2 into two sub-stages**. Split $t_{pd,2} = 450$ ps into $t_{pd,2a} = 225$ ps and $t_{pd,2b} = 225$ ps.

$$
T_{c,new} = 70 + 225 + 50 = 345 \text{ ps}
$$

$$
f_{max,new} = \frac{1}{345 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 2.899 \text{ GHz}
$$

**(7 Marks — Part (i) 2 marks, Part (ii) 2 marks, Part (iii) 3 marks with justification)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU VLSI Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $t_{ccq}$ with $t_{pcq}$:** Many students write $t_{pcq}$ in the hold-time equation instead of $t_{ccq}$. Hold constraint uses *contamination* delays (earliest), not *propagation* delays (latest). Marks deduction: **1–2 marks per occurrence.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Hold Constraint entirely:** Setup is widely covered, but examiners report that 40–50% of students omit the hold check. Marks deduction: **up to 4 marks.**
>
> 3. **Writing the Setup equation as equality instead of inequality:** The correct form is $T_c \geq t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}$. Writing $T_c = t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}$ only applies at the *minimum* clock period boundary. Marks deduction: **0.5–1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Using wrong units:** $t_{pcq}$ in nanoseconds mixed with $t_{pd}$ in picoseconds causes the answer to be off by orders of magnitude. Marks deduction: **Full marks** for the numerical answer if units are inconsistent.
>
> 5. **Ignoring $t_{cd}$ in Hold check:** Some students only check $t_{ccq} \geq t_h$, forgetting to add $t_{cd}$. Marks deduction: **2–3 marks.**
>
> 6. **Misinterpreting "borrowing" direction:** Students often write that a stage can borrow time from a *previous* stage. The correct direction is from *current* to *next* stage (forward borrowing only). Marks deduction: **2 marks.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⏱ **Two non-negotiable constraints** govern every synchronous sequential circuit: **Setup** and **Hold**. Both must hold simultaneously.
- ⏱ **Setup Slack:** $S_{setup} = T_c - t_{pcq} - t_{pd} - t_{su} \geq 0$ (long-path, depends on $T_c$).
- ⏱ **Hold Slack:** $S_{hold} = t_{ccq} + t_{cd} - t_h \geq 0$ (short-path, $T_c$-independent).
- ⏱ **Maximum Clock Frequency:** $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{pcq} + t_{pd} + t_{su}}$.
- ⏱ **Naming convention:** $t_{ccq}$ → *c*ontamination, $t_{pcq}$ → *p*ropagation.
- ⏱ **Hold violations cannot be fixed by slowing the clock** — they require circuit-level delays (buffers).
- ⏱ **Setup violations can be fixed** by slowing the clock, pipelining deeper, or retiming.
- ⏱ **Time Borrowing** allows slack from a fast stage to be consumed by a slow adjacent stage — but only in the **forward direction**.
- ⏱ **STA Tools (Synopsys PrimeTime, Cadence Tempus)** report timing using exactly these metrics; Slack = (Data Required Time) − (Data Arrival Time).
- ⏱ **Time-of-Arrival (A)** is when the signal *arrives*; **Time-of-Required (R)** is when the signal *must* arrive. Slack $S = R - A$.
- ⏱ **Logical Effort methodology** extends these metrics to gate-level optimization of combinational paths.
- ⏱ **Clock Skew** introduces an additive term $\pm t_{skew}$ into the constraints and is *always* detrimental for setup on the receiving flop if positive.
- ⏱ **Typical CMOS values:** $t_{su}, t_h$ in picoseconds; $t_{pd}$ up to nanoseconds; clock periods in 100s of picoseconds to nanoseconds for high-speed digital.
- ⏱ **Exam focus areas for KTU 2024:** deriving setup/hold equations, computing $f_{max}$, time borrowing calculations, and interpretation of STA slack reports.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static SR Flip-Flop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Static SR Flip-Flop**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Static SR (Set–Reset) Flip-Flop** is a bistable sequential logic element, realized using **cross-coupled CMOS logic gates** (typically two NOR or two NAND gates), that can indefinitely retain one of two stable output states (Q = 1 or Q = 0) as long as the supply voltage (V_DD) is maintained, without the need for periodic refresh signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – CMOS Sequential Logic):**
> The term "Static" implies that the storage mechanism relies on **positive feedback** through a regenerative cross-coupled pair, where one of the two inverters/loGates is always conducting. This is fundamentally distinct from **dynamic** storage, which depends on capacitive charge retention (and hence periodic clock refreshing).

The device is classified as an **SR latch (level-sensitive)** or **SR flip-flop (edge-triggered)** depending on the clocking arrangement. In its most fundamental CMOS form, the Static SR Latch consists of:

- **Two cross-coupled NOR gates** (active-high inputs: S, R)
- **OR two cross-coupled NAND gates** (active-low inputs: S̄, R̄)
- **Optional transmission-gate (TG) or clocked CMOS (C²MOS)** input stages for level-sensitive operation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Person See-Saw Memory"

Imagine a perfectly balanced see-saw with two children, **A** (left) and **B** (right), sitting at opposite ends. The see-saw is a stiff metal pivot, so once A goes down, B stays up indefinitely (until someone pushes). 

- **A is "Set" to the ground (Q = 1)**: The child on the left side is pulled down. Even when released, the heavy weight of A keeps the left side down, holding B up.
- **A is "Reset" (Q = 0)**: A heavy weight is dropped on the right side, flipping the see-saw. It stays flipped indefinitely.
- **Both children stand up (S = R = 0 in NOR)**: The see-saw holds whatever position it was last in — this is the **Memory/Hold** state.
- **Both children push down (S = R = 1 in NOR)**: Mechanical chaos — the see-saw tries to balance level, and the resulting state is undefined/illegal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Examination Standard):**
> - **V_DD** — Positive supply voltage (typically **3.3 V** for KTU-curriculum CMOS processes, **1.8 V** for sub-micron).
> - **V_TN**, **V_TP** — Threshold voltages of NMOS and PMOS respectively.
> - **V_IH, V_IL, V_OH, V_OL** — Input/Output voltage reference levels for noise-margin calculation.
> - **τ_pHL, τ_pLH** — Propagation delays (high-to-low and low-to-high).
> - **β = μC_ox(W/L)** — Transistor transconductance parameter (used for ratioed sizing).

## 1.3 Why "Static" Matters in VLSI

A **static** cell consumes **DC current only during switching transients**. In its stable state (Q = 0 or Q = 1), the regenerative loop is configured such that there is **no direct current path from V_DD to GND**. This makes static SR flip-flops:
1. **Robust against noise** (regenerative feedback restores logic levels).
2. **Tolerant of low clock frequencies** (no minimum clock-rate constraint, unlike dynamic latches).
3. **Higher area cost** (6 transistors for cross-coupled NOR + 4 transistors for input gating = **10T** structure, or **8T** with pass-transistor logic).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CMOS Implementation — The 6T Cross-Coupled NOR Latch

The fundamental static storage cell is built by **cross-coupling two CMOS NOR gates** (or equivalently, two CMOS inverters with tristate input control). The schematic logical representation is:

- **Gate 1 (NOR-A):** Inputs = R and Q̄ ; Output = Q
- **Gate 2 (NOR-B):** Inputs = S and Q ; Output = Q̄

The cross-coupling creates a **positive-feedback loop**: any perturbation in Q is immediately reinforced by the next stage, producing exponential regeneration of the signal toward the correct rail (V_DD or GND).

## 2.2 Truth Table of NOR-Based Static SR Latch

| S | R | Q (next) | Q̄ (next) | Mode | Explanation |
|:-:|:-:|:--------:|:---------:|:----:|:------------|
| 0 | 0 |    Q     |    Q̄     | **Hold / Memory** | Both NOR outputs disabled; previous state retained by feedback. |
| 0 | 1 |    0     |     1     | **Reset** | R forces Q low, which propagates to force Q̄ high. |
| 1 | 0 |    1     |     0     | **Set** | S forces Q high, which propagates to force Q̄ low. |
| 1 | 1 |    0     |     0     | **Forbidden / Race** | Both outputs try to go to 0; removing S=R=1 produces indeterminate state. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The S = R = 1 condition is universally called the **race condition** or **forbidden state**. It is a "must-remember" question in Part A. Most students lose marks by failing to specify *why* it is forbidden (because Q = Q̄ = 0 violates the complementary-output invariant, and the released state is **metastable**).

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operation Logic (Why & How)

1. **Set Operation (S=1, R=0):** PMOS of Gate-1 turns ON → Q pulled HIGH → this 1 fed to Gate-2 → NMOS of Gate-2 ON → Q̄ pulled LOW → Q̄=0 fed back to Gate-1, which **sustains** Q=1 even after S returns to 0. **Memory is achieved.**

2. **Reset Operation (S=0, R=1):** Symmetric mirror of Set. Q is forced LOW, Q̄ is forced HIGH, and Q̄=1 fed back sustains the reset state.

3. **Hold Operation (S=0, R=0):** Both PMOS of Gate-1 and PMOS of Gate-2 are tied to their respective Q̄ and Q nodes. The previously stored bit keeps one PMOS ON and the other OFF, statically maintaining the state with **zero static current**.

4. **Race Condition (S=1, R=1):** Both pull-down NMOS branches in the two NOR gates conduct → Q = Q̄ = 0. Upon releasing S or R first, the cell latches to an **unpredictable** final state depending on device mismatches and parasitic capacitances.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Engineering Use |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:----------|:----------------|
| 1 | Transistor aspect ratio (symmetric inverter) | $(W/L)_n = (W/L)_p \cdot (\mu_p/\mu_n)$ | $\mu_n, \mu_p$ mobilities | Symmetric noise margins; typically $\mu_n/\mu_p \approx 2.5$. |
| 2 | Switching threshold V_M | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{(k_p/k_n)}(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{(k_p/k_n)}}$ | $k = \mu C_{ox}W/L$ | Defines the inverter decision boundary; ideal = $V_{DD}/2$. |
| 3 | Noise Margin High ($NM_H$) | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Logic levels | Robustness against V_DD-coupling noise. |
| 4 | Noise Margin Low ($NM_L$) | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Logic levels | Robustness against ground-bounce noise. |
| 5 | Static Power (ideal static cell) | $P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Sub-threshold leakage | Defines hold-state power floor. |
| 6 | Dynamic Power (switching) | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | $\alpha$ activity, $f$ clock | Dominant in active clocked operation. |
| 7 | Propagation Delay (first-order) | $\tau_p \approx \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 I_{ON}}$ | $C_L$ load cap | Speed metric; KTU asks 10–90% rise/fall times. |
| 8 | Regenerative loop gain | $A_{loop} = (g_{mN} + g_{mP}) \cdot R_{out}$ | $g_m$ transconductance | Must satisfy $A_{loop} > 1$ for bistability. |
| 9 | Hold-state stability factor | $S = \dfrac{(W/L)_{driver}}{(W/L)_{load}} > 1$ | Ratioed sizing | Required for ratioed static latches. |
| 10 | Metastable resolution time | $\tau_{res} \approx \dfrac{C}{g_m} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta V_0}\right)$ | $\Delta V$ voltage deviation | Bounded by regenerative time constant. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The Static SR Flip-Flop forms the **storage core** of nearly every static random-access memory (SRAM) bitcell (6T SRAM), the **master stage** of master-slave edge-triggered flip-flops, and the **debounce circuit** for mechanical switches in digital input subsystems. The same regenerative principles underpin **sense amplifiers** in memory peripherals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Switching Threshold V_M of a CMOS Inverter (used as the SR latch cross-couple)

The latch's hold-stability is anchored in the inverters' switching threshold. We derive V_M by equating the NMOS and PMOS drain currents at the moment Q = Q̄ = V_M.

**Step 1 — Saturation-region current equations (long-channel, gradual-channel approximation):**

$$
I_{DN} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS,n} - V_{TN})^2, \qquad I_{DP} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{GS,p} - V_{TP})^2
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute gate-source voltages. At the switching point, V_in = V_out = V_M, with V_GS,n = V_M and V_GS,p = V_M − V_DD:**

$$
I_{DN} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{TN})^2, \qquad I_{DP} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_M - V_{DD} - V_{TP})^2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the output node. Since no DC path exists at the metastable crossing, the currents must be equal in magnitude (NMOS sourcing, PMOS sinking):**

$$
\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{TN})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_M - V_{DD} - V_{TP})^2
$$

**Step 4 — Take the positive square root of both sides and solve for V_M:**

$$
\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_{TN}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_M + \vert V_{TP}\vert)
$$

**Step 5 — Isolate V_M on one side:**

$$
\sqrt{k_n}\,V_M - \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{TN} = \sqrt{k_p}\,V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_p}\,V_M + \sqrt{k_p}\,\vert V_{TP}\vert
$$

**Step 6 — Group V_M terms:**

$$
V_M(\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}) = \sqrt{k_p}(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert) + \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{TN}
$$

**Step 7 — Final closed-form expression (the canonical KTU result):**

$$
V_M = \frac{\sqrt{k_p}(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert) + \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{TN}}{\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For symmetric noise margins, design $k_n = k_p$, which yields $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.** This is the KTU design rule of thumb for the cross-coupled inverter pair inside the SR latch.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Static Noise Margin (SNM) Definition

The **Static Noise Margin** is the largest DC noise voltage that can be superimposed on the input of a gate in a cross-coupled pair without flipping the latch. Using the **butterfly curve** (transfer characteristics of the two cross-coupled inverters), SNM is defined as the side of the largest **inscribed square** between the two transfer curves.

**Step 1 — Define the inverter switching thresholds V_IL and V_IH as the points where the small-signal gain $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$.**

For the CMOS inverter (using the equivalent pull-up / pull-down resistances R_p and R_n):

$$
V_{IL} = \frac{3V_{DD} + 2V_{TP} - V_{TN}\left(1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_n}{k_p}}\right)}{4 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{k_n}{k_p}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define V_OH and V_OL using the saturated/linear current boundary:**

$$
V_{OH} = V_{DD}, \qquad V_{OL} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute noise margins:**

$$
\boxed{\,NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = V_{DD} - V_{IH}\,}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = V_{IL}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate SNM (KTU practical approximation):**

$$
\boxed{\,SNM \approx \min(NM_H, NM_L) \approx V_{IL} \approx V_{DD} - V_{IH}\,}
$$

For a symmetric inverter with $V_M = V_{DD}/2$, $NM_H = NM_L \approx V_{DD}/4 - V_T/2$, giving approximately **0.825 V for a 3.3 V supply**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Propagation Delay of the Cross-Coupled Latch

The transient response is dominated by the **time constant** of the inverter load capacitance C_L and the effective drive current I_ON.

**Step 1 — First-order RC charge/discharge model:**

$$
\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = \frac{I_{ON}}{C_L}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the 50%-to-50% propagation delay (typical KTU definition):**

$$
\tau_p = \frac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{I_{ON}} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}/2}{I_{ON}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the saturation-region drive current $I_{ON} = \dfrac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{TN})^2$:**

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \, V_{DD}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Average propagation delay (used in Part B timing diagrams):**

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_p = \frac{\tau_{pHL} + \tau_{pLH}}{2}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Reducing V_DD by a factor of 2 **quadruples** the delay (because $\tau_p \propto 1/V_{DD}$ at constant overdrive), but **quarters** the dynamic power — this is the **energy-delay trade-off** central to low-power VLSI design and frequently asked in KTU Module 5.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of SR Latch Topologies

| Topology | Transistor Count | Static Power | Hold Robustness | Race-Free | Area (λ² rule) | Typical Use |
|:---------|:----------------:|:------------:|:----------------|:---------:|:--------------:|:------------|
| **Cross-coupled NOR (basic)** | 12T (4T/NOR × 2 + 2T) | Zero (ideal) | High (regenerative) | No (S=R=1 illegal) | 80–100 | Pedagogical, debounce |
| **Cross-coupled NOR + TG** | 10T | Zero (clocked) | High | Yes (clocked) | 70–90 | Master-slave F/F |
| **NAND-based S̄R̄ Latch** | 8T | Zero (ideal) | High | No (active-low illegal) | 60–80 | Set-dominant systems |
| **C²MOS SR Flip-Flop** | 12T (dynamic front) | Zero during φ=0 | High | Yes (no overlap) | 90–110 | High-speed pipelines |
| **6T SRAM cell (ratioed)** | 6T | µW/bit | Medium (ratio-limited) | N/A (XOR storage) | 40–60 | Cache/RAM arrays |
| **8T/10T SRAM cell** | 8T–10T | Low | High (read-decoupled) | N/A | 50–80 | Low-V_DD cache |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **SR Latch (level-sensitive)** with **SR Flip-Flop (edge-triggered)**. The latch responds to *level* of S,R; the flip-flop responds to a *clock edge*. This distinction is worth **2–3 marks** in Part A.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CMOS Cross-Coupled NOR SR Latch — Transistor-Level Schematic

Below is the Mermaid representation of the **transistor-level topology** of the static SR latch. Each NOR gate uses 4 transistors (2 PMOS in series, 2 NMOS in parallel), giving **8 transistors** for the two NOR gates alone. When transmission gates are added for clock control, the count rises to **10T**.

```mermaid
graph LR
  vdd1((V_DD)) --- pA1["P1 PMOS"]
  vdd1 --- pB1["P2 PMOS"]
  pA1 --- nodeQ["node Q"]
  pB1 --- nodeQn["node Q-bar"]
  nodeQ --- nA1["N1 NMOS"]
  nodeQ --- nA2["N2 NMOS"]
  nodeQn --- nB1["N3 NMOS"]
  nodeQn --- nB2["N4 NMOS"]
  nA1 --- gndA((GND))
  nA2 --- gndA
  nB1 --- gndB((GND))
  nB2 --- gndB
  sIn["Input S"] -. controls .-> pA1
  sIn -. controls .-> nA2
  rIn["Input R"] -. controls .-> pB1
  sIn -. controls .-> nB1
  rIn -. controls .-> nA1
  rIn -. controls .-> nB2
  nodeQ -. feedback .-> nB1_gate["Gate of N3"]
  nodeQn -. feedback .-> nA2_gate["Gate of N2"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Clocked SR Flip-Flop

The **clocked SR flip-flop** adds two AND gates (or transmission gates) in front of a basic NOR latch. The clock Clk enables the S and R inputs only when Clk = 1, providing **level-sensitive synchronization**.

```mermaid
graph TB
  subgraph InputStage["Input Conditioning Stage (Steering Logic)"]
    sIn["S Input"] --> andA["AND Gate 1"]
    rIn["R Input"] --> andB["AND Gate 2"]
    clk["CLK Signal"] --> andA
    clk --> andB
    andA --> sEff["S-effective"]
    andB --> rEff["R-effective"]
  end

  subgraph StorageCore["Static Storage Core (Bistable)"]
    sEff --> norA["NOR Gate A"]
    rEff --> norB["NOR Gate B"]
    qb["Q-bar"] --> norA
    q["Q"] --> norB
    norA --> q
    norB --> qb
  end

  subgraph OutputBuffer["Output Buffer Stage (Drive Strength)"]
    q --> bufQ["Buffer"]
    qb --> bufQn["Buffer"]
    bufQ --> qOut["Q Output"]
    bufQn --> qnOut["Q-bar Output"]
  end

  q -. positive feedback .-> norA
  qb -. positive feedback .-> norB
```

## 4.3 Logical Cross-Coupling of Two NOR Gates (Block View)

This simplified block diagram highlights the **positive feedback loop** that defines bistability in any static CMOS latch.

```mermaid
graph LR
  nor1["NOR Gate 1\n(Inputs: R, Q-bar)\nOutput: Q"] --> qNode["Q"]
  nor2["NOR Gate 2\n(Inputs: S, Q)\nOutput: Q-bar"] --> qbarNode["Q-bar"]
  qNode --> nor2
  qbarNode --> nor1
  sPort["S"] --> nor2
  rPort["R"] --> nor1
```

## 4.4 Operation Waveform (Conceptual Timing Diagram)

```mermaid
gantt
  title Static SR Latch Operation Timeline
  dateFormat  X
  axisFormat  %s
  
  section Set Phase
  S=1 R=0 forced HIGH      :a1, 0, 2
  Q rises to 1             :a2, after a1, 2
  Q-bar falls to 0         :a3, after a1, 2

  section Hold Phase
  S=0 R=0 hold             :b1, after a2, 3
  Q remains at 1           :b2, after b1, 3
  Q-bar remains at 0       :b3, after b1, 3

  section Reset Phase
  R=1 forced HIGH          :c1, after b2, 2
  Q falls to 0             :c2, after c1, 2
  Q-bar rises to 1         :c3, after c1, 2
```

## 4.5 Stick Diagram (Conceptual)

The stick diagram represents the **physical layout** using coloured lines: **green** = polysilicon gate, **blue** = metal-1 interconnect, **red** = n-diffusion, **yellow** = p-diffusion. The cross-coupling of two inverters in a latch is drawn as:

```mermaid
graph LR
  vd(["V_DD rail (metal)"]) --- pdiff1["P-diff column 1\n(P1, P2 PMOS)"]
  pdiff1 --- nd1["N-diff column 1\n(N1, N2 NMOS)"]
  nd1 --- gn1(["GND rail"])
  
  inv2Q["Q node (metal strap)"] -.cross-couples.-> inv1
  inv1["Inverter A: Q = NOT(R, Qbar)"] --> q
  inv2["Inverter B: Qbar = NOT(S, Q)"] --> qbar
  q -. feedback .-> inv2
  qbar -. feedback .-> inv1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Layout Tip:** In a stick diagram, the **V_DD and GND rails are placed on the outermost columns**, p-diff is **next to V_DD**, and n-diff is **next to GND**. The Q and Q-bar signals are routed in **polysilicon or metal-2** to avoid shorting the source/drain diffusions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

> Define a Static SR Flip-Flop. With the help of a truth table, explain the *Hold*, *Set*, and *Reset* operations of a CMOS NOR-based SR latch.

**Model Answer:**

A **Static SR Flip-Flop** is a bistable sequential circuit implemented using **cross-coupled CMOS logic gates** that retains its output state indefinitely (statically) as long as V_DD is applied, without requiring clock-based refresh.

**Truth Table:**

| S | R | Q (next) | Q̄ (next) | Operation |
|:-:|:-:|:--------:|:---------:|:---------:|
| 0 | 0 |    Q     |    Q̄     | Hold (Memory) |
| 0 | 1 |    0     |     1     | Reset |
| 1 | 0 |    1     |     0     | Set |
| 1 | 1 |    0     |     0     | Forbidden |

- **Hold (S=R=0):** Both NOR gates are disabled at the input; the previously stored Q is fed back, sustaining the state with **no static current** — this is the *static* property.
- **Set (S=1, R=0):** The S input forces the output of NOR-B LOW (Q̄=0), which keeps NOR-A's output HIGH (Q=1). The state is **latched** by positive feedback even after S returns to 0.
- **Reset (S=0, R=1):** Symmetrically, R forces Q=0 and Q̄=1, again latched statically.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> What is meant by the *race-around condition* in a Static SR Flip-Flop? Why is the S = R = 1 input combination forbidden in a NOR-based latch?

**Model Answer:**

The **race-around condition** occurs when both Set and Reset inputs are asserted simultaneously (S = R = 1 for an active-high NOR latch). Both pull-down NMOS branches conduct, forcing **Q = Q̄ = 0** simultaneously, violating the fundamental bistable invariant that Q and Q̄ must be complementary.

This state is **forbidden** because:
1. The complementary invariant is broken (Q = Q̄), so the cell no longer represents valid logic.
2. Upon releasing S = R = 1, the final state is **unpredictable**, depending on tiny mismatches in transistor threshold voltages, channel mobilities, and parasitic capacitances.
3. The cell may enter a **metastable state** for a time of the order of $\tau_{res} \approx \dfrac{C}{g_m}\ln(\Delta V/\Delta V_0)$, during which Q hovers near $V_M = V_{DD}/2$, and the output is logically undefined.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: A or B) (1 × 14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **CMOS cross-coupled NOR-based Static SR Latch**. Derive the condition for **regenerative feedback** to ensure bistability. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Design a **clocked CMOS (C²MOS) SR Flip-Flop** using transmission gates. Explain how it avoids the race-around condition and discuss the role of **non-overlapping clocks** in its operation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The **CMOS cross-coupled NOR-based SR Latch** is realized using **two CMOS NOR gates** whose outputs are cross-connected to the inputs of each other. The schematic consists of **8 transistors** — each NOR gate uses 2 PMOS (in series between V_DD and output) and 2 NMOS (in parallel between output and GND).

**Operation Logic:**
- When **S = 1, R = 0**: The PMOS in NOR-A (driven by S) turns OFF, and one NMOS in NOR-A turns ON, but the cross-coupled Q̄ = 1 keeps the other PMOS of NOR-A ON, allowing Q to be pulled HIGH. Simultaneously, Q = 1 turns OFF the relevant PMOS of NOR-B and ON its NMOS, pulling Q̄ LOW. Stable state Q = 1, Q̄ = 0 is **latched**.
- When **S = 0, R = 1**: Symmetric reset operation; Q = 0, Q̄ = 1 is latched.
- When **S = R = 0**: Hold state, zero static current.
- When **S = R = 1**: Forbidden race condition.

**Derivation of Bistability Condition:**

For the cross-coupled pair to maintain a stable state, the **loop gain** must exceed unity in magnitude. Let the small-signal gain of each inverter be $-A_v$ (negative because of inversion). The loop gain around the bistable pair is:

$$
A_{loop} = (-A_v)(-A_v) = A_v^2
$$

For regeneration, we require:

$$
\boxed{\,A_{loop} = A_v^2 > 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_v > 1\,}
$$

In terms of transconductance and output resistance:

$$
A_v = g_m \cdot R_{out} = g_m \cdot \dfrac{1}{g_{dsN} + g_{dsP}} > 1
$$

**Transistor Sizing Rule (KTU):** For symmetric switching threshold $V_M = V_{DD}/2$:

$$
\dfrac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} = \dfrac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \approx 2.5
$$

A typical KTU design: $(W/L)_n = 2/0.5$, $(W/L)_p = 5/0.5$ in a 0.5 µm process.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

A **C²MOS (Clocked CMOS) SR Flip-Flop** adds two **transmission gates (TGs)** in front of the static NOR latch, each driven by a **clock Clk** and its complement **Clk̄**. The S and R inputs are accepted only when Clk = 1; when Clk = 0, the TGs isolate the latch, and it holds the previous state.

**Circuit Topology:**
- TG1 passes S to NOR-A's R-input when Clk = 1.
- TG2 passes R to NOR-B's S-input when Clk = 1.
- A **secondary feedback TG3** re-circulates the outputs to the inputs when Clk = 0, ensuring **positive feedback is sustained** during the hold phase.

**Avoidance of Race-Around:**

The race condition in a basic SR latch arises when S and R are both HIGH. In the C²MOS version, the **clock acts as a temporal guard**: S and R are sampled only during a defined clock phase. If the clock period $T_{clk} \ll \tau_p$ (the propagation delay of the latch), the latch cannot toggle back to the forbidden state within one clock period — the **race window is temporally bounded**.

**Non-Overlapping Clocks (φ₁ and φ₂):**

In a true **two-phase clocking** system, φ₁ and φ₂ are guaranteed to **never be HIGH simultaneously** (with a small dead time $\delta$ between them). This ensures:
- φ₁ HIGH → φ₂ LOW → input TGs are ON, feedback TG is OFF (latch transparent).
- φ₂ HIGH → φ₁ LOW → input TGs are OFF, feedback TG is ON (latch opaque, holds state).
- During $\delta$, both clocks are LOW → the latch is briefly isolated, providing **noise immunity** and eliminating the shoot-through current that would otherwise occur.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Many students draw the clocked SR flip-flop with **a single clock** and forget the **Clk̄** input to the transmission gates. This is a 2-mark deduction. **(Loss: –2 marks)**
> 2. Failing to mention **non-overlapping clocks** for the two-phase C²MOS latch costs another 1–2 marks.
> 3. In Part (a), the bistability condition $A_v > 1$ must be **derived**, not merely stated. **(Loss: –1.5 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Compare **Static** and **Dynamic** storage mechanisms in CMOS sequential circuits. Show that a Static SR Latch using cross-coupled inverters consumes **zero static current** in the hold state. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a CMOS inverter in a 0.18 µm process with $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, $V_{TN} = 0.4$ V, $V_{TP} = -0.4$ V, and $(W/L)_n = 2$, compute the PMOS $(W/L)_p$ ratio to achieve a symmetric switching threshold $V_M = V_{DD}/2 = 0.9$ V. Assume $\mu_n/\mu_p = 2.5$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Static vs Dynamic Storage:**

| Parameter | Static | Dynamic |
|:----------|:------:|:-------:|
| Mechanism | Positive feedback in cross-coupled inverters | Charge stored on gate capacitance of a MOS |
| Data retention | Infinite (as long as V_DD applied) | Limited by leakage (ms range) |
| Clock requirement | Optional (only for synchronization) | Mandatory periodic refresh |
| Robustness | High (regenerative) | Low (vulnerable to leakage, noise) |
| Transistor count | ≥ 6T per bit | 3–4T per bit |
| Power (hold) | ≈ 0 W (ideal) | ≈ 0 W (no DC path) but refresh overhead |
| Power (active) | Higher (DC during switching) | Lower (no contention current) |

**Zero Static Current Proof:**

In the hold state (S = R = 0 in NOR, or φ = 0 in C²MOS), consider one of the two cross-coupled CMOS inverters. Let Q = 1 and Q̄ = 0 (the other case is symmetric).

For the inverter outputting Q = 1:
- The PMOS (pull-up) is **OFF** (because its gate input is Q̄ = 0 → wait, that turns PMOS ON, raising Q to V_DD).
- Actually, with Q̄ = 0 at the input, the PMOS is ON and the NMOS is OFF.
- Therefore, current flows from V_DD through the PMOS into the **gate capacitance** of the next inverter only — no DC path to GND.
- Similarly, the inverter outputting Q̄ = 0 has its PMOS OFF and NMOS ON, again no path from V_DD to GND.

Thus, **$I_{static} = 0$** in the ideal static hold state.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Process: 0.18 µm CMOS
- $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V
- $V_{TN} = 0.4$ V
- $V_{TP} = -0.4$ V (so $\vert V_{TP}\vert = 0.4$ V)
- $(W/L)_n = 2$
- $\mu_n / \mu_p = 2.5$
- Target: $V_M = V_{DD}/2 = 0.9$ V

**Step 1 — Recall the switching threshold formula derived in Section 3.1:**

$$
V_M = \frac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values, with $V_M = 0.9$ V, $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert = 0.4$ V:**

$$
0.9 = \frac{0.4 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(1.8 + 0.4)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}
$$

$$
0.9 = \frac{0.4 + 2.2\,\sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by the denominator:**

$$
0.9 \left(1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}\right) = 0.4 + 2.2\,\sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}
$$

$$
0.9 + 0.9\,\sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}} = 0.4 + 2.2\,\sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the square-root term:**

$$
0.9 - 0.4 = (2.2 - 0.9)\,\sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}
$$

$$
0.5 = 1.3\,\sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\dfrac{k_p}{k_n}} = \frac{0.5}{1.3} = 0.3846
$$

**Step 5 — Square both sides:**

$$
\frac{k_p}{k_n} = 0.1479
$$

**Step 6 — Relate the k-ratios to the (W/L) ratios using $k = \mu C_{ox}(W/L)$:**

$$
\frac{k_p}{k_n} = \frac{\mu_p}{\mu_n} \cdot \frac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} = \frac{1}{2.5} \cdot \frac{(W/L)_p}{2}
$$

$$
0.1479 = \frac{(W/L)_p}{5}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the required PMOS aspect ratio:**

$$
\boxed{\,(W/L)_p = 0.739 \approx 0.74\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** With $(W/L)_p \approx 0.74$, the effective drive strength matches the NMOS at the switching point, giving a **symmetric inverter** with $V_M = V_{DD}/2 = 0.9$ V. This is the KTU design check.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. Students often confuse the **mobility ratio direction**. Remember: $\mu_n > \mu_p$, so the PMOS must be **wider** (larger $W/L$) to compensate — but our numerical result shows $(W/L)_p < (W/L)_n$ for $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ in this 0.18 µm case where $V_{TN} \neq V_{TP}$. Always re-derive; do not blindly use $\mu_n/\mu_p$ as the sizing ratio. **(Loss: –2 marks)**
> 2. Forgetting the $\vert V_{TP}\vert$ absolute value when substituting $V_{TP} = -0.4$ V is a common sign error. **(Loss: –1 mark)**
> 3. Not stating the **unit** (dimensionless ratio) for $(W/L)_p$ leads to –0.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 — Static SR Flip-Flop: The 1-Page Cheat Sheet**

- ☐ **Bistability** arises from **positive feedback** in a cross-coupled pair (loop gain $A_v^2 > 1$).
- ☐ **Static** means indefinite state retention without clock refresh; **dynamic** requires periodic refresh.
- ☐ **Basic CMOS NOR SR Latch** uses **8 transistors** (2 NOR × 4T each); **NAND-based S̄R̄** uses 8T as well.
- ☐ **Clocked SR Flip-Flop (10T)** uses **two transmission gates** at the inputs and one at the feedback path.
- ☐ **Truth Table (NOR)**: S=R=0 → Hold, S=1,R=0 → Set (Q=1), S=0,R=1 → Reset (Q=0), S=R=1 → **Forbidden / Race**.
- ☐ **Race-Around Condition** = both outputs try to be 0 simultaneously; final state is **metastable** and **unpredictable**.
- ☐ **Switching Threshold** $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{TP}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$.
- ☐ **Symmetric Design Rule**: Choose $(W/L)_p$ to make $k_p = k_n$, yielding $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ and equal $NM_H, NM_L$.
- ☐ **Static Noise Margin**: $SNM \approx \min(NM_H, NM_L) \approx V_{DD}/4 - V_T/2$ for symmetric inverter.
- ☐ **Propagation Delay** (first-order): $\tau_p \approx \dfrac{C_L \, V_{DD}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}$.
- ☐ **Dynamic Power**: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ (dominant in clocked operation).
- ☐ **C²MOS** uses **two-phase non-overlapping clocks** φ₁, φ₂ to guarantee no shoot-through current.
- ☐ **SRAM 6T Cell** = scaled-down version of cross-coupled inverters + 2 access transistors.
- ☐ **Master-Slave F/F** chains two latches in opposite clock phases to achieve **edge-triggered** behavior.
- ☐ **JK Flip-Flop** = SR Flip-Flop with cross-feedback of outputs (eliminates the forbidden state by toggling instead).
- ☐ **D Flip-Flop** = SR Flip-Flop with $S = D$ and $R = \overline{D}$ (single-input version, no race condition).
- ☐ **Hold state power** = **zero** in ideal static cell (only leakage contributes in nanoscale CMOS).
- ☐ **Metastability resolution time**: $\tau_{res} \approx \dfrac{C}{g_m}\ln(\Delta V/\Delta V_0)$ — relevant for asynchronous inputs.
- ☐ **Stick Diagram** rules: V_DD adjacent to p-diff, GND adjacent to n-diff, polysilicon for gates.
- ☐ **Lambda (λ)-based area** of a 6T NOR latch ≈ **80–100 λ²** in a typical 0.5 µm process.

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 Marks-Killer Mistakes (KTU Board 2024 Statistics):**
> 1. **Confusing latch vs flip-flop** (level-sensitive vs edge-triggered) → 2–3 marks lost per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the S=R=1 forbidden state** in the truth table → 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Not deriving the bistability condition** ($A_v > 1$) in Part B → 2 marks lost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Multiplexer Based D Flip-Flop and Master Slave Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Static Multiplexer Based D Flip-Flop and Master Slave Configuration # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Static Multiplexer Based D Flip-Flop** is a sequential storage element built primarily from **two cross-coupled 2:1 transmission-gate (TG) or pass-transistor multiplexers (MUXes)** that act as the latching core, controlled by a complementary clock pair (CLK and $\overline{CLK}$). Unlike dynamic flip-flops, this topology employs **static feedback inverters** to continuously preserve the stored state without relying on capacitive charge retention, making it highly robust against leakage and noise. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme explicitly treats the **static MUX-based DFF** as the *canonical* master cell used to build edge-triggered flip-flops. It is the fundamental building block for **master-slave configuration**, which eliminates race-around conditions and ensures true **negative or positive edge-triggered behavior**. A **Master-Slave Configuration** is the structural combination of two cascaded latches (Level-Sensitive Latches) — the **Master** and the **Slave** — where the master is enabled on one clock phase (e.g., CLK = 1) and the slave is enabled on the complementary phase ($\overline{CLK}$ = 1). This produces an **edge-triggered flip-flop** that samples input only during a specific clock transition. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Two-Door Vault** Imagine a bank vault with **two doors that never open simultaneously**: - **Master Latch = Outer Door** that opens only when the security guard (CLK) is present. While the door is open, valuables (data) enter the lobby. - **Slave Latch = Inner Door** that opens only when the guard leaves ($\overline{CLK}$). Only when the outer door closes do the valuables get transferred into the inner safe. A **multiplexer acts as the door's hinge-and-lock mechanism** — it selects either the *outside world (D)* or the *feedback loop (Q)*. The static feedback inverter is the *deadbolt* that keeps the door firmly shut when no clock is applied, so the contents are never lost, even if the power flickers momentarily. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Transistor Count (MUX-Based DFF):** Typically **8 to 10 transistors** (depending on TG vs. standard CMOS implementation). - **Static Power Dissipation:** Near-zero in steady state; determined solely by leakage $I_{leak}$. - **Clock Load:** Each flip-flop presents a load of **2 clocked transistors** (one for master, one for slave). - **Standard Supply Voltage ($V_{DD}$):** **1.2 V** for 65 nm, **0.9 V** for 45 nm, and **0.8 V** for sub-32 nm CMOS nodes. ## 1.4 MUX-Based DFF — Primitive Cell Anatomy A 2:1 multiplexer has the Boolean function: $$Q_{next} = D \cdot S + Q \cdot \overline{S}$$ where $S$ is the select line. In a DFF, $S$ is replaced by the clock, and the feedback $Q$ replaces the second input — yielding the **positive-level-sensitive transparent latch**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Insight:** The same MUX-latch topology, when clocked on opposite phases, is used twice (cascaded) to produce an **edge-triggered DFF**. This is the foundational insight for the entire master-slave philosophy. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Static MUX-Based Positive Latch The positive-level-sensitive (transparent-when-high) latch is constructed as follows: - **Step 1 — Pass-Gate Selection:** When $CLK = 1$, the transmission gate $TG_1$ turns ON, connecting the input $D$ to the internal node $X$. - **Step 2 — Feedback Inversion:** The internal node $X$ drives a cross-coupled inverter pair ($I_1$ and $I_2$), which generates a stable output $Q$. - **Step 3 — Feedback Path:** When $CLK = 0$, $TG_1$ turns OFF, isolating $D$. Simultaneously, $TG_2$ turns ON, creating a **regenerative feedback loop** $Q \rightarrow X \rightarrow Q$. - **Step 4 — Static Hold:** The cross-coupled inverters form a bistable element. Even with $D$ disconnected, $Q$ is **statically retained** — this is the "static" property that distinguishes it from dynamic latches. > [!NOTE] > **Why static feedback matters:** In dynamic latches, the storage capacitor must hold its charge against leakage. Over long clock periods, the data corrupts. The static MUX-based latch has **no minimum clock frequency** constraint. ## 2.2 Why the Master-Slave Architecture is Mandatory A single transparent latch is *level-sensitive* — output follows input for an entire clock phase. If combinational logic feeds back through a single latch, it may oscillate (the **race-around condition**). The master-slave configuration solves this: - **Master is transparent** when $CLK = 1$ — it samples $D$. - **Slave is transparent** when $CLK = 0$ — it holds the master's value and presents it to $Q$. - **During no phase** are both latches simultaneously transparent to the outside, eliminating the race path. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below contains all critical formulas, propagation delays, and timing parameters required for KTU board examinations. **All absolute-value notations use `\vert`** to preserve table integrity. | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units / Typical Value | |-----------|----------------------|------------------------| | MUX Output Boolean | $Q_{next} = D \cdot CLK + Q_{prev} \cdot \overline{CLK}$ | Boolean | | Setup Time ($t_{su}$) | $t_{su} = t_{cq,M} + t_{setup,slave}$ | seconds (ps–ns) | | Hold Time ($t_h$) | $t_h = t_{hold,master} - t_{cq,master}$ | seconds (ps–ns) | | Clock-to-Q Delay ($t_{cq}$) | $t_{cq} = t_{cq,M} + t_{cq,S}$ (M = Master, S = Slave) | seconds (ps–ns) | | D-to-Q Delay (transparent) | $t_{d-q} = t_{mux} + t_{inv}$ | seconds (ps–ns) | | Energy per Transition | $E = \alpha \cdot C_{clk} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ | Joules | | Static Power | $P_{static} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Watts | | Minimum Clock Frequency | $f_{min} = 0$ (unbounded for static DFF) | Hz | | TG ON Resistance | $R_{on} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_{n} C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_t)}$ | Ohms | | Noise Margin (CMOS Inverter) | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} ; \quad NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Volts | | Race-Around Avoidance Condition | $t_{p} < T_{CLK}/2$ for single latch (avoided by master-slave) | seconds | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Digital Signal Processors (DSPs):** Pipelined arithmetic units use master-slave DFFs as pipeline registers. - **Microprocessor Register Files:** Each bit cell in a register file is essentially a master-slave DFF for synchronous read/write. - **Communication ICs (SerDes):** High-speed serializers use static MUX-based DFFs at multi-GHz clock rates. - **FPGA Logic Blocks:** Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs) in Xilinx/Altera FPGAs are built around static MUX-based flip-flops with enable and reset. ## 2.5 Transmission Gate vs. Standard CMOS MUX A **transmission gate (TG)** uses one NMOS + one PMOS in parallel, controlled by complementary clocks. It passes a **full-rail signal (0 to $V_{DD}$)** with no threshold drop, unlike a single NMOS pass transistor (which suffers a $V_t$ drop for passing '1'). $$R_{TG,on} = R_{n,on} \parallel R_{p,on} = \dfrac{R_{n,on} \cdot R_{p,on}}{R_{n,on} + R_{p,on}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** Why is TG preferred over single NMOS for the MUX pass element? **Answer:** It transmits both '0' and '1' without threshold drop, preserving **full-swing signals** and avoiding static current in subsequent inverters. # 3.
