> 3. **Misapplying Checkpoint Theorem:** The theorem applies to **single** stuck-at faults. For multiple faults, it gives *sufficient* (not necessary) coverage.
> 4. **Wrong exponent in DL formula:** A common error is writing `DL = 1 - Y^FC`. The correct form is `DL = 1 - Y^(1-FC)`. Remember: the *uncovered* fraction `(1-FC)` is the exponent.
> 5. **In D-algorithm, setting the wrong non-controlling value:** AND requires the other input to be **1**, OR requires the other input to be **0**. Mixing this up causes propagation to fail.
> 6. **Skipping the "Verify" step:** Always plug the test vector back into both good and faulty circuits to confirm mismatch — KTU evaluators deduct 1–2 marks if verification is missing.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Stuck-At Faults:**

- ✅ **Stuck-At fault** = signal line permanently fixed to 0 or 1; denoted as `line/s-a-v`.
- ✅ **SSF count = 2N**, **MSF count = 3^N − 1** (each line has 3 states).
- ✅ **Industry standard model is SSF**, justified by the **Checkpoint Theorem**.
- ✅ **Fault equivalence** = faulty functions identical; tests overlap completely (bidirectional).
- ✅ **Fault dominance** = one fault's test set is a *proper subset* of the other's (unidirectional).
- ✅ **Equivalence collapsing** keeps **one representative** per class (gives ~N faults).
- ✅ **Dominance collapsing** discards the **dominated** fault (keeps the dominant one).
- ✅ **Checkpoint Theorem:** Testing SSFs on all **PIs + fan-out branches** covers the entire circuit.
- ✅ **Fault Coverage** `FC = (F_detected / F_total) × 100%`. Target ≥ 99 % for high quality.
- ✅ **Defect Level** `DL = 1 − Y^(1−FC)`. Industry target ≤ 100 DPM.
- ✅ **D-Algorithm steps:** Fault Activation → D-Propagation → Line Justification → Verification.
- ✅ **AND gate** propagating `D`: other input = **1** (non-controlling).
- ✅ **OR gate** propagating `D`: other input = **0** (non-controlling).
- ✅ **NAND/NOR** invert the `D` notation to `D'` or `\bar D` during propagation.
- ✅ **For NAND output s-a-1 ≡ each input s-a-0** (key equivalence rule).
- ✅ **For NOR output s-a-0 ≡ each input s-a-1** (key equivalence rule).
- ✅ **ATPG tools (TetraMAX, FastScan)** all internally use the SSF model.
- ✅ **DFT techniques** (scan, BIST, boundary scan) exist primarily to make SA faults *controllable* and *observable*.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** When you see a fault-modeling question, **always** (1) list the lines, (2) compute `2N`, (3) apply equivalence rules, (4) apply dominance rules, (5) report the collapsed count, (6) compute `FC` and `DL` if numerical data is given. Following this six-step template guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Test Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **VLSI System Design**, a **Test Procedure** is a formally defined, ordered sequence of operations—comprising *test specification*, *test generation*, *test simulation*, *test application*, and *diagnosis*—executed on a fabricated chip (Device Under Test, **DUT**) using an **Automatic Test Equipment (ATE)** to detect, locate, and characterize physical or logical defects introduced during semiconductor manufacturing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner's Wording):**
> A test procedure is the engineering protocol that translates a circuit-under-test (CUT) behavioural model into a deterministic, time-stamped pattern of input stimuli and expected output responses, executed under controlled environmental conditions to validate conformance to the design specification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Master Health Checkup"

Imagine every chip coming out of a fabrication plant as a **newborn baby** that must pass a standardized health checkup before being released to customers:

| Hospital Step | VLSI Test Procedure Equivalent | Purpose |
|---------------|-------------------------------|---------|
| Patient registration & ID logging | **Handler docking & DUT identification** | Track the chip through the test line |
| Doctor's questionnaire (symptoms) | **Test vector application (stimuli)** | Probe the chip's responses |
| Blood test / X-ray reports | **Response capture & comparison** | Detect hidden defects |
| Specialist referral (if abnormal) | **Fault diagnosis & binning** | Classify failures |
| Final fitness certificate | **Pass/Fail decision & binning** | Decide ship / reject |
| Health record archival | **STDF / datalog storage** | Post-silicon yield learning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** A test procedure is *not* the same as a test vector. A **test vector** is a single input/output pattern, whereas a **test procedure** is the *complete framework*—hardware setup, vector ordering, timing, environmental limits, and pass/fail criteria—governing how vectors are applied.

## 1.3 Standard Test Metrics (KTU Board-Weighted Vocabulary)

- **Fault Coverage (FC)** — percentage of modelled faults detected
- **Defect Level (DL)** — number of defective parts per million (DPPM) shipped
- **Test Application Time (TAT)** — total seconds spent per chip on the ATE
- **Test Cost (C<sub>test</sub>)** — economic footprint of testing in the product lifecycle
- **Yield (Y)** — fraction of die passing all tests
- **Controllability & Observability** — ease of forcing/observing internal signals

> [!TIP]
> Examiners in KTU frequently award marks for explicitly using these exact terms. Memorize the **bold** metric names verbatim.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Phases of a VLSI Test Procedure

A KTU-compliant test procedure is decomposed into **five sequential phases**. Each phase produces a specific deliverable that feeds the next.

### Phase 1 — Test Specification
- Translates the **design-for-test (DFT)** architecture (e.g., scan chains, BIST, boundary scan) into a written document.
- Specifies: number of test pins, voltage/current limits, clock domain handling, expected test length, coverage targets.
- Deliverable: **Test Specification Document (TSD)**.

### Phase 2 — Test Generation
- Algorithmic or fault-simulation-based creation of input stimuli.
- Tools: **ATPG (Automatic Test Pattern Generator)** — e.g., D-algorithm, PODEM, FAN.
- Output: a set of **test vectors (test patterns)** stored in tester memory (e.g., STIL, WGL, VCD formats).

### Phase 3 — Test Simulation
- Verifies that the generated vectors actually detect the intended faults using a **fault simulator** (parallel, deductive, or concurrent).
- Produces a **fault coverage report** and **test grading curve**.

### Phase 4 — Test Application (Production Test)
- Physical execution on the ATE.
- Steps:
  1. **Contact test** (probe card or socket) — verify continuity.
  2. **DC parametric test** — supply current (I<sub>DDQ</sub>), voltage levels, leakage.
  3. **AC functional test** — apply test vectors at rated clock speed.
  4. **At-speed test** — verify timing closure under transition delay.
  5. **Pass/Fail binning** — sort dies.

### Phase 5 — Diagnosis & Failure Analysis (Off-line)
- Uses **failure bitmaps** and **adaptive diagnosis** to localize defect to a logic region.
- Feeds back to foundry for **process yield learning**.

## 2.2 Operational Logic Flow (Bullet Breakdown)

- **Why** Phase 1 first? → Without a specification, vector generation is aimless and untestable in production.
- **Why** Phase 2 before Phase 3? → You must generate *something* before you can simulate its effectiveness.
- **Why** Phase 4 after Phase 3? → Simulation is software-only; production test is the real-world validation.
- **How** does each phase consume the previous one's output? → A linear dependency: TSD → ATPG vectors → Coverage report → ATE program → Failure logs.
- **Where** is the bottleneck? → **Test data volume** and **ATE memory depth** — modern SoCs produce >Gb test vectors.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation a KTU 2024 student is expected to recognize for the Test Procedure topic. **Avoid using the pipe `|` character inside table cells — use `\vert` instead.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $FC = \dfrac{N_{det}}{N_{tot}} \times 100\%$ | Fault Coverage | %; N<sub>det</sub> = detected faults, N<sub>tot</sub> = total modelled faults |
| 2 | $N_{ssaf} = 2n$ | Number of single stuck-at faults | dimensionless; for $n$ signal lines in combinational logic |
| 3 | $DL = 1 - Y^{\,(1-N)}$ | Defect Level (Williams–Brown) | DPPM; Y = yield, N = average # faults per chip |
| 4 | $T_{test} = \dfrac{N_{vec} \cdot P}{f_{clk}}$ | Test Application Time | seconds; P = pattern length (bits), f<sub>clk</sub> = tester clock |
| 5 | $C_{test} = C_{ATE} \cdot T_{test} + C_{prog} + C_{handler}$ | Total Test Cost | USD; C<sub>ATE</sub> = per-second ATE cost, C<sub>prog</sub> = engineering, C<sub>handler</sub> = handler amortized |
| 6 | $S = \dfrac{1}{N_{vec}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{vec}} w_i$ | Test compaction ratio | $w_i$ = weight (0 or 1) of pattern $i$ |
| 7 | $E_{energy} = V_{DD} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N_{vec}} I_i \cdot \Delta t_i$ | Test Energy | Joules; relevant for low-power test |
| 8 | $C_{obs}, C_{ctrl} \in [0,1]$ | Controllability/Observability scores | SCOAP-style; 0 = hard, 1 = easy |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Yield Engineering:** Test procedures feed the **Williams–Brown defect level formula** to estimate shipped quality, which directly affects warranty cost and customer trust.
- **Automotive ICs (ISO 26262):** Mandate **near 100% fault coverage** and **multi-temperature (cold/hot/room) test procedures**.
- **AI/ML Accelerator Chips:** Demand **at-speed scan test** and **burn-in procedures** to catch infant mortality.
- **Space-grade ICs:** Use **radiation-hardened test procedures** with **TID (Total Ionizing Dose)** and **SEU (Single Event Upset)** characterization.
- **Economics:** Test cost often equals **15–30% of total IC manufacturing cost** — motivating **DFT-driven test procedure optimization**.

## 2.5 KTU Examiner Tip — Test Procedure vs. Test Methodology

> [!WARNING]
> Students frequently confuse **Test Procedure** (the operational sequence on ATE) with **Test Methodology** (e.g., BIST, scan, Iddq — the design-time strategy). In the exam: *Procedure = HOW* you test; *Methodology = WHAT* strategy you built in. Writing BIST under "Test Procedure" loses marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fault Coverage from a Fault Simulation Report

**Given:**
- A combinational block with $n = 500$ signal lines.
- A single stuck-at fault (SSAF) model is adopted.
- An ATPG tool reports that out of all possible faults, $N_{det} = 980$ were detected by a vector set of $N_{vec} = 240$ patterns.

**Find:** Fault Coverage $FC$.

**Step 1 — Total modelled faults.**

$$N_{tot} = 2n = 2 \times 500 = 1000$$

**Step 2 — Plug into coverage formula.**

$$FC = \frac{N_{det}}{N_{tot}} \times 100\% = \frac{980}{1000} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$FC = 0.98 \times 100\% = 98\%$$

**Conclusion:** The test procedure achieves **98% single stuck-at fault coverage**, which is the industry-accepted minimum for digital logic blocks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Defect Level from Williams–Brown Equation

**Given:**
- A chip's average yield $Y = 0.85$ (i.e., 85%).
- The test procedure achieves $FC = 98\%$, so undetected fault fraction is $1 - FC = 0.02$.
- Assume average number of faults per chip at un-fault-free state $N = 0.5$ (Poisson model).

**Find:** Defect Level $DL$ in DPPM (defective parts per million).

**Step 1 — Recall Williams–Brown formula.**

$$DL = 1 - Y^{\,(1-N)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$DL = 1 - (0.85)^{\,(1 - 0.5)} = 1 - (0.85)^{0.5}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponent.**

$$(0.85)^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.85} = 0.92195$$

**Step 4 — Subtract from 1.**

$$DL = 1 - 0.92195 = 0.07805$$

**Step 5 — Convert to DPPM.**

$$DL_{ppm} = 0.07805 \times 10^{6} \approx 78{,}050 \text{ DPPM}$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **78,050 defective parts per million** would escape the test if the yield remains at 85% and only 98% coverage is achieved. This is far above the **< 100 DPPM** target demanded by automotive OEMs — proving why test procedures must push coverage above 99% and yield must improve concurrently.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Test Application Time Calculation

**Given:**
- $N_{vec} = 10{,}000$ scan vectors.
- Each scan pattern length $P = 200$ bits (200 flip-flops in scan chain).
- ATE tester clock $f_{clk} = 100$ MHz.

**Find:** Test Application Time $T_{test}$.

**Step 1 — Recall formula.**

$$T_{test} = \frac{N_{vec} \cdot P}{f_{clk}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$T_{test} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 200}{100 \times 10^{6}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$N_{vec} \cdot P = 10{,}000 \times 200 = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ clock cycles}$$

**Step 4 — Final division.**

$$T_{test} = \frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{10^{8}} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** Even with 10k vectors, the ATE finishes in **20 ms** — but the ATE cost amortized over millions of dies per month makes this commercially significant.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose Which Test Procedure Type

| Chip Class | Recommended Test Procedure | Coverage Target | Cost Driver |
|------------|---------------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| Consumer MCU | Scan + functional at-speed | ≥ 95% | ATE time |
| Automotive ECU | Scan + Iddq + BIST + burn-in | ≥ 99% | Multi-corner |
| Server CPU | Scan + at-speed + BIST + SLT | ≥ 99.5% | Test data volume |
| RF / Mixed-signal | Functional + spec-driven | Spec-dependent | RF test head |
| Memory (DRAM/NAND) | March algorithms + redundancy repair | ≥ 99.9% | BISR + ECC |
| Space / Mil-grade | Radiation + burn-in + screening | ≥ 99.99% | Hermeticity + TID |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix appears in KTU Module 4 expected-answer rubrics. Drawing it in the exam earns full method marks even without numerical examples.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Canonical VLSI Test Procedure Lifecycle

The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture flow** of the five-phase test procedure, illustrating inputs/outputs at each stage and feedback loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Design Specification] --> A2[DFT Architecture Insertion]
    A2 --> A3[Netlist with Scan/BIST/BSCAN]
    A3 --> B1[Phase 1: Test Specification Document]
    B1 --> C1[Phase 2: ATPG Test Generation]
    C1 --> C2[Test Vectors STIL or WGL]
    C2 --> D1[Phase 3: Fault Simulation]
    D1 --> D2[Fault Coverage Report]
    D2 --> D3{Coverage Target Met?}
    D3 -- No --> C1
    D3 -- Yes --> E1[Phase 4: ATE Program Conversion]
    E1 --> E2[ATE Test Program]
    E2 --> E3[Wafer Prober or Final Test Handler]
    E3 --> E4[Phase 4b: DC Parametric Test]
    E4 --> E5[Phase 4c: AC Functional At-Speed Test]
    E5 --> E6{Bin: Pass or Fail}
    E6 -- Pass --> F1[Ship to Customer]
    E6 -- Fail --> G1[Phase 5: Failure Diagnosis]
    G1 --> G2[Failure Bitmap to Foundry]
    G2 --> G3[Process Yield Learning]
    G3 --> A1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — ATE-Handled Test Procedure Sub-Architecture

This block diagram details the **internal architecture of the test procedure as it executes on the ATE**, showing the role of the **tester channels, DUT board, and pattern sequencer**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[ATE Pattern Memory] --> H2[Vector Sequencer]
    H2 --> H3[Pin Electronics Driver Comparator]
    H3 --> H4[Load Board]
    H4 --> H5[DUT Socket or Probe Card]
    H5 --> H6[Device Under Test]
    H6 --> H4
    H4 --> H3
    H3 --> H7[Pass Fail Latch]
    H7 --> H8[Bin Classifier]
    H8 --> H9[Reject Bin]
    H8 --> H10[Pass Bin Ship]
    H1 --> H11[Timing Generator]
    H11 --> H3
    H2 --> H12[STDF Datalog]
    H12 --> H13[Yield Analysis Server]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Vector Application Timeline

The following diagram visualizes the **temporal order** of vector application, response capture, and result comparison — a common KTU short-answer item.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T as ATE Tester
    participant D as DUT
    participant M as Memory
    T->>M: Load Test Vector V1
    T->>D: Apply Stimulus at t0
    D-->>T: Capture Response at t0+Δ
    T->>T: Compare vs Expected
    T->>M: Store Pass/Fail Bit
    T->>M: Load Test Vector V2
    T->>D: Apply Stimulus at t1
    D-->>T: Capture Response at t1+Δ
    T->>T: Compare vs Expected
    T->>M: Store Pass/Fail Bit
    Note over T,D: Repeat for all N_vec vectors
    T->>T: Compute Fault Coverage and Bin
```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU answer sheets, even a hand-drawn version of the **5-phase test procedure flowchart** (Spec → Generate → Simulate → Apply → Diagnose) earns 2–3 marks automatically as a structure question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Test Procedure" in VLSI design. List its five canonical phases.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Test Procedure** in VLSI is the formally defined, ordered sequence of operations executed on a fabricated chip using an **Automatic Test Equipment (ATE)** to validate conformance to the design specification.

The five canonical phases are:
1. **Test Specification** — defining TSD inputs/outputs, pin map, DFT interface.
2. **Test Generation** — ATPG-driven creation of test patterns.
3. **Test Simulation** — fault simulation to grade coverage.
4. **Test Application** — DC parametric + AC functional at-speed testing on ATE.
5. **Diagnosis & Failure Analysis** — failure bitmap generation and feedback to the foundry.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for listing, 1 for correctness of phase names.)*

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Test Procedure, Test Vector, and Test Methodology with one-line definitions.**

**Model Answer:**

| Term | One-line Definition |
|------|---------------------|
| **Test Procedure** | The ordered operational protocol (specification → generation → application → diagnosis) executed on the ATE. |
| **Test Vector** | A single input stimulus and its corresponding expected output response applied to the DUT. |
| **Test Methodology** | The design-time DFT strategy (e.g., scan, BIST, boundary scan) embedded in the chip architecture. |

*(3 marks: 1 per correct differentiation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each part is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Solutions must be **rigorous and stepwise** to score full marks.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the five phases of a VLSI test procedure with their inputs, outputs, and engineering purpose.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A combinational logic block has $n = 800$ signal lines. An ATPG tool generates 5,000 test vectors and detects 1,540 single stuck-at faults.
   (i) Compute the **fault coverage**.
   (ii) If the chip yield is $Y = 0.90$ and average fault count $N = 0.4$, calculate the **defect level (DL) in DPPM** using the Williams–Brown equation.
   (iii) Comment on whether this test procedure is acceptable for **automotive-grade ICs** (target DL < 100 DPPM).

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (a)

The **five phases of a VLSI test procedure** are:

**Phase 1 — Test Specification**
- **Input:** Design spec, DFT architecture, pin map, timing constraints.
- **Output:** Test Specification Document (TSD).
- **Purpose:** Define WHAT will be tested, under WHAT conditions, with WHAT resources.

**Phase 2 — Test Generation**
- **Input:** Gate-level netlist, fault list, TSD.
- **Tool:** ATPG (D-algorithm, PODEM, FAN).
- **Output:** Test vector set in STIL/WGL/VCD format.
- **Purpose:** Produce minimum-pattern set that maximizes fault detection.

**Phase 3 — Test Simulation**
- **Input:** Test vectors, fault list, netlist.
- **Tool:** Fault simulator (parallel/deductive/concurrent).
- **Output:** Fault coverage report + grading curve.
- **Purpose:** Verify effectiveness *before* expensive ATE time.

**Phase 4 — Test Application**
- **Input:** ATE test program, DUT on handler/prober.
- **Steps:** Contact → DC parametric → AC functional at-speed → binning.
- **Output:** Pass/fail classification + STDF log.
- **Purpose:** Real-world execution and yield sorting.

**Phase 5 — Diagnosis & Failure Analysis**
- **Input:** Failing bitmaps, STDF logs.
- **Output:** Defect localization report, process feedback.
- **Purpose:** Yield ramp learning.

*(7 marks: 1.4 marks per phase, partial credit for partial coverage.)*

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Fault Coverage**

**Step 1 — Total faults.**

$$N_{tot} = 2n = 2 \times 800 = 1600$$

**Step 2 — Apply FC formula.**

$$FC = \frac{1540}{1600} \times 100\% = 96.25\%$$

**(ii) Defect Level**

**Step 1 — Apply Williams–Brown.**

$$DL = 1 - Y^{\,(1-N)} = 1 - (0.9)^{\,(1-0.4)}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate exponent.**

$$1 - N = 0.6$$
$$(0.9)^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \cdot \ln(0.9)} = e^{0.6 \cdot (-0.10536)} = e^{-0.06321} = 0.93874$$

**Step 3 — Subtract.**

$$DL = 1 - 0.93874 = 0.06126$$

**Step 4 — Convert to DPPM.**

$$DL_{ppm} = 0.06126 \times 10^{6} \approx 61{,}260 \text{ DPPM}$$

**(iii) Comment on Automotive Acceptability**

The computed defect level is **~61,260 DPPM**, which is **far above the automotive target of < 100 DPPM** (ISO 26262 / AEC-Q100). Hence, **this test procedure is NOT acceptable for automotive-grade ICs**. Remedial actions:
- Increase fault coverage above 99.5% via additional ATPG patterns or **BIST augmentation**.
- Implement **Iddq testing** to catch bridging defects.
- Apply **multi-temperature testing** (cold, room, hot).
- Consider **redundancy / repair mechanisms** to improve effective yield.

*(7 marks: 2 for (i), 3 for (ii), 2 for (iii) justification.)*

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the role of **Automatic Test Equipment (ATE)** in executing a VLSI test procedure. List its main subsystems and explain the data flow from pattern memory to bin classifier.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A scan-based test procedure has $N_{vec} = 50{,}000$ vectors, each of pattern length $P = 500$ bits, and the ATE runs at $f_{clk} = 200$ MHz.
   (i) Compute the **test application time**.
   (ii) If the ATE cost is $C_{ATE} = \$0.05$ per second, calculate the **test cost per chip** (neglect handler/programming cost).
   (iii) Suggest **two engineering techniques** to reduce the test cost without sacrificing fault coverage.

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (a)

**Role of ATE:** The ATE is the **hardware platform** that physically executes the test procedure. It generates precise input stimuli, captures DUT responses at picosecond timing accuracy, compares them against expected values, and classifies each die as pass or fail.

**Main Subsystems:**
1. **Pattern Memory** — stores millions of test vectors.
2. **Vector Sequencer** — controls pattern order and looping.
3. **Pin Electronics (Driver/Comparator)** — applies and compares signals.
4. **Timing Generator** — provides picosecond-edge timing.
5. **Load Board / Socket** — interfaces the ATE to the DUT.
6. **Pass/Fail Latch** — records per-vector result.
7. **Bin Classifier** — assigns dies to pass/reject bins.
8. **STDF Datalog Generator** — writes standardized test data for yield analysis.

**Data Flow:**
Pattern Memory → Vector Sequencer → Pin Electronics → Load Board → DUT → Load Board → Pin Electronics → Pass/Fail Latch → Bin Classifier.

*(7 marks: 2 for role, 2 for subsystems, 3 for data flow.)*

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Test Application Time**

**Step 1 — Apply formula.**

$$T_{test} = \frac{N_{vec} \cdot P}{f_{clk}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$T_{test} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 500}{200 \times 10^{6}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator.**

$$50{,}000 \times 500 = 2.5 \times 10^{7} \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 4 — Final division.**

$$T_{test} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{7}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 0.125 \text{ s} = 125 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Test Cost per Chip**

**Step 1 — Neglecting handler/programming cost:**

$$C_{test} = C_{ATE} \cdot T_{test} = 0.05 \times 0.125 = \$0.00625$$

$$C_{test} \approx 0.625 \text{ cents per chip}$$

**(iii) Two Techniques to Reduce Test Cost**

1. **Test Compression (e.g., EDT — Embedded Deterministic Test):** Reduces pattern volume by 10×–100× using XOR-based decompressors on-chip, cutting ATE memory and time without losing coverage.
2. **Multi-Site Testing:** Test 4–32 DUTs in parallel on the same ATE, amortizing the per-second tester cost across multiple dies and dramatically reducing per-chip test cost.
3. *(Optional 3rd)* **BIST (Built-In Self-Test):** Eliminates external ATE memory loading for blocks with embedded PRPG/MISR.

*(7 marks: 1.5 for (i), 1.5 for (ii), 4 for (iii) — 2 marks per technique with explanation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing 2–3 Marks Per Answer:**
> 1. **Confusing Test Procedure with Test Methodology** — never write BIST/scan details when asked for "procedure." Stay operational (specification, generation, application, diagnosis).
> 2. **Skipping the units** in numerical answers — examiners award a separate mark for correct units (e.g., **DPPM**, **seconds**, **%**).
> 3. **Forgetting the formula context** — writing $FC = 0.96$ without stating "fault coverage = 96%" loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing Williams–Brown inputs** — using $N$ as "number of vectors" instead of "average faults per chip" gives wildly wrong DL.
> 5. **Omitting the at-speed test step** in Phase 4 — KTU 2024 specifically weights at-speed testing because it catches **delay faults** missed by stuck-at tests.
> 6. **No block diagram** in 7-mark answers — always draw the 5-phase flow; even a rough sketch earns 1 mark for structure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Definition:** Test Procedure = ordered operational protocol executed on ATE to validate manufactured ICs.
- 🔹 **Five Phases:** Specification → Generation → Simulation → Application → Diagnosis.
- 🔹 **Core Formulae (must memorize):**
  - $FC = \dfrac{N_{det}}{N_{tot}} \times 100\%$
  - $N_{ssaf} = 2n$
  - $DL = 1 - Y^{\,(1-N)}$
  - $T_{test} = \dfrac{N_{vec} \cdot P}{f_{clk}}$
  - $C_{test} = C_{ATE} \cdot T_{test} + C_{prog} + C_{handler}$
- 🔹 **ATE Subsystems:** Pattern Memory, Sequencer, Pin Electronics, Timing Generator, Load Board, P/F Latch, Bin Classifier, STDF Datalog.
- 🔹 **Test Procedure ≠ Test Methodology ≠ Test Vector.** Differentiate clearly in Part A answers.
- 🔹 **Always quote units:** %, DPPM, seconds, USD.
- 🔹 **Always include a block diagram** in 7-mark answers to earn structural marks.
- 🔹 **Industry-acceptable coverage:** ≥ 95% consumer, ≥ 99% automotive, ≥ 99.5% server.
- 🔹 **At-speed test is mandatory** for delay-fault detection in modern nodes.
- 🔹 **Cost reduction techniques:** Test compression, multi-site, BIST, Iddq, adaptive test.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logic Testing vs Memory Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Logic Testing vs Memory Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 — Logic Testing vs Memory Testing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Testing (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> *Logic Testing* refers to the process of applying pre-computed test patterns to the **combinational and sequential logic blocks** of a digital IC in order to detect manufacturing defects that manifest as logical faults (e.g., **stuck-at**, **bridging**, **transition**, or **delay faults**). It targets **non-regular, heterogeneous** structures such as ALUs, controllers, datapaths, and finite-state machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Testing (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> *Memory Testing* refers to the process of applying deterministic algorithmic patterns (e.g., **March tests**, **checkerboard**, **galloping patterns**) to the **regular, highly-repetitive memory array** of a digital IC (SRAM, DRAM, ROM, CAM) in order to detect functional faults such as **stuck-at**, **transition**, **coupling**, and **address-decoder faults** in the cell array, sense amplifiers, decoders, and write drivers.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Classroom Exam" 🏫

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture (For a First-Time Reader)**

Imagine a school conducting two different kinds of exams:

- **Logic Testing = A Customised Viva for Each Student**
  Every student (each logic gate / flip-flop) is *unique* and sits in a *different position* in the classroom. The examiner cannot ask everyone the same set of questions — she must **craft a unique question paper for each student** to expose his weakness. The number of students (transistors) is huge, and they are scattered irregularly. This is exactly what **ATPG (Automatic Test Pattern Generation)** does for logic circuits.

- **Memory Testing = A Mass-Scale MCQ Test for Identical Twins**
  All students in a memory array are **identical twins** sitting in an orderly grid (row × column). One carefully-designed set of questions, when applied systematically (say, "up the row, then back down the row"), can catch defects in any twin. Hence a *single short algorithmic pattern* (a **March test**) is enough. The students are uniform, the structure is regular, and the testing can be very efficient.

This analogy is the **single most important intuition** for the whole module: **irregular structure → logic test complexity; regular structure → memory test brevity.**

---

### 1.3 Why Two Different Worlds?

| Property | Logic Block | Memory Block |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Random / irregular | Highly regular (2-D array) |
| **Transistor share of die** | ~30–50 % (modern SoCs) | ~60–70 % (memory-dominated ICs) |
| **Fault density** | Lower | Higher (nano-scale cells) |
| **Test-generation strategy** | ATPG, scan, BIST | March tests, BIST, redundancy repair |
| **Pattern count** | Large (often > N) | Small (often ≤ 4N to 12N) |
| **Fault model** | Stuck-at dominant | Functional Fault Model (FFM) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** Modern SoCs are *memory-dominated*. On a typical smartphone SoC, embedded SRAM can occupy **> 60 %** of the total silicon area. Therefore, **memory testing consumes the dominant share of test time and cost**, even though memory test patterns are themselves very short.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logic Testing — Operational Breakdown

**Why each step exists (The "Why & How"):**

1. **Fault Modelling → Why?** Real defects are infinite in variety (bridges, opens, partial shorts). The industry agreed-upon *canonical model* is the **single stuck-at fault (SSF)** because it:
   - Models ~50 % of physical defects in CMOS.
   - Has polynomial-time ATPG algorithms (D-algorithm, PODEM, FAN, SAT-based).
   - Gives predictable coverage metrics.

2. **ATPG (Automatic Test Pattern Generation) → How?**
   - The algorithm *activates* the fault (forces the line to the opposite value) and *propagates* the fault effect to a primary output (PO).
   - Tools: D-Algorithm (Roth), PODEM, FAN, SOCRATES, **modern SAT-based ATPG**.

3. **Scan Design → Why?** Sequential circuits are too hard to test directly. By converting flip-flops into a long shift-register (**scan chain**), we can:
   - Control/observe internal state from primary I/O.
   - Reduce the sequential test problem to a combinational one.

4. **Fault Coverage Metric → Why?** A single number grades the test quality for management and yield-learning.

5. **BIST (Built-In Self Test) → Why?** External ATE is expensive. Embed a *pseudo-random pattern generator (PRPG)* + *signature analyser (MISR)* + *BIST controller* on-chip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic Test Flow (Standard Industrial Order):**
> `Design → Insert DFT (Scan/BIST) → ATPG → Fault Simulation → Coverage Report → Manufacture → Production Test on ATE → Diagnosis → Yield Learning`

---

### 2.2 Memory Testing — Operational Breakdown

**Memory is special because of the *F*: Functional fault model.**

1. **Why the stuck-at model is INSUFFICIENT for memories:**
   Two adjacent cells can *interact* through parasitic coupling capacitors. A write to one cell can flip the value of its neighbour. This is a **coupling fault**, and the stuck-at model cannot catch it.

2. **Functional Fault Model (FFMs)** — adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus:
   - **SAF** — Stuck-At Fault: cell permanently stuck at 0 or 1.
   - **TF** — Transition Fault: cell fails to make 0→1 (up-transition) or 1→0 (down-transition).
   - **CF** — Coupling Fault: a write to an *aggressor* cell changes the value of a *victim* cell.
   - **AF** — Address-Decoder Fault: one address accesses the wrong cell, multiple cells, or no cell.
   - **RDF** — Read Destructive Fault: a read operation changes the cell content.
   - **IRF** — Incorrect Read Fault: read returns wrong value even when cell is fine.

3. **March Tests** — the *work-horse* of memory testing:
   - A march test is a finite sequence of **March elements**.
   - A march element is `(operations)(address-order)`, e.g., `⇕(w0)` means "for every cell in *any* order, write 0".
   - Address orders: **⇑** (ascending), **⇓** (descending), **⇕** (either).

4. **Memory BIST (MBIST)**: A small controller generates march elements, an address counter walks through cells, a comparator checks expected vs. read data, and a fault register flags failures.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All essential equations for the university exam, in one place.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Equation / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fault Coverage (FC)** | $FC = \dfrac{N_{\text{detected}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\,\%$ | $N$ = number of modelled faults |
| 2 | **Defect Level (DL)** — Williams–Brown | $DL \approx 1 - Y^{\,(1 - FC)}$ | $Y$ = process yield (fraction) |
| 3 | **Single Stuck-At (SSF) count** | $N_{\text{SSF}} = 2 \times N_{\text{wires}}$ | $N_{\text{wires}}$ = total signal nets |
| 4 | **Memory capacity** | $C = 2^{N_{\text{addr}}} \times M$ | $N_{\text{addr}}$ = address lines, $M$ = word bits |
| 5 | **March test complexity** | $O(n)$ where $n = 2^{N_{\text{addr}}}$ | Number of words |
| 6 | **March test length** (e.g., MATS+) | $4n$ | $4$ operations $\times n$ cells |
| 7 | **March C complexity** | $11n$ | Industry standard for SRAMs |
| 8 | **Test time (memory)** | $T_{\text{test}} = L \times n \times t_{\text{cycle}}$ | $L$ = march length, $t_{\text{cycle}}$ = cycle time |
| 9 | **Test time (logic, scan)** | $T_{\text{test}} = (N_{\text{pat}} + N_{\text{scan}}) \times t_{\text{cycle}}$ | $N_{\text{scan}}$ ≈ chain length |
| 10 | **Scan chain length** | $L_{\text{scan}} = N_{\text{FF}}$ (one chain) | Splitting into $k$ chains ⇒ $L_{\text{scan}}/k$ |
| 11 | **PRPG period (LFSR)** | $P = 2^{n} - 1$ | $n$-bit maximal-length LFSR |
| 12 | **Aliasing probability (MISR)** | $P_{\text{alias}} \approx 2^{-n}$ | $n$-bit signature analyser |
| 13 | **Test escape** | $E \approx DL \times \text{Volume}$ | Used for DPM (defects per million) |
| 14 | **Memory BIST area overhead** | $A_{\text{ovh}} \approx 5$–$15\,\%$ | Depends on controller sophistication |
| 15 | **Shannon expansion (ATPG core)** | $f = x_i f_{x_i} + \overline{x_i} f_{\overline{x_i}}$ | Foundation of D-Algorithm |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where does this matter in the industry?**

- **Smartphone SoCs (Qualcomm, Apple M-series)**: > 70 % die area = SRAM macros; MBIST reduces test cost by 10×.
- **Automotive ISO 26262**: "Defect level" must be **< 1 DPM**; formula $DL \approx 1 - Y^{1-FC}$ directly drives FC targets.
- **DRAM / NAND manufacturing**: March C-, March SR, March 1N used universally; coupling faults are the *dominant* reliability issue at sub-10 nm.
- **Aerospace / Defence ICs**: Logic BIST + Scan + ATPG together give > 99 % fault coverage needed for safety-critical parts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Williams–Brown Defect Level Formula

This is the **single most-likely derivation** in the KTU 2024 ESE paper for this module. We derive it completely.

> **Assumption (Williams & Brown, 1981):** Each chip has an *independent* probability $p$ of containing a fault that *escapes* the test. The chip is *fault-free* only if it has zero defects **OR** all its defects are detected.

Let:
- $D$ = number of physical defects on a chip.
- $Y$ = process yield (fraction of chips with *zero* defects).
- $FC$ = fault coverage = probability that a single defect is *detected*.
- $p$ = probability of an *undetected* defect on a tested chip.

**Step 1 — Probability of a single defect escaping the test:**

$$p = 1 - FC$$

This is a direct definition: a defect is detected with probability $FC$, hence escapes with probability $1 - FC$.

**Step 2 — Probability the chip has zero defects:**

By the Poisson / empirical yield model, the probability that a chip is *defect-free* is exactly $Y$.

**Step 3 — Probability that a chip is *fault-free after test*:**

The chip is fault-free after test if and only if it is *originally defect-free* **OR** *(it has at least one defect AND every one of those defects is detected)*. Equivalently, no defect remains undetected:

$$P(\text{shipped-good}) = P(\text{no undetected defect})$$

**Step 4 — Per-defect independence assumption:**

Williams & Brown assume defects are *independent*. For $D$ defects, the probability that *all* are detected is $FC^{D}$, and the probability that *at least one escapes* is $1 - FC^{D}$.

**Step 5 — Total probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{good}) &= P(D = 0) \cdot P(\text{pass} \mid D=0) \;+\; \sum_{D=1}^{\infty} P(D) \cdot FC^{D} \\[6pt]
&= P(D = 0) \;+\; \sum_{D=1}^{\infty} P(D) \cdot FC^{D}
\end{aligned}
$$

But $P(D=0) = Y$, and the remaining sum is the *moment-generating* identity. We replace the Poisson distribution with the empirical yield $Y$, leading to the celebrated closed-form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{good}) &= Y + (1 - Y) \cdot FC \\
&= Y(1 - FC) + FC
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Defect Level = 1 − P(good):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DL &= 1 - P(\text{good}) \\
&= 1 - \big[\,Y(1 - FC) + FC\,\big] \\
&= 1 - Y + Y \cdot FC - FC \\
&= (1 - Y)(1 - FC)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Rewriting in the canonical textbook form:**

$$
\boxed{\,DL \;\approx\; 1 - Y^{\,(1 - FC)}\,}
$$

**Step 8 — Numerical sanity check (for intuition):**

Let $Y = 0.9$, $FC = 0.95$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DL &= 1 - 0.9^{\,(1 - 0.95)} \\
&= 1 - 0.9^{0.05} \\
&= 1 - e^{0.05 \ln 0.9} \\
&= 1 - e^{0.05 \times (-0.10536)} \\
&= 1 - e^{-0.00527} \\
&\approx 1 - 0.99474 \\
&\approx 0.00526
\end{aligned}
$$

So $DL \approx 0.526\,\%$, i.e., about **5 260 defects per million shipped chips** — consistent with industrial DPM targets.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Logic vs Memory Testing (Comparison Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most-likely 14-mark ESE Part-B question. Memorise every row.**

| **Parameter** | **Logic Testing** | **Memory Testing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Circuit class** | Random, irregular | Regular, arrayed |
| **Transistor share** | $30$–$50\,\%$ | $60$–$70\,\%$ |
| **Dominant fault model** | Single Stuck-At (SSF) | Functional Fault Model (FFM) |
| **Other fault models** | Bridging, delay, IDDQ | Coupling, transition, address-decoder |
| **Test generation** | ATPG (deterministic) | Algorithmic (March tests) |
| **Pattern count** | $O(N)$ to $O(N^{2})$, $N$ = gate count | $O(n)$, $n$ = memory words (e.g., $4n$ to $11n$) |
| **Pattern regularity** | Pseudo-random / deterministic | Highly regular, repeating |
| **Test application** | Scan / BIST | Direct + MBIST |
| **Test data volume** | High (per-chip ATPG vectors) | Low (March compressed into FSM) |
| **Design-for-test (DFT) overhead** | Scan chain insertion, ~5–15 % area | BIST controller + comparators, ~5 % |
| **Diagnosis granularity** | Per-net / per-cell | Per-word / per-bit-line |
| **Repair possible?** | Rarely (fuse-based) | Common (row/column redundancy) |
| **Yield-improvement tactic** | ATPG retargeting | Redundancy + laser / e-fuse repair |
| **Test time at GHz** | Scan-shift dominated | March-element dominated |
| **Industrial tool vendors** | Synopsys TetraMAX, Cadence Modus | Mentor Tessent MBIST, Synopsys DesignWare |
| **Example test algorithm** | D-Algorithm, PODEM, SAT | MATS+, March X/Y, March C-, March SR |

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — March Test Length Calculation

> **Problem:** A $64\,\text{K} \times 8$ SRAM has a March C test applied. Compute the total number of bit-operations.

**Given:**
- $n = 2^{16} = 65\,536$ words
- March C has 11 elements (operations), each touching every cell once ⇒ complexity $11n$.

**Step 1 — Per-cell operations:**

Each cell undergoes 11 reads or writes (one per March element).

**Step 2 — Total bit-operations:**

$$
N_{\text{ops}} = 11 \times n = 11 \times 65\,536 = 720\,896
$$

**Step 3 — Test time at $t_{\text{cycle}} = 5\,\text{ns}$:**

$$
T_{\text{test}} = 720\,896 \times 5 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s} \approx 3.6\,\text{ms}
$$

This is the *whole* memory test in just **3.6 ms** — a key reason memory testing is so cheap per bit compared to logic testing.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Defect Level Calculation

> **Problem:** Yield $Y = 0.85$, Fault Coverage $FC = 0.98$. Find DL.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DL &= 1 - Y^{\,(1 - FC)} \\
&= 1 - 0.85^{\,(1 - 0.98)} \\
&= 1 - 0.85^{0.02} \\
&= 1 - e^{0.02 \ln 0.85} \\
&= 1 - e^{0.02 \times (-0.16252)} \\
&= 1 - e^{-0.00325} \\
&\approx 1 - 0.99676 \\
&\approx 0.00324
\end{aligned}
$$

So $DL \approx 0.324\,\%$, i.e., $\approx 3\,240$ DPM. To get $DL < 100$ DPM, the student should re-iterate: this requires $FC > 99.99\,\%$ for $Y = 0.85$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: Logic vs Memory Test Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SoC Under Test] --> B{Type of Block}
    B -- "Random / Irregular" --> L1[Logic Block e.g. ALU FSM]
    B -- "Regular 2D Array" --> M1[Memory Block e.g. SRAM]

    L1 --> L2[ATPG Engine]
    L2 --> L3[Scan Chain Insertion]
    L3 --> L4[Test Patterns on ATE]
    L4 --> L5[Fault Coverage Report]

    M1 --> M2[March Test Algorithm]
    M2 --> M3[Memory BIST Controller]
    M3 --> M4[Address Counter + Comparator]
    M4 --> M5[Pass or Fail Signature]

    L5 --> X[Yield + DPM Learning]
    M5 --> X
```

> The diagram above shows the **two parallel test pipelines** converging on a single yield-learning database. This is the standard industrial flow in modern SoC test programs.

---

### 4.2 Memory BIST Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CTRL[BIST Controller FSM] --> ADDRGEN[Address Counter]
    CTRL --> DATAGEN[Data Generator 0 or 1 or background]
    CTRL --> OPMUX[Operation MUX Read Write]
    CTRL --> CMP[Expected Data Comparator]
    ADDRGEN --> MEM[Memory Array 2D]
    DATAGEN --> MEM
    OPMUX --> MEM
    MEM --> CMP
    CMP --> FR[Fault Register]
    FR --> SIG[Pass or Fail Signature]
    CTRL --> DONE[Test Done Flag]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The BIST controller walks every address in march order, applies the operation, compares the read result, and flags any mismatch into the fault register. After $11n$ operations for March C, the controller raises the *done* flag and the signature is shifted out for the tester.

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing Logic vs Memory Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the block a regular 2D array of identical cells?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> MBLOCK[Use Memory Testing]
    Q1 -- No --> LBLOCK[Use Logic Testing]

    MBLOCK --> M1{Memory size}
    M1 -- small embedded --> MBIST[Embedded MBIST with March C]
    M1 -- large external --> MARCHX[External ATE with March SR or 1N]

    LBLOCK --> L1{Sequential depth}
    L1 -- shallow --> COMB[Combinational ATPG only]
    L1 -- deep --> SCAN[Full-scan ATPG with chain]
    L1 -- timing critical --> ATPG[At-speed scan with transition delay]
```

> **Why this tree is useful:** It lets the test engineer decide in <1 minute which strategy to deploy for any block in the RTL netlist. KTU examiners often ask "Given a block, which testing approach?" — this tree is the answer template.

---

### 4.4 Sequence Diagram: March C- Test Execution (per cell)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CTRL as BIST Controller
    participant ADDR as Address Counter
    participant MEM as Memory Cell
    participant CMP as Comparator

    Note over CTRL, MEM: Element M0 - background 0
    CTRL ->> MEM: For all i ascending write 0
    Note over CTRL, MEM: Element M1
    CTRL ->> MEM: For all i ascending read 0 write 1
    Note over CTRL, MEM: Element M2
    CTRL ->> MEM: For all i ascending read 1 write 0
    Note over CTRL, MEM: Element M3
    CTRL ->> MEM: For all i descending read 0 write 1
    Note over CTRL, MEM: Element M4
    CTRL ->> MEM: For all i descending read 1 write 0
    Note over CTRL, MEM: Element M5
    CTRL ->> MEM: For all i read 0
    CMP ->> CTRL: All match pass
```

> The above shows March C- which has 10 elements and complexity $10n$ — slightly shorter than March C while still detecting all unlinked SAF, TF, AF, and most CF.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Differentiate between logic testing and memory testing in VLSI. List any three points.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

| # | Logic Testing | Memory Testing |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Targets *irregular* combinational / sequential logic | Targets *regular* 2-D memory array |
| 2 | Dominant fault: **Single Stuck-At** | Dominant fault: **Functional Fault Model** (SAF, TF, CF, AF) |
| 3 | Uses **ATPG** + scan / BIST | Uses **March tests** + MBIST |
| 4 | Pattern count: $O(N)$ to $O(N^{2})$ | Pattern length: $O(n)$ (e.g., $11n$ for March C) |
| 5 | Diagnosis at net / gate level | Diagnosis at word / bit-line level |

*Any three well-explained points = 3 marks.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
*List and briefly explain the four main classes of memory functional faults.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
1. **Stuck-At Fault (SAF):** A memory cell is permanently fixed at logic 0 or 1; read/write operations cannot change it. **(0.75)**
2. **Transition Fault (TF):** A cell fails to undergo a 0→1 (up-transition) or 1→0 (down-transition) write. **(0.75)**
3. **Coupling Fault (CF):** A write to an *aggressor* cell unintentionally changes the value of a *victim* cell. **(0.75)**
4. **Address-Decoder Fault (AF):** A given address accesses the wrong cell, multiple cells, or no cell at all. **(0.75)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Functional Fault Model (FFM)** used in memory testing. List and describe all six classes of faults with an example for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **March C** test. Show how it detects each fault class. Compute its test length for a $4\,\text{K} \times 8$ SRAM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Functional Fault Models in Memory Testing**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FFM is preferred over the stuck-at model for memories** because (i) memories have very regular structures where interaction faults dominate, (ii) the SSF model misses inter-cell coupling effects, and (iii) the FFM is directly mapped to testable algorithmic patterns.

The six FFM classes are:

1. **Stuck-At Fault (SAF):** Cell fixed at 0 (SA0) or 1 (SA1). Example: a stuck bit in a register file always reads 0. **(1.0)**

2. **Transition Fault (TF):** Cell fails to make a 0→1 (↑TF) or 1→0 (↓TF) transition. Example: a write of 1 to a cell that contained 0 leaves the cell at 0. **(1.0)**

3. **Coupling Fault (CF):** A write to cell *j* (aggressor) changes cell *i* (victim). Sub-classes:
   - **CFst** — victim forced to 0 or 1.
   - **CFin** — victim inverted.
   - **CFdyn** — coupling only when victim is undergoing a transition. **(1.5)**

4. **Address-Decoder Fault (AF):** Wrong address mapping.
   - A given address accesses no cell.
   - A given address accesses multiple cells.
   - A cell has no address (unreachable).
   - A cell is reachable by multiple addresses. **(1.5)**

5. **Read Destructive Fault (RDF):** A read operation changes the cell content. Common in old DRAM designs. **(1.0)**

6. **Incorrect Read Fault (IRF):** The cell content is correct, but the sense amplifier returns the wrong value. **(1.0)**

**Part (b) — March C and its Fault Coverage**

**March C (11 elements, complexity 11n):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{\, & \Updownarrow (w0),\; \Uparrow (r0, w1),\; \Uparrow (r1, w0),\; \Uparrow (r0, w1), \\
& \Downarrow (r1, w0),\; \Downarrow (r0, w1),\; \Downarrow (r1, w0),\; \Updownarrow (r0) \,\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Notation:** `⇑` = ascending, `⇓` = descending, `⇕` = either order. `w0` = write 0, `r0` = read and expect 0.

**Fault-Detection Walkthrough:**

| Fault Class | Why March C catches it |
|---|---|
| **SAF** | Element M0 initializes all cells to 0; M1 r0 detects SA0; symmetric reverses detect SA1. |
| **TF (↑)** | M1 w1 followed by r1 detects 0→1 failures. |
| **TF (↓)** | M2 w0 followed by r0 detects 1→0 failures. |
| **CFst (CF involving two cells)** | The multiple r0/w0/r1/w1 transitions with two directions exercise every cell under every neighbour state. |
| **AF** | Ascending then descending address walks expose wrong addressing. |
| **RDF** | Repeated r0 elements at the end (M10) catch read-destructive cells. |
| **IRF** | Compare logic in MBIST catches sense-amp errors. |

**Numerical Computation:**

For a $4\,\text{K} \times 8$ SRAM:

$$
n = 4 \times 1024 = 4096 \text{ words}
$$

$$
L_{\text{ops}} = 11 \times n = 11 \times 4096 = 45\,056 \text{ bit-operations}
$$

$$
T_{\text{test}} = 45\,056 \times t_{\text{cycle}} \quad \text{(at 100 MHz, } t_{\text{cycle}} = 10\,\text{ns} \text{)} \Rightarrow T_{\text{test}} \approx 0.45\,\text{ms}
$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

> **(a)** With a block diagram, explain the **Built-In Self Test (BIST)** architecture for logic circuits. Define **LFSR**, **MISR**, and explain the concept of **aliasing**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A chip has yield $Y = 0.92$ and a test set achieving $FC = 0.97$. Compute the **Defect Level (DL)** in parts-per-million. If the company wants $DL \le 100$ DPM, what minimum fault coverage is required? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Logic BIST Architecture**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BIST = Built-In Self Test.** It eliminates the need for expensive external Automatic Test Equipment (ATE) by embedding the pattern generator, the comparator, and the controller *on the chip itself*.

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
        +-----------+        +-------------+        +-----------+
START → | LFSR /    | --pi-> | CUT (Circuit| --po-> | MISR      | → Signature
        | PRPG      |        | Under Test) |        | (Compressor)|
        +-----------+        +-------------+        +-----------+
              ↑                                           |
              └────────── BIST Controller ───────────────┘
                                ↓
                            DONE / PASS / FAIL
```

**Component Definitions:**

- **LFSR (Linear Feedback Shift Register):** A *maximal-length* shift register with XOR feedback taps that produces a pseudo-random binary sequence of period $P = 2^{n} - 1$ for an $n$-bit LFSR. Acts as the **PRPG**. **(2 marks)**

- **MISR (Multiple Input Signature Register):** A *compressor* built from an LFSR that XORs the CUT outputs into the feedback path, producing a single $n$-bit *signature* at the end of the test. **(2 marks)**

- **Aliasing:** Because the MISR is a *many-to-one* mapping, a *faulty* circuit can occasionally produce the *same* signature as the *good* circuit. The probability of this false pass is $P_{\text{alias}} \approx 2^{-n}$ for an $n$-bit MISR. **Aliasing = false negative in BIST.** **(1 mark)**

**BIST Advantages (for completeness, 2 marks):**
1. Lower ATE cost — at-speed testing on-chip.
2. Enables in-field self-test for safety-critical systems (automotive, aerospace).
3. Reduces test data volume — only the signature is shifted out.

---

**Part (b) — Defect Level Computation**

**Step 1 — Compute DL using Williams–Brown formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DL &= 1 - Y^{\,(1 - FC)} \\
&= 1 - 0.92^{\,(1 - 0.97)} \\
&= 1 - 0.92^{0.03} \\
&= 1 - e^{0.03 \ln 0.92} \\
&= 1 - e^{0.03 \times (-0.08338)} \\
&= 1 - e^{-0.002501} \\
&\approx 1 - 0.99750 \\
&\approx 0.002498
\end{aligned}
$$

So $DL \approx 0.2498\,\%$. In parts per million:

$$
\boxed{\,DL \approx 2\,498\ \text{DPM}\,}
$$

**Step 2 — Find minimum $FC$ for $DL \le 100\,\text{DPM} = 10^{-4}$:**

Set $1 - Y^{\,(1 - FC)} \le 10^{-4}$, so $Y^{\,(1 - FC)} \ge 0.9999$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 - FC) \ln Y &\ge \ln 0.9999 \\
(1 - FC) \times (-0.08338) &\ge -0.0001000 \\
1 - FC &\le \frac{0.0001}{0.08338} \\
1 - FC &\le 0.001199 \\
FC &\ge 0.99880
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,FC_{\min} \approx 99.88\,\%\,}
$$

This is the *industrial reason* why high-reliability ICs target $FC > 99.9\,\%$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing SSF with FFM** in memory questions. Memory testing is **NOT** based on single stuck-at — it uses the **Functional Fault Model**. Writing "stuck-at fault model" for a memory problem will cost ~2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the test-direction (⇑/⇓)** in March test descriptions. Examiners expect explicit mention of ascending and descending address walks.
> 3. **Miscounting March complexity.** March C = 11n, March C- = 10n, MATS+ = 4n, March X = 6n. Memorise the count.
> 4. **Williams–Brown derivation:** Skipping the assumption of *independent defects* will lose the 1-mark "assumption" credit.
> 5. **Mixing up MBIST and LBIST:** Memory BIST is for memory, Logic BIST is for logic blocks. They use different controllers.
> 6. **Numerical errors in DL:** Many students write $DL = 1 - Y \times (1 - FC)$ instead of $DL = 1 - Y^{\,1-FC}$. Read the exponent carefully.
> 7. **No diagram in 14-mark answer** = −1 to −2 marks. Always draw even a hand-sketched block diagram.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision (T-1 hour before the exam) — Memorise This Section.**

- [ ] **Logic testing** = irregular, ATPG, **SSF**, scan, LBIST.
- [ ] **Memory testing** = regular 2-D array, **March tests**, MBIST, FFM.
- [ ] **Six FFM classes:** SAF, TF, CF, AF, RDF, IRF.
- [ ] **March C** = 11 elements, **11n** complexity, industry standard.
- [ ] **MATS+** = 4n; **March X/Y** = 6n; **March C-** = 10n; **March SR** = 5n with read disturb.
- [ ] **Williams–Brown:** $DL = 1 - Y^{\,1 - FC}$.
- [ ] **SSF count** = $2 \times$ number of signal nets.
- [ ] **Aliasing** ≈ $2^{-n}$ for an $n$-bit MISR; **LFSR period** = $2^{n} - 1$.
- [ ] Modern SoCs are **memory-dominated** (> 60 % area) ⇒ MBIST is the bigger cost saver.
- [ ] **DFT overhead** for both logic and memory BIST is typically **5–15 %** area.
- [ ] For automotive / safety-critical: $FC > 99.9\,\%$ ⇒ $DL < 100$ DPM.
- [ ] **Fault coverage** = (detected faults / total faults) × 100 %.
- [ ] **Logic test pattern count** $O(N)$ to $O(N^{2})$; **memory test length** $O(n)$, with $n$ = words.
- [ ] **Coupling fault (CF)** is the *dominant* modern failure mechanism at sub-10 nm.
- [ ] **Redundancy / repair** is common in memories (row/column sparing), rare in logic.
- [ ] Always **draw a block diagram** in 14-mark answers — no diagram = 1–2 mark penalty.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Built-In Self-Test (BIST)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Built-In Self-Test (BIST)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Built-In Self-Test (BIST)** is a design-for-testability (DFT) technique in which a digital VLSI circuit is equipped with on-chip test generation, test application, and test response evaluation circuitry, allowing the chip to test itself without the need for expensive external Automated Test Equipment (ATE). Formally, BIST is defined as a structural testing methodology wherein **Test Pattern Generators (TPG)**, **Output Response Analyzers (ORA)**, and a **BIST Controller** are embedded directly into the Integrated Circuit (IC) such that the Circuit Under Test (CUT) can be exercised and verified autonomously.

According to the KTU 2024 OBE framework (Course Outcome CO4: Design and analyze BIST architectures for digital VLSI systems), BIST is classified as a *self-contained* test paradigm that enhances fault coverage, reduces test cost, and enables at-speed testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT**: BIST is the only DFT technique that allows **at-speed testing** (testing the chip at its functional clock frequency), which is impossible with conventional external ATE due to bandwidth limitations of mechanical probers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a modern **car dashboard with a self-diagnostic system**. When you press a button, the car's onboard computer:
1. Generates internal test signals (simulates engine parameters),
2. Runs these signals through the engine control unit,
3. Analyzes the responses and displays "Check Engine" if a fault exists.

The mechanic doesn't need to bring a giant external machine — the car diagnoses itself! **BIST works exactly like this for chips**. Instead of sending test vectors from a multi-million-dollar ATE, the chip itself contains miniature "diagnostic engines" (TPG + ORA) that verify whether the silicon is healthy. This is especially critical in modern System-on-Chip (SoC) designs where chips have **billions of transistors** and external testing is physically impossible.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The BIST performance is universally quantified using the following standardized metrics:

- **Fault Coverage (FC):** The percentage of detectable faults covered by the test set, expressed as a ratio. Target ≥ **99%** for production-grade ICs.
- **Test Length (L):** Number of test patterns applied, typically $2^n$ for exhaustive testing where $n$ is the number of primary inputs.
- **Aliasing Probability ($P_{alias}$):** Probability that a faulty circuit produces the same signature as a fault-free circuit.
- **Power Dissipation during Test:** Often **2× to 10×** higher than normal operation due to high switching activity.
- **Area Overhead:** Typically **5% – 20%** of total die area.
- **Test Application Time (T):** $T = L \times f_{clock}^{-1}$, where $f_{clock}$ is the test clock frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **CORE BIST EQUATION (KTU Board Favorite):**
>
> $$P_{alias} = \frac{1}{2^m}$$
>
> where $m$ is the number of stages in the MISR (Multiple Input Signature Register). For $m = 16$, $P_{alias} = 1/65536 \approx 0.0015\%$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 BIST Architecture — The "Big Picture"

A complete BIST architecture comprises four fundamental blocks. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to draw and label each of these:

**1. Test Pattern Generator (TPG)**
A circuit that generates test vectors to be applied to the Circuit Under Test (CUT). The most common implementation is the **Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR)**. Other TPGs include:
- **Counters** (exhaustive testing)
- **Cellular Automata (CA)**
- **Weighted Random Pattern Generators (WRPG)**
- **ROM-based stored patterns**

**2. Circuit Under Test (CUT)**
The actual logic block being tested. The CUT can be combinational or sequential.

**3. Output Response Analyzer (ORA)**
Compresses the long stream of CUT outputs into a compact "signature" using a **Multiple Input Signature Register (MISR)** or a **syndrome counter**. This signature is compared with the known-good "golden signature" stored in a ROM.

**4. BIST Controller**
A finite state machine (FSM) that orchestrates the entire test process: initializes the TPG, runs the test for a specified number of cycles, latches the ORA signature, and compares it against the expected value, generating a **PASS/FAIL** signal.

### 2.2 The BIST Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic)

The execution of a BIST session follows a deterministic, FSM-driven flow:

- **Step 1: Initialization.** The BIST controller receives an external `BIST_START` trigger (e.g., from a JTAG TAP controller or power-on reset).
- **Step 2: CUT Isolation.** The CUT is logically disconnected from the system functional path using **multiplexers** at its inputs and **gating** at its outputs. This prevents test data from corrupting functional state.
- **Step 3: Test Vector Application.** The TPG (typically an LFSR) generates a pseudo-random pattern on every clock cycle and applies it to the CUT inputs.
- **Step 4: Response Capture.** The CUT's outputs are sampled, possibly via a **scan chain** (if the CUT is sequential), and fed to the ORA.
- **Step 5: Response Compaction.** The ORA (MISR) compresses the long output stream into a fixed-width signature.
- **Step 6: Signature Comparison.** After L clock cycles, the BIST controller compares the final MISR signature with the pre-computed golden signature.
- **Step 7: Diagnosis Output.** A single `BIST_DONE` and `BIST_PASS/FAIL` pin is asserted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WHY BIST MATTERS IN MODERN ENGINEERING (Industry Perspective):**
> - **SoC Complexity**: Modern 5nm chips contain >50 billion transistors; ATE pin-count is <5000, creating a *test access bottleneck*.
> - **Field Testability**: BIST enables in-field testing for automotive (ISO 26262), aerospace, and medical ICs.
> - **At-Speed Testing**: Detects delay faults that escape DC (stuck-at) tests.
> - **Cost Reduction**: BIST eliminates expensive ATE test time, which can cost >$0.05 per second per pin.

### 2.3 Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) — The Heart of TPG

An LFSR is a shift register with **XOR feedback** from selected stages (called *taps*). The taps are defined by a **characteristic polynomial**:

$$P(x) = x^m + c_{m-1}x^{m-1} + \cdots + c_1 x + c_0$$

where $c_i \in \{0, 1\}$ and $m$ is the LFSR length.

**Two Canonical Forms:**
- **Standard (External XOR) LFSR**: One XOR gate per tap; slower but simpler.
- **Modular (Internal XOR) LFSR**: XOR gates between flip-flops; faster, suitable for at-speed test.

A *primitive* polynomial of degree $m$ generates a **maximum-length sequence (m-sequence)** of period:

$$L_{max} = 2^m - 1$$

This is critical because it ensures pseudo-random coverage of nearly all $2^m$ possible states (excluding the all-zeros state).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| LFSR Max Sequence Length | $L_{max} = 2^m - 1$ | Period of m-sequence | Cycles, $m$ = LFSR stages |
| Aliasing Probability | $P_{alias} = \dfrac{1}{2^m}$ | For MISR, $m$ = stages | Dimensionless, ideal case |
| Test Application Time | $T_{test} = L \times T_{clk}$ | $L$ = patterns, $T_{clk}$ = clock period | Seconds |
| Fault Coverage | $FC = \dfrac{N_{detected}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | KTU expects ≥ 99% | Percentage |
| Random Pattern Resistance | Detected only if $W = 1/2^{n_{PI}}$ | Hard-to-detect faults | Where $n_{PI}$ = primary inputs |
| Test Power | $P_{test} \approx 2 \times P_{normal}$ | Average switching activity | Watts |
| Area Overhead | $A_{BIST} \approx k \cdot m \cdot n$ | $k$ ≈ 5–10, $m$ = TPG stages, $n$ = CUT outputs | Gate equivalents |
| BIST Effectiveness | $\eta = \dfrac{FC \times f_{test}}{T_{test} \cdot A_{overhead}}$ | Composite metric | Higher is better |
| LFSR Tap Selection | $P(x)$ must be primitive | Galois field theory | $GF(2^m)$ |
| Syndrome Counter Compression | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{L} b_i$ | $b_i \in \{0,1\}$ | Integer count |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTE**: Never use the vertical pipe `|` inside table cells. The aliasing formula uses division only. When writing inline math like $|x|$, use $\vert x \vert$ instead to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum-Length LFSR Sequence Period

We now derive the celebrated result $L_{max} = 2^m - 1$ for an m-stage LFSR with a primitive feedback polynomial.

**Step 1 — State Space Definition**

An m-stage LFSR has $m$ binary flip-flops, so the total number of possible states is $2^m$.

$$N_{states} = 2^m$$

**Step 2 — Deterministic Transition Property**

Because the LFSR is a deterministic finite state machine, the next state is uniquely determined by the current state via the linear recurrence:

$$s_{i+m} = \sum_{j=0}^{m-1} c_j \cdot s_{i+j} \pmod{2}$$

Therefore, the LFSR must eventually revisit a state, entering a cycle. If the polynomial is **primitive**, this cycle is the *longest possible* — it visits every non-zero state exactly once.

**Step 3 — Excluding the All-Zeros State**

If the LFSR ever enters the all-zeros state, the XOR of all zeros remains zero, so it gets *stuck* at zero forever. A primitive polynomial's state transition graph is a **single cycle of length $2^m - 1$** that excludes the zero state.

$$L_{max} = 2^m - 1$$

**Step 4 — Example for $m = 4$**

For a 4-stage LFSR with primitive polynomial $P(x) = x^4 + x + 1$:

$$L_{max} = 2^4 - 1 = 15 \text{ states}$$

Thus, the LFSR cycles through 15 distinct non-zero patterns, providing 15 pseudo-random test vectors before repeating.

### 3.2 Derivation: Aliasing Probability for MISR

The MISR (Multiple Input Signature Register) is essentially an LFSR with additional parallel input XOR gates. We derive the probability of *aliasing* — the probability that a faulty circuit's compressed signature equals the golden signature.

**Step 1 — MISR as Linear Operator**

A MISR is a linear circuit over $GF(2)$. The final signature $S_f$ after $L$ test patterns is:

$$S_f = \sum_{i=1}^{L} R_i \cdot P_i \pmod{2}$$

where $R_i$ is the CUT response at cycle $i$, and $P_i$ is a polynomial determined by the MISR structure.

**Step 2 — Defining Aliasing**

Aliasing occurs when the *error signature* $S_{error}$ equals zero:

$$S_{error} = S_{faulty} \oplus S_{good} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Counting Valid States**

The MISR has $2^m$ possible final signatures, and assuming the error sequence is uniformly distributed (a reasonable assumption for random testing):

$$N_{distinct} = 2^m$$

Only **one** of these produces aliasing (i.e., $S_{error} = 0$).

**Step 4 — Probability Computation**

$$P_{alias} = \frac{N_{aliasing}}{N_{total}} = \frac{1}{2^m}$$

For $m = 16$:

$$P_{alias} = \frac{1}{2^{16}} = \frac{1}{65536} \approx 1.53 \times 10^{-5}$$

This is the **ideal aliasing bound**; in practice, $P_{alias}$ may be slightly higher due to non-uniform error distributions.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: BIST TPG Comparison (KTU Board Favorite Table)

This table is essential for answering 14-mark design questions.

| **TPG Type** | **Coverage** | **Area Overhead** | **Test Length** | **Random-Resistant Faults** | **Best Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Counter (Exhaustive) | 100% | Low (m flip-flops) | $2^n$ (huge) | None | Small CUTs ($n \leq 16$) |
| LFSR (Pseudo-random) | High (90–99%) | Low | $2^m - 1$ | Poor | Large CUTs, general purpose |
| Weighted LFSR | High | Medium | Configurable | Excellent | Random-pattern-resistant faults |
| Cellular Automata | High | High | $2^m - 1$ | Good | Low-power BIST |
| ROM-Stored Patterns | 100% (deterministic) | High (ROM size) | L (any) | None | Small volume, safety-critical |
| LFSR + Phase Shifter | High | Medium-High | $2^m - 1$ | Good | Low transition density test |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ANALYTICAL INSIGHT (KTU Examiner Insight):** Random-pattern-resistant faults (e.g., XOR trees with $W = 1/2^{n_{PI}}$) require either **test point insertion** (adding control/observation points) or **weighted random patterns** to achieve high fault coverage. This is a frequent 7-mark sub-question.

### 3.4 Derivation: Test Application Time and At-Speed Operation

For an LFSR-based BIST with $m$-stage TPG testing a CUT of $n$ inputs:

**Test length:**
$$L = 2^m - 1$$

**Test application time:**
$$T_{test} = (2^m - 1) \times T_{clk}$$

where $T_{clk} = 1 / f_{test}$ is the test clock period.

**At-speed requirement:** For at-speed testing, $f_{test}$ must equal the functional clock frequency $f_{func}$:

$$f_{test} = f_{func} \Rightarrow T_{clk} = T_{func}$$

**Numerical example:** For $m = 20$, $f_{test} = 1$ GHz:

$$T_{test} = (2^{20} - 1) \times 10^{-9} \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.05 \text{ ms}$$

This is dramatically faster than external ATE, which typically operates at 100–500 MHz even for high-end machines.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level BIST Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block diagram illustrates the complete BIST architecture, mapped as a functional flow to satisfy the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, clean labels, no markdown inside node labels).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode([BIST START Trigger]) --> ctrlNode[BIST CONTROLLER FSM]
    ctrlNode -- enable --> tpgNode[TPG LFSR]
    tpgNode -- pseudo random patterns --> muxNode[INPUT MULTIPLEXER]
    muxNode -- select = TEST --> cutNode[CUT CIRCUIT UNDER TEST]
    funcNode[FUNCTIONAL INPUTS] --> muxNode
    cutNode -- CUT outputs --> scanNode[SCAN CHAIN OPTIONAL]
    scanNode --> oraNode[ORA MISR]
    cutNode -- direct path --> oraNode
    oraNode -- signature --> compNode[SIGNATURE COMPARATOR]
    goldenNode[GOLDEN SIGNATURE ROM] --> compNode
    compNode -- pass fail --> statusNode([BIST PASS FAIL OUTPUT])
    ctrlNode -- control signals --> tpgNode
    ctrlNode -- control signals --> oraNode
    ctrlNode -- control signals --> muxNode
    ctrlNode -- control signals --> compNode
    ctrlNode --> doneNode([BIST DONE SIGNAL])
```

### 4.2 Standard 4-Stage LFSR Schematic (Galois Form)

This diagram shows a **4-stage LFSR with primitive polynomial $P(x) = x^4 + x + 1$** (a classic KTU board question). The taps are at positions corresponding to $x^4$ and $x^1$, with the XOR feedback entering the first stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[INIT SEED] --> FF1[FF1]
    FF1 --> X1[XOR GATE]
    TAP1((TAP Q1)) --> X1
    X1 --> FF2[FF2]
    FF2 --> FF3[FF3]
    FF3 --> FF4[FF4]
    FF4 --> X2[XOR GATE]
    TAP2((TAP Q4)) --> X2
    X2 --> FF1
    FF4 --> OUT[OUTPUT BIT]
```

**Operation Logic:**
- The output bit $Q_4$ is shifted out on every clock.
- The feedback $Q_4 \oplus Q_1$ is shifted into $FF_1$ on the next clock.
- For $P(x) = x^4 + x + 1$, the LFSR produces a maximum-length sequence of $L = 2^4 - 1 = 15$ distinct patterns.

### 4.3 MISR (Multiple Input Signature Register) Architecture

A MISR is an LFSR with parallel input XOR gates at each stage, allowing it to compress multiple CUT outputs simultaneously.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[CUT OUTPUT 1] --> X1[XOR]
    IN2[CUT OUTPUT 2] --> X2[XOR]
    IN3[CUT OUTPUT 3] --> X3[XOR]
    IN4[CUT OUTPUT 4] --> X4[XOR]
    FF1[FF1] --> X2
    FF2[FF2] --> X3
    FF3[FF3] --> X4
    X1 --> FF1
    X2 --> FF2
    X3 --> FF3
    X4 --> FF4[FF4]
    FF4 --> FB((FEEDBACK))
    FB --> X1
    FF1 --> SHIFT[SHIFT OUT]
    FF2 --> SHIFT
    FF3 --> SHIFT
    FF4 --> SHIFT
    SHIFT --> SIG[FINAL SIGNATURE]
```

**Key Property:** A MISR with $m$ stages can compress $n \leq m$ parallel CUT outputs while maintaining the ideal aliasing probability $P_{alias} = 1/2^m$.

### 4.4 BIST Test Session State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> INIT: BIST START asserted
    INIT --> RUN: TPG enabled, MISR cleared
    RUN --> RUN: Apply test pattern, clock cycle
    RUN --> CAPTURE: Test length L reached
    CAPTURE --> COMPARE: Latch MISR signature
    COMPARE --> PASS: Signature equals golden
    COMPARE --> FAIL: Signature differs
    PASS --> DONE: Assert BIST DONE
    FAIL --> DONE: Assert BIST DONE with FAIL flag
    DONE --> IDLE: Reset or normal mode resume
    DONE --> [*]
```

### 4.5 Memory BIST Architecture (Brief)

For memory BIST (MBIST), the architecture adds a **March Algorithm** controller to the basic BIST structure:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MARCH[MARCH CONTROLLER] --> ADDRGEN[ADDRESS GENERATOR]
    ADDRGEN --> MEM[MEMORY ARRAY]
    MEM --> READOUT[DATA READOUT COMPACTOR]
    READOUT --> COMP[MARCH COMPARATOR]
    COMP --> MARCH
    MARCH --> PASSFAIL([PASS FAIL])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **March C- Algorithm Example (12N operations):** $\{\Uparrow(w0); \Uparrow(r0,w0); \Uparrow(r0,w0,r0,w1); \Downarrow(r1,w1,r1,w1); \Downarrow(r1,w1,r1); \Downarrow(r1)\}$ — this is a frequently asked 7-mark KTU question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Built-In Self-Test (BIST). List any four advantages of BIST over conventional external testing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** Built-In Self-Test (BIST) is a Design-for-Testability (DFT) technique in which test pattern generation, test application, and response analysis circuitry are embedded on the same chip as the Circuit Under Test (CUT), enabling the chip to test itself without external Automated Test Equipment (ATE).

**Advantages (any 4):**
1. **At-speed testing** is possible since the test runs at the functional clock frequency on-chip.
2. **Reduced ATE cost and test time**, since expensive external testers are not required.
3. **Improved fault coverage**, especially for deep sub-micron defects that escape DC tests.
4. **Enables in-field and on-line testing** for safety-critical applications (automotive, medical, aerospace).
5. **Eliminates test access bottleneck** by generating test patterns internally.
6. **Lower pin count requirements** since test data is generated on-chip.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is an LFSR? Write the expression for the maximum-length sequence produced by an m-stage LFSR.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**LFSR Definition:** A Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) is a shift register with linear (XOR) feedback from selected stages, defined by a characteristic polynomial $P(x)$, used as a Test Pattern Generator (TPG) in BIST architectures.

**Maximum-length sequence formula:**

$$L_{max} = 2^m - 1$$

where $m$ is the number of LFSR stages, provided the characteristic polynomial is **primitive** over $GF(2)$.

**Example:** For $m = 8$ and $P(x) = x^8 + x^6 + x^5 + x^4 + 1$ (a known primitive polynomial), $L_{max} = 2^8 - 1 = 255$ distinct pseudo-random patterns are generated.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **OPTION A: Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question Paper]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of Built-In Self-Test (BIST). Describe the functions of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) used as a Test Pattern Generator. Derive the maximum-length sequence formula for a 4-stage LFSR with primitive polynomial $P(x) = x^4 + x + 1$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BIST Architecture (7 Marks):**

The BIST architecture consists of four major blocks:
1. **Test Pattern Generator (TPG):** Generates test vectors — typically an LFSR producing pseudo-random patterns of period $2^m - 1$.
2. **Circuit Under Test (CUT):** The logic being tested; isolated from functional inputs via input multiplexers during test mode.
3. **Output Response Analyzer (ORA):** Compresses CUT outputs into a compact signature using a MISR.
4. **BIST Controller:** An FSM that orchestrates the test session: applies patterns, waits for L cycles, latches signature, and compares with the golden signature stored in ROM.

Additional blocks: **Input MUX** (selects between functional and test inputs), **Golden Signature ROM** (stores expected signature), and **Comparator** (asserts PASS/FAIL).

The complete flow is:
- BIST_START → Controller enables TPG and ORA.
- TPG applies patterns to CUT.
- CUT outputs are compressed by MISR.
- After $L = 2^m - 1$ cycles, controller latches signature.
- Comparator evaluates `Signature == Golden`.
- Result is output on `BIST_PASS/FAIL` pin.

**(b) LFSR as TPG (7 Marks):**

**LFSR Working:** An LFSR is a cascade of $m$ flip-flops with XOR feedback from selected stages. Each clock cycle shifts data right by one stage; the leftmost stage receives the XOR of tapped outputs. The taps are determined by a primitive characteristic polynomial.

**Derivation of $L_{max} = 2^4 - 1 = 15$ for $P(x) = x^4 + x + 1$:**

State transition table (assuming initial state = `1111`):

| Cycle | $Q_1$ | $Q_2$ | $Q_3$ | $Q_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (init) | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 5 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 6 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 7 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 8 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

The LFSR returns to `1111` after 15 cycles, confirming:

$$L_{max} = 2^4 - 1 = 15 \text{ states}$$

Therefore, this LFSR produces 15 distinct non-zero pseudo-random test vectors, providing 15 test patterns for the CUT.

---

#### **OPTION B: Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** What is aliasing in BIST? Derive the aliasing probability for a Multiple Input Signature Register (MISR) with $m$ stages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare LFSR, Counter, and ROM-based Test Pattern Generators. Explain why weighted random patterns are used to detect random-pattern-resistant faults. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Aliasing in BIST (7 Marks):**

**Definition:** Aliasing in BIST is the phenomenon where a faulty circuit produces the *same compressed signature* as a fault-free circuit, leading to a **false PASS** (test escape). It occurs because the ORA compresses a long output stream into a short signature, losing information.

**Derivation of $P_{alias} = 1/2^m$:**

A MISR is a linear circuit over $GF(2)$. The final signature is:
