$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{L} R_i \cdot M^i \pmod{2}$$

where $R_i$ is the CUT response at cycle $i$ and $M$ is the MISR transition matrix.

The error signature is $S_{err} = S_{faulty} \oplus S_{good}$.

Aliasing occurs when $S_{err} = 0$.

The MISR has $2^m$ possible final states. Assuming the error sequence is uniformly distributed over $GF(2^m)$, the probability that $S_{err}$ equals any specific value (including zero) is:

$$P_{alias} = \frac{1}{2^m}$$

**Numerical example:** For $m = 16$, $P_{alias} = 1/65536 \approx 0.0015\%$.

**Note:** This is the *ideal* bound; actual aliasing may be higher due to non-random error patterns.

**(b) Comparison of TPGs (7 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Counter** | **LFSR** | **ROM-based** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coverage | 100% (exhaustive) | ~99% (pseudo-random) | 100% (deterministic) |
| Test Length | $2^n$ (huge for large $n$) | $2^m - 1$ (compact) | $L$ (user-defined) |
| Area | Low | Low-Medium | High (ROM size) |
| Power | Low (1 transition/cycle) | High (~50% transitions) | Low (controllable) |
| Best For | Small CUTs | Large CUTs | Safety-critical |

**Weighted Random Patterns (Why):**

A fault is *random-pattern-resistant* if its detection probability under uniform random patterns is extremely low, e.g., $P_{detect} = 1/2^{n_{PI}}$ where $n_{PI}$ is the number of primary inputs. For such faults, achieving 99% fault coverage would require:

$$L \approx \frac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(1 - 1/2^{n_{PI}})} \approx 2^{n_{PI}} \text{ patterns}$$

This is computationally infeasible. **Weighted random patterns** solve this by biasing the probability of 0s and 1s in the test vectors. For example, a 0-weight of 0.9 and 1-weight of 0.1 at specific inputs dramatically increases the detection probability of AND-gate-type faults.

A **Weighted LFSR** uses multiple LFSRs with XOR-combination to produce biased patterns at specific input pins, achieving high fault coverage with manageable test length.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS:**
> 1. **Confusing MISR with SISR**: Students frequently write "Single Input Signature Register" instead of "Multiple Input Signature Register". Remember: MISR = multiple CUT outputs in parallel; SISR = single bit stream.
> 2. **Forgetting the All-Zeros Trap**: An LFSR with $L_{max} = 2^m - 1$ does *not* generate the all-zeros pattern. If you must include it, use $L = 2^m$ with explicit seed reset.
> 3. **Wrong Tap Positions**: For $P(x) = x^4 + x + 1$, taps are at $Q_1$ and $Q_4$, NOT at $Q_2$ and $Q_4$. Verify taps against the polynomial carefully.
> 4. **Aliasing Derivation**: KTU examiners deduct 2 marks if you don't show the assumption of uniform error distribution.
> 5. **Test Length Confusion**: For BIST, test length is determined by TPG (LFSR period), not by CUT size. Do not write $L = 2^n$ for LFSR-based BIST on a large CUT.
> 6. **BIST vs. Scan**: BIST is a *self-test* technique; Scan is a *test access* mechanism. They are complementary, not the same.
> 7. **Power Issue Ignored**: Mentioning test power consumption (2× normal) is essential for 14-mark design questions. Examiners reward this awareness.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **BIST = On-chip TPG + CUT + ORA + Controller.** Memorize this block diagram for instant 7-mark credit.
- ✅ **LFSR uses XOR feedback**; characteristic polynomial must be *primitive* for max-length sequence.
- ✅ **Maximum-length formula:** $L_{max} = 2^m - 1$. The "-1" excludes the all-zeros state.
- ✅ **MISR aliasing probability:** $P_{alias} = 1/2^m$. For $m=16$, $P_{alias} \approx 0.0015\%$.
- ✅ **Test Application Time:** $T_{test} = L \times T_{clk}$. At-speed test requires $f_{test} = f_{functional}$.
- ✅ **Fault Coverage Target:** $\geq 99\%$ for production-grade ICs (KTU expects this number).
- ✅ **Random-pattern-resistant faults** need weighted LFSRs or test point insertion.
- ✅ **BIST for Memory** uses March algorithms (e.g., March C- with 12N complexity).
- ✅ **Standard polynomials to memorize:** $P(x) = x^4 + x + 1$, $P(x) = x^8 + x^6 + x^5 + x^4 + 1$.
- ✅ **Aliasing is the #1 failure mode** in BIST — it causes false PASS.
- ✅ **Area overhead:** 5–20% is the industry-accepted range; trade-off with test quality.
- ✅ **BIST categories:** Offline (during manufacturing/idle) vs. Online (concurrent, during operation).
- ✅ **BIST reduces ATE pin count** from thousands to a few (BIST_START, BIST_DONE, BIST_PASS/FAIL).
- ✅ **Industry standards:** IEEE 1149.1 (JTAG) often coexists with BIST in modern SoCs.
- ✅ **Cellular Automata** are an alternative to LFSR for low-power BIST.
- ✅ **Golden signature** must be pre-computed via fault simulation — store in on-chip ROM.
- ✅ **BIST Controller** is typically a 4–5 state FSM (IDLE → INIT → RUN → COMPARE → DONE).
- ✅ **Syndrome counter** is a simpler ORA alternative (counts 1s in output stream) but has higher aliasing for certain fault distributions.

> [!TIP]
> **FINAL EXAM STRATEGY (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - For 7-mark BIST architecture questions, **always draw the block diagram first** — it earns 2 marks instantly.
> - For 14-mark design questions, **state formulas explicitly** ($L_{max}$, $P_{alias}$, $T_{test}$) before plugging in numbers.
> - Always conclude with **practical applications** (SoC testing, automotive, at-speed) to demonstrate CO4 (Design and analyze) mastery.
> - Use **bullet points and tables** in your answer sheet — KTU examiners scan for structured answers, and partial marking is more generous.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Test Pattern Generation (ATPG)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET525 - VLSI SYSTEM DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automatic Test Pattern Generation (ATPG)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automatic Test Pattern Generation (ATPG)** is the electronic design automation (EDA) process of generating a set of input stimulus vectors (test patterns) that, when applied to a digital circuit under test (CUT), enable the detection of specific manufacturing defects by distinguishing the faulty circuit behavior from the fault-free (golden) circuit behavior at primary outputs (POs) and/or scan flip-flop outputs.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, ATPG sits at the heart of **Design-for-Testability (DFT)** and is governed by the syllabus outcomes **CO4** (Apply fault modeling and test generation techniques to combinational and sequential circuits) and **CO5** (Analyze testability metrics and BIST architectures). ATPG formally operates on a **netlist** (gate-level structural description) together with a **fault list** (typically all collapsed *stuck-at* faults), and produces a **test set** *T* such that for every modeled fault *f* in the fault list, at least one test pattern *t* ∈ *T* activates and propagates the fault effect (the *D* or *D̄* symbol) to an observable point.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Doctor and the Patient:**
> Imagine a doctor is handed a sealed, complex electronic black box (the manufactured IC). She cannot open it, but she can poke specific wires (apply inputs) and look at specific lights (observe outputs). She has a manual of the **healthy behavior** (the golden netlist simulation). Her job is to find the *minimum number of pokes* that will guarantee to expose any *one* hidden short or open circuit inside the box.
> - The **black box** = the fabricated chip.
> - The **doctor's pokes** = the ATPG-generated test vectors.
> - The **hidden short/open** = a *stuck-at fault*.
> - The **doctor's manual** = the fault-free simulation database.
> - The **lights** = the primary outputs and scan-chain outputs.
>
> ATPG is essentially an automated doctor that, using mathematical logic (Boolean satisfiability, implication, sensitization), prescribes the optimal poking strategy.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (KTU Board Important)

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Boundary / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **FC** | Fault Coverage | $\mathbf{FC} = \frac{\text{Detected Faults}}{\text{Total Faults}} \times 100\%$ — Industry target $\geq$ **98.5 %** |
| **N** | Number of test patterns in test set *T* | Minimized via **test compaction** |
| **CL** | Combinational depth (levels of logic) | Affects backtrace difficulty |
| *k* | Number of backtracks per D-frontier decision | PODEM limit typically 5–9 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The term **"fault collapsing"** (equivalence and dominance collapsing) is a *favorite 3-mark definition question* and the reduction ratio $\approx$ **40–60 %** of the original fault list must be memorized.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ATPG Problem — Formal Decomposition

The ATPG engine must solve, for each fault *f*, a three-phase Boolean problem:

**Phase 1 — Fault Activation (Excitation):**
Set the value at the faulty site *s* to the *opposite* of the stuck-at value. Formally, for a *stuck-at-0* fault, drive logic **1** at *s*; for *stuck-at-1*, drive **0**. This produces the symbolic fault effect **D** (= 1 good / 0 faulty) or **D̄** (= 0 good / 1 faulty).

**Phase 2 — Fault Propagation (Sensitization):**
Select a path *P* from the fault site to a primary output such that every gate along *P* has its *non-controlling value* (NCV) on its *side inputs*. The NCVs are:
- AND / NAND → side input = **1**
- OR / NOR → side input = **0**
- XOR / XNOR → side input = **0** (or **1** — both sensitize)

**Phase 3 — Line Justification (Consistency):**
For every gate whose output has been assigned, find input assignments that *justify* it, recursively back to the primary inputs (PIs).

### 2.2 The Search-Space Nature of ATPG

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ATPG is a **decision problem** over Boolean assignments. For an *n*-input circuit, the search space is $2^n$. Pure exhaustive simulation is **exponential**, so all practical ATPG algorithms use *structural* + *heuristic* pruning. This is why ATPG sits in the complexity class **NP-complete** ( Ibarra & Sahni, 1975 ).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Symbolic Expression | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fault Coverage (FC) | $FC = \dfrac{\vert F_{\text{det}} \vert}{\vert F_{\text{total}} \vert} \times 100\%$ | $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes cardinality; use `\vert` in markdown |
| 2 | Defect Level (DL) | $DL = 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$ | $Y$ = manufacturing yield; Williams-Brown formula |
| 3 | Test Length *L* | $L = N \times d$ | $N$ = patterns, $d$ = scan-chain depth |
| 4 | Test Application Time (TAT) | $TAT = (L + d) \times T_{clk}$ | $T_{clk}$ = tester clock period |
| 5 | Fault Collapsing Ratio | $R_c = 1 - \dfrac{\vert F_{\text{collapsed}} \vert}{\vert F_{\text{full}} \vert}$ | Equivalence + dominance reduction |
| 6 | D-Algorithm D-Cube | $D \cdot \overline{D} = 0$ | *D* and *D̄* never both true |
| 7 | PODEM Objective | $\min(\text{backtracks})$ per decision | NP-hard heuristic |
| 8 | FAN Unique Sensitization | $\text{NCV-based single-path selection}$ | Faster than D-algo |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse **Defect Level** (DL, in *parts per million*, ppm) with **Fault Coverage** (FC, in %). The Williams-Brown cubic equation $DL = 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$ is a *very high-yield 14-mark derivation topic* — it is asked almost every December cycle.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Industry

ATPG-generated patterns feed the **Automatic Test Equipment (ATE)** such as Teradyne UltraFlex or Advantest V93000. In modern System-on-Chip (SoC) designs:
- A single 7 nm SoC has $\sim 10^9$ gates → $\sim 10^9 \times 2 = 2 \times 10^9$ collapsed stuck-at faults.
- ATPG tool (Synopsys TetraMAX, Cadence Modus, Mentor FastScan) must achieve **> 99 % FC** in **< 12 hours** wall-clock.
- Compression codecs (e.g., Embedded Deterministic Test, EDT) reduce tester data volume by 30–100×.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Williams-Brown Defect Level Formula

**Step 1 — Define defect level.**
Let $DL$ = probability that a shipped chip contains a *defect* (unmodeled physical fault). Industry measures it in **defects per million (DPM)**.

**Step 2 — Define yield.**
Let $Y$ = probability that a chip is fault-free (passes all tests of the *same* fault model). For a chip with $A$ total fault sites, each having independent fault probability $p$, yield is:
$$Y = (1-p)^{A}$$

**Step 3 — Define fault coverage rigorously.**
$FC$ = probability that a *random defect* will be detected by the test set, **conditional on the defect being of a type covered by the fault model**. The complement $(1 - FC)$ is the *escape probability* per fault.

**Step 4 — Probability that a faulty chip escapes all tests.**
A chip with exactly $k$ defects is faulty but may still pass if every defect lies outside the test's detection net. For *one* defect, escape probability is $(1 - FC)$. Assuming independence across defects:
$$P(\text{escape} \mid k \text{ defects}) = (1 - FC)^{k}$$

**Step 5 — Total escape probability (mix over defect count k).**
$$P(\text{escape}) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} P(\text{escape} \mid k) \cdot P(k \text{ defects}) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} (1-FC)^{k} \cdot \binom{A}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{A-k}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the Binomial expansion.**
Recall the identity:
$$(1-p + p(1-FC))^{A} = (1 - p \cdot FC)^{A} = \sum_{k=0}^{A} \binom{A}{k} p^{k}(1-FC)^{k}(1-p)^{A-k}$$

**Step 7 — Extract the $k \geq 1$ sum (subtract the $k = 0$ term).**
$$P(\text{escape}) = (1 - p \cdot FC)^{A} - (1-p)^{A}$$

**Step 8 — Express in terms of yield $Y = (1-p)^A$.**
$$P(\text{escape}) = (1 - p \cdot FC)^{A} - Y$$

**Step 9 — Apply the identity $(1 - p \cdot FC) = (1-p) + p(1-FC) = 1 - p + p - p \cdot FC$.** Raise to power $A$:
$$(1 - p \cdot FC)^{A} = \left[ (1-p) \left( 1 + \tfrac{p(1-FC)}{1-p} \right) \right]^{A} = Y \left( 1 + \tfrac{p(1-FC)}{1-p} \right)^{A}$$

**Step 10 — Approximation via the Williams-Brown model.**
For large $A$ and small $p$, the geometric term $\tfrac{p(1-FC)}{1-p} \approx \tfrac{p(1-FC)}{1}$ is small. Take the limit $A \cdot p \to \lambda$ (Poisson defect clustering, Williams 1981):
$$(1 - p \cdot FC)^{A} \approx e^{-\lambda \cdot FC} = Y^{FC}$$

**Step 11 — Final Williams-Brown formula.**
$$\boxed{DL = 1 - Y^{(1 - FC)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Doubling yield (e.g., from 50 % to 70 %) at constant FC reduces DL by roughly $(1 - 0.5^{1-FC}) \to (1 - 0.7^{1-FC})$. Even a *1 % improvement* in FC is more cost-effective than a *massive* yield-improvement campaign — a key exam justification.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Fault Coverage Calculation

Consider a circuit with **1000 collapsed stuck-at faults**. ATPG detects **972** of them.

**Step 1:** Identify detectable faults.
$$\vert F_{\text{det}} \vert = 972$$

**Step 2:** Identify total faults.
$$\vert F_{\text{total}} \vert = 1000$$

**Step 3:** Apply the FC formula.
$$FC = \frac{972}{1000} \times 100\% = 97.2\%$$

**Step 4:** Compute residual DPM at yield $Y = 0.85$.
$$DL = 1 - 0.85^{(1 - 0.972)} = 1 - 0.85^{0.028}$$
$$= 1 - e^{0.028 \times \ln 0.85} = 1 - e^{0.028 \times (-0.1625)} = 1 - e^{-0.00455}$$
$$\approx 1 - 0.99546 = 0.00454 = 4540 \text{ DPM}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ATPG Algorithms

| Property | D-Algorithm (Roth 1966) | PODEM (Goel 1981) | FAN (Fujiwara 1983) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Search space | Line assignments + D-cubes | **Primary inputs only** | Primary inputs + head lines |
| Backtrace target | Internal lines | **PIs only** | PIs + reconvergent *stem* lines |
| Multiple path sensitization | Yes (expensive) | Single path implicit | **Multiple simultaneous** |
| Backtracking | Cubic blowup possible | Bounded by 5 | Very low, near-optimal |
| Time complexity (worst) | Exponential | Exponential, **much lower constant** | Near-linear on many benchmarks |
| KTU exam status | Must know D-cube | **Most asked algorithm** | Asked for comparison |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Define the term **fault coverage**. Why is **99 %+ FC** a widely accepted industrial target?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Definition: $FC = \dfrac{\vert F_{\text{det}} \vert}{\vert F_{\text{total}} \vert} \times 100\%$.
- **(1 mark)** It represents the *fraction of modeled faults detected* by the generated test set.
- **(1 mark)** Below 99 % the residual defect level (per Williams-Brown $DL = 1 - Y^{1-FC}$) exceeds the **100 DPM Six-Sigma quality benchmark**, raising field-return and customer-escape risks exponentially — hence the industrial target.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
Distinguish between **fault activation** and **fault propagation** in the D-algorithm.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** *Fault activation* sets the faulty line to the **opposite** of its stuck-at value (e.g., line *s* stuck-at-0 is driven to **1** to produce symbol **D**).
- **(1 mark)** *Fault propagation* sensitizes a **path from the fault site to a primary output** by placing **non-controlling values** on the *side inputs* of every gate on the chosen path.
- **(1 mark)** Activation is the *cause* (creating D); propagation is the *effect* (carrying D to an observable point). The two phases are interdependent because line justifications of activation may destroy the chosen propagation path.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the **Williams-Brown defect level formula** $DL = 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a manufacturing process with yield $Y = 0.90$, plot (or tabulate) the relationship between **fault coverage FC** (from 90 % to 99.9 %) and the resulting **defect level in DPM**. Identify the FC at which DL falls below **500 DPM**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **(1 mark)** Define $Y$ as fraction of chips with no defects, $FC$ as the conditional detection probability per fault site.
2. **(1 mark)** Write escape probability for one fault: $(1 - FC)$.
3. **(1 mark)** For $k$ independent defects, escape probability is $(1 - FC)^k$.
4. **(1 mark)** Sum over the Binomial distribution of defect count: $P(\text{escape}) = (1 - p \cdot FC)^A - (1-p)^A$.
5. **(1 mark)** Substitute $Y = (1-p)^A$ and use the Poisson limit $A p = \lambda$: $(1 - p FC)^A \to e^{-\lambda \cdot FC} = Y^{FC}$.
6. **(1 mark)** Hence $DL = 1 - Y^{(1-FC)}$.
7. **(1 mark)** List assumptions: single-fault model, independent defects, large $A$, faults of modeled type only.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

Use $DL = 1 - 0.90^{(1 - FC)}$:

| FC (%) | $1 - FC$ | $0.90^{(1-FC)}$ | DL | DPM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 90.0 | 0.1000 | 0.7943 | 0.2057 | 205,720 |
| 95.0 | 0.0500 | 0.9019 | 0.0981 | 98,100 |
| 97.0 | 0.0300 | 0.9404 | 0.0596 | 59,600 |
| 99.0 | 0.0100 | 0.9786 | 0.0214 | 21,400 |
| 99.5 | 0.0050 | 0.9893 | 0.0107 | 10,700 |
| 99.9 | 0.0010 | 0.9979 | 0.0021 | 2,100 |

(1 mark for formula application, 1 mark for correct intermediate computation, 1 mark for at least 5 rows, 1 mark for the final identification)
- **(1 mark)** Working backwards: $0.90^{x} = 0.9995 \Rightarrow x \log 0.90 = \log 0.9995 \Rightarrow x = 0.000217$, so $1 - FC = 0.000217 \Rightarrow$ **FC ≈ 99.978 %** gives DL < 500 DPM.
- **(1 mark)** Conclude: achieving sub-500 DPM requires **FC > 99.97 %** — extremely tight, justifying modern test-point insertion.
- **(1 mark)** Curve description: DL drops super-linearly as $FC \to 1$; small FC improvements near 99 % yield massive DPM gains.

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified] (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Explain the **PODEM algorithm** with a flowchart. Why does PODEM restrict backtracing to **primary inputs only**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply PODEM to detect the **stuck-at-0 fault at line *a*** in the circuit:
- $F = A \cdot (B + C)$ ; $G = \overline{F}$ ; $H = B \cdot C$ ; $Y = F \oplus H$
- Identify the **minimum test vector(s)**, show backtrace and implication steps in a table. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **(1 mark)** PODEM = **Path-Oriented Decision Making**, by Prabhakar Goel (1981).
2. **(1 mark)** Algorithm phases: (i) choose an *objective* (e.g., set line to D), (ii) backtrace to a PI by traversing inverse NCV rules, (iii) set the PI to 0 or 1 and run *logic simulation/implication*, (iv) check if the test is achieved; otherwise *backtrack*.
3. **(1 mark)** Flowchart: PI selection → Objective → Backtrace → Imply → Check decision → D-frontier status.
4. **(1 mark)** Restricting backtrace to PIs: there are only $n$ PIs (vs. internal lines, which can be $\Theta(n^2)$), so the **decision tree has bounded depth**.
5. **(1 mark)** This reduces redundant decisions; the algorithm is not misled by internal line assignments that conflict with downstream propagation.
6. **(1 mark)** The result: **exponential worst-case** but with much smaller constant than D-algorithm; typically 5–9 backtracks per fault.
7. **(1 mark)** PODEM uses *X-path-check* to prune decisions that cannot propagate the D-frontier.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

Objective: set line *a* = 1 (since stuck-at-0 fault at *a*).

| Step | Decision (PI) | Implication | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Objective: *a* = 1 → backtrace to PI **A** | A = 1 | F = 1·(B+C) = B+C |
| 2 | Need D propagate through XOR(Y = F⊕H) | Need F ≠ H | F = B+C; H = B·C |
| 3 | Try **B = 0, C = 1** | H = 0·1 = 0, F = 0+1 = 1 | Y = 1⊕0 = 1 |
| 4 | Check D-frontier | D at F, propagated to Y | **DETECTED** ✓ |

- **(1 mark)** Correct identification: test vector **(A, B, C) = (1, 0, 1)**.
- **(1 mark)** Verify in fault-free circuit: F = 1, H = 0, Y = 1.
- **(1 mark)** Verify in faulty circuit (a stuck-at-0): F = 0, H = 0, Y = 0.
- **(1 mark)** Y differs: fault observed at output → detection confirmed.
- **(1 mark)** Show second minimum vector (A, B, C) = (1, 1, 0) — also detects, due to symmetry.
- **(1 mark)** Total minimum 2 tests for *a* SA0 (after equivalence collapse).
- **(1 mark)** Brief remark: this is a **combinational** test; for sequential would require 5-pattern sequence (Initialize → Activate → Propagate).

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-mark Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing FC with DL**: They are *not* the same. FC is a *test-quality* metric; DL is the *shipped-quality* metric. The Williams-Brown equation links them — examiners deduct 2 marks if you don't quote it.
> 2. **Skipping the Poisson-limit assumption** in the derivation. Without it, you get $(1 - p FC)^A$, which is *not* the Williams-Brown formula. Always state the assumption explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the *X-path-check* in PODEM**: A PODEM backtrace that doesn't verify an *X-path* to an output can never succeed — but many students omit this check, losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Using *|x|* (vertical pipe) inside a markdown table cell**: This *breaks* table rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` (LaTeX) for absolute value inside KTU formula tables.
> 5. **Stating "PODEM = O(n)" or "PODEM = polynomial"**: It is **NP-hard in the worst case**. A defensible claim is "PODEM achieves linear-like behavior on most ISCAS-85 benchmarks".
> 6. **Skipping fault collapsing**: Examiners award 1 mark for invoking **equivalence** (F = stuck-at-0 ≡ F' stuck-at-1 through an inverter) and **dominance** (one fault's test set contains the other's).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **ATPG = automated generation of test vectors** from netlist + fault list.
- **Three phases**: Activation → Propagation → Justification.
- **D-Algorithm** uses **primitive D-cubes of failure (PDCF)** and **D-intersection**.
- **PODEM** searches over **PIs only**; bounded backtrack (~5).
- **FAN** introduces *head lines* and *multiple-path sensitization* — fastest classical algorithm.
- **Fault Coverage (FC)** = detected / total faults; **Defect Level (DL)** is in DPM.
- **Williams-Brown**: $DL = 1 - Y^{1-FC}$; memorize derivation steps.
- **Fault collapsing** (equivalence + dominance) cuts fault list by 40–60 %.
- **Untestable faults** are *proven* (not assumed) — modern ATPG uses SAT solvers.
- **Industrial tools**: Synopsys **TetraMAX**, Cadence **Modus**, Siemens EDA **FastScan / Tessent**.
- **Modern SoC target**: FC $\geq$ 99 %, ATPG runtime $\leq$ 12 h, pattern count $\leq$ 10 K.
- **Test compression (EDT, Illinois Scan)** reduces tester data volume by 30–100×.
- **Sequential ATPG** is *much harder* — motivates **full-scan DFT**.
- **Idempotency rule for implication**: X → 0 if all inputs of AND/ NAND are 0; *don't confuse with X → 1*.
- **KTU 2024 most-asked algorithms**: PODEM (algorithm + flowchart) and Williams-Brown (derivation).
- **Memorize the 6-valued logic symbols** (0, 1, X, D, D̄, and the static/dynamic hazards if asked for *transition fault* ATPG).
- **Re-read the CO4–CO5 mapping** in the syllabus — questions tagged CO4 are usually 7-markers, CO5 are 14-markers.
